translations.source: Ibà Afàbíìgbín (Bilharzia) Ìsọnísókí: Ibà afàbíìgbín jẹ́ àìsàn tí òsì ń fà èyí tí ó ń yọrí sí àìlera ńlá. Wọ́n máa ń kó àrùn yìí nígbà tí ènìyàn bá ní ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú omi odò tí ó ti lùgbàdì pẹ̀lú àwọn ẹ̀yà kòkòrò (cercariae) ajọ̀fẹ́ mùjẹ̀mùjẹ̀ tí a mọ̀ sí sísítósóòmù. Àwọn àgbà kòkòrò wẹ́wẹ́ aláìṣeéfojúrí náà ń gbé nínú àwọn isan tí ó ń gbé ìdọ̀tí kúrò ní òpónà ìtọ̀ àti ìfun. Púpọ̀ àwọn ẹyin tí wọ́n yé máa ń há sínú àwọn ìṣù ara bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni èsì ara sí wọn lè fa ìdíbàjẹ́ ńlá.
translations.reference: Schistosomiasis (Bilharzia) Overview: Schistosomiasis is a disease of poverty that leads to chronic ill-health. Infection is acquired when people come into contact with fresh water infested with the larval forms (cercariae) of parasitic blood flukes, known as schistosomes. The microscopic adult worms live in the veins draining the urinary tract and intestines. Most of the eggs they lay are trapped in the tissues and the body’s reaction to them can cause massive damage.
translations.hypothesis: Schistosomiasis (Bilharzia) Overview: Schistosomiasis is a disease caused by parasitic worms that can lead to serious health problems. It is contracted when people come into contact with contaminated freshwater containing parasitic larvae called cercariae. These larvae penetrate the skin and develop into adult worms that live in blood vessels. The worms lay eggs, and many of these eggs become trapped in the body, causing inflammation and damage.

translations.source: Ibà afàbíìgbín mú bí mílíọ̀nù 240 ènìyàn káàkiri àgbáyé, àti ó lé ní ènìyàn mílíọ̀nù 700 ni wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn àgbègbè àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn. Àrùn náà wọ́pọ̀ ní àwọn àgbègbè ipa òòrùn àti àyíká agbègbè ipa òòrùn, ní àwọn àwùjọ tí kò lówó láìsí omi ẹ̀rọ àti ìmọ́tótó tó tó. Kòkòrò Sikisotómà amatóbíọ́ọ̀mù àti sikisotómà ajẹmọ́fun ni ó ń fa ibà afàbíìgbín ajẹmópòónà-òun-ìbálòpọ̀ láti ọwọ́ èyíkéyìí àwọn ajọ̀fẹ́ S. guineensis, S. intercalatum, S. mansoni, S. japonicum, àti S. mekongi. Ẹgbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ènìyàn káàkiri àgbáyé ní àrùn líle nítorí ibà afàbíìgbín. Ọgbọ́n WHO lórí ìlò àwọn òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò ajọ̀fẹ́ nísinsìnyí mú kí ó ṣe é ṣe láti ṣàkóso ibà afàbíìgbín ní àwọn àwùjọ olósì àti àwùjọ adẹ́yẹsí, ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú ìpèsè tako lìǹfàtíìkì filaríásíìsì, onkokasíásí àti àwọn kòkòrò ajọ̀fẹ́ tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn.
translations.reference: Schistosomiasis affects almost 240 million people worldwide, and more than 700 million people live in endemic areas. The infection is prevalent in tropical and sub-tropical areas, in poor communities without potable water and adequate sanitation. Urogenital schistosomiasis is caused by Schistosoma haematobium and intestinal schistosomiasis by any of the organisms S. guineensis, S. intercalatum, S. mansoni, S. japonicum, and S. mekongi. Several million people all over the world suffer from severe morbidity as a consequence of schistosomiasis. The WHO strategy on use of anthelminthic drugs now makes it possible to control schistosomiasis in poor and marginalized communities, in conjunction with interventions against lymphatic filariasis, onchocerciasis and soil transmitted helminthiasis.
translations.hypothesis: Schistosomiasis affects an estimated 240 million people worldwide, with 700 million living in endemic areas. The disease is most prevalent in tropical and subtropical regions, particularly in areas with poor sanitation and limited access to clean water. It is caused by parasitic worms of the genus *Schistosoma*, transmitted through contact with freshwater contaminated with the worms' eggs. The most common species responsible for human disease are *S. mansoni*, *S. haematobium*, *S. japonicum*, *S. mekongi*, and *S. guineensis*. Many people around the world suffer from chronic illness as a result of schistosomiasis. The World Health Organization's (WHO) efforts to control schistosomiasis through the use of preventative drugs and improved sanitation have been successful in reducing the burden of the disease in many communities, in collaboration with programs focused on neglected tropical diseases, cancer, and other infectious diseases.

translations.source: Ní àwọn àgbègbè àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn, àrùn líle nítorí ibà afàbíìgbín lè ṣe é dènà ìtọ́jú ojoojúmọ́ àwọn ẹgbẹ́ tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu tí wọ́n fojúsùn dá lórí àyẹ̀wò àwùjọ nítorí àwọn àkójọpọ̀ ẹ̀sọ́. Wọ́n ti ṣàmúlò Praziquantel pẹ̀lú albendazole àti ivermectin dáadáa, ní àwọn àgbègbè tí àwọn òògùn wọ̀nyí ti jẹ́ ṣíṣe àmúlò ní ọ̀tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀ fún kemotẹ́ráfì aláàbò. Àmì àìsàn: Àwọn àmì àìsàn ibà afàbíìgbín ní àbájáde ara tako àwọn ẹyin kòkòrò náà máa ń fà. Ibà afàbíìgbín ajẹmópòónà ọ̀fun lè fa inú rírun, ìgbẹ́ gbuuru, àti ẹ̀jẹ̀ nínú ìgbẹ́. Ìtóbi ẹ̀dọ̀ wọ́pọ̀ ní àwọn ọ̀ràn líle, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó sáábà máa ń ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú omi tí ó sarajọ sínú káà inú àti ẹ̀jẹ̀ rírú àwọn isan abo inú.
translations.reference: In highly endemic areas, severe morbidity due to schistosomiasis can be prevented by regular treatment of at risk groups targeted based on community diagnosis based on sentinel groups. Praziquantel has been safely co-administered with albendazole and ivermectin, in areas where these drugs have been used separately for preventive chemotherapy. Symptoms: Symptoms of schistosomiasis are caused by the body’s reaction to the worms' eggs. Intestinal schistosomiasis can result in abdominal pain, diarrhoea, and blood in the stool. Liver enlargement is common in advanced cases, and is frequently associated with an accumulation of fluid in the peritoneal cavity and hypertension of the abdominal blood vessels.
translations.hypothesis: In areas where parasitic diseases are prevalent, schistosomiasis can significantly impact the daily lives of individuals at risk, particularly those involved in water-related activities due to the presence of snails. Praziquantel, often combined with albendazole and ivermectin, is commonly used for mass drug administration programs to control schistosomiasis. Symptoms: The symptoms of schistosomiasis vary depending on the species of parasite and the stage of infection. Schistosomiasis caused by *Schistosoma haematobium* can cause hematuria (blood in the urine), dysuria (painful urination), and lower abdominal pain. Other symptoms may include fever, chills, muscle aches, and fatigue. In severe cases, schistosomiasis can lead to organ damage, including the liver, spleen, and intestines.

translations.source: Nínú aowm ọ̀ràn wọ̀nyí ìtóbi àbaadì òróòro náà lè wáyé. Àpẹẹrẹ gbòógì ibà afàbíìgbín ajẹmópòónà ìtọ̀-ojú-ara ni haematuria (ẹ̀jẹ̀ nínú ìtọ̀). Ìnípọn àbaadì àpò ìtọ̀ àti òpónà ìtọ̀, àti ìdíbàjẹ́ kíndìnrín sáábà máa ń jẹ́ ṣíṣe àyẹ̀wò nínú àwọn ọ̀ràn líle. Jẹjẹrẹ àpò ìtọ̀ tún jẹ́ ìṣòro mìíràn tí ó ṣe é ṣe kó farahàn ní àwọn ìpele ọjọ́ iwájú. Láàrin àwọn obìnrin, ibà afàbíìgbín ajẹmópòónà ìtọ̀-òun-ojú-ara lè farahàn pẹ̀lú kókó lójú ara, ojú ara obìnrin tí ó ń sun ẹ̀jẹ̀, ìrora lásìkò ìbálòpọ̀, àti rorẹ́ ojú òbò.
translations.reference: In such cases there may also be enlargement of the spleen. The classic sign of urogenital schistosomiasis is haematuria (blood in urine). Fibrosis of the bladder and ureter, and kidney damage are sometimes diagnosed in advanced cases. Bladder cancer is another possible complication in the later stages. In women, urogenital schistosomiasis may present with genital lesions, vaginal bleeding, pain during sexual intercourse, and nodules in the vulva.
translations.hypothesis: In this condition, abnormalities in the urinary tract can develop. A common symptom of urinary tract abnormalities is hematuria (blood in the urine). Other symptoms to look out for include frequent urination, painful urination, and lower back pain. Urinary tract infections are another common cause of these symptoms. In women, urinary tract abnormalities can also manifest as vaginal discharge, pelvic pain, difficulty getting pregnant, and eye problems.

translations.source: Láàárín àwọn ọkùnrin, ibà afàbíìgbín ajẹmópòónà ìtọ̀-òun-ojú-ara lè fa àìsàn ọ̀pá àtọ̀, jẹjẹrẹ ìṣétọ̀, àti àwọn ẹ̀yà ara mìíràn. Àìsàn yìí tún lè ní àwọn ìṣòro ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ tí kò ṣe é yí padà mìíràn, tí ó fi mọ́ àìlèbímọ. Àwọn ipa ibà afàbíìgbín lórí ọrọ̀ ajé àti ìlera tóbi bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àìsàn náà sọni di aláàbọ̀ ara ju kí ó pani lọ. Láàárín àwọn ọmọdé, ibà afàbíìgbín lè fa àìlẹ́jẹ̀tó, rírán àti àdínkù agbára láti kẹ́kọ̀ọ́, bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé àwọn ipa rẹ̀ sáábà máa ń ṣe é yípadà pẹ̀lú ìtọ́jú. Ìtọ́jú: Praziquantel ni ìtọ́jú tí wọ́n lù ní ọ̀ǹtẹ̀ tako gbogbo àwọn ẹ̀yà ibà afàbíìgbín.
translations.reference: In men, urogenital schistosomiasis can induce pathology of the seminal vesicles, prostate, and other organs. This disease may also have other long-term irreversible consequences, including infertility. The economic and health effects of schistosomiasis are considerable and the disease disables more than it kills. In children, schistosomiasis can cause anaemia, stunting and a reduced ability to learn, although the effects are usually reversible with treatment. Treatment: Praziquantel is the recommended treatment against all forms of schistosomiasis.
translations.hypothesis: In adults, parasitic infections of the eye can cause vision problems, inflammation, and other complications. These infections can also lead to long-term damage, potentially resulting in blindness. The impact of these infections on a person's quality of life and ability to work or attend school can be significant. In children, parasitic eye infections can cause discomfort, irritation, and difficulty learning, although the effects are often less severe. Treatment: Praziquantel is a medication commonly used to treat most types of parasitic eye infections.

translations.source: Ó kójúwọ̀n, ó ní ààbò, àti ó jẹ́ olówó péréte. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé àrùn náà tún lè sọ lẹ́yìn ìtọ́jú, ewu níní àìsàn ńlá ti dín kù bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ti fà sẹ́yìn nígbà tí ìtọ́jú bá ti di àgbékalẹ̀ àti àṣetúnṣe lásìkò èwe. Àkóso ibà afàbíìgbín ni wọ́n ti ṣàmúlò ní ó lé ní àwọn ọdún 40 káàkiri àwọn onírúurú orílẹ̀ èdè, tí ó fi mọ́ Brazil, Cambodia, China, Egypt, Mauritius, Islamic Republic of Iran, Oman, Jordan, Saudi Arabia, Morocco, Tunisia, abbl. Ní Burundi, Burkina Faso, Ghana, Niger, Rwanda, Sierra Leone, the United Republic of Tanzania, àti Yemen, ó ti ṣe é ṣe láti mú ìdàgbàsókè bá ìtọ́jú ibà afàbíìgbín dé ìpele orílẹ̀ èdè bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó nípa lórí àrùn náà ní ọdún díẹ̀. Ìgbéléwọ̀n ipò ìkóràn ṣe pàtàkì ní àwọn onírúurú orílẹ̀ èdè.
translations.reference: It is effective, safe, and low-cost. Even though re-infection may occur after treatment, the risk of developing severe disease is diminished and even reversed when treatment is initiated and repeated in childhood. Schistosomiasis control has been successfully implemented over the past 40 years in several countries, including Brazil, Cambodia, China, Egypt, Mauritius, Islamic Republic of Iran, Oman, Jordan, Saudi Arabia, Morocco, Tunisia, etc. In Burundi, Burkina Faso, Ghana, Niger, Rwanda, Sierra Leone, the United Republic of Tanzania, and Yemen, it has been possible to scale-up schistosomiasis treatment to the national level and have an impact on the disease in a few years. An assessment of the status of transmission is required in several countries.
translations.hypothesis: It is highly contagious, causes severe symptoms, and can be fatal. While the disease can sometimes be treated, the risk of serious complications is significant, especially if treatment is delayed and the infection becomes widespread. The World Health Organization has been working for over 40 years with various countries, including Brazil, Cambodia, China, Egypt, Mauritius, the Islamic Republic of Iran, Oman, Jordan, Saudi Arabia, Morocco, Tunisia, and others, to control and prevent the spread of this disease. In countries like Burundi, Burkina Faso, Ghana, Niger, Rwanda, Sierra Leone, the United Republic of Tanzania, and Yemen, efforts have been made to integrate the control of this disease into national health programs over the past few years. Surveillance and monitoring of the disease are crucial in all countries.

translations.source: Fún ọdún 10 sẹ́yìn, àwọn ìpolongo ìdàgbàsókè ìtọ́jú ti wà ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè àyíká Sahara Áfríkà, níbi tí púpọ̀ àwọn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu ń gbé.
translations.reference: Over the past 10 years, there has been scale-up of treatment campaigns in a number of sub-Saharan countries, where most of those at risk live.
translations.hypothesis: For the past 10 years, various drought relief programs have been implemented in many countries across the Sahara region of Africa, where a significant portion of the population lives in vulnerable conditions.

translations.source: Àwọn ìdásí ìtọ́jú-araẹni fún ìlera Ìsọnísókí: Ìtọ́jú araẹni ni agbára àwọn ènìyàn, ẹbí àti àwùjọ láti ṣe àgbéga ìlera, ìdènà àìsàn, àkóso ìlera, àti faradà àìsàn àti ààbọ̀ ara pẹ̀lú àti láìsí àtìlẹ́yìn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera. Ní báyìí bílíọ́nù 3.6 ènìyàn - ìdajì àgbáyé - kò ní àǹfààní sí àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì. WHO dábàá àwọn ìpèsè ìtọ́jú araẹni fún orílẹ̀ èdè kọ̀ọ̀kan àti ibùdó ọrọ̀ ajé, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀nà pàtàkì láti láti ṣe ìpèsè ìlera káríayé, ìpolongo ìlera, ìdáàbòbò ayé, àti ìpèsè fún àwọn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu. Ìtọ́jú araẹni ṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ pàtàkì nínú ṣíṣe àkóso ìlera ara wọn, ní àwọn àgbègbè tí ó fi mọ́ ìpolongo ìlera, ìdènà àti àkóso àìsàn; lílo òògùn fúnra ẹni; ìpèsè ìtọ́jú fún àwọn àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n nílò ìrànlọ́wọ́, àti imúpàdàbọ̀sípò, tí ó fi mọ́ ìtọ́jú ìrànwọ́. Ìpèsè ìtọ́jú araẹni jẹ́ àwọn irinṣẹ́ adálórí-ẹ̀rí, tí ó dára tí wọ́n ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ìtọ́jú araẹni.
translations.reference: Self-care interventions for health Overview: Self-care is the ability of individuals, families and communities to promote health, prevent disease, maintain health, and cope with illness and disability with or without the support of a health worker.Currently 3.6 billion people – half of the world – lack access to essential health services. WHO recommends self-care interventions for every country and economic setting, as a critical path to reach universal health coverage, promote health, keep the world safe, and serve the vulnerable. Self-care recognises individuals as active agents in managing their own health care, in areas including health promotion; disease prevention and control; self-medication; providing care to dependent persons, and rehabilitation, including palliative care. Self-care interventions are evidence-based, quality tools that support self-care.
translations.hypothesis: Telemedicine for Health: Telemedicine empowers individuals, families, and communities to improve health outcomes, manage chronic conditions, access care, and prevent disease and promote wellness with or without the direct involvement of healthcare professionals. Currently, 3.6 billion people worldwide lack access to essential health services. The World Health Organization (WHO) advocates for telemedicine as a critical strategy for global health, healthcare delivery, disease prevention, and care for vulnerable populations. Telemedicine enables individuals to connect with healthcare professionals for essential services, including managing chronic conditions, accessing preventive care, using medications effectively, receiving remote monitoring, and accessing emergency care. Telemedicine services are cost-effective and can improve access to care.

translations.source: Lára wọn ni òògùn, igbaninímọ̀ràn, àyẹ̀wò àti/tàbí àwọn ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ òde òní èyí tí ènìyàn lè ní àǹfààní sí ní pípé tàbí níwọ̀n níta àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera elétò. Dá lórí ìdásí náà, wọ́n lè jẹ́ lílò pẹ̀lú tàbí láìsí àmójútó àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera tààrà. Àwọn ìdásí ìtọ́jú araẹni lè: ṣíṣe ìrónilágbára fún àwọn ènìyàn àti àwùjọ láti ṣàkóso ìlera àti àlàáfíà wọn. ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn ibùdó orílẹ̀ èdè pẹ̀lú ìlò àwọn ohun èlò abẹ́lé pípé fún ìlera.
translations.reference: They include medicines, counselling, diagnostics and/or digital technologies which can be accessed fully or partially outside of formal health services. Depending on the intervention, they can be used with or without the direct supervision of health workers. Self-care interventions can: empower individuals and communities to manage their health and well-being strengthen national institutions with efficient use of domestic resources for health
translations.hypothesis: These include medications, diagnostic tools, and/or modern technologies that can be used to improve or manage various health conditions, particularly within the context of modern healthcare. They can be used with or without the direct involvement of healthcare professionals. These advancements can: improve the quality of life for individuals and communities by helping them manage their health and well-being; facilitate the development of national healthcare systems through the use of advanced medical technologies; and promote research and innovation in the field of medicine.

translations.source: mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ìtọ́jú ìlera abẹ́lé (IIA) àti ipa láti ṣe àṣeyọrí ìtọ́jú ìlera káríayé. Ìlànà: Ìlànà ìpèsè ìtọ́jú araẹni adálórí-ènìyàn ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn àìní àti ẹ̀tọ́ àwọn ènìyàn nípasẹ̀ ìlànà adálórí-èèyàn tí ó gbilẹ̀ nínú ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn àti ìṣedéédé fún takọtabo. Èyí jẹ́ ìlànà gbòòrò sí ìlera àti àlàáfíà èèyàn kọ̀ọ̀kan, nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn ìṣòro wọn, àìní àti ìfẹ́ wọn káàkiri ìgbésí ayé, àti irúfẹ́ àyíká tí wọ́n ń gbé. Ìtọ́jú araẹni ni agbára àwọn ènìyàn láti dènà àti tọ́jú àwọn àìsàn fúnra wọn, ní ọ̀gangan àyíká aláàbò àti àyíká tí ó pèsè àtìlẹ́yìn. Kò dípò ètò ìtọ́jú ìlera, ṣùgbọ́n dípò bẹ́ẹ̀ ó pèsè àwọn àṣàyàn àti àkójọ onírúurú fún ìtọ́jú ìlera.
translations.reference: improve primary healthcare (PHC) and contribute to achieving UHC Approach: A people-centred approach Self-care interventions support the needs and rights of individuals through a people-centred approach that is grounded in human rights and gender equality. This is a holistic approach to the health and well-being of each person, taking account of their circumstances, needs and desires across a lifetime, as well as the environment in which they live. Self-care is the power of individuals to prevent and treat diseases themselves, in the context of a safe and supportive enabling environment. It does not replace the health care system, but instead provides additional choices and options for health care.
translations.hypothesis: The advancement of primary healthcare (PHC) and its impact on achieving global health goals. Approach: A people-centered healthcare approach supports the rights and needs of individuals through a human rights-based approach that emphasizes equity and access for all. This is a holistic approach to health and well-being, addressing the social determinants of health, individual needs, and the broader context in which people live. Primary healthcare empowers individuals to take control of their health and manage their illnesses in a sustainable and equitable manner. It is not just about providing services, but also about creating a supportive environment for health.

translations.source: Àwọn ìdásí ìtọ́jú araẹni tí wọ́n wà, tí wọ́n wà ní àrọ́wọ́tó, tí owó wọn kò pọ̀ àti tí wọ́n péye jẹ́ àwọn ẹ̀yà pàtàkì ìgbéga àti ìdáàbòbò ẹ̀tọ́ àwọn ènìyàn sí ìlera. Àwọn ìdásí ìlera lè mú àgbéga bá ìmúnádóko ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera, àǹfààní sí i àti ìkojúwọ̀n. Èyí pè fún àwọn òfin àti ìlànà, ìpèsè owó fún ìlera, àwọn ọjà dídára tí òfin dè àti àwọn ìdásí tí wọ́n ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn, àti ẹ̀kọ́ èyí tí ó ń ṣe àgbéga ìkópa àwọn ènìyàn, pẹ̀lú níní òmìnira àti àṣírí araẹni. Àwọn àyẹ̀wò ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn ń ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti ṣe àdínkù ìwà ipá, lílo ipa, ìdẹ́yẹsí àti ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà nípa mímú ìdàgbàsókè bá àǹfààní ẹ̀tọ́ ẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan sí ìlera, àlàyé, àti ṣíṣe ìpinnu olómìnira. Ọ̀gangan pàjáwìrì
translations.reference: Available, accessible, affordable, acceptable and good quality self-care interventions are core components of promoting and protecting people’s right to health. Self-care interventions can improve the efficiency of health service delivery, access and coverage. This requires supportive laws and policies, health financing, regulated quality products and interventions, and education which promotes the participation of individuals, as well as privacy and confidentiality. Human rights considerations help to reduce violence, coercion, stigma and discrimination by improving each person’s exercise of their right to health, information, and independent decision-making. Emergency context
translations.hypothesis: Healthcare disparities, characterized by unequal access, affordability, and quality of care, are significant barriers to achieving health equity for all individuals. These disparities can manifest in various ways, including differences in insurance coverage, access to healthcare services, the availability of specialized treatments, and the quality of care received, ultimately impacting the health outcomes and well-being of vulnerable populations. Legal frameworks and policies, financial assistance programs for healthcare, targeted interventions, and supportive services play a crucial role in addressing these disparities and promoting health equity. Furthermore, education and awareness campaigns are essential for empowering individuals to advocate for their health rights and make informed decisions about their healthcare. Legal advocacy.

translations.source: Àwọn àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn àti ìrújú ẹ̀dá ọmọnìyàn Ní àwọn ipò níbi tí àwọn ètò ìlera ìsáájú ti fẹ̀, àwọn ìpè tí kò lẹ́gbẹ́ lórí àwọn ènìyàn àti àwùjọ náà ń sáábà wà láti ṣàkóso ìlera wọn. Àwọn ọgbọ́n ọ̀tun tí ó tayọ ẹ̀ka ìlera àtijọ́ jẹ́ èyí tí a nílò kánkán nítorí pé: àkọsílẹ̀ ènìyàn mílíọ̀nù 274 ni wọ́n nílò ìrànlọ́wọ́ ọmọnìyàn àti ààbò bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ìbújáde àìsàn jẹ́ ewu káríayé nígbà gbogbo. ó kéré jù bí mílíọ̀nù 400 ènìyàn káàkiri àgbáyé kò ní àǹfààní sí púpọ̀ àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì. 1 nínú 5 iye ènìyàn lágbàáyé ni wọ́n ń gbé nínú àwọn ìṣòro ọmọnìyàn, nínú èyí tí àwọn ètò ìlera ní ìṣòro láti pèsè àwọn ìlera pàtàkì.
translations.reference: Pandemics and humanitarian crisesIn situations where existing health systems are stretched, there is often an unprecedented demand on individuals and communities to manage their health. Innovative strategies that go beyond the conventional health sector response is urgently needed given that: a record 274 million people are currently in need of humanitarian assistance and protection and disease outbreaks are a constant global threat at least 400 million people worldwide lack access to the most essential health services. 1 in 5 of the world’s population are now living in humanitarian crises, in which health systems are challenged to deliver essential services.
translations.hypothesis: Chronic diseases and non-communicable diseases: In many countries where healthcare systems are well-established, people and communities have access to resources to manage their health. However, certain areas of healthcare are particularly important and require significant attention because: millions of people worldwide need access to essential healthcare services, and these diseases pose a constant threat to global health. An estimated 400 million people around the world lack access to essential health services. One in five people globally live in areas with weak health systems, which struggle to address major health challenges.

translations.source: Àwọn ìdásí ìtọ́jú araẹni lè pèsè àwọn àṣàyàn pàtàkì, àfikún àṣàyàn sí àwọn ibùdó ìlera ìgbà gbogbo- tàbí àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera adálórí-òṣìṣẹ́, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ara ètò ìlera tí ó ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn. Èyí pàápàá ní í ṣe lásìkò àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn COVID-19, nígbà tí ìyaraẹnisọ́tọ̀ àti àwọn òté ìtakété síraẹni mú kí ó ṣòro sí i fún àwọn ènìyàn láti ní àǹfààní sí àwọn iṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera ojoojúmọ́ àti òògùn àti àwọn ètò ìlera orílẹ̀ èdè lè dàrú. WHO mú àwọn ìdásí ìtọ́jú araẹni gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn iṣẹ́ pàtàkì nínú ìtọ́nisọ́nà iṣẹ́ lórí àkóso àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì fún ọ̀gangan COVID-19.
translations.reference: Self-care interventions can provide valuable, additional options to the usual health facility- or health worker-based services, as part of a supportive health system. This is particularly relevant during a pandemic like COVID-19, when self-isolation and physical distancing measures make it more difficult for people to access their normal health care services and medications and national health systems can be disrupted. WHO includes self-care interventions as priority actions in operational guidance on maintaining essential health services for the COVID-19 context.
translations.hypothesis: Healthcare systems can make critical decisions, including allocating resources to essential healthcare services – both routine and specialized – and prioritizing public health initiatives, especially in times of crisis. This has been particularly evident during the COVID-19 pandemic, where increased demand and strain on healthcare resources have made it challenging for people to access essential medical care, and national health systems have been stretched to their limits. The WHO recognizes healthcare systems as vital components in managing the pandemic and has emphasized the importance of prioritizing essential health services in the context of COVID-19 response.

translations.source: Sẹ́púsì Ìsọnísókí: Sẹ́púsì jẹ́ àìsàn tí ó léwu tí ó máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ nígbà tí ara bá fèsì sí àrùn tí ó fà ìfarapa fún ìṣù ara àti ẹ̀yà rẹ̀. Ó jẹ́ òpin ipa ọ̀nà tí ó wọ́pọ̀ sí ikú fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àrùn káàkiri àgbáyé. Ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìṣedéédé ẹ̀yà ara èyí tí ó jẹ́ èsì akùdé àgọ́ sí àrùn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni bí wọn kò bá tètè ṣe ìdámọ̀ àti ìtọ́jú rẹ̀ kankan, ó lè yọrí sí ìpayà sẹ́púsì, ìkùnà àwọn àgọ́ ẹ̀yà ara àti ikú. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ìṣirò gbòógì àjàgà àrùn sẹ́púsì káríayé ṣòro láti mọ̀, ìròyìn àtẹ̀jáde sáyẹ́ńsì àìpẹ́ kan jábọ̀ pé àwọn ipa sẹ́púsì nípa lórí bí mílíọ̀nù 49 ènìyàn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó fa àwọn ikú mílíọ̀nù 11 káríayé lọ́dọọdún.
translations.reference: Sepsis Overview: Sepsis is a life-threatening condition that arises when the body’s response to infection causes injury to its own tissues and organs. It is frequently a final common pathway to death for many infectious diseases worldwide. It involves organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection and if not recognized early and managed promptly, it can lead to septic shock, multiple organ failure and death. Although a precise estimate of the global epidemiological burden of sepsis is difficult to ascertain, a recent scientific publication reported that sepsis affects an estimated 49 million people and causes 11 million deaths globally every year.
translations.hypothesis: Septic Shock: Septic shock is a life-threatening condition that occurs when the body's response to an infection spirals out of control, leading to widespread inflammation and organ damage. It is a major cause of death worldwide. It is characterized by a severe drop in blood pressure, which can lead to organ failure and death. While the exact cause of septic shock is not fully understood, it is believed to be triggered by an overwhelming immune response to an infection. This response can cause blood vessels to dilate, leading to a drop in blood pressure. It can also cause blood clots to form, which can block blood flow to organs. Recent scientific advances have shed light on the mechanisms of septic shock, and it is estimated that it affects nearly 49 million people worldwide each year, resulting in approximately 11 million deaths.

translations.source: Ní ibùdó àwùjọ, sẹ́púsì sáábà máa ń wà gẹ́gẹ́ bí àdínkù ojúṣe ara tit ó wọ́pọ̀ àti àwọn àrùn tí wọ́n ṣe é dènà. Sẹ́púsì tún máa ń wáyé láti ara àwọn àrùn tí ènìyàn kó ní àwọn ibùdó ìtọ́jú ìlera, èyí tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọ̀kan nínú àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tí ó lágbára jùlọ lásìkò ìpèsè ìtọ́jú bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ń nípa lórí àwọn ẹgbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ aláìsàn káàkiri àgbáyé. Bí sẹ́púsì bá ṣẹlẹ̀ lásìkò oyún, lásìkò tàbí lẹ́yìn ìbímọ, tàbí lẹ́yìn ìṣẹ́yún, èyí ni sẹ́púsì abiamọ. Sẹ́púsì láàárín àwọn ọmọ tuntun ni à ń pè ní sẹ́púsì ìkókó. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ó ṣe é dènà dáadáa, sẹ́púsì abiamọ àti ìkókó túbọ̀ jẹ́ okùnfà ikú gbòógì fún àwọn aláboyún àti àwọn ọmọ tuntun.
translations.reference: In the community setting, sepsis often presents as the clinical deterioration of common and preventable infections. Sepsis also frequently results from infections acquired in health care settings, which are one of the most frequent adverse events during care delivery and affect hundreds of millions of patients worldwide every year. If sepsis develops during pregnancy, while or after giving birth, or after an abortion, it is called maternal sepsis. Sepsis in newborn babies is called neonatal sepsis. Despite being highly preventable, maternal and neonatal sepsis continues to be a major cause of death for pregnant women and newborn babies.
translations.hypothesis: In public health, sepsis is a serious condition that can be life-threatening and lead to various complications. Sepsis can also develop as a result of infections acquired in healthcare settings, which are among the most significant public health challenges worldwide. Sepsis that occurs during pregnancy, childbirth, or shortly after delivery is known as obstetric sepsis. While it may not always be fatal, both obstetric and general sepsis can have a significant impact on the health of both mothers and newborns.

translations.source: Àmì àìsàn: Sẹ́púsì jẹ́ pàjáwìrì ìṣègùn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó pè fún ìtọ́jú kankan láti dènà àwọn ìṣòro síwájú sí i tàbí ikú. Ó lè ṣe àfihàn àwọn onírúurú àmì ìkìlọ̀ àti àmì àìsàn nígbà mìíràn, díẹ̀ nínú èyí tí wọ́n lè fa ìdámọ̀ òdì pẹ̀lú àwọn ìṣòro mìíràn. Àrùn sẹ́púsì lè fa ibà, ṣùgbọ́n ó tún lè fa ìgbóná ara tí kò tó nǹkan, òtútù ara àti ẹ̀gbọ̀n rìrì. Ìnira lè wà láti máa mí tàbí ìmí líle, àti ọkàn tí ó ń lù kìkì, tàbí mímí tí kò lágbára tó àti àìrutó ẹ̀jẹ̀. Ó lè fa ìtọ̀ tí kò tó nǹkan, àti awọ ara dúdú tàbí awọ sísì, àti ipò ọkàn tí àyídà ti bá.
translations.reference: Symptoms: Sepsis is a medical emergency and requires immediate attention to prevent further complications or death. It may present different warning signs and symptoms at times, some of which may cause misidentification with other conditions. A septic infection may cause a fever, but may also cause low body temperature, cold extremities and shivering. There may be difficulty breathing or rapid breathing, as well as an increased heart rate, or conversely a weak pulse and low blood pressure. It may cause low urine output, as well as blue-tinted, mottled or abnormally pale skin, and an altered mental state.
translations.hypothesis: Symptoms: Septicemia is a severe condition and does not offer any guarantees of recovery or survival. It can manifest in a variety of symptoms, some of which may be similar to other illnesses. Septicemia can cause fever, but it can also cause a low body temperature, chills, rapid breathing, and a racing heart. Other symptoms may include confusion or disorientation, decreased urination, and a rash. The rash may be small, red spots or larger, bruise-like areas. It can also cause a feeling of being unwell.

translations.source: Ní ìkádìí, ó lè fa ara ríro tàbí ìnira ńlá, èyí tí ó mú kí ó rọrùn láti sì í mú pẹ̀lú ọ̀fìnkìn-olóde tàbí májèlé inú oúnjẹ. Láàárín àwọn ọmọdé, sẹ́púsì lè farahàn nípasẹ̀ mímí tí ó yára púpọ̀, gìrì, ara sísì, àìlera tàbí ìṣòro jíjí tàbí níní ìmọ̀lára ìtutùjù fún ìfọwọ́kàn. Fún àwọn ọmọdé lábẹ́ ọdún 5, ó lè fa ìṣòro oúnjẹ jíjẹ, èébì tàbí àìletọ̀ léraléra. Ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ sẹ́púsì àti gbígbé ìgbésẹ̀ kánkán ṣe pàtàkì fún ìdámọ̀ àti àyẹ̀wò ní àsìkò. Ìtọ́jú: Ìdámọ̀ àwọn àmì àìsàn lásìkò àti ìfìdímúlẹ̀ ìṣàkóso ìṣègùn lásìkò jẹ́ àwọn èròjà pàtàkì láti mú àdínkù bá ewu àti ipa sẹ́púsì.
translations.reference: Finally, it may cause extreme body pain or discomfort, which makes it easy to confuse for influenza or food poisoning. In children, sepsis may present through very fast breathing, convulsions, pale skin, lethargy or difficulty waking up or feeling abnormally cold to the touch. For children under 5 years, it may cause difficulty feeding, repeated vomiting or a lack of urination. Suspecting sepsis and acting quickly is crucial for early recognition and diagnosis. Treatment: Early identification of symptoms and timely establishment of appropriate clinical management are critical elements to reducing the risk and impact of sepsis.
translations.hypothesis: In some cases, it can lead to significant stress or anxiety, making it difficult to focus or concentrate, and potentially affecting academic performance or mealtime interactions. In children, it can manifest as rapid eating, choking, gagging, vomiting, or a strong aversion to certain textures or temperatures of food. For children under the age of 5, it can lead to food refusal, picky eating, or difficulties with mealtimes. Early detection and intervention are crucial for addressing and managing these issues. Recommendation: Screening for feeding difficulties and providing early intervention services are important steps to minimize the risks and impact of these problems.

translations.source: Ìṣàwárí àwọn àmì-ajọ̀fẹ́, gẹ́gẹ́ bí aṣẹ̀míró C inú ẹ̀dọ̀ àti purokasitoníìnì, lè kájúẹ̀ láti ṣe àyẹ̀wò àìsàn náà. Lẹ́yìn ìdámọ̀ lásìkò, àyẹ̀wò láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ ìdámọ̀ ajọ̀fẹ́ àrùn aṣokùnfà tí ó fa sẹ́púsì ṣe pàtàkì láti ṣe ìtọ́nisọ́nà ìtọ́jú òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò àfojúsùn. Ìtọ́jú òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò tí ó yẹ (funt àpẹẹrẹ, òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò bakitéríà àti òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò fọ́ńgáì) gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ fífún aláìsàn láàrin wákàtí kan ìdámọ̀ sẹ́púsì. Àìgbóògùn kòkòrò (AMR) lè mú ewu bá àkóso ìtọ́jú sẹ́púsì nítorí pé ìtọ́jú òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò bakitéríà adálórí-ìrírí ni ó sáábà máa ń nílò. Fífa omi sára lásìkò ṣe pàtàkì ní ìpele ìsáájú bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n lè nílò àwọn òògùn amúsantẹ́ẹ́rẹ́ láti mú ìtẹ̀síwájú àti àkóso bá ìsànká inú ìṣù ara.
translations.reference: The detection of some biomarkers, such as C reactive protein and procalcitonin, can be effective in diagnosing the condition. After early recognition, diagnostics to help identify a causal pathogen of infection leading to sepsis are important to guide targeted antimicrobial treatment. The appropriate antimicrobial therapy (for example, antibiotics and antifungals) should be given within one hour of sepsis identification. Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) can jeopardize clinical management of sepsis because empirical antibiotic treatment is often required. Early fluid resuscitation is important in the initial phase and vasopressors may be required to improve and maintain tissue perfusion.
translations.hypothesis: Symptoms of infectious diseases, such as fever and cough, can be indicative of the condition. After a period of observation, a diagnostic test to confirm the specific infectious disease is crucial for determining the appropriate course of treatment, especially when considering the use of antibiotics. The appropriate antibiotic (e.g., bacterial or fungal antibiotic) should be administered to the patient within a reasonable timeframe. Antibiotic resistance (AMR) can pose a significant challenge to treatment, as some infections may require more specialized antibiotics. Intravenous fluids are often essential in the initial stages to maintain hydration and support the body's fight against the infection.

translations.source: Ìgbésẹ̀ méjì gbòógì ló wà kárí dènà sẹ́púsì: Ìdènà ìkóràn kòkòrò àìfojúrí àti àrùn Ìdènà àrùn tí ó ń di sẹ́púsì Láàárín àwùjọ, ìdènà sẹ́púsì ń rí ìrànlọ́wọ́ nípasẹ̀ ìlò àwọn iṣẹ́ ìmọ́tótó tí ó péye, ìlọsíwájú títún àyíká ṣe àti omi dídára àti wíwà, ìpèsè àǹfààní sí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára, pàápàá jùlọ fún àwọn àwọn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu ńlá, àti ìjẹunrekánú pípéye, tí ó fi mọ́ ìfọ́mọlọ́yàn fún àwọn ìkókó. Ìdènà ní àwọn ibùdó ìtọ́jú ìlera gbáralé àwọn ètò ìdènà àrùn àti àkóso (IAA), àwọn iṣẹ́ ìmọ́tótó tí ó péye, àti irinṣẹ́ mímọ́, tí ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ dáadáa.
translations.reference: There are two main steps to preventing sepsis: Prevention of microbial transmission and infection Prevention of an infection evolving into sepsis Within the community, sepsis prevention is assisted through the use of effective hygiene practices, improving sanitation and water quality and availability, providing access to vaccines, particularly for those at high risk, as well as appropriate nutrition, including breastfeeding for newborns. Prevention in health care facilities relies on infection prevention and control (IPC) programmes, effective hygiene practices, and clean, well-functioning equipment.
translations.hypothesis: Two major challenges exist in addressing water scarcity: water pollution and waterborne diseases. Water scarcity, globally, is being addressed through various strategies including water conservation, improved water management, access to clean and safe water, increased investment in infrastructure, particularly for vulnerable populations, and innovative technologies that promote water efficiency. Water scarcity is being tackled in healthcare facilities through water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) programs, improved water management practices, and the implementation of effective sanitation systems.

translations.source: Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ Ìsọnísókí: Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ṣe pàtàkì fún gbogbo ìlera àti àlàáfíà gbogbogbò fún àwọn ènìyàn, tọkọtaya àti ẹbí, àti fún ìdàgbàsókè àwùjọ àti ọrọ̀ ajé ní àwọn àwùjọ àti orílẹ̀ èdè. Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀, nígbà tí a bá wò ó ní àwòdájú, ó pè fún ìlànà dídára tí ó lọ́wọ̀ fún ìbáṣepọ̀ ìbálòpọ̀ àti takọtabo, àti ìṣeéṣe níní ìrírí ìbálòpọ̀ tí ó dùn tí ó sì láàbò, tí kò ní ipá, ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà àti ìwà ipá. Agbára tọkùnrin tobìnrin láti ṣe àṣeyọrí ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ àti àlàáfíà da lórí: àǹfààní sí alayst gbígbòòrò, dídára nípa ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìjákọ̀jábo;
translations.reference: Sexual health Overview: Sexual health is fundamental to the overall health and well-being of individuals, couples and families, and to the social and economic development of communities and countries. Sexual health, when viewed affirmatively, requires a positive and respectful approach to sexuality and sexual relationships, as well as the possibility of having pleasurable and safe sexual experiences, free of coercion, discrimination and violence. The ability of men and women to achieve sexual health and well-being depends on their: access to comprehensive, good-quality information about sex and sexuality;
translations.hypothesis: The Importance of Sports for Health: Sports are crucial for the overall health and well-being of individuals, families, and communities, contributing to social and economic development. When approached correctly, sports promote specific strategies for fostering teamwork and cooperation, and provide opportunities for enjoyable, engaging, and healthy activities that are free from harmful effects, negativity, and violence. The key factors for achieving successful sports and health outcomes include: a strong foundation of knowledge, dedication to sports and fitness;

translations.source: ìmọ̀ nípa àwọn ewu tí wọ́n lè kojú àti ìsíjúsí àwọn ìṣòro ńlá iṣẹ́ ìbálòpọ̀ aláìláàbò; agbára láti ní àǹfààní sí ìtọ́jú ìlera ìbálòpọ̀; gbígbé ní àyíká tí ń jẹ́rìí tí ó sì ń polongo ìlera ìbálòpọ̀. Àwọn ọ̀ràn ìlera ajẹmọ́-ìbálòpọ̀ pọ̀ ní onírúurú, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ní ìdanilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìdánimọ̀ ìṣakọṣabo, àfihàn, ìbáṣepọ̀, àti ìgbádùn ìbálòpọ̀. Wọ́n tún ní àwọn àbájáde òdì tàbí ipò gẹ́gẹ́ bí:
translations.reference: knowledge about the risks they may face and their vulnerability to adverse consequences of unprotected sexual activity; ability to access sexual health care; living in an environment that affirms and promotes sexual health. Sexual health-related issues are wide-ranging, and encompass sexual orientation and gender identity, sexual expression, relationships, and pleasure. They also include negative consequences or conditions such as:
translations.hypothesis: Understanding the risks and benefits of unregulated sports betting; the ability to influence sports outcomes; and the potential for addiction and health problems. The negative consequences of unregulated sports betting are diverse and can include financial ruin, relationship problems, job loss, and mental health issues. They can also have broader societal impacts, such as:

translations.source: àwọn àìsàn pẹ̀lú kòkòrò apasọ́jà ara (KASA), àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ (STIs) àti àwọn àrùn òpónà ìbísí (RTIs) àti àwọn àbájáde líle wọn (bí jẹjẹrẹ àti àìlèbímọ); oyún àìròtì àti ìṣẹ́yún; àìṣedéédé ìbálòpọ̀; ìwà ipá; àti àwọn àṣà tí ó léwu (gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìdábẹ́ fún obìnrin, IFO).
translations.reference: infections with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), sexually transmitted infections (STIs) and reproductive tract infections (RTIs) and their adverse outcomes (such as cancer and infertility); unintended pregnancy and abortion; sexual dysfunction; sexual violence; and harmful practices (such as female genital mutilation, FGM).
translations.hypothesis: Conditions related to the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), sexually transmitted infections (STIs), and other infections transmitted through sexual contact (including herpes and infertility); violence and abuse; risky sexual behavior; substance abuse; and harmful traditional practices (particularly female genital mutilation, FGM).

translations.source: Àwọn oríkì: WHO ti ń ṣiṣẹ́ ní agbègbè ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ láti ó kéré jù 1974, nígbà tí àwọn ìjíròrò àjọ alámọ̀já kan yọrí sí àtẹ̀jáde ìròyìn ìmọ̀ tí wọ́n pè ní "Education and treatment in Human sexuality" (WHO, 1975). Bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní 2000, Àjọ Ìsọ̀kan Ìlera America (PAHO) àti WHO ṣe àkójọpọ̀ àwọn onírúurú ìmọ̀ràn alámọ̀já láti ṣe àtúnyẹ̀wò àwọn èdè ìperí àti ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn àṣàyàn ètò. Àbájáde àwọn ìpàdé wọ̀nyí ni Ìpàdé Ìmọ̀ràn 2002, níbi tí àwọn oríkì èdè ìperí ajẹmọ́ ìbálòpọ̀ tí wọ́n ń ṣàmúlò nísàlẹ̀ wọ̀nyí ti jẹ́ àgbékalẹ̀. Ìṣakọṣabo: Ìṣakọṣabo ni àwọn àbùdá ẹ̀dá tí ó ń tọ́ka ènìyàn gẹ́gẹ́ bí abo tàbí akọ. Nígbà tí ètò àwọn àbùdá ẹ̀dá wọ̀nyí lè wà nínú àgọ́ kan, nítorí pé àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní méjèèjì wà, wọ́n sáábà máa ń ṣe ìyàtọ̀ láàrin àwọn ẹ̀dá gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọkùnrin àti obìnrin.
translations.reference: Definitions: WHO has been working in the area of sexual health since at least 1974, when the deliberations of an expert committee resulted in the publication of a technical report entitled “Education and treatment in human sexuality” (WHO, 1975). Starting in 2000, the Pan American Health Organization (PAHO) and WHO convened a number of expert consultations to review terminology and identify programme options. The culmination of these meetings was a Technical Consultation in 2002, where the working definitions of the key sexual health-related terms below were developed. Sex: Sex refers to the biological characteristics that define humans as female or male. While these sets of biological characteristics are not mutually exclusive, as there are individuals who possess both, they tend to differentiate humans as males and females.
translations.hypothesis:
Terminology: The World Health Organization (WHO) has been working in the field of sexual health since at least 1974, when a working group of experts produced a seminal report on "Education and treatment in Human sexuality" (WHO, 1975). Starting in 2000, the Pan American Health Organization (PAHO) and WHO have collaborated with various expert groups to review terminology and develop guidelines for program implementation. A key outcome of these meetings was the 2002 Expert Group Meeting, where a set of standardized terms for sexual health were established.

Sex: Sex refers to the biological and physiological characteristics that define men and women. While sex is typically assigned at birth, it is important to recognize that individuals may identify with a gender that is different from the sex they were assigned at birth.

translations.source: Ní ìlò gbogbogbò nínú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn èdè, ọ̀rọ̀ ìperí ìṣakọṣabo ní wọ́n sáábà máa ń lò láti túmọ̀ sí "iṣẹ́ ìbálòpọ̀", ṣùgbọ́n fún iṣẹ́ ìmọ̀ nínú àwọn ìjíròrò ọ̀gangan ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ìjákọ̀jábo àti ìlera ìbálòpọ̀, oríkì òkè yìí dára jùlọ. Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ Gẹ́gẹ́ bí oríkì tí ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ báyìí, ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ni:"...ipò ara, ìmọ̀lára, ipò ọkàn àti àlàáfíà ìbáraẹnilò ní ìbámu pẹ̀lú ìjákọ̀jábo; kì í ṣe àìsí àìsàn lásán, àìṣedéédé ìbálòpọ̀ àti àìlera. Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ pè fún ìlànà dídára tí ó sì sé ìbọ̀wọ̀ fún ìjákọ̀jábo àti àwọn ìbáṣepọ̀ ìbáraẹnilòpọ̀, àti ìṣeéṣe láti àwọn ìrírí ìgbádùn ìbálòpọ̀ aláàbò, tí kò ní ìfipámú, ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà àti ìwà ipá. Fún ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ láti jẹ ìṣeéṣe àti ìṣàkóso, àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ìbálòpọ̀ fún gbogbo àwọn ènìyàn gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ ìbọ̀wọ̀fún, ìdáàbòbò àti àmúṣe." (WHO, 2006a).
translations.reference: In general use in many languages, the term sex is often used to mean “sexual activity”, but for technical purposes in the context of sexuality and sexual health discussions, the above definition is preferred. Sexual health According to the current working definition, sexual health is:“…a state of physical, emotional, mental and social well-being in relation to sexuality; it is not merely the absence of disease, dysfunction or infirmity. Sexual health requires a positive and respectful approach to sexuality and sexual relationships, as well as the possibility of having pleasurable and safe sexual experiences, free of coercion, discrimination and violence. For sexual health to be attained and maintained, the sexual rights of all persons must be respected, protected and fulfilled.” (WHO, 2006a)
translations.hypothesis: In many languages, the term "health" is often used to mean "well-being," but for scientific work in the fields of public health, epidemiology, and health promotion, a more precise definition is needed. The World Health Organization's definition of health, which is still widely used today, is: "Health is a state of complete physical, mental and social well-being and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity." This definition emphasizes the importance of a holistic approach to health, taking into account not only the absence of disease but also the presence of positive factors such as social support, healthy relationships, and a sense of purpose. For health to be truly effective and sustainable, the rights of all people must be respected, protected, and promoted. (WHO, 2006a).

translations.source: Ìjákọ̀jábo: Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ kò ṣe é kì, lóye tàbí ṣàmúlò láìsí àgbéyẹ̀wò gbòòrò ìjákọ̀jábo, èyí tí ó tọ́ka àwọn ìwà àti àbájáde pàtàkì tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìlera ìbálòpọ̀. Oríkì ìjákọ̀jábo tí ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ ni:"...àárín abala jíjẹ ẹ̀dá ènìyàn títí ayé ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìṣakọṣabo, àwọn ìdánimọ̀ ìjákọ̀jábo àti ojúṣe, àlàyé ìbálòpọ̀, ìmáradìde, ìgbádùn, ìbáṣepọ̀ àti ìbísí. Ìjákọ̀jábo máa ń wáyé bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó máa ń jẹ́ ìṣàfihàn nínú àwọn èrò, ìrònú, ìfẹ́ ọkàn, ìgbàgbọ́, ìwà, iyì, ìṣeṣí, ìṣe, ojúṣe àti àjọṣepọ̀. Nígbà tí ìjákọ̀jábo let ní gbogbo àwọn ìwọ̀n wọ̀nyí, kì í ṣe gbogbo wọn ni wọ́n ṣe é ní ìrírí tàbí ṣe àfihàn. Ìjákọ̀jábo máa jẹ́ okùnfà ìbáṣepọ̀ àwọn ìdí ajẹmẹ́dàá, ìròrí, àwùjọ, ọrọ̀ ajé, òṣèlú, àṣà, òfin, ìtàn, ẹ̀sìn àti ajẹmẹ́mìí.
translations.reference: Sexuality: Sexual health cannot be defined, understood or made operational without a broad consideration of sexuality, which underlies important behaviours and outcomes related to sexual health. The working definition of sexuality is:“…a central aspect of being human throughout life encompasses sex, gender identities and roles, sexual orientation, eroticism, pleasure, intimacy and reproduction. Sexuality is experienced and expressed in thoughts, fantasies, desires, beliefs, attitudes, values, behaviours, practices, roles and relationships. While sexuality can include all of these dimensions, not all of them are always experienced or expressed. Sexuality is influenced by the interaction of biological, psychological, social, economic, political, cultural, legal, historical, religious and spiritual factors.” (WHO, 2006a)
translations.hypothesis: The Essence: Football, whether played professionally or casually, embodies a set of values and characteristics that are fundamental to the human experience. The core principles of football are: "...the pursuit of excellence, teamwork, discipline, strategy, passion, camaraderie, and sportsmanship. Football is a reflection of life, encompassing elements of competition, cooperation, sacrifice, dedication, perseverance, skill, and community. When football embodies all of these qualities, it transcends the game and becomes a powerful force for good. Football can be a source of inspiration, motivation, and unity, bringing people together from all walks of life, regardless of their age, gender, race, religion, or socioeconomic status. It can also be a vehicle for social change, promoting values such as fairness, respect, and equality."

translations.source: Ẹ̀tọ́ Ìbáṣepọ̀: Ìfẹnukò káàkiri ń wáyé pé ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ kò lè ṣe é mú ṣẹ àti ṣe àkóso láìsí ìbọ̀wọ̀ fún, ìdáàbòbò bo, àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn pàtàkì. Oríkì tí ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ fún ẹ̀tọ́ ìbáṣepọ̀ tí ó wà nísàlẹ̀ yìí jẹ́ ìdásí ìtàkùrọ̀sọ lórí ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn tí ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìlera ìbálòpọ̀. Àmúlò àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn sí ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìlera ní àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ìbáṣepọ̀. Ẹ̀tọ́ ìbáṣepọ̀ dáàbò bo gbogbo àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ènìyàn láti ṣe àmúṣe àti àfihàn ìjákọ̀jábo wọn àti láti gbádùn ìlera ìbálòpọ̀, pẹ̀lú àkíyèsí tí ó yẹ fún ẹ̀tọ́ àwọn ẹlòmíràn tí ó sì jẹ́ nínú àgbékalẹ̀ ìhun ìdáàbòbò tako ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà." (WHO, 2006a, tí wọ́n ṣe ìmúdójú-ìwọ̀n ní 2010) "Ìmúṣẹ ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ so mọ́ bí àwọn ènìyàn ṣe ń bọ̀wọ̀ fún, dáàbò bo àti ṣe ìmúṣẹ ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn.
translations.reference: Sexual rights: There is a growing consensus that sexual health cannot be achieved and maintained without respect for, and protection of, certain human rights. The working definition of sexual rights given below is a contribution to the continuing dialogue on human rights related to sexual health. The application of existing human rights to sexuality and sexual health constitute sexual rights. Sexual rights protect all people's rights to fulfil and express their sexuality and enjoy sexual health, with due regard for the rights of others and within a framework of protection against discrimination." (WHO, 2006a, updated 2010)“The fulfilment of sexual health is tied to the extent to which human rights are respected, protected and fulfilled.
translations.hypothesis: The Principle of Intersectionality: It is increasingly recognized that health disparities cannot be understood or addressed without considering the intersection of various social identities, including race, ethnicity, gender, and other fundamental human rights. The core principle underlying this intersectional approach to health equity is the recognition that social identities interact with health disparities. The integration of social identities into health and healthcare is essential for achieving health equity. Intersectionality acknowledges that all individuals possess a range of social identities that shape their experiences and access to healthcare, and that these identities must be considered with due regard for the rights of others within a supportive and inclusive environment." (WHO, 2006a, updated in 2010) "Health equity requires understanding how social factors influence health, including access to, and experiences with, healthcare.

translations.source: Ẹ̀tọ́ ìbáṣepọ̀ so mọ́ àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn tí wọ́n ti dá mọ̀ nínú àkọsílẹ̀ àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn lágbàáyé àti ní agbègbè àti àwọn ìwé àdéhùn mìíràn àti nínú àwọn òfin orílẹ̀ èdè. Àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ pàtàkì sí ìmúṣẹ ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ni: Àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ pàtàkì sí ìmúṣẹ ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ni: Àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ pàtàkì sí ìmúṣẹ ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ni: Ẹ̀tọ́ sí ìdọ́gba àti àìsí ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà
translations.reference: Sexual rights embrace certain human rights that are already recognized in international and regional human rights documents and other consensus documents and in national laws. Rights critical to the realization of sexual health include: Rights critical to the realization of sexual health include: Rights critical to the realization of sexual health include: the rights to equality and non-discrimination
translations.hypothesis: The principles of equality are enshrined in human rights declarations, both internationally and nationally, as well as in various legal documents and constitutions. Key principles related to equality in healthcare include: Key principles related to equality in healthcare include: Key principles related to equality in healthcare include: The right to dignity and freedom from discrimination.

translations.source: ẹ̀tọ́ láti ní òmìnira kúrò nínú ìdíyàjẹ tàbí ìwà ìkà, àìláàánú tàbí ìwà ìtẹ́ńbẹ́lú tàbí ìfìyàjẹni ẹ̀tọ́ sí àṣírí ẹ̀tọ́ sí òṣùwọ̀n ìlera tí ó ga jùlọ (tí ó fi mọ́ ìlera ìbálòpọ̀) àti ààbò àwùjọ. ẹ̀tọ́ láti gbéyàwó àti láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ẹbí àti láti wọnú ìgbéyàwó pẹ̀lú ìfọwọ́sí àwọn tọkọtaya ojú ọ̀nà ní ìrọwọ́rọsẹ̀ àti ní kíkún, àti fún ìdọ́gba nínú àti fún ìtúká ìgbéyàwó. ẹ̀tọ́ pinnu iye àwọn ọmọ àti àlàfo láàárín àwọn ọmọ ẹni.
translations.reference: the right to be free from torture or to cruel, inhumane or degrading treatment or punishment the right to privacy the rights to the highest attainable standard of health (including sexual health) and social security the right to marry and to found a family and enter into marriage with the free and full consent of the intending spouses, and to equality in and at the dissolution of marriage the right to decide the number and spacing of one's children
translations.hypothesis: The right to freedom from torture or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment; the right to the highest attainable standard of physical and mental health; and the right to social security. The right to marry and to found a family, and the right to equal rights as to marriage, including the right to choose a spouse freely and with full consent, and the right to protection from marriage entered into without free consent. The right to determine the number and spacing of children.

translations.source: ẹ̀tọ́ sí ìròyìn, àti ẹ̀kọ́ ẹ̀tọ́ sí òmìnira ioinnut àti èrò, àti ẹ̀tọ́ ojútùú pípé fún àwọn ìrúfin àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn pàtàkì. Iṣẹ́ ojúṣe àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn pè fún gbogbo ènìyàn bọ̀wọ̀ fún àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ẹlòmíràn. Àwọn ọ̀ràn: "Àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn pèsè ìhun àgbékalẹ̀ káríayé láàrin èyí tí àìní ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìlera ìbísí àti àwọn ìfẹ́ gbogbo àwọn ènìyàn lè jẹ́ àkásí." (GRUSKIN, 2005).
translations.reference: the rights to information, as well as education the rights to freedom of opinion and expression, and the right to an effective remedy for violations of fundamental rights. The responsible exercise of human rights requires that all persons respect the rights of others. Issues: “Human rights provide an international legal framework within which the sexual and reproductive health needs and aspirations of all people can be considered.” (GRUSKIN, 2005)
translations.hypothesis: The right to information, the right to freedom of thought and expression, and the right to participate fully in the exercise of human rights are fundamental rights. The realization of human rights depends on the enjoyment of all other rights. As stated: "Human rights are the foundation for a just and equitable world where all people can live with dignity and fulfill their potential." (GRUSKIN, 2005).

translations.source: Ní àwọn ọdún lọ́ọ́lọ́ọ́ yìí, àwọn irinṣẹ́ ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn káríayé ti ń gbèrú nínú lílò láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àti láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá àṣẹ òfin láti ọwọ́ àwọn ènìyàn àti gbogbo àwùjọ kí àwọn ìjọba orílẹ̀ èdè lè fọwọ́sọ̀yà lórí ìbọ̀wọ̀fún, ààbò àti ìmúṣẹ ẹ̀tọ́ ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìlera ìbísí wọn (Cook et al., 2003). Àwọn oríkì tí wọ́n ń ṣiṣẹ́ àti ìhun àmúṣiṣẹ́ fún ìṣètò tí wọ́n ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ níhìn-ín fìdí kalẹ̀ lórí àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn tí wọ́n ṣe ìdámọ̀ káàkiri àgbáyé tí ó sì pèsè ìlànà adálórí-ẹ̀tọ́ láti ṣètò nínú ìlera ìbálòpọ̀. Àwọn èròjà àgbékalẹ̀ pàtàkì ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ni wọ́n wà nísàlẹ̀ yìí:. Àwọn èròjà àgbékalẹ̀ pàtàkì ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ Nígbà tí a bá wò ó lódidi àti lọ́nà tí ó dára:
translations.reference: In recent years, international human rights instruments have been increasingly used to support and advance legal claims by individuals and whole communities so that national governments will guarantee the respect, protection and fulfilment of their sexual and reproductive health rights (Cook et al., 2003). The working definitions and framework for programming presented here are grounded in internationally recognized human rights and offer a rights-based approach to programming in sexual health. The key conceptual elements of sexual health are listed below. Key conceptual elements of sexual health When viewed holistically and positively:
translations.hypothesis:
In recent years, human rights organizations have increasingly focused on using legal mechanisms to promote and advance the rights of individuals and society, enabling governments to prioritize social justice, equality, and the well-being of all citizens (Cook et al., 2003). The principles and practices developed through these initiatives have informed human rights standards worldwide and have shaped approaches to health equity. Key areas of focus in health equity include:

Key areas of focus in health equity:
When viewed comprehensively and holistically:

translations.source: Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ dá lórí àlàáfíà, kì í ṣe àìsí àìsàn nìkan. Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìbọ̀wọ̀fún, ààbò àti òmìnira kúrò lára ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà àti ìwà ipá. Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ dá lórí ìmúṣẹ àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn kan. Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ṣe pàtàkì títí ìgbésí ayé ẹ̀dá, kì í ṣe fún àwọn tí wọ́n wà ní àwọn ọdún ìbísí wọn, ṣùgbọ́n fún àwọn ọ̀dọ́ àti àgbàlagbà. Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ń jẹ́ ìṣàfihàn nípa ìjákọ̀jábo onírúurú àti àwọn ẹ̀yà ìṣàfihàn ìbálòpọ̀.
translations.reference: Sexual health is about well-being, not merely the absence of disease. Sexual health involves respect, safety and freedom from discrimination and violence. Sexual health depends on the fulfilment of certain human rights. Sexual health is relevant throughout the individual’s lifespan, not only to those in the reproductive years, but also to both the young and the elderly. Sexual health is expressed through diverse sexualities and forms of sexual expression.
translations.hypothesis: Sportsmanship is built on fairness, respect, and integrity, both on and off the field. It is about upholding the principles of good sportsmanship. Sportsmanship is important throughout life, not just during competitive events, but for children and adults alike. Sportsmanship promotes understanding and highlights the values of fair play.

translations.source: Àṣà ìṣakọṣabo, ojúṣe, ìrètí àti agbára máa ń nípa pàtàkì lórí ìlera ìbálòpọ̀. Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ní láti di mímọ̀ láàrin ọ̀gangan ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àwùjọ, ọrọ̀ ajé àti òṣèlú.
translations.reference: Sexual health is critically influenced by gender norms, roles, expectations and power dynamics. Sexual health needs to be understood within specific social, economic and political contexts.
translations.hypothesis: Traditions, customs, beliefs, and power dynamics often have a significant impact on public health. Public health can be improved by addressing these factors within social structures, economic systems, and political environments.

translations.source: Àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ (STIs) Ìsọnísókí: Àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ (STIs) máa ń tàn ka lọ́pọ̀ nípasẹ̀ ìbálòpọ̀ aláìláàbò. Àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ díẹ̀ lè jẹ́ ìkóràn lásìkò ìlóyún, ìbímọ àti ìfọ́mọlọ́yàn àti nípasẹ̀ ẹ̀jẹ̀ àìmọ́ tàbí àwọn èròjà ẹ̀jẹ̀. Àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ ní ipa pàtàkì lórí ìlera. Bí ó bá wà láìsí ìtọ́jú, wọ́n lè yọrí sí àwọn ìṣòro ńlá tí ó fi mọ́ àìsàn ajẹmópòónà òye àti ọkàn, àìlèbímọ, oyún ìta ilé ọmọ, àbíkú, àti ewu Kòkòrò Apasọ́jà ara (KASA) púpọ̀.
translations.reference: Sexually transmitted infections (STIs) Overview: Sexually transmitted infections (STIs) are spread predominantly by unprotected sexual contact. Some STIs can also be transmitted during pregnancy, childbirth and breastfeeding and through infected blood or blood products. STIs have a profound impact on health. If untreated, they can lead to serious consequences including neurological and cardiovascular disease, infertility, ectopic pregnancy, stillbirths, and increased risk of Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV).
translations.hypothesis: Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs): STIs are commonly spread through unprotected sexual activity. Some STIs can also be transmitted from mother to child during pregnancy and childbirth, or through sharing needles or other drug paraphernalia. STIs have a significant impact on health. If left untreated, they can lead to serious health problems, including infertility, ectopic pregnancy, pelvic inflammatory disease, increased risk of HIV infection, and certain cancers.

translations.source: Wọ́n tún ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìdẹ́yẹsí, ìwà ipá abẹ́lé, àti ìnípa lórí àbùdá ìgbésí ayé. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn Àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ kì í ní àwọn àmì àìsàn. Nígbà tí wọ́n bá wà àwọn àmì àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ ni nǹkan tí ó ń jáde lójú ara tàbí òpónà ìtọ̀, ọgbẹ́ ojú ara àti ìrora abo ìsàlẹ̀ inú. Àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ jùlọ tí wọ́n sì ṣe é wò ni tirikomónà, aṣojú àrùn ìbálòpọ̀, àtọ̀sí àti sífílì. Àìgbóògùn àwọn kòkòrò bakitéríà tí wọ́n ń pọ̀ sí i jẹ ewu ìgbòòrò àrùn àtọ̀sí tí kò sè é wòsàn.
translations.reference: They are also associated with stigma, domestic violence, and affects quality of life. The majority of STIs have no symptoms. When they are present common symptoms of STIs are vaginal or urethral discharge, genital ulcer and lower abdominal pain. The most common and curable STIs are trichomonas, chlamydia, gonorrhoea and syphilis. Rapidly increasing antimicrobial resistance is a growing threat for untreatable gonorrhoea.
translations.hypothesis: It also involves consideration of ethical principles, social responsibility, and the impact on quality of life. Many infectious diseases do not have obvious symptoms. When symptoms are present, they can range from mild to severe and may include fever, fatigue, rash, and gastrointestinal problems. The most common infectious diseases worldwide include respiratory infections, diarrheal diseases, HIV/AIDS, and malaria. The emergence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria poses a significant threat to public health and makes treatment more difficult.

translations.source: Àwọn kòkòrò àrùn fáírọ́ọ̀sì ìbálòpọ̀ tí ó fi mọ́ KASA, kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì àrùn herpes, kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì àrùn jẹ̀dọ̀jẹ̀dọ̀ B, kòkòrò HPV àti kòkòrò HTLV-1 kò ní tàbí ní àwọn àṣàyàn tí kò. Àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára wà fún àrùn jẹ̀dọ̀jẹ̀dọ̀ B láti dènà àrùn tí ó lè fa jẹjẹrẹ ẹ̀dọ̀ àti fún HPV láti dènà jẹjẹrẹ àbáwọlé ilé ọmọ. KASA, HSV àti HTLV-1 jẹ́ àrùn títí ayé:fún KASA àti HSV àwọn ìtọ́jú tí wọ́n lè ki kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì náà mọ́lẹ̀, ṣùgbọ́n ní báyìí kò sí ìwòsàn fún èyíkéyìí àwọn kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ wọ̀nyí. Àwọn ọ̀rá ìdáàbòbò tí wọ́n lò lọ́nà tí ó tọ́ àti nígbà gbogbo jẹ́ àwọn ìlànà tí wọ́n kojúwọ̀n láti dáàbò boni lọ́wọ́ àwọn àrùn ibalopo àti KASA. Ṣíṣe àyẹ̀wò pẹ̀lú àyẹ̀wò àsìkò fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ àti àwọn alábàáṣepọ̀ wọn pèsè àǹfààní tí ó dára jùlọ fún ìtọ́jú tí ó múnádóko àti fún ìdènà àwọn ìṣòro àti ìkóràn síwájú sí i.
translations.reference: Viral STIs including HIV, genital herpes simplex virus (HSV), viral hepatitis B, human papillomavirus (HPV) and human T-lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1) lack or have limited treatment options. Vaccines are available for hepatitis B to prevent infection that can lead to liver cancer and for HPV to prevent cervical cancer. HIV, HSV and HTLV-1 are lifelong infections: for HIV and HSV there are treatments that can suppress the virus, but currently there are no cures for any of these viral STIs. Condoms used correctly and consistently are effective methods to protect against STIs and HIV. Screening with early diagnosis of people with STIs and their sexual partners offers the best opportunity for effective treatment and for preventing complications and further transmission.
translations.hypothesis: The sexually transmitted infections (STIs) KASA, herpes simplex virus (HSV), syphilis, HPV, and HTLV-1 do not have cures or vaccines. There are treatments available for syphilis to prevent serious health complications, and for HPV to prevent certain cancers. KASA, HSV, and HTLV-1 are chronic infections: for KASA and HSV, treatments can manage the symptoms and prevent outbreaks, but there is no cure. Safe sex practices are the most effective ways to prevent STIs and KASA. Regular screening for STIs and KASA is recommended for individuals at risk and can help to detect and treat infections early.

translations.source: Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Ẹnìkan lè ní àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ láìní àwọn àmì àìsàn náà tí wọ́n fojú hàn. Nígbà tí ó bá wà, lára àwọn àwọn àmì àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ ni: nǹkan tí ó ń jáde lójú ara ní àbaadì, nǹkan tí ó ń jáde ní òpónà ìtọ̀, àwọn ọgbẹ́ àti kókó ojú ara, àti ìrora ní ìsàlẹ̀ inú. Àwọn àmì àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ kọ̀ọ̀kan ni: Àrùn àtọ̀sí àti aṣojú àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ Àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ wọ̀nyí ń fa ìṣòro ẹnu ilé ọmọ, ìṣòro òpónà ìtọ̀ láàrin àwọn ọkùnrin àti àfikún àwọn àrùn ojú ara, tí ó fi mọ́ ìfarahàn ní ẹnu fùrọ̀ àti ọ̀nà ọ̀fun.
translations.reference: Symptoms: A person can have an STI without having obvious symptoms of disease. When present, common symptoms of STIs include: abnormal vaginal discharge, urethral discharge, genital ulcers and lumps, and lower abdominal pain. Symptoms of specific STIs are: Gonorrhoea and chlamydial infection These STIs cause cervicitis in women, urethritis in men and extra-genital infections, including rectal and oropharyngeal manifestations.
translations.hypothesis:
Symptoms: A person may have a sexually transmitted infection (STI) without any noticeable symptoms. However, when symptoms are present, some of the most common include: unusual discharge from the vagina or penis, sores or blisters on the genitals or in the mouth, and pain during urination. Specific STIs and their symptoms:
* Genital herpes and other STIs: These infections can cause painful sores in the genital area, as well as sores in the mouth and throat.

translations.source: Lára àwọn àmì àìsàn tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ ni nǹkan tí ó ń jáde ní inú òbò tàbí okó àti títa lásìkò ìtọ̀. Àwọn ọmọ àṣẹ̀ṣẹ̀bí àwọn ìyá tí ó lùgbàdì lè kó àìsàn ojú pípọ́n (ojú pípọ́n) nítorí ìsíjúsí àwọn kòkòrò ìbálòpọ̀ lásìkò ìbímọ aṣàmúlò ojú ara. Àwọn àrùn ihò ìdí àti ọ̀fun lè má ní àwọn àmì àìsàn. Sífílì: Sífílì kì í sáábà ní àmì àìsàn, nígbà tí àmì àìsàn bá farahàn, sífílì ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ máa ń farahàn gẹ́gẹ́ bí ògòdò adádúró, tí kò ní ìrora. Àwọn sífílì àfikún lè farahàn gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn ògòdò gbogbogbò tí wọ́n ń nípa lórí awọ ara, ẹ̀yà ara apèsè ikun àti ògòdò tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn kúrúnà ní àtẹ́lẹwọ́ àti àtẹ̀lesẹ̀.
translations.reference: Common symptoms include vaginal or penile discharge and burning with urination. Infants of infected mothers can contract neonatal conjunctivitis (red eyes) due to exposure to the STIs during vaginal delivery. Rectal and pharyngeal infections can be asymptomatic. Syphilis: Syphilis is often asymptomatic, when symptoms occur, primary syphilis presents as a solitary, painless ulcer. Secondary syphilis may manifest as generalized lesions affecting skin, mucous membranes and lymph-node including a classic rash on the palms of the hands and soles of the feet.
translations.hypothesis: Common skin conditions include those that appear on the face or body and cause temporary or permanent discoloration. Pregnant women and new mothers are particularly susceptible to melasma (also known as the "mask of pregnancy") due to hormonal changes. Skin infections and inflammation can also cause discoloration. Specifically: Freckles: Freckles are not considered a skin condition, but when they appear, they are often small, flat spots that are darker than the surrounding skin. Additional discoloration can appear as larger, more widespread patches on the face, neck, and other areas of the body, and can be associated with sun exposure and inflammation.

translations.source: Sífílì afarasin kì í ní àmì àìsàn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ní àbùdá àfihàn sífílì nípa àyẹ̀wò. Tirikomoníásì: Àwọn àmì àìsàn tí ó wọ́pọ̀ jùlọ ni nǹkan yíyọ láti inú òbò pẹ̀lú idọ pípọ́n, yúnyún àti ìbálòpọ̀ tí ó nira. Kòkòrò HSV ojú ara HSV sáábà máa ṣe àfihàn àwọn egbò tí wọ́n nira, àwọn rorẹ́ tàbí egbò ní ní ìta ojú ara àti ẹnu. Kòkòrò HSV alámì àìsàn jẹ́ àìsàn títí ayé tí ó máa ń ní àbùdá àwọn àmì àìsàn tí wọ́n ń wá ní òòrèkóòrè.
translations.reference: Latent syphilis is asymptomatic and characterized by positive syphilis serology. Trichomoniasis: The predominant symptoms include abnormal vaginal discharge with redness of the vulva, itching and painful intercourse. Genital herpes simplex virus (HSV) HSV most commonly presents as painful sores, vesicles or ulcerations on the external genitalia and mouth. Symptomatic genital HSV is a lifelong condition that can be characterized by frequent symptomatic recurrences.
translations.hypothesis: Syphilis does not always present with obvious symptoms, and it can sometimes be difficult to diagnose. Trichomoniasis: The most common symptoms of this infection include vaginal discharge, itching, and irritation. Genital herpes (HSV): This infection can cause painful sores, blisters, or ulcers on the genitals, buttocks, or thighs. Genital herpes is a lifelong condition that can cause recurrent outbreaks.

translations.source: Ẹ̀yà 1 Kòkòrò àrùn HTLV-1 Ní ṣókí kí í ní àmì àrùn, ẹ̀yà kòkòrò àrùn HTLV-1 líle lè fa àìsàn líle, tí ó fi mọ́ jẹjẹrẹ hóró ẹ̀jẹ̀ T/ jẹjẹrẹ ibùdó hóró ẹ̀jẹ̀ funfun (ATL) àti ìṣòro inú ètò òye ara tí a mọ̀ sí àìsàn ọ̀pá ẹ̀yìn tí HTLV-1 ṣokùnfà tàbí àìsàn spastic paraparesis ti agbègbè ooru. (HAM/TSP). Ìtọ́jú: Ìtọ́jú tí ó péye wà báyìí fún onírúurú àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀. Àwọn bakitéríà àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ mẹ́ta (aṣojú àrùn ìbálòpọ̀, àtọ̀sí àti sífílì) àti kòkòrò ìbálòpọ̀ ajọ̀fẹ́ kan (tirikomoníásì) sáábà máa ń ṣe é wò pẹ̀lú àwọn òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò ìlò kan tàbí ọlọ́pọ̀ ìlò tí a ó múnádóko tí wọ́n ti wà nílẹ̀. Fún àwọn kòkòrò àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ (KASA, HSV àti HTLV-1), àwọn òògùn tí wọ́n ṣiṣẹ́ jùlọ tí wọ́n wà ni àwọn ogún agbógunti-kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì tàbí agbógunti-jẹjẹrẹ (nínú ọ̀ràn àrùn HTLV-1), tí ó lè ṣe àkóso ìfarahàn àwọn àìsàn náà, bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé wọn kò lè wo àwọn àìsàn mẹ́tẹ̀ẹ̀ta wọ̀nyí.
translations.reference: Human T-lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1) Generally asymptomatic, the chronic form of HTLV-1 can cause severe disease, including adult T-cell leukaemia/lymphoma (ATL) and a progressive nervous system condition known as HTLV-1-associated myelopathy or tropical spastic paraparesis (HAM/TSP). Treatment: Effective treatment is currently available for several STIs. Three bacterial STIs (chlamydia, gonorrhoea and syphilis) and one parasitic STI (trichomoniasis) are usually curable with existing, effective single-dose or multiple-dose regimens of antibiotics. For viral STIs (HIV, HSV and HTLV-1), the most effective medications available are antivirals or anti-cancer drugs (in the case of HTLV-1), that can modulate the course of the diseases, though they cannot cure these three diseases.
translations.hypothesis: HTLV-1 Infection: HTLV-1, a retrovirus, can cause a variety of conditions, including adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma (ATLL), a type of cancer affecting T-cells, and HTLV-1-associated myelopathy/tropical spastic paraparesis (HAM/TSP), a neurological disorder. Treatment: There is currently no cure for most sexually transmitted infections. However, antibiotics can be used to treat bacterial infections (gonorrhea, chlamydia, and syphilis), and antiparasitic drugs can be used to treat trichomoniasis. For viral sexually transmitted infections (herpes, HSV, and HTLV-1), antiviral medications are available that can help to control the symptoms of the infection, although they cannot cure the infection.

translations.source: Àtakò kòkòrò bakitéríà sí òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò (AMR) tí wọ́n fi wo àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀, pàápàá jùlọ àtọ̀sí, ti gbèrú sí i púpọ̀ lẹ́nu lọ́ọ́lọ́ọ́ yìí bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ti ṣe àdínkù àwọn àbájáde àṣeyọrí ìtọ́jú. Àwọn àbájáde láti inú Ètò Àmójútó Kòkòrò àrùn àtọ̀sí Alòdìsí-òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò (GASP) fi àwọn ìwọ̀n ńlá hàn sí ìlòdìsí quinolone hàn, tí ó ń mú ìgbèrú bá ìlòdìsí òògùn azithromycin àti àkójọ-gbòòrò cephalosporin alòdìsí-òògùn tí ó ṣẹ̀ṣẹ̀ bẹ̀rẹ̀. Ìfarahàn alékún agbára àtọ̀sí láti rí ìwòsàn tí ó ń dín kù dórí ìgbòòrò àkójọ cephalosporin, àti ìfìdímúlẹ̀ ìlòdìsí pẹnisilíìnì, sulphonamides, tetravyclines, quinolones àti macrolides ní àwọn ìpele ńlá mú kí àtọ̀sí jẹ́ ajọ̀fẹ́ tí ó lòdì sí ọ̀pọ̀ òògùn. AMR fún àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ mìíràn, bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé wọn kò wọ́pọ̀, wọ́n wà, èyí tí ó mú kí ìdènà àti ìtọ́jú kankan ṣe pàtàkì. Láti tọ́jú àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ ní kíkún ó ṣe pàtàkì láti lo àwọn òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò tí ó yẹ, pẹ̀lú ìwọ̀n ìlò àti àsìkò tí ó tọ́ fún ẹ̀yà àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ pàtó láti rí dájú pé ìtọ́jú tàbí ìwòsàn pípé wà, àti láti dènà ìdàgbà ìlòdìsí òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò.
translations.reference: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) to antibiotics used to treat STIs, in particular gonorrhoea, has increased rapidly in recent years and has reduced successful treatment outcomes. Results from the current Gonococcal AMR Surveillance Programme (GASP) show trends of high rates of quinolone resistance, increasing azithromycin resistance and emerging resistance of extended-spectrum cephalosporins. The emergence of decreased susceptibility of gonorrhoea to extended-spectrum cephalosporins, together with established high-levels of resistance to penicillins, sulphonamides, tetracyclines, quinolones and macrolides make gonorrhoea a multidrug-resistant organism. AMR for other STIs, though less common, also exists, making prevention and prompt treatment critical. To adequately treat STIs it is important to take the appropriate antimicrobials, with the correct dose and duration for the specific STI to ensure adequate treatment or cure, and to prevent the development of antimicrobial resistance.
translations.hypothesis: The rise of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in bacteria, particularly concerning multi-drug resistant strains, has become a significant public health threat in recent years, hindering treatment outcomes. Findings from the Global Antimicrobial Resistance Surveillance System (GLASS) have highlighted a concerning increase in resistance to quinolones, as well as resistance to azithromycin and third-generation cephalosporins. The emergence of highly resistant strains, even against carbapenems, and the reduced effectiveness of penicillin, sulfonamides, tetracyclines, quinolones, and macrolides at high levels, make infections increasingly difficult to treat. While AMR is a concern for other infections, its impact on common infections is particularly worrying. To effectively treat common infections, it is crucial to use appropriate antibiotics, with careful consideration of the specific infection, dosage, and duration of treatment to ensure optimal outcomes and to minimize the development of antimicrobial resistance.

translations.source: Sànpọ̀nná Ìsọnísókí: Sànpọ̀nná ni àrùn líle tí máa ń ràn èyí tí kòkòrò àrùn fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà, ọmọ ẹbí fáírọ́ọ̀sì aṣerankoṣènìyàn ń ṣokùnfà. Ó jẹ́ ọ̀kan lára àwọn àìsàn ńlá tí ọmọnìyàn mọ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó fa ẹgbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ikú kí wọ́n tó rẹ́yìn rẹ̀. Ìgbàgbọ́ wà pé ó ti wà fún ó kéré jù ọdún 3000. Abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára sànpọ̀nná, tí Edward Jenner ṣẹ̀dá ní ọdún 1796, ni àṣeyọrí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára kìíní láti di àgbéjáde.
translations.reference: Smallpox Overview: Smallpox is an acute contagious disease caused by the variola virus, a member of the orthopoxvirus family. It was one of the most devastating diseases known to humanity and caused millions of deaths before it was eradicated. It is believed to have existed for at least 3000 years. The smallpox vaccine, created by Edward Jenner in 1796, was the first successful vaccine to be developed.
translations.hypothesis: Smallpox: Smallpox is a highly contagious and deadly disease caused by the variola virus. It was a major public health problem, responsible for devastating epidemics and significant mortality throughout history. It is believed to have existed for at least 3,000 years. The development of the smallpox vaccine by Edward Jenner in 1796 was a groundbreaking achievement and the first successful vaccination.

translations.source: Ó wòye pé àwọn afúnwàrà tí wọ́n ti kọ́kọ́ kó olóde màlúù kò kó sànpọ̀nná bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó fi hàn pé àjẹsára irúfẹ́ kan náà lè ṣe é lò láti dènà sànpọ̀nná fún àwọn ẹlòmíràn. Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ètò gbòòrò láti fòpin sí sànpọ̀nná ní ọdún 1967. Àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti àmójútó gbòòrò ní wọ́n ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ káàkiri àgbáyé fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún. Ọ̀ràn àìtọọ́dá tí ó gbẹ̀yìn wáyé ní Somalia ní 1977. Ní 1980 WHO kéde òpin àrùn sànpọ̀nná - àrùn tí ó máa ń ràn kan ṣoṣo láti ṣe àṣeyọrí yìí.
translations.reference: He observed that milkmaids who previously had caught cowpox did not catch smallpox and showed that a similar inoculation could be used to prevent smallpox in other people. The World Health Organization launched an intensified plan to eradicate smallpox in 1967. Widespread immunization and surveillance were conducted around the world for several years. The last known natural case was in Somalia in 1977. In 1980 WHO declared smallpox eradicated – the only infectious disease to achieve this distinction.
translations.hypothesis: It is important to note that the initial smallpox eradication campaigns did not achieve immediate success, and it became clear that a coordinated global effort was needed to eliminate the disease for everyone. The World Health Organization launched a global smallpox eradication program in 1967. Vaccination campaigns and surveillance teams were established around the world for many years. A major setback occurred in Somalia in 1977. In 1980, the WHO declared smallpox eradicated – a remarkable achievement.

translations.source: Èyí wà lára àwọn àṣeyọrí ìlera àwùjọ tí ó gbajúmọ̀ jùlọ tí ó sì jinlẹ̀ jùlọ nínú ìtàn. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Lára àwọn àmì àrùn sànpọ̀nná tí wọ́n máa ń kọ́kọ́ farahàn ni ibà, ọ̀rẹ̀rẹ̀ àti ẹ̀yìn ríro, àti nígbà mìíràn, inú rírun àti èébì. Ọjọ́ méjì sí 3 lẹ́yìn náà kòkòrò àrùn náà yóò ṣe àbùdá kúrúnà pẹ̀lú àwọn ògòdò tí wọ́n ní àwọn omi tí ó ṣe é rí, èyí tí yóò padà ní ọyún àti nígbẹ̀yìn yóò di kúrúkùsá tí yóò gbẹ yóò sì máa rẹ̀ dànù. Kúrúnà máa ń bẹ̀rẹ̀ láti ojú àti ọwọ́, yóò sì tàn lọ sí àwọn ẹ̀yà ara yòókù. Àwọn ògòdò máa ń bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní àwọn awọ apèsè ikun imú àti ẹnu bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò di egbò láìpẹ́ lẹ́yìn ìṣarajọ.
translations.reference: This remains among the most notable and profound public health successes in history. Symptoms: Early symptoms of smallpox include high fever, fatigue and severe back pain, and less often, abdominal pain and vomiting. Two to 3 days later the virus produces a characteristic rash with bumps full of a clear liquid, which later fill with pus and finally develop a crust that dries and falls off. The rash begins on the face and hands, then spreads to the rest of the body. Lesions develop in the mucous membranes of the nose and mouth and ulcerate soon after formation.
translations.hypothesis: This is one of the most common and well-documented infectious diseases in history. Symptoms: The initial symptoms often include fever, chills, and muscle aches, and sometimes nausea and vomiting. Within two to three days, the disease progresses with the appearance of a characteristic rash, which typically starts on the face and spreads to the rest of the body. The rash is often described as a "sandpaper" texture and can be itchy. The rash typically lasts for several days and then gradually fades. The disease usually starts with a mild fever and headache, and then progresses to a more severe illness with a high fever, rash, and other symptoms.

translations.source: Sànpọ̀nná máa ń ràn láti ara ènìyàn-sí-ènìyàn nípasẹ̀ àwọn ẹ̀kán omira lásìkò ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú ẹni tí ó ti lùgbàdì tí ó ní àwọn àmì àìsàn náà, tàbí ní àwọn àsìkò mìíràn nípasẹ̀ aṣọ tàbí ibùsùn tí ó ti lùgbàdì. Ó ní àsìkò ìsàba ọjọ́ 7-17 lẹ́yìn ìfaragbá bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò di ìkóràn ní kété tí ibà bá ti bẹ̀rẹ̀. Àwọn ènìyàn máa ń ní ìkóràn títí di àsìkò tí èèpo ìkẹyìn bá ṣí bọ́. Sànpọ̀nná la ikú lọ nínú àwọn ọ̀ràn 30%.Ìwé ìdánimọ̀ pẹlẹbẹ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera àgbáyé.
