Èsì WHO: Àsìkò láti ìgbà ìfòpinsí ní ó ti ní àríyànjiyàn gígùn tí ó lọ́jú èyí tí ó fojúsun ìparun àwọn àkójọ alààyè fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà tí ó kù.
translations.reference: Smallpox is transmitted from person to person via infective droplets during close contact with infected people who have symptoms of the disease, or in some cases through contaminated clothing and bedding. It has an incubation period of 7–17 days after exposure and only becomes infectious once a fever develops. People remain infectious until the last scabs fall off. Smallpox was fatal in up to 30% of cases.WHO smallpox recognition card WHO response: The period since eradication has been defined by a lengthy and complex debate focussed on the destruction of the last remaining stocks of live variola virus.
translations.hypothesis: Scabies is a skin infestation caused by tiny mites that burrow into the skin, typically in areas where there is close contact with an infected person, or through shared clothing or bedding. It usually takes 2-6 weeks after exposure for symptoms to appear. People typically experience itching, which can be intense, especially at night. Scabies can persist for weeks or even months if left untreated. In some cases, it can lead to death. According to the World Health Organization, scabies is a neglected tropical disease. WHO statement: The period from initial infection to the appearance of symptoms is typically 2-6 weeks, but can be shorter in individuals with weakened immune systems.

translations.source: Ní ọdún 1996, ní Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé 49, àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ pinnu láti ba àwọn àkójọ yìí jẹ́ ní Oṣù Kẹfà 1999, àti láti fààyè gba àwọn àpẹẹrẹ kékeré láti jẹ́ ìpamọ́ fún ìwádìí ní àwọn láàbù aláàbò méjì tí wọ́n yà sọ́tọ̀ - ọ̀kan ní United States àti ọkàn ní orílẹ̀ èdè Russia. Ìṣàmúlò ìgbà díẹ̀ àwọn àkójọ fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà ni wọ́n ń ṣàmúlò fún ìwádìí pàtàkì síwájú sí i, èyí tí Àjọ Agbani-nímọ̀ràn fún Ìwádìí Fáírọ́ọ̀sì Fàríólà ń darí. Ẹgbẹ́ yìí máa ń pàdé ní ọdọọdún. Ilé iṣẹ́ Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé fún sànpọ̀nná tí ó wà ní olú ilé iṣẹ́ Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ni wọ́n ń darí gbogbo ọrọ̀ tí ó ní ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn iṣẹ́ mímú òpin bá sànpọ̀nná àti ẹ̀yìn ìfòpinsí. Lára àwọn èyí ni àmójútó àwọn ìwádìí tí wọ́n fààyè gbà pẹ̀lú ààyè fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà fún ìdàgbàsókè àwọn òté ìgbógunti gẹ́gẹ́ bí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì.
translations.reference: In 1996, at the 49th World Health Assembly, Member States decided to have this stock destroyed in June 1999, and only to allow small samples to be kept for research purposes at two designated secure laboratories – one in the United States and one in the Russian Federation. Temporary retention of the existing stocks of variola virus are used for further essential research, which is overseen by the Advisory Committee for Variola Virus Research. This group meets on an annual basis. The WHO Smallpox Secretariat based in WHO Headquarters coordinates all smallpox eradication-related and post-eradication activities. These include overseeing permitted research with live variola virus for the development of countermeasures such as vaccine and antivirals.
translations.hypothesis: In 1996, at the 49th World Health Assembly, member states agreed to establish a framework for addressing these issues by September 1999, and to collect smallpox samples for research purposes, storing them in two high-security laboratories – one in the United States and one in Russia. The limited number of remaining smallpox virus samples were being preserved for critical research, under the leadership of the World Health Organization's Expert Committee on Smallpox Eradication. This committee met annually. The World Health Organization's office for smallpox eradication, located at the organization's headquarters, was responsible for all activities related to smallpox vaccine production and distribution. Among these activities was the collection of smallpox vaccine samples for the development of new and improved vaccines, particularly for vulnerable populations such as children and those with weakened immune systems.

translations.source: Láti àsìkò ìfòpinsí, àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti ìtọ́jú pàtó ni wọ́n ti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ fún sànpọ̀nná àti àwọn àìsàn tí wọ́n rí bákan náà gẹ́gẹ́ bí sànpọ̀nná. Ibùdó náà tún ń rí sí ìgbáradì pàjáwìrì àti àkóso àkójọpọ̀ pàjáwìrì abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára sànpọ̀nná ní àwọn ìgbà tí èèyàn kò fi ọkàn sí tí àìsàn náà tún yọjú. Ibùdó náà ń mójútó àmójútó ìpamọ́ ẹ̀dá àti ààbò ẹ̀dá àwọn àkójọpọ̀ fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà tí wọ́n fọwọ́sí gẹ́gẹ́ bí Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé ṣe là á kalẹ̀, àti rírí i dájú pé ìjábọ̀ ọdọọdún wà fún Ẹgbẹ́ Adarí Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé. Ibùdó Sànpọ̀nná ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn Àká Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé láti ṣe ìpamọ́ ìtàn sànpọ̀nná káríayé àti àkọsílẹ̀ àwọn eko tí wọ́n kọ́ láti ara àṣeyọrí rírí ẹ̀yìn sànpọ̀nná. Lára àwọn ojúṣe àti iṣẹ́ Ibùdó Sànpọ̀nná Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ni:
translations.reference: Since the eradication era, safer vaccines and specific treatments have been developed for smallpox and related diseases such as monkeypox. The Secretariat is also responsible for emergency preparedness and manages a smallpox vaccine emergency stockpile in the unlikely event that the disease re-emerges. The Secretariat manages the biosafety and biosecurity inspections of the authorized variola virus repositories as mandated by the World Health Assembly, and ensures annual reporting to WHO Governing Bodies. The Smallpox Secretariat supports WHO Archives in preserving the global history of smallpox and documenting the lessons learned from successful eradication of smallpox. The WHO Smallpox Secretariat's current roles and responsibilities include:
translations.hypothesis: Since its inception, the specialized units and dedicated teams have focused on preventing and treating diseases, particularly those with a high mortality rate, such as cancer. These units also address the psychological and social challenges faced by cancer patients and their families during treatment. They promote early detection and access to quality care, as recommended by the World Health Organization, and advocate for policies that support cancer research and treatment. The Cancer Unit supports the World Health Organization's efforts to reduce the global burden of cancer and to translate research findings into improved patient outcomes. Among the key initiatives and activities of the World Health Organization's Cancer Unit are:

translations.source: Ìwádìí fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà àti ìdàgbàsókè àwọn òté agbógunti. ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìpàdé ọdọọdún Àjọ Agbani-nímọ̀ràn lórí Ìwádìí Fáírọ́ọ̀sì Fàríólà ti Àjọ Elétò Àgbáyé (ACVVR). àkóso àgbéyẹ̀wò àwọn àlàkalẹ̀ ìwádìí fún iṣẹ́ lórí fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà nípasẹ̀ ACVVR àkóso àwọn ìbáṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àkójọpọ̀ fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà méjì (tí wọ́n tún jé àwọn Ibùdó Àjọṣepọ̀ WHO) àkásílẹ̀, ṣíṣe iṣẹ́ àti àmójútó àwọn ìbéèrè láti ọwọ́ àwọn aṣèwádìí lórí DNA fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà láti inú àkójọpọ̀ 2 lábẹ́ àwọn pàtó.
translations.reference: Variola virus research and development of countermeasures organizing annual meetings of the WHO Advisory Committee on Variola Virus Research (ACVVR) coordinating review of research proposals for work with variola virus through the ACVVR managing relationships with the two variola virus repositories (which are also WHO Collaborating Centres) recording, processing and monitoring requests from researchers for variola virus DNA from the 2 repositories under specific rules
translations.hypothesis: The Fairtrade certification program and the traceability of agricultural products involve the establishment of annual meetings of the Fairtrade Standards Committee within the framework of the Association of World Eleemosynary (ACVVR). The ACVVR manages the review of certification standards for Fairtrade products through the management of partnerships with two Fairtrade organizations (which are also WHO Collaborative Centers). Specifically, it conducts research and answers questions from producers regarding the DNA of Fairtrade products from the two organizations, under specific guidelines.

translations.source: ṣíṣe àmójútó ààbò-ẹ̀dá àti ìdáàbòbò-ẹ̀dá èèkàn ní ọdún méjì àwọn àkójọpọ̀ fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà tí wọ́n káríayé 2. ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwùjọ lórí ìwádìí lórí DNA fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà àti àwọn ọ̀ràn tí wọ́n jẹmọ́ ara wọn. Ìgbáradì àti ìfèsì pàjáwìrì àkóso àti ìgbáradì ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwùjọ lórí ìdàgbàsókè àti ìlò àwọn òté agbógunti fún àwọn fáírọ́ọ̀sì aṣerankoṣènìyàn. ìgbáradì ìdàgbàsókè àti ọgbọ́n ìfèsì, àti irinṣẹ́ láti ṣàmúlò àwọn òté ìgbáradì
translations.reference: conducting biennial biosafety and biosecurity inspections of the 2 global variola virus repositories developing normative guidance regarding research on variola virus DNA and related matters Emergency preparedness and response coordinating and preparing normative guidance on development and use of medical countermeasures for orthopoxviruses developing preparedness and response strategies, and tools to implement preparedness measures
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba text:

"The implementation of creative and innovative solutions in the field of genetics has seen significant progress in the last two years, thanks to various research initiatives. This includes the development of social impact assessment methodologies related to genetic research and its implications for human health. Furthermore, there has been an advancement in the ethical control and social impact assessment of the development and application of advanced technologies for personalized medicine. This involves the development of assessment tools, knowledge dissemination, and guidelines for responsible innovation."

translations.source: àkóso àti àmójútó àkójọ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára sànpọ̀nná lágbàáyé. àkóso ìdàgbàsókè ìṣàṣepọ̀ láàbù fún ìdámọ̀ àti àyẹ̀wò àwọn fáírọ́ọ̀sì aṣerankoṣènìyàn. ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwùjọ àti àwọn ọgbọ́n ìfèsì fún olóde-ẹdun ènìyàn àti àwọn fáírọ́ọ̀sì aṣerankoṣènìyàn mìíràn. àtìlẹ́yìn agbára ìṣẹ̀dá àti ìṣẹ̀dá àwọn èròjà ìdanilẹ́kọ̀ọ́. ìnípa lórí àwọn ètò ààbò ìlera àgbáyé
translations.reference: maintaining and managing the smallpox global vaccine stockpile coordinating development of a laboratory network for orthopoxvirus detection and diagnostics developing normative guidance and response strategies for human monkeypox and other orthopoxviruses supporting capacity-building and developing training materials contributing to global health security initiatives
translations.hypothesis: Global health governance and coordination are essential for addressing complex health challenges. It involves the development and implementation of policies and programs to improve health outcomes and address health inequities. It also includes the promotion of social determinants of health and the strengthening of health systems for both human health and animal health. Furthermore, it supports innovation and the development of new products and technologies. This is particularly important in the context of global health security.

translations.source: Ìpamọ́ ìtàn sànpọ̀nná lágbàáyé àmójútó àwọn àká Ètò Ìfòpinsí Sànpọ̀nná ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àká Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé àti ilé ìyáwèékàwé ìdarí lórí àwọn ìjíròrò ìlànà àti ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ mánigbàgbé tí ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìfòpinsí sànpọ̀nná ìjábọ̀ lọ́dọọdún fún Ìgbìmọ̀ Adarí Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé àti Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé Ibùdó iṣẹ́ náà ṣe àkójọpọ̀ àti àkóso owó ìrànwọ́ fún àwọn iṣẹ́ wọ̀nyí àti fún àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ nígbà tí ó yẹ nígbà tí ó nílò gẹ́gẹ́ bí igbaninímọ̀ràn àti ìdanilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ ìmọ̀.
translations.reference: Preserving global smallpox history overseeing the Smallpox Eradication Programme archives in collaboration with WHO Archives and the WHO Library leading on policy discussions and commemorative events regarding smallpox eradication reporting annually to the WHO Executive Board and the World Health Assembly The Secretariat also mobilizes and manages funding for these activities and for ad hoc events as required such as technical consultations and training.
translations.hypothesis: The Global Antimicrobial Resistance Surveillance System (GLASS) collaborates with national public health agencies and reference laboratories to collect data on antimicrobial resistance patterns and emerging threats related to antimicrobial resistance. This data is reported to the World Health Organization and the Global Antimicrobial Resistance and Use Surveillance System. The system also provides funding and technical support for these activities, as well as for outbreak investigations and research.

translations.source: Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò Ìsọnísókí: Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò jẹ́ àìsàn tí ó léwu fún ẹ̀mí tí àwọn oró ẹnu ejò tí ó lóró ń fà. Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò tún lè wáyé nípasẹ̀ oró ejò tí ó fọ́n sí ojú láti ọwọ́ àwọn ẹ̀yà ejò kan tí wọ́n ní agbára láti tu oró gẹ́gẹ́ bí òté ààbò. Àwọn akitiyan àtijọ́ láti ṣàkóso àìsàn láti ara oró ejò ti pèsè àwọn ìwífún-àlàyé ìmọ̀ àrùn tí ó fọ́n ká, tí kò pé. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn afaragbá kì í lọ sí ibùdó ìlera tàbí ilé ìwòsàn àti dípò bẹ́ẹ̀ wọ́n máa ń gbáralé àwọn ìtọ́jú abẹ́lé.
translations.reference: Snakebite envenoming Overview: Snakebite envenoming is a potentially life-threatening disease caused by toxins in the bite of a venomous snake. Envenoming can also be caused by having venom sprayed into the eyes by certain species of snakes that have the ability to spit venom as a defence measure. Inadequate past efforts to control snakebite envenoming has produced fragmented, inaccurate epidemiological data. Many victims do not attend health centres or hospitals and instead rely on traditional treatments.
translations.hypothesis: Fungal infections of the nail: Nail fungal infections are a common and often persistent condition caused by various types of fungi that invade the nail. These infections can also be caused by fungi that enter the nail through small cracks or injuries, sometimes from contact with contaminated surfaces or objects. Traditional treatments for nail fungal infections have often been ineffective or have significant side effects. Many people seek alternative remedies or visit healthcare professionals for diagnosis and treatment.

translations.source: Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé, àwọn ìwífún-àlàyé tí ó wà fi hàn pé àwọn ènìyàn 4.5-5.4 ni ejò bù jẹ lọ́dọọdún. Nínú èyí, mílíọ̀nù 1.8-2.7 ní àìsàn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni 81 000 sí 138 000 kú láti ara àwọn àìsàn. Lára àwọn ọ̀wọ́ tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu jùlọ ni àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ iṣẹ́ àgbẹ̀, darandaran, ọdẹ, àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n ń ṣiṣẹ́, àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ní ilé tí wọ́n kò kọ́ dáradára àti àwọn tí wọn kò ní àǹfààní sí ẹ̀kọ́ àti ìtọ́jú ìlera. Òjòjò àti ikú máa ń sáábà wáyé láàárín àwọn ọ̀dọ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ọmọdé ní ìṣòro ọ̀ràn ikú púpọ̀. Síwájú sí i, àwọn obìnrin máa ń ní ìrírí àwọn ìdènà sí ìṣàmúlò ìtọ́jú ìṣègùn ní àwọn àwùjọ kan bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn aláboyún wà nínú ewu.
translations.reference: However, available data show 4.5–5.4 million people get bitten by snakes annually. Of this, 1.8–2.7 million develop clinical illness and 81 000 to 138 000 die from complications. High-risk groups include rural agricultural workers, herders, fishermen, hunters, working children, people living in poorly constructed houses and those with limited access to education and healthcare. Morbidity and mortality occur most frequently among young people and children suffer higher case fatality. Furthermore, women experience increased barriers to accessing medical care in some cultures and pregnant women are extremely vulnerable.
translations.hypothesis: While estimates vary, studies suggest that approximately 4.5 to 5.4 million people worldwide suffer from mental health conditions each year. Within this group, an estimated 1.8 to 2.7 million experience severe mental illness, resulting in 81,000 to 138,000 deaths attributable to these conditions. Those at higher risk include agricultural workers, veterans, orphans, children in low-income families, and individuals with limited access to education and healthcare. Mental health issues and deaths are particularly prevalent among young people, with a significant number experiencing mental health crises. Furthermore, women often face stigma and barriers to accessing mental health services, and certain populations are disproportionately vulnerable.

translations.source: Ìṣòro kan tí ó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ tí ó ń dènà àǹfààní sí ìtọ́jú òògùn agbógunti-oró ejò tí ó láàbò, tí ó sì múnádóko ní àwọn ọ̀pọ̀ agbègbè, àti pàápàá jùlọ ní àyíká àgbègbè Sahara Afíríkà, jẹ́ ìdí kan tí ó nípa lórí ewu wíwá ìrànlọ́wọ́ nípasẹ̀ òògùn ìbílẹ̀. Àmì àìsàn: Ẹnu tàbí oró fífọ́n láti ara àwọn ejò olóró lè fa onírúurú àwọn pàjáwìrì ìṣègùn líle tí ó lágbára. Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò láti ara àwọn oríṣìí ejò lè fa onírúurú àmì àìsàn, àwọn kan lágbára jù àwọn mìíràn lọ. Èyí mú kí ìpèsè òògùn agbógunti-oró ejò tí ó tọ́ jẹ́ ìṣòro tí ó ń lọ lọ́wọ́. Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò lè fa rọpárọsẹ̀ líle tí ó lè dènà mímí, èyí tí yóò mú kí ìtọ́jú ìṣègùn ayákánkán ṣe pàtàkì.
translations.reference: An ongoing crisis restricting access to safe, effective antivenom treatment in many regions, and particularly sub-Saharan Africa, is one factor that contributes to the predisposition for seeking help through traditional medicine. Symptoms: Bites or sprayed venom from venomous snakes can cause a range of acute and serious medical emergencies. Envenoming from different types of snakes can cause different symptoms, some more serious than others. This makes the preparation of correct antivenoms an ongoing problem. Envenoming can cause severe paralysis that may prevent breathing, making immediate medical attention critical.
translations.hypothesis: A persistent and widespread problem is the increasing threat of antimicrobial resistance, particularly in bacteria, which poses a significant challenge in many regions, especially in the African Sahara region. This issue is primarily driven by the overuse of antibiotics. Symptoms: Infections caused by resistant bacteria can be difficult to treat and may lead to serious complications. Resistance to antibiotics can develop in bacteria from various sources, with some strains being more resistant than others. This makes the problem of antimicrobial resistance a growing concern. Infections caused by resistant bacteria can be life-threatening and require intensive treatment.

translations.source: Àwọn ènìyàn lè ní ìrírí ìṣòro ẹ̀jẹ̀ sísun tí ó lè yọrí sí ẹ̀jẹ̀ àsunsínú tàbí akùdé kíndìnrín tí kò ṣe é wò. Ìbàjẹ́ ìṣù ara líle tún lè wáyé, èyí tí ó lè yọrí sí ààbọ̀ ara títí ayé àti ọwọ́ gígé pẹ̀lú. Àwọn ọmọdé nínú ipa ewu ńlá nítorí ara wọn tí ó kéré jù tí àwọn àgbàlagbà lọ. Ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ikú àti àbájáde láti ara ẹnu ejò ṣe é dènà dáadáa nípa pípèsè àwọn òògùn aporó ejò tí wọ́n wà káàkiri tí wọ́n sì ṣe é ṣàmúlò, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn àgbègbè tí ewu pọ̀ sí. Àwọn òògùn aporó ejò alágbára nìkan ni ìtọ́jú pípéye láti dènà tàbí ṣe ìwòsàn ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ipa àwọn oró ejò.
translations.reference: People may also experience bleeding disorders that can lead to fatal haemorrhages or irreversible kidney failure. Severe local tissue destruction may also occur, which can lead to permanent disability and even limb amputation. Children are at higher risk of severe effects due to lower body mass than adults. Most deaths and serious consequences from snake bites are entirely preventable by making safe and effective antivenoms more widely available and accessible, particularly in high-risk areas. High quality snake antivenoms are the only effective treatment to prevent or reverse most of the venomous effects of snake bites.
translations.hypothesis: People can experience symptoms of a cold, which can range from mild to severe and may include fever or chills. Other symptoms can include body aches, fatigue, and persistent coughs. Children are particularly vulnerable because their immune systems are not as developed as those of adults. The widespread use of antibiotics has contributed to the problem by promoting the development of antibiotic-resistant strains of bacteria. Effective hygiene practices are crucial in preventing the spread of infection, especially in areas with high population density. Only strong antibiotics are effective in treating many bacterial infections.

translations.source: Ìtọ́jú: Àwọn òògùn aporó ejò jẹ́ ìtọ́jú pípé láti dènà tàbí ṣe ìwòsàn ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ipa burúkú oró ejò bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n wà nínú àlàkalẹ̀ àwọn òògùn pàtàkì WHO. Wíwà àti níní àǹfààní àwọn òògùn aporó wọ̀nyí, pẹ̀lú ṣíṣe ìpolongo lórí àwọn ìlànà ìdènà abẹ́lé láàrin àwọn àwùjọ àti àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera, ni àwọn ọ̀nà tí wọ́n dára jùlọ láti ṣe àdínkù àwọn ìṣòro ńlá àti ikú láti ara oró ejò. Lẹ́yìn tí ejò tí ó ṣe é ṣe kí ó lóró bá sán ènìyàn, ẹ tẹ́lẹ̀ àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ wọ̀nyí: Ní kété ẹ kúrò ní agbègbè tí sísán náà ti waye. Ẹ yọ ohunkóhun tí ó bá fún kúrò ní ẹ̀yà ara tí ejò tí náà sán láti yàgò fún ewu bí wíwú bá wáyé.
translations.reference: Treatment: Snake antivenoms are effective treatments to prevent or reverse most of the harmful effects of snakebite envenoming and are included in the WHO list of essential medicines. The availability and accessibility of these antivenoms, along with raising awareness on primary prevention methods among communities and health workers, are the best ways to limit serious consequences and deaths from snakebite envenoming. After a bite by a snake suspected of being venomous, follow these steps: Immediately move away from the area where the bite occurred. Remove anything tight from around the bitten part of the body to avoid harm if swelling occurs.
translations.hypothesis: Antivenom: Antivenoms are highly effective in neutralizing or treating a wide range of snake venom effects and are included in the World Health Organization's list of essential medicines. The availability and accessibility of these antivenoms, along with the implementation of effective snakebite treatment protocols among communities and healthcare workers, are crucial for reducing the morbidity and mortality associated with snakebites. If a snake has bitten someone, follow these steps: First, move the person away from the area where the bite occurred. Remove any clothing or jewelry that may constrict blood flow if swelling occurs.

translations.source: Ẹ fi ọkàn afaragbá balẹ̀, nítorí pé kì í ṣe gbogbo oró ejò ni ó ń ikú kíákíá. Ẹ jẹ́ kí ẹni náà wà láìmira pátápátá kí ẹ sì gbé ẹni náà lọ sí ibùdó ìlera ní kíákíá. Fífi ọ̀ǹtẹ̀ sí ojú sísán náà pẹ̀lú èèfún lè jẹ́ ohun tí ó dára ní àwọn ọ̀ràn kan. Ẹ yàgò fún àwọn ìlànà ìtọ́jú ìbílẹ̀ àkọ́gbà tàbí àwọn òògùn ìbílẹ̀. Ẹ lè fún ẹni náà ní Parasitamọ́ fún ìrora abẹ́lé (èyí tí ó lè lágbára).
translations.reference: Reassure the victim, as most venomous snake bites do not cause immediate death. Immobilize the person completely and transport the person to a health facility as soon as possible Applying pressure at the bite site with a pressure pad may be suitable in some cases. Avoid traditional first aid methods or herbal medicines. Paracetamol may be given for local pain (which can be severe).
translations.hypothesis: It's important to stay calm, as not every fever indicates a serious illness. It's best to monitor the person closely and seek medical attention if the fever persists or worsens. Applying a cool compress to the forehead can help to lower the temperature. You can also try traditional remedies or over-the-counter medications. In some cases, it may be necessary to administer paracetamol for fever (this should be done with caution and according to the recommended dosage).

translations.source: Èébì lè wáyé, nítorí náà ẹ mú kí ẹni náà sún pẹ̀lú ẹ̀gbẹ́ rẹ̀ òsì ní ipò ìmúláradá. Ẹ mójútó káà ìmí àti ìmí rẹ̀ kí ẹ sì gbáradì láti jí i bí ó bá ṣe pàtàkì.
translations.reference: Vomiting may occur, so place the person on their left side in the recovery position. Closely monitor airway and breathing and be ready to resuscitate if necessary
translations.hypothesis: Problems can arise, and therefore, it's important to communicate with your partner openly and honestly, especially in sensitive situations. Be understanding and empathetic, and strive to find solutions together.

translations.source: Àwọn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe ìkóràn Ìsọnísókí: Àwọn àìsàn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn wà lára àwọn àìsàn tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ jùlọ káàkiri àgbáyé tí wọ́n sì ń nípa lórí àwọn àwùjọ tí wọ́n tòṣì jùlọ tí wọ́n kò sì ní àǹfààní jùlọ. Wọ́n máa ń ràn nípa àwọn ẹyin tí ó wà nínú ìgbẹ́ ènìyàn èyí tí ó ń sọ ilẹ̀ di àìmọ́ ní àwọn àgbègbè tí kò ti sí ìmọ́tótó. Àwọn ẹ̀yà gbòógì tí wọ́n máa ń mú ènìyàn ni aràn roboto (Ascaris lumbricoides), aràn kòbókò (Trichuris trichuria) àti kán-ànyàn (Necator americanus àti Ancylostoma duodenale). Aràn inú máa ń pèsè àwọn onírúurú àmì àìsàn tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ìṣòro inú bí (ìgbẹ́ gbuuru, inú rírun), ìrora àti àìlera gbogbogbò.
translations.reference: Soil-transmitted helminthiases Overview: Soil-transmitted helminth (STH) infections are among the most common infections worldwide and affect the poorest and most deprived communities. They are transmitted by eggs present in human faeces which in turn contaminate soil in areas where sanitation is poor. The main species that infect people are the roundworm (Ascaris lumbricoides), the whipworm (Trichuris trichiura) and the hookworms (Necator americanus and Ancylostoma duodenale). Intestinal worms produce a wide range of symptoms including intestinal manifestations (diarrhoea, abdominal pain), general malaise and weakness.
translations.hypothesis: Soil-transmitted helminth infections: These infections, also known as parasitic worm infections, are among the most common infectious diseases worldwide, particularly affecting vulnerable populations with limited access to sanitation. They are caused by various types of worms that live in the soil and infect humans through contact with contaminated soil. The most common types of worms that cause these infections are roundworms (Ascaris lumbricoides), whipworms (Trichuris trichuria), and hookworms (Necator americanus and Ancylostoma duodenale). These intestinal worms can cause a wide range of health problems, including abdominal pain, diarrhea, anemia, and stunted growth.

translations.source: Kán-ànyàn ń fa àdánù ẹ̀jẹ̀ inú líle èyí tí ó lè fa àìlẹ́jẹ̀tó. Àwọn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn máa ń ràn nípa àwọn ẹyin tí wọ́n máa ń jáde láti ara ìgbẹ́ àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n jẹ́ afaragbá. Àwọn àgbà aràn máa ń gbé nínú ìfun níbi tí wọ́n ti máa ń pèsè àwọn ẹgbẹẹgbẹ̀rún ẹyin lójoojúmọ́. Ní àwọn agbègbè tí wọn kò ní ìmọ́tótó tó tó, àwọn ẹyin wọ̀nyí maat ń sọ ilẹ̀ di àìmọ́. Èyí lè ṣẹlẹ̀ ní àwọn onírúurú ọ̀nà: àwọn ẹyin tí wọ́n lẹ̀ mọ́ ẹ̀fọ́ tí ènìyàn jẹ nígbà tí wọn kò bá ṣe, fọ̀ tàbí bó ẹ̀fọ́ náà dáadáa.;
translations.reference: Hookworms cause chronic intestinal blood loss that result in anaemia. Soil-transmitted helminths are transmitted by eggs that are passed in the faeces of infected people. Adult worms live in the intestine where they produce thousands of eggs each day. In areas that lack adequate sanitation, these eggs contaminate the soil. This can happen in several ways: eggs that are attached to vegetables are ingested when the vegetables are not carefully cooked, washed or peeled;
translations.hypothesis: Pests can cause significant damage to crops, leading to food shortages. These pests are often insects that emerge from the soil and feed on the roots and leaves of plants. Farmers often struggle to control these pests, using various methods to try and protect their crops. In areas with limited resources, these pests can devastate entire harvests. This can happen in a number of ways: pests can be resistant to pesticides, or the pesticides may not be effective against the specific pests that are present; the pests may reproduce quickly, making it difficult to control their population; or the pests may be able to adapt to new environments, making it difficult to eradicate them.

translations.source: wọ́n máa ń jẹ ẹyin láti ara àwọn orísun omi tí kò mọ́; àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n ń ṣeré ní orí ilẹ̀ tí kò mọ́ máa ń kó ẹyin be sì ni wọ́n yóò fi àwọn ọwọ́ wọn sí ẹnu wọn láìfọ wọ́n. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Àìsàn máa ń ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú iye àwọn aràn tí àgọ̀ gbà. Àwọn ènìyàn tí àìsàn wọn jẹ́ àwọn aràn tí kò pọ̀ (aràn níwọ̀n) kì í sáábà jìyà láti ara àìsàn yìí. Àwọn àìsàn líle lè fa onírúurú àwọn àmì àìsàn tó fi mọ́ àwọn ìfarahàn nínú ìfun (ìgbẹ́ gbuuru àti inú rírun), àìjẹunrekánú, ìrora àti àìlera gbogbogbò, àti àìdàgbàbóṣeyẹ̀ àti àìgbèrú.
translations.reference: eggs are ingested from contaminated water sources; eggs are ingested by children who play in the contaminated soil and then put their hands in their mouths without washing them. Symptoms: Morbidity is related to the number of worms harboured. People with infections of light intensity (few worms) usually do not suffer from the infection. Heavier infections can cause a range of symptoms including intestinal manifestations (diarrhoea and abdominal pain), malnutrition, general malaise and weakness, and impaired growth and physical development.
translations.hypothesis: They are often found in areas with contaminated water sources; children playing in dirt may ingest them, and they can also be transferred to the mouth through contaminated hands. Symptoms: The severity of the symptoms depends on the amount of parasites ingested. People infected with intestinal worms may experience abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea, and vomiting. In some cases, parasitic infections can lead to more serious complications, such as anemia, weight loss, and developmental delays in children.

translations.source: Àwọn àìsàn tí wọ́n le lè fa ìdíwọ́ inú tí ènìyàn gbọ́dọ̀ tọ́jú pẹ̀lú iṣẹ́ abẹ. S. stercolaris lè fa àìsàn awọ ara àti òpónà ọ̀fun bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìjẹunkánú láàrin àwọn ọmọdé. Ní ti àdínkù àjẹsára ẹni tí ó gbàlejò wọn, ajọ̀fẹ́ náà lè fa òòrèkóòrè àìsàn/àpẹẹrẹ àpínká tí ó lágbára bí wọn kò bá tètè tọ́jú wọn bí ó ṣe tọ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó máa léwu bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ìtọ́jú wà. Ní 2001, àwọn aṣojú ní Àpérò Àgbáyé fẹnukò papọ̀ lórí ọ̀nà àbáyọ (WHA54. 19) tí ó ń rọ àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n ní àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn láti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí ní kojú aràn, pàápàá jùlọ sikosomíásì àti àwọn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn. Ọgbọ́n láti ṣàkóso àwọn àìsàn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn ni láti ṣàkóso ewu àìsàn nípasẹ̀ ìtọ́jú òòrèkóòrè àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu ní àwọn agbègbè àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn.
translations.reference: Infections of very high intensity can cause intestinal obstruction that should be treated surgically. S. stercoralis may cause dermatological and gastro-intestinal morbidity and is also known to be associated with chronic malnutrition in children. In case of reduced host immunity, the parasite can cause the hyperinfection/dissemination syndrome that is invariably fatal if not promptly and properly cured and is often fatal despite the treatment. In 2001, delegates at the World Health Assembly unanimously endorsed a resolution (WHA54.19) urging endemic countries to start seriously tackling worms, specifically schistosomiasis and soil-transmitted helminths. The strategy for control of soil-transmitted helminth infections is to control morbidity through the periodic treatment of at-risk people living in endemic areas.
translations.hypothesis: Certain diseases that can be addressed through public health interventions include skin and eye infections. For example, *S. stercolaris* can cause skin infections and conjunctivitis, and it is often associated with poor hygiene among children. Furthermore, neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) can lead to chronic health problems and disability if left untreated. These diseases are a significant public health burden, particularly in low-income countries. In 2001, the World Health Assembly (WHA54.19) adopted a resolution urging member states to prioritize the control and elimination of neglected tropical diseases, including schistosomiasis and other endemic diseases. A key strategy for controlling these diseases is to implement public health interventions that target individuals at risk in endemic areas.

translations.source: Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu ni: àwọn ọmọ jéléósinmi àwọn ọmọ ọjọ́ orí wọn lọ ilé ẹ̀kọ́ àwọn obìnrin tí ọjọ́ orí wọn ti tó bímọ (tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn aláboyún ní ìpele kejì Tai ìkẹta oyún àti obìnrin tí wọ́n ń fún ọmọ lọ́yàn) Àwọn àgbàlagbà tí wọn ń ṣe àwọn iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n léwu bí àwọn olùkórè tíì tàbí awakùsà.
translations.reference: People at risk are: preschool children school-age children women of reproductive age (including pregnant women in the second and third trimesters and breastfeeding women) adults in certain high-risk occupations such as tea-pickers or miners.
translations.hypothesis: The people at risk include: young, unmarried women who are of childbearing age (especially those who are pregnant or breastfeeding), and older adults who are engaged in risky behaviors such as smoking or drinking.

translations.source: Ìtọ́jú Àjọ WHO dábàá àwọn òògùn - albendazole (400mg) àti mebendazole (500mg) - kájúẹ̀, kò wọ́n bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n rọrùn láti lo láti ọwọ́ àwọn ènìyàn tí wọn kì í ṣe òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera (b.a àwọn olùkọ́). Wọ́n ti là ìdánwò ààbò gbòòrò kọjá bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ẹgbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ènìyàn ti lò wọ́n pẹ̀lú ipa ẹ̀yìn níwọ̀n àti èyí tí kò tó nǹkan. Àti albendazole àti mebendazole ni wọ́n máa ń fún àwọn àjọ ìlera ìjọba orílẹ̀ èdè nípasẹ̀ Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ní gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n ní àrùn fún ìtọ́jú gbogbo àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n wà ní ọjọ́ orí láti lọ sí ilé ẹ̀kọ́. Ifamẹkitíìnì fún àkóso S. stercolaris ni ìrètí wà pé yóò wà ní iye tí kò jara lọ láti ọdún 2021.
translations.reference: TreatmentThe WHO recommended medicines –albendazole (400 mg) and mebendazole (500 mg) – are effective, inexpensive and easy to administer by non-medical personnel (e.g. teachers). They have been through extensive safety testing and have been used in millions of people with few and minor side-effects. Both albendazole and mebendazole are donated to national ministries of health through WHO in all endemic countries for the treatment of all children of school age. Ivermectin for the control of S. stercoralis is expected to be available at affordable price from 2021.
translations.hypothesis: The WHO recommends the use of two medications – albendazole (400mg) and mebendazole (500mg) – for treating intestinal worms, and these are readily available for use by individuals who are not healthcare professionals (e.g., teachers). They have been extensively tested and are generally safe and effective with minimal side effects. Both albendazole and mebendazole are provided to government health agencies through the World Health Organization in all countries affected by worm infections, specifically for the treatment of all school-aged children. It is expected that the cost of controlling *Schistosoma* infection will remain relatively stable from 2021 onwards.

translations.source: Àbíkú Ìsọnísókí: Ọmọ tí ó kú lẹ́yìn ọ̀sẹ̀ méjìdínlọ́gbọ̀n oyún, ṣùgbọ́n síwájú tàbí lẹ́yìn ìbí, ni a mọ̀ sí àbíkú. Ó tó mílíọ̀nù 2 àwọn àbíkú ni ó wà lọ́dọọdún - ọ̀kọ̀ọ̀kan ní ìṣẹ́jú àáyá 16 kan. Ó lé ní ìdá 40% gbogbo àwọn àbíkú ṣẹlẹ̀ ní àsìkò ìbímọ - àdánù tí ó ṣe é dènà pẹ̀lú ìtọ́jú dídára tí ó sì pọ́nnilé lásìkò ìbímọ tí ó fi mọ́ ètò àmójútó àti àǹfààní àsìkò sí ìtọ́jú aláboyún pàjáwìrì nígbà tí ó nílò. Níní ìrírí àbíkú lásìkò ìlóyún tàbí ìbímọ jẹ́ àjálù tí wọ́n kò ṣe àmójútó tí ó tó nínú àwọn ètò, ìlànà àti ìrànwọ́ àgbáyé. Àwọn ìdíyelé wà, pàápàá jùlọ fún àwọn obìnrin àti ẹbí wọn, bí ìbànújẹ́ alábiamọ, ìṣòro owó àti àwọn àbájáde ọrọ̀ ajé, àti ìdẹ́yẹsí àti èèwọ̀ pẹ̀lú. Ní 2014, Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé fọwọ́sí Ètò Ọmọ tuntun Kọ̀ọ̀kan, èyí tí a ti rí àfojúsùn àgbáyé 12 tàbí àwọn àbíkú onípele kẹta (pípẹ́) díẹ̀ nínú àpapọ̀ ìbí 1, 000 ní orílẹ̀ èdè kọ̀ọ̀kan ní 2030.
translations.reference: Stillbirth Overview: A baby who dies after 28 weeks of pregnancy, but before or during birth, is classified as a stillbirth.There are nearly 2 million stillbirths every year – one every 16 seconds. Over 40% of all stillbirths occur during labour – a loss that could be avoided with improved quality and respectful care during childbirth including routine monitoring and timely access to emergency obstetric care when required. Experiencing a stillbirth during pregnancy or childbirth is a tragedy insufficiently addressed in global agendas, policies and funded programmes. There are psychological costs, especially to women and their families, such as maternal depression, financial consequences and economic repercussions, as well as stigma and taboo.In 2014, the World Health Assembly endorsed the Every Newborn Action Plan (ENAP), which includes a global target of 12 or fewer third trimester (late) stillbirths per 1,000 total births in every country by 2030.
translations.hypothesis: Abiku Phenomenon: A child who dies within the first two weeks of life, or shortly after birth, is often referred to as an "abiku." It is estimated that there are millions of abiku children worldwide – approximately 16 per minute. A significant 40% of all abiku deaths occur during childbirth, a statistic that highlights the need for improved prenatal care and access to emergency obstetric services, particularly in underserved communities. The phenomenon of abiku deaths during infancy or childbirth is a major public health concern, especially for women and their families. Factors contributing to this include complications during pregnancy, poverty, lack of access to healthcare, and cultural beliefs and practices. In 2014, the World Health Organization endorsed the Every Newborn initiative, which aims to reduce the neonatal mortality rate (deaths within the first 28 days of life) to less than 20 per 1,000 live births in every country by 2030.

translations.source: Ní 2021, àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi àti alábọ́dé èrè 139 gbòógì ti fẹnukò lórí àfojúsùn yìí, ṣùgbọ́n àwọn orílẹ̀ 56 kò ní ṣe àṣeyọrí àfojúsùn Ètò Ọmọ tuntun Kọ̀ọ̀kan ní 2030 bí àwọn ìgbìyànjú síwájú sí i kò bá jẹ́ ṣíṣe. Bí àwọn àṣà ìwòyí bá tẹ̀síwájú, àwọn ìkókó mílíọ̀nù 15.9 yóò jẹ́ àbíkú; ó sún mọ́ ìdajì àwọn wọ̀nyí (mílíọ̀nù 7.7, 48%) yóò ṣẹlẹ̀ ní agbègbè ẹkùn Sahara Afíríkà. Okùnfà: Pẹ̀lú ìtọ́jú ìlera dídára káàkiri àsìkò ìlóyún àti ìbímọ, ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn àbíkú ṣe é dènà. Àwọn okùnfà àbíkú gbòógì ni àwọn ìṣòro oyún àti ìbímọ, oyún ọjọ́ pípẹ́, àìsàn ìyálọ́mọ bí ibà maléríà, sífílì àti KASA, àwọn ìṣòro ìyálọ́mọ pàápàá jùlọ ẹ̀jẹ̀ ríru, àti àtọ̀gbẹ, àti gbèdéke ìwọ̀n títóbi (nígbà tí ọmọ tí wọ́n kò tíì bí kò bá lè dàgbà bí ó ṣe tọ́ nítorí náà ó kéré jù bí ó ṣe yẹ lọ). Àwọn ìṣòro àsìkò ìbí ni wọ́n ṣokùnfà kò tó ìdá 10% àwọn àbíkú ni wọ́n jábọ̀ káàkiri àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ibùdó elérè pínnísín kò ní àkọsílẹ̀ nítorí àwọn gbèdéke inú àwọn àyẹ̀wò.
translations.reference: In 2021, 139 mainly high-income and upper middle-income countries had met this target, but 56 countries will not each the ENAP target by 2030 if further efforts are not made. If current trends continue, 15.9 million babies will be stillborn; nearly half of these (7.7 million, 48%) will occur in sub-Saharan Africa. Causes: With quality health care throughout pregnancy and childbirth, most stillbirths are preventable. The major causes of stillbirth include pregnancy and childbirth-related complications, prolonged pregnancy, maternal infections such as malaria, syphilis and HIV, maternal conditions especially hypertension, and diabetes, and fetal growth restriction (when an unborn baby is unable to achieve its growth potential and therefore smaller than it should be). Congenital abnormalities are responsible for less than 10% of stillbirths reported nationally in high-income countries and the exact proportion in low-income settings is unknown due to limitations in diagnostics.
translations.hypothesis: In 2021, 139 high-income and upper-middle-income countries reported progress on this issue, but 56 countries failed to achieve the targets of the Early Childhood Development Programme by 2030, given the current trajectory. As trends continue, an estimated 15.9 million children will die; the majority of these deaths (7.7 million, or 48%) will occur in the sub-Saharan Africa region. Causes: Many child deaths are preventable. The leading causes of child mortality are pneumonia, diarrhea, preterm birth complications, malaria, birth asphyxia and trauma, and neonatal sepsis. Malnutrition also contributes significantly to child deaths, as it weakens children's immune systems and makes them more vulnerable to infections. While less than 10% of child deaths occur in high-income countries, disparities persist within countries due to inequalities in access to quality services.

translations.source: Àwọn ìdí tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ọjọ́ orí ìyá àti fífa nǹkan náà lè ṣe àgbéga ewu àìsàn ìyálọ́mọ àti àbíkú. Àwọn àbíkú lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ ètò ìfètòsọ́mọbíbí láti dènà oyún àìmọ̀dí, ìlera àti ìjẹunrekánú gidi síwájú àti lásìkò ìlóyún, dídára àti ìbọ̀wọ̀fún ìtọ́jú àsìkò ìlóyún àti ìbímọ tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn alámọ̀já òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera tí wọ́n tó dórí àwọn agbẹ̀bí. Ìtọ́jú sífílì lásìkò ìlóyún lè dènà ìṣirò àwọn àbíkú 200 000, nígbà tí àmójútó ìwọ̀n ìmí olẹ̀ àti àmójútó ìbímọ àti ìdásí àsìkò nígbà tí wọ́n nílò ṣe pàtàkì láti dènà àwọn àbíkú 832 000 lásìkò ìbí àti àdínkù àwọn ikú lẹ́yìn ìbí. Wọ́n ní àjọṣepọ̀ wà láàrin àbíkú pẹ̀lú àwọn aṣokùnfà àwùjọ àti ọrọ̀ ajé líle lórí ìlera. Ìdènà àti ìtọ́jú afèsì nílò láti jẹ́ ṣíṣe àmúlò káàkiri ìtẹ̀síwájú ìtọ́jú ìlera ìyálọ́mọ, àti jù bẹ́ẹ̀ lọ.
translations.reference: Factors related to age of the mother and practicing smoking can also increase the risk of maternal disease and stillbirth. Stillbirths can be prevented through family planning to avoid unwanted pregnancies, good health and nutrition prior to and during pregnancy, quality and respectful antenatal and childbirth care including adequate skilled health personnel including midwives. Syphilis treatment in pregnancy could also prevent an estimated 200 000 stillbirths, while fetal heart rate monitoring and labour surveillance and prompt interventions when needed are crucial for preventing 832 000 intrapartum stillbirths and reducing neonatal deaths. Stillbirths are strongly linked to adverse social and economic determinants of health. Prevention and responsive care need to be integrated across the continuum of maternal health care, and beyond.
translations.hypothesis: Factors associated with maternal health and well-being can significantly increase the risk of maternal mortality and morbidity. Healthcare interventions can reduce these risks through programs that address underlying social, economic, and environmental factors, as well as provide comprehensive and quality care before, during, and after childbirth. Improved access to prenatal care can reduce the number of maternal deaths by an estimated 200,000, while interventions focused on nutrition, safe childbirth practices, and postpartum care can prevent an additional 832,000 maternal deaths and stillbirths. Collaboration between healthcare providers, social services, and community organizations is essential. Further research and investment are needed to advance maternal health and reduce these preventable tragedies.

translations.source: Lára èyí ni ìtọ́jú alápọ̀n-ọ́nlé àti alátìlẹ́yìn lásìkò ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ikú. Fífi etí sílẹ̀ sí ìrírí àti ohùn àwọn obìnrin àti àwùjọ wọn ṣe pàtàkì láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún kíkojú àwọn ọ̀ràn ìdẹ́yẹsí tí ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àbíkú. Èsì WHO: Ohun púpọ̀ ní láti di ṣíṣe sí i láti ṣàmúlò ìdènà àbíkú láàrin ètò àgbáyé àti orílẹ̀ èdè fún ìtọ́jú ìlera ńlá fún àwọn obìnrin, ọ̀dọ́mọdé, àti ìkókó. Nípasẹ̀ ọgbọ́n Àgbáyé fún Ìlera àwọn Obìnrin, Ìkókó àti Ọ̀dọ́mọdé 2016-2030 àti ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn àbá Ètò Iṣẹ́ Ọmọ tuntun Kọ̀ọ̀kan (2014), Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ń ṣiṣẹ́ láti fòpin sí àwọn àbíkú tí wọ́n ṣe é dènà, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ara akitiyan láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlera ìyálọ́mọ, ọmọ tuntun, ọmọdé àti ọ̀dọ́mọdé. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn àbíkú kì í gba ìwé ẹ̀rí ìbí bẹ́ẹ̀ sit ni wọn kì í forúkọ wọn sílẹ̀.
translations.reference: This includes respectful and supportive care in the event of a death. Listening to the experiences and voices of women and their communities is essential to help address issues of stigma associated with stillbirth. WHO response: More needs to be done to integrate stillbirth prevention within global and national agendas for high quality health care for women, adolescents, and babies. Through the Global Strategy for Women's, Children's and Adolescents Health 2016–2030 and implementing the recommendations of the Every Newborn Action Plan (2014), WHO is working to end preventable stillbirths, as part of efforts to improve maternal, newborn, child and adolescent health. Most stillbirths do not receive a birth certificate and are not registered.
translations.hypothesis: The following outlines the challenges and considerations related to maternal and newborn mortality. Addressing the emotional and psychological needs of women and their families is crucial in supporting those grieving the loss of a child. WHO's Perspective: There is a significant need to improve data collection on maternal mortality rates globally and within individual countries to better inform public health interventions for women, newborns, and children. Through the Global Strategy for Women's, Children's and Adolescents' Health (2016-2030) and the Every Woman Every Child movement (2014), the global health community is working to address preventable deaths, particularly those related to maternal, newborn, child, and adolescent health. Many maternal deaths go unrecorded, making it difficult to accurately assess the problem.

translations.source: Mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ètò ìjábọ̀ àwọn ìbí àti ikú ẹ̀yìn ìbímọ jẹ́ ọ̀rọ̀ ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn àti àmúyẹ fún mímú àdínkù bá àwọn àbíkú. Ní àfikún, ó nílò kí wọ́n mú ìrólágbára bá àwọn ètò ìròyìn ojoojúmọ́ láti sọ iye àti láti jábọ̀ àwọn àbíkú pérépéré fún àmójútó àti ìlọsíwájú ìtọ́jú ìlera. WHO pèsè àwọn irinṣẹ́ láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti mú ìlọsíwájú ìwífun-àlàyé wọn lórí àwọn àbíkú àti àwọn ikú ìkókó. WHO tún ní ìtọ́nisọ́nà láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti ṣàyẹ̀wò àti láti wádìí ikú ẹ̀dá kọ̀ọ̀kan kí wọ́n lè dábàá kí wọ́n sì ṣàmúlò àwọn ọ̀nà àbáyọ láti dènà irúfẹ́ àwọn nǹkan bẹ́ẹ̀ ní ọjọ́ iwájú àti ìpínsọ́wọ̀ọ́ ètò fún àwọn ikú àbíkú (ICD-PM). WHO àti UNICEF ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ìpè fún iṣẹ́ àjọṣe láti fòpin sí àwọn àbíkú tí wọ́n ṣe é dènà nípa:
translations.reference: Improving systems for reporting births and neonatal deaths is a matter of human rights and a prerequisite for reducing stillbirths. In addition, there is a need to strengthen routine health information systems to accurately capture and report stillbirths for monitoring and improving the quality of care. WHO provides tools to help countries improve their data on both stillbirths and neonatal deaths. WHO also has guidance to help countries review and investigate individual deaths so they can recommend and implement solutions to prevent similar ones in the future and a standard classification system for perinatal deaths (ICD-PM). WHO and UNICEF are supporting a call for collective action to end preventable stillbirths by:
translations.hypothesis: The integration of mortality data into vital registration systems and cause-of-death reporting is a crucial and essential step for improving data quality and accurately reporting on deaths for public health monitoring and progress. Furthermore, it is necessary to strengthen routine data collection systems to capture the quantity and quality of death records for improved health surveillance. The World Health Organization (WHO) provides guidance to countries to improve their capacity to collect and report on mortality data. The WHO also provides tools to help countries analyze and interpret cause-of-death data to identify trends and develop strategies to reduce mortality and morbidity, and to standardize the classification of causes of death (the International Classification of Diseases for Maternal Mortality – ICD-MM). WHO and UNICEF are advocating for increased investment in civil registration and vital statistics systems to ensure that all deaths are recorded and that data are used to inform policy and program decisions. They are focusing on the following areas:

translations.source: mímú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá ìpolongo àti àdínkù ìdẹ́yẹsí ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn obìnrin àti mọ̀lẹ́bí tí ọ̀fọ́ sẹ̀ ìrólágbára àwọn ètò ìlera láti pèsè ìtọ́jú pípéye àmúlò àwọn àfojúsùn àbíkú ní orílẹ̀ èdè àti lábẹ́lé mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ìdíwọ̀n àwọn àbíkú láti mú ìdàgbàsókè bá ẹ̀rí àti ìmọ̀.
translations.reference: increasing awareness and reducing stigma supporting bereaved women and families strengthening health systems to provide high-quality care nationalizing and localizing stillbirth targets improving measurement of stillbirths to enhance evidence and knowledge.
translations.hypothesis: The advancement of women and the empowerment of families through healthcare programs aims to reduce the high mortality rates among mothers and children in the country. Furthermore, the initiative focuses on providing support to bereaved families, offering them comfort and guidance during their time of grief.

translations.source: Àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ. Ìsọnísókí: Ó tó bílíọ́nù méjì àwọn ènìyàn káàkiri àgbáyé ni wọn kò ní àǹfààní sí àwọn òògùn, abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára, irinṣẹ́ ìṣègùn tí wọ́n ṣe pàtàkì tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn irinṣẹ́ àyẹ̀wò àìsàn, àti àwọn ohun èlò ìlera mìíràn, èyí tí ó ń ṣẹ̀dá àlàfo tí ó máa ń sáábà jẹ́ dídí láti ọwọ́ àwọn ohun èlò ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ. Ìṣòro yìí ń dàgbà sí i bí ẹ̀wọ́n ìpèsè káàkiri àgbáyé ṣe ń gbòòrò sí i, tí ó túmọ̀ sí pé àwọn ohun èlò tí wọ́n pèsè ní orílẹ̀ èdè kan lè jẹ́ dídì ní orílẹ̀ èdè kejì tí wọn yóò sì máa pín wọn káàkiri àwọn odi ìlú láti polówó tàbí tà fún swkmt oníbàárà ní ìkẹta. Ìdàgbàsókè okòwò orí ẹ̀rọ tún ń nípa lórí àṣà yìí nípa mímú kí ó rọrùn láti ra àwọn òògùn lórí ẹ̀rọ ayélujára, láti ọwọ́ àwọn orísun tí wọ́n kò ní àṣẹ ní ọ̀pọ̀ ìgbà. WHO ti ṣe ìdámọ̀ ọ̀ràn yìí ọ̀kan nínú àwọn ìṣòro ìlera kíákíá fún ọdún mẹ́wàá tí ó ń bọ̀ nítorí pé ó lé ní ọ̀kan nínú mẹ́wàá àwọn òògùn ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè ni wọ́n ṣírò pé wọ́n jẹ́ ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ.
translations.reference: Substandard and falsified medical products Overview: Up to two billion people around the world lack access to necessary medicines, vaccines, medical devices including in vitro diagnostics, and other health products, which creates a vacuum that is too often filled by substandard and falsified products. This problem is growing as global supply chains become more complex, meaning products manufactured in one country may be packaged in a second country and distributed across borders to be marketed or sold to consumers in a third. The growth of e-commerce also contributes to this trend by making it easier to purchase medicines online, often from unauthorized sources. WHO has identified this issue one of the urgent health challenges for the next decade given that more than one in ten medicines in low- and middle-income countries are estimated to be substandard or falsified.
translations.hypothesis: Counterfeit and substandard medicines. Problem: An estimated two billion people worldwide lack access to essential medicines, vaccines, diagnostic tools, and other health products, leading to preventable illnesses and deaths. This problem is exacerbated by the increasing prevalence of substandard and counterfeit medicines, which can be more common in low- and middle-income countries and are often distributed through informal channels. The rise of online pharmacies also contributes to this issue, as it can be difficult to verify the authenticity of products sold through these channels. The World Health Organization has identified this as one of the most pressing global health challenges, as it is estimated that up to 10% of medicines in low- and middle-income countries are substandard or counterfeit.

translations.source: Kò sí orílẹ̀ èdè tí kò tíì faragbá nínú ọ̀ràn yìí, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni WHO gba àwọn ìròyìn òògùn, abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti irinṣẹ́ àyẹ̀wò àìsàn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ ní gbogbo àwọn ẹkùn àgbáyé. Àti àwọn òògùn abéròjàjórúkọ àti àwọn òògùn àtọọ́dá ni wọ́n ṣe é gbélẹ̀rọ, láti orí orí àwọn ohun èlò olówó gọbọi fún jẹjẹrẹ dórí àwọn ohun èlò tí wọ́n kò wọ́n rárá fún ìtọ́jú ìrora. Àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé fẹnukò lórí ọgbọ́n gbòòrò káríayé tí ó fojúsun ìdènà, àyẹ̀wò àti ìfèsì láti tẹ̀síwájú lórí àṣeyọrí níní àǹfààní púpọ̀ sí ohùn èlò ìṣègùn dídára, aláàbò, tí ó múnádóko tí ó sì dára. Ipa: Àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ ṣẹ̀dá ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìṣòro fún ati awon ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń lo àwọn òògùn náà àti fún àwọn ẹbí, àwùjọ gbòòrò, ètò ìtọ́jú ilera àti ẹ̀wọ́n ìpèsè. Nígbà tí àwọn òògùn kò bá ṣiṣẹ́ bí ó ṣe yẹ kí wọ́n ṣiṣẹ́, wọ́n lè kùnà láti ṣèwòsàn tàbí dènà àìsàn, ń dá kún ìlòdìsí òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò, ìfiṣòfò àwọn àlùmọ́ọ́nì iyebíye tí yóò sì yọrí sí àdánù ìgboyà nínú lílo àwọn òògùn àti àìfọkàntán àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera.
translations.reference: No country remains untouched from this issue, and WHO has received reports of substandard or falsified medical medicines, vaccines and in vitro diagnostics from all regions of the world. Both generic and innovator medicines can be falsified, ranging from very expensive products for cancer to very inexpensive products for treatment of pain. WHO Member States have agreed on a comprehensive global strategy focused on prevention, detection and response to move towards achieving increased access to quality, safe, effective and quality medical products. Impact: Substandard and falsified medical products create many problems for both the individual taking the medicine and for families, the larger society, health care system and supply chain. When medicines do not work the way they should, they can fail to treat or prevent disease, contribute to antimicrobial resistance, waste precious resources and lead to a loss of confidence in medicines and distrust of healthcare providers.
translations.hypothesis: No country is immune to this issue, and the WHO receives reports of substandard, spurious, falsely labeled, and falsely packaged medical products from all over the world. These products range from simple over-the-counter medications to sophisticated devices, and they can pose a serious risk to public health. Countries that are members of the World Health Assembly are concerned about the global problem of substandard and falsified medical products, which undermine public health, erode trust in healthcare systems, and threaten lives. Impact: Substandard and falsified medical products pose a significant threat to patients, healthcare systems, and public health in general. When these products do not contain the correct ingredients or are manufactured improperly, they can fail to treat or prevent diseases, leading to treatment failure, prolonged illness, and even death. This can also lead to increased healthcare costs, loss of confidence in the healthcare system, and a decline in the quality of life for patients.

translations.source: Èyí lè ní ipa ajẹmọ́ àwùjọ òun ọrọ̀ ajé òdì, níní ipa lórí àdánù tí ó ní í ṣe nínú ìpèsè àti ṣíṣe àfikún àwọn owónàá fún ènìyàn náà àti ètò ìlera orílẹ̀ èdè. Ó ṣe é ṣe kí wọ́n kàn sí àwọn aláìsàn ní àwọn ipò níbi tí ìdènà àǹfààní ti wà sí àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn dídára àti aláàbò, ìdarí tí kò dára àti ipa ìmọ̀ tí kò lágbára. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ọ̀ràn yìí ń nípa lórí gbogbo orílẹ̀ èdè, àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè níbi tí wọ́n kò ti ètò ìdarí aláìlágbára tàbí tí kò sí ti ń rú àjàgà púpọ̀ àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ. Ní ayé tí ó ti lajú sí i, kò sí orílẹ̀ èdè tí ó ní àwọn èròjà tí ó tó àti agbára láti gbógun ti ọ̀ràn náà ní àdáwà. Nípasẹ̀ àwọn ètò àti ìgbésẹ̀ àgbáyé bí Ètò Àkóso àti Àmójútó Káríayé ẹgbẹ́ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé pẹ̀lú ọ̀ràn yìí ní àdáwà.
translations.reference: This can have a negative socioeconomic impact, contributing to related loss in productivity and adding expenses to the individual and national health system. They are most likely to reach patients in situations where there is constrained access to quality and safe medical products, poor governance and weak technical capacity. Although this issue impacts all countries, countries where there are weak or non-existent regulatory systems bear the greatest burden of substandard and falsified medical products. In an increasingly globalized world, no one country has sufficient resources and capacity to deal with this issue in isolation. Through global systems and processes such as the WHO Global Surveillance and Monitoring System and WHO Member State Mechanism,
translations.hypothesis: This issue has significant social and economic implications, impacting the well-being of individuals and the healthcare systems of affected countries. It often leads to increased strain on healthcare resources, as patients require specialized treatment and long-term care, resulting in higher costs and reduced access to other essential services. While this problem affects countries worldwide, those with limited healthcare infrastructure or a high prevalence of underlying health conditions are particularly vulnerable. In the future, it is likely that this issue will continue to pose a significant challenge. International organizations and collaborations, such as the Global Action Plan for the Prevention and Control of Noncommunicable Diseases, are working to address this challenge.

translations.source: Àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti dènà, ṣàyẹ̀wò àti fèsì sí àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ.
translations.reference: Member States are working together to prevent, detect and respond to substandard and falsified medical products.
translations.hypothesis: Member states are working together to promote, monitor, and regulate the development and use of renewable energy technologies.

translations.source: Ìdènà ìgbẹ́míaraẹni Ìsọnísókí: Ó lé ní àwọn ènìyàn 700 000 ni wọ́n kú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni lọ́dọọdún. Fún ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan, ìṣirò àwọn ìgbìyànjú láti gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni tí ó ju 20 ló wà. Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni lè wáyé ní èyíkéyìí ìpele ìgbésí ayé àti ní gbogbo ẹkùn àgbáyé. Ní 2019, ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni asíwájú okùnfà ikú kẹrin láàrin àwọn ọmọ ọdún 15-29 káàkiri àgbáyé, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni 77% gbogbo àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ṣẹlẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti àti elérè gọbọi.
translations.reference: Suicide prevention Overview: More than 700 000 people die by suicide every year. For each suicide, there are an estimated more than 20 suicide attempts. Suicide can occur at any stage of life and in all regions of the world. In 2019, suicide was the fourth leading cause of death among 15–29-year-olds globally, and 77% of all suicides happened in low- and middle-income countries.
translations.hypothesis: Suicide Statistics: Approximately 700,000 people die from suicide each year. For every suicide, there are more than 20 attempted suicides. Suicide can occur at any age and in all parts of the world. In 2019, suicide was the fourth leading cause of death among 15-29 year olds globally, and 77% of all suicides occurred in low- and middle-income countries.

translations.source: Ó fẹ́rẹ̀ẹ́ tó 20% àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni lágbàáyé ṣẹlẹ̀ láti ara mímu májèlé òògùn apako. Nígbà tí àjọṣepọ̀ láàrin ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti ìṣòro ìlera orí (pàápàá jùlọ, ìbànújẹ́ ọkàn àti ìṣòro ọtí mímu) bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni tẹ́lẹ̀ fẹsẹ̀ múlẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ lásìkò ìṣòro. Àwọn okùnfà ewu fún ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni ìrírí àdánù, ìdáwà, ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà, ìyapa ibáraẹniṣepọ̀, ìṣòro owó, ìrora ńlá àti àìsàn, ìwà ipá, àti ìjà tàbí àwọn pàjáwìrì ajẹmọ́ ọmọnìyàn mìíràn. Ìdẹ́yẹsí tí ó yí ìṣòro ilerat orí ká àti ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni túmọ̀ sí pé ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń ronú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kò wá ìrànlọ́wọ́. Àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti ìgbìyànjú láti gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni ní agbára tí ó ń nípa lórí ẹbí, ọ̀rẹ́, alábàáṣiṣẹ́pọ̀, àwùjọ àti ìlú.
translations.reference: Almost 20% of global suicides were due to pesticide self-poisoning. While the link between suicide and mental health conditions (in particular, depression and alcohol use disorders) and a previous suicide attempt is well established in high-income countries, many suicides happen impulsively in moments of crisis. Further risk factors for suicide include experience of loss, loneliness, discrimination, a relationship break-up, financial problems, chronic pain and illness, violence, abuse, and conflict or other humanitarian emergencies. Stigma around mental health conditions and suicide means many people thinking about suicide do not seek help. Suicides and suicide attempts have a ripple effect that impacts on families, friends, colleagues, communities and societies.
translations.hypothesis: Approximately 20% of global mental health issues stem from the effects of armed conflict. While the relationship between mental health and conflict is complex (often involving trauma, displacement, and loss), many mental health conditions manifest during times of conflict. The risks associated with conflict include increased rates of anxiety, depression, post-traumatic stress disorder, financial hardship, serious illness, substance abuse, violence, and other forms of social disruption. The stigma surrounding mental health and the lack of access to mental health services in conflict zones mean that many people who are suffering do not receive the help they need. The impact of conflict on mental health extends to families, communities, workplaces, and entire societies.

translations.source: Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni: àwọn òtítọ́ àti iye káàkiri àgbáyé Ìròyìn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni káàkiri àgbáyé fún 2019 GBÉ AYÉ: ṣíṣe àmúlò ìtọ́nisọ́nà funt ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè Ìdènà: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ nǹkan lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe ní àwọn ìpele ẹ̀dá kọ̀ọ̀kan, àwùjọ àti orílẹ̀ èdè láti dènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni. Ìlànà GBÉ AYÉ àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé dábàá àwọn ìpèsè mẹ́rin pàtàkì tí wọ́n múnádóko, adálórí-ẹ̀rí èyí tí ó gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ fífi sí ara ìtọ́jú orílẹ̀ èdè kọ̀ọ̀kan dórí ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni:
translations.reference: Suicide: facts and figures globally Suicide worldwide in 2019 report LIVE LIFE: an implementation guide for suicide prevention in countries Prevention: Much can be done at individual, community and national levels to prevent suicide and suicide attempts. WHO’s LIVE LIFE approach recommends four effective, evidence-based key interventions which should be included in every national response to suicide:
translations.hypothesis:
Global Health: Facts and Figures
Global Health News for 2019
WORLD: Initiatives for improving global health in various countries.
Initiatives: Many initiatives can be implemented at different levels – individual, community, and national – to improve global health and well-being.
WORLD: The World Health Organization (WHO) has identified four key priorities that must be addressed by each country to improve global health:

translations.source: fífi gbèdéke sí àǹfààní sí àwọn ọ̀nà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni níní àjọsọ pẹ̀lú ilé-iṣẹ́ ìròyìn fún ìròyìn tí ó tọ̀nà lórí ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ìgbèrú àwọn ìmọ̀-ọ́nṣe ìgbésí ayé àwùjọ ajẹmọ́ ìmọ̀lára láàrin àwọn ọ̀dọ́mọdé ìdámọ̀ àsìkò, ìgbéléwọ̀n, àkóso àti ìtẹ̀lé ẹnikẹ́ni tí ó ní ìpalára nípa àwọn ìwà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni. Àwọn òpó ìpìlẹ̀ ìdásí náà ni àlàyé ipò, àjọṣepọ̀ ọlọ́pọ̀ ẹ̀ka, ṣíṣe ìpolongo, ìṣẹ̀dá agbára, ṣíṣe ètò ìṣúná, àmójútó, àkóso àti ìgbéléwọ̀n.
translations.reference: limit access to the means of suicide interact with the media for responsible reporting of suicide foster socio-emotional life skills in adolescents early identify, assess, manage and follow up anyone who is affected by suicidal behaviours. The foundational pillars of the interventions are situation analysis, multisectoral collaboration, awareness raising, capacity building, financing, surveillance, monitoring and evaluation.
translations.hypothesis: Promoting ethical behavior in society through collaboration with media organizations to provide accurate information on ethical issues related to the lives of citizens, including social responsibility, media literacy, time management, respect, and addressing ethical dilemmas faced by individuals. The key areas of focus include: education, multi-stakeholder partnerships, policy development, capacity building, program implementation, advocacy, governance, and monitoring.

translations.source: Àwọn akitiyan ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni pè fún àkóso àti àjọṣepọ̀ láàrin àwọn ọ̀pọ̀ ẹ̀ka, tí ó fi mọ́ ìlera, ẹ̀kọ́, iṣẹ́, iṣẹ́ àgbẹ̀, okòwò, ìdájọ́, òfin, òṣèlú àti iṣẹ́ ìròyìn. Ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kì í sáábà jẹ́ wíwá ojútùú sí nítorí ìdẹ́yẹsí àti àìsí ìpolongo pé ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni jẹ́ ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ ńlá. Àwọn àwùjọ náà ń kó ipa pàtàkì nínú ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni. Irinṣẹ́ àmúlò ìdanilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àwùjọ ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ń ṣe ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwọn àwùjọ láti ṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn nǹkan pàtàkì àti ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn iṣẹ́ gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn ọ̀gangan ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ abẹ́lé. Èsì WHO: Ìpòǹgbẹ láti ṣiṣẹ́ dènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni wọ́n ti ṣe ìdámọ̀ tí wọ́n sì ti sọ di pàtàkì ní àwọn ìpele gíga jùlọ.
translations.reference: Suicide prevention efforts require coordination and collaboration among multiple sectors, including health, education, labour, agriculture, business, justice, law, defence, politics and the media. Suicide prevention is often not adequately addressed because of stigma and lack of awareness that suicide is a major public health problem. Communities also play a crucial role in preventing suicide. The WHO community engagement toolkit guides communities in identifying priorities and implementing activities according to local contexts. WHO response: The urgency to act to prevent suicides has been recognized and prioritized at the highest levels.
translations.hypothesis: Public health initiatives focus on coordination and collaboration across various sectors, including health, education, employment, agriculture, transportation, housing, law, social services, and media. Public health is not always a top priority due to competing priorities and the perception that public health issues are distant or abstract. However, communities play a crucial role in public health initiatives. The World Health Organization's (WHO) framework for community engagement provides guidance to communities on how to identify key issues and implement effective interventions, such as disease prevention programs. WHO's commitment: The organization has prioritized working with communities and has made it a key focus at all levels.

translations.source: Àdínkù ìwọ̀n ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè jẹ́ ìtọ́ka nínú àwọn Àfojúsùn ti UN (ìtọ́ka fún ìlera orí kan ṣoṣo), Ètò Gbogbogbò Iṣẹ́ ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé àti Ètò Iṣẹ́ Ìlera orí Gbòòrò 2013-2030. Ìròyìn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àgbáyé kìíní ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé, Ìdènà Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni: ìmọ̀ abágbàmu káríayé, tí wọ́n tẹ̀ jáde ní 2014, tí ó ní àfojúsùn láti mú ìgbéga bá ìpolongo pàtàkì ìlera àwùjọ ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti àwọn ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni àti sísọ ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni di pàtàkì ìlera àwùjọ. Wọ́n gba àwọn ìjọba ní ìyànjú láti ṣàmúlò àgbékalẹ̀ GBÉ AYÉ ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ti àwọn ìdásí pàtàkì pípé àti adálórí-ẹ̀rí fún ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti láti mú ìdàgbàsókè tàbí ìrólágbára bá àwọn ọgbọ́n ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àgbáyé gbígbòòrò nínú ìlànà ìlera àwùjọ ọlọ́pọ̀ ẹ̀ka. Ìdàrí àti àkóso àgbáyé lórí ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni wọ́n túbọ̀ ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún nípa àtẹ̀jáde àwọn ọgbọ́n ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni orílẹ̀ èdè: ìlọsíwájú, àwọn àpẹẹrẹ àti ìtọ́ka. Ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ ìwífún àlàyé àti ìṣirò hebt fún ìkéde àti ṣíṣe ìpinnu, àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé tẹ Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni jákèjádò àgbáyé ní 2019: Àwọn Ìṣirò Ìlera Àgbáyé.
translations.reference: The reduction of suicide rates in countries is an indicator in the UN Sustainable Development Goals (the only indicator for mental health), WHO’s General Programme of Work and WHO’s Comprehensive Mental Health Action Plan 2013–2030. The first WHO world suicide report, Preventing suicide: a global imperative, published in 2014, aimed to increase awareness of the public health significance of suicide and suicide attempts and make suicide prevention a public health priority. Governments are encouraged to implement WHO’s LIVE LIFE package of effective and evidence-based key interventions for suicide prevention, and to develop or strengthen comprehensive national suicide prevention strategies in a multisectoral public health approach. National leadership and coordination on suicide prevention are further supported by the publication of National suicide prevention strategies: progress, examples and indicators. Recognizing that data and statistics are necessary for advocacy and decision-making, WHO published Suicide worldwide in 2019: Global Health Estimates.
translations.hypothesis: Global health priorities are reflected in the Sustainable Development Goals (specifically Goal 3 for good health and well-being), the World Health Organization's General Programme of Work, and the Global Action Plan for Noncommunicable Diseases 2013-2030. A key global health priority identified by the World Health Organization is "health in all policies," launched in 2014, which aims to integrate health considerations into all policy areas and to make health a central consideration in decision-making. It encourages governments to implement the WHO's Global Action Plan for Health in All Policies, which provides a framework for integrating health considerations into a wide range of policy areas. International coordination and governance on health in all policies have been strengthened through the development of national action plans, examples, and indicators. To promote evidence-based policymaking and decision-making, the World Health Organization published "Health in All Policies: Towards a Framework for Action" in 2019.

translations.source: Àfojúsùn àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ni ayé tí ikú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kò ti síwájú okùnfà ikú àti níbi tí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbìyànjú tàbí ronú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ti lè rí ìrọ̀rùn láti béèrè fún ìrànlọ́wọ́ èyí tí wọ́n ń pèsè nínú ipá àti ọ̀wọ̀ fún gbogbo ènìyàn.
translations.reference: WHO’s vision is a world where death by suicide is no longer a leading cause of death and where people who are attempting or thinking about suicide feel comfortable seeking help which is provided capably and respectfully to all.
translations.hypothesis: The World Health Organization's initiative to combat malaria has made significant progress in reducing mortality rates and providing access to care for people who are vulnerable or concerned about the disease, offering hope and support to everyone.

translations.source: Ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése Ìsọnísókí: Ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése ni ọ̀rọ̀ ìperí gbòòrò láti ṣàpèjúwe àwọn ìlànà, iṣẹ́ àkànṣe àti ìdókòwò tí ó ń pèsè àwọn àǹfààní lónìí láì kọlu ìlera àyíká, àwùjọ àti ti ara ẹni lọ́jọ́ iwájú. Àwọn ìlànà wọ̀nyí ni wọ́n sáábà máa ń ṣàpèjúwe gẹ́gẹ́ bí àìléwu nítorí pé wọ́n fojúsùn mímú àdínkù bá ipa ìdàgbàsókè lórí àyíká. Àmọ́ sá, àwọn àǹfààní ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése ń jẹ́ rírí káàkiri àwọn onírúurú ẹ̀ka ìlera àti àlàáfíà ọmọnìyàn, tí ó fi mọ́ àdínkù abayíkájẹ́- àti àìsàn adálórí àyíká, àwọn àbájáde ìlera tí ó lọ síwájú àti àdínkù ìṣòro. Bí ìṣòro àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ ṣe ń ṣe pàtàkì sí i, àwọn ọgbọ́n àmúṣiṣẹ́ fún àdínkù ìbàjẹ́, ìtẹ̀síwájú ìlera àti díndín iyeó kù ń pèsè oríṣiríṣi àǹfààní fún àwọn onírúurú àwọn àjọ.
translations.reference: Sustainable development Overview: Sustainable development is a broad term to describe policies, projects and investments that provide benefits today without sacrificing environmental, social and personal health in the future. These policies are often described as green because they focus on limiting the impact of development on the environment. However, the benefits of sustainable development are also felt across a wide cross section of human health and well-being, including reductions in pollution- and environment-related disease, improved health outcomes and decreased stress. As the threats of climate change become increasingly important, win-win strategies for mitigation, health improvement and cost savings offer a range of advantages for various stakeholders.
translations.hypothesis: The Impact of Advancements in Medicine: Advancements in medicine refer to the innovations, procedures, and technologies that are improving healthcare, societal well-being, and individual lives today and in the future. These advancements are often described as breakthroughs because they significantly improve the quality of life. Consequently, the benefits of advancements in medicine are far-reaching, impacting various sectors of healthcare and well-being, including disease prevention, advanced treatments, and improved quality of life. For example, advancements in eye care, such as improved surgical techniques, diagnostic tools, and treatments, offer numerous benefits for individuals with various eye conditions, leading to better vision and overall quality of life. Similarly, advancements in injury management, healthcare development, and cost reduction offer various benefits for different communities.

translations.source: Fún àpẹẹrẹ, àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera kánrinkése lè ṣẹ̀dá àwọn àǹfààní ìlera aláìsàn àti òṣìṣẹ́ nígbà tí wọ́n tún ń pa agbára mọ́, ṣe àdínkù ewu ojú ọjọ́ àti ìṣẹ̀dá àdínkù iyeó ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera àgbáyé ti pinnu láti lépa ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése nínú gbogbo iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ dènà àwọn ènìyàn ọjọ́ iwájú lọ́wọ́ àwọn ewu ìlera ọjọ́ òní tí wọ́n ń dàgbà. Ipa: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ọgbọ́n ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése lè pèsè àwọn àṣeyọrí pàtàkì fún ìlera, ojú ọjọ́ àti àyíká, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àǹfààní rẹ̀ lè jẹ́ rírí lẹ́sẹ̀kẹsẹ̀. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àfojúsùn ìlera àti ìdàgbàsókè lè jẹ́ àṣeyọrí papọ̀ nípa gbígbógun ti ìbàfẹ́fẹ́jẹ́, èyí tí ó ń ṣokùnfà ìṣirò mílíọ̀nù 7 ikú àìtọ́jọ́ lọ́dọọdún. Lára àwọn ohun abàyíkájẹ́ ni àwọn abojú-ọjọ́-jẹ́ alágbára ọlọ́jọ́ díẹ̀ (SLCPs) – pàápàá jùlọ èédú dúdú, òyì àrá àti gáàsì mẹtíìnì– tí wọ́n léwu púpọ̀ fún ìlera ṣùgbọ́n àsìkò ìsẹ̀mí wọn kì í pẹ́.
translations.reference: For example, greener health care operations can generate patient and worker health benefits while also saving energy, mitigating climate risks and creating long-term cost savings. The World Health Organization is committed to pursuing sustainable development in all its work to help protect the people of tomorrow from the health growing health risks of today. Impact: Many sustainable development strategies can offer significant wins for health, climate and the environment, and the benefits can be seen almost immediately. For example, many health and development goals can be achieved simultaneously by tackling air pollution, which is responsible for an estimated 7 million premature deaths annually. Polluting emissions include powerful short-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs)—particularly black carbon, ozone and methane—which are very harmful to health but have only brief atmospheric lifetimes.
translations.hypothesis: For example, the widespread use of certain chemicals in healthcare settings can create hazardous waste and expose workers to dangerous substances, increasing the risk of occupational illnesses and long-term health problems. The World Health Organization has committed to reducing the use of hazardous chemicals in all of its operations to protect the health of future generations from the risks of today's environmental hazards. Impact: The reduction of hazardous chemicals can have significant benefits for public health, the environment, and the economy, and its impact can be far-reaching. For example, the reduction of certain medical and industrial chemicals can lead to improved air and water quality, which can reduce the incidence of respiratory illnesses and other health problems. Among the most concerning substances are persistent organic pollutants (POPs) – including pesticides, industrial chemicals, and byproducts – which are highly toxic to humans and the environment, and can persist in the environment for long periods.

translations.source: Àwọn ìlànà ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ àti ìdókòwò tí wọ́n ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ àwùjọ mímọ́, pẹ̀lú ìrìn àti kẹ̀kẹ́ gígùn, lè ní ipa rere lórí dídára afẹ́fẹ́. Èyí lè ṣe àdínkù àwọn ewu ìlera lásìkò kan náà, tí ó fi mọ́ ẹ̀gbà, àìsàn ọkàn, àìsàn ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró àti àwọn jẹjẹrẹ kan. Wọ́n tún lè ṣe àdínkù ìṣirò àwọn ikú mílíọ̀nù 1.25 ọdọọdún láti ara ìfarapa òpópónà, nígbà tí ó ń mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá àǹfààní sí àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn àgbègbè tí àwọn ènìyàn pọ̀ sí. Àwọn ìlànà ilégbèé ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése lè túbọ̀ ṣe àdínkù àwọn ìtújáde nípasẹ̀ àwọn ìpinnu bí ilẹ̀ ilé àti ìlò ilẹ̀, àwọn àṣàyàn ohun èlò ìkọ́lé, àwọn àbùdá àgbékalẹ̀ àti afẹ́fẹ́ òhun agbára. Èsì WHO: Iṣẹ́ WHO ń gbà ìtọ́nisọ́nà láti ọwọ́ àwọn Àfojúsùn Ìdàgbàsókè Kánrinkése, pàápàá jùlọ Àfojúsùn 3: "Ẹ rí i dájú pé ẹ ní ìgbé ayé ìlera àti ìgbéga àlàáfíà fún gbogbo ọjọ́ orí."
translations.reference: Transport policies and investments that favour clean public transport, along with walking and cycling, can have a dramatic impact on air quality. This can reduce multiple health risks at the same time, including stroke, heart attack, lung disease and some cancers. They can also reduce the estimated 1.25 million deaths annually from traffic injury, while also improving access to health services, particularly in densely populated areas. Sustainable development housing policies can further reduce emissions through considerations such as building siting and land use, choices of construction materials, design features and ventilation and energy. WHO response: WHO’s work is guided by the Sustainable Development Goals, particularly Goal 3: “Ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages.”
translations.hypothesis: Strategies for reducing air pollution from transportation and industry, including cleaner fuels and stricter regulations, can have a significant positive impact on public health. This can lead to a reduction in the incidence of certain diseases, such as respiratory illnesses, heart disease, and stroke, and can also help to prevent millions of premature deaths annually, while also contributing to economic benefits by increasing productivity and reducing healthcare costs, especially in densely populated areas. Furthermore, sustainable development practices can further mitigate these risks through measures such as green building design, efficient land use, and the promotion of renewable energy sources. WHO Statement: The World Health Organization works closely with Member States, particularly through Resolution 3: "To ensure a healthy and safe environment for all."

translations.source: Àwọn Àfojúsùn náà mú àwọn iṣẹ́ àkànṣe àti àwọn ìlànà káàkiri Àjọ wà, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ìtọ́nisọ́nà lórí ilégbèé, ìdàgbàsókè ìlú, àwọn ètò ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ àti àgbékalẹ̀ ìtọ́jú ìlera. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, àwọn ilégbèé WHO àti ìtọ́nisọ́nà ìlera, tí wọ́n kọ́kọ́ tẹ̀ jáde ní 2018, ṣe ìdámọ̀ ipa àwọn ìṣòro ilégbèé tí ó ń pọ̀ sí i lórí ìlera ọmọnìyàn nítorí ìdàgbàsókè ìlú, ojú ọjọ́ àti àwọn àyípadà iye ènìyàn. Àwọn ìtọ́nisọ́nà ń pèsè àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́, àwọn alábàáṣiṣẹ́pọ̀ àti àwùjọ pẹ̀lú àwọn àbá adálórí-ẹ̀rí tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìtó ààyè ìgbé (ọ̀pọ̀ èèyàn), àwọn ìwọ̀n ìgbóná kékeré àti gọbọi inú ilé, àwọn ewu ìfarapa nínú ilé, àti níní àǹfààní sí ilégbèé fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní ìṣòro iṣẹ́ ṣíṣe. Ètò Ìlera Ìgboro (EII) tún fojúsun àwọn ọ̀nà fún ìlú láti fààyè gba ìlera rere nípa ṣíṣe àyàṣe iṣẹ́ tí ó múnádóko lórí ìbàfẹ́fẹ́jẹ́ ìlú àti àwọn abàyíkájẹ́ ojú ọjọ́ ọlọ́jọ́ péréte (SLCPs) – gbígba ẹ̀mí là nípa mímú àjọṣepọ̀ bá ìlera, àyíká àti ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése. Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé tún ṣe àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àgbájọ Afẹ́fẹ́ àti Ojú ọjọ́ mímọ́ lórí Ìpolongo Ayé Ìmí, èyí tí ó fojúsun mímú àdínkù bá ìbàfẹ́fẹ́jẹ́ àti mímú àdínkù bá mílíọ̀nù 7 ikú àìtọ́jọ́ lọ́dọọdún tí ó ń fà.
translations.reference: The Goals inform projects and policies across the Organization, including guidelines on housing, urban development, transportation systems and health care delivery.
