For example, the WHO Housing and health guidelines, first published in 2018, highlight the increasing impact of housing conditions on human health in light of urban growth, climate and demographic changes. The guidelines provide Member States, partners and the public with evidence-based recommendations relevant to inadequate living space (crowding), low and high indoor temperatures, injury hazards in the home, and accessibility of housing for people with functional impairments. The Urban Health Initiative (UHI) also focuses on ways for cities to enable good health by catalysing effective action on urban air pollution and short-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs)—saving lives by linking health, environment and sustainable development. WHO also collaborates with the Climate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAC) on the Breathe Life campaign, which aims to cut air pollution and reduce the 7 million premature deaths annually that it causes.
translations.hypothesis: The guidelines and recommendations address various aspects of health, including housing, urban development, transportation, and environmental health. For example, the WHO and UN guidelines on healthy housing, first released in 2018, assess the impact of housing conditions on health, considering factors such as urbanization, climate change, and population density. The guidelines provide recommendations for member states, health professionals, and communities on issues such as indoor air quality (a major public health concern), water and sanitation, household safety, and the impact of housing on people with disabilities. The Healthy Cities initiative also focuses on strategies for cities to promote health by addressing social determinants of health and reducing exposure to sources of pollution and environmental hazards – a comprehensive approach to public health. The World Health Organization has also partnered with the BreatheLife campaign to address air pollution, with a goal of reducing premature deaths caused by air pollution by a million each year.

translations.source: Sífílì Ìsọnísókí: Sífílì jẹ́ àìsàn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ tí kòkòrò bakitéríà ń tàn ká láti ọwọ́ Treponema pallidum èyí tí ó máa ń fa ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ewu àìsàn àti ikú, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ṣe é wòsàn. Sífílì máa ń ràn ká nípasẹ̀ ìfarakínra ìbálòpọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn ògòdò àrùn, nípasẹ̀ ìfàjẹ̀sínilára tàbí láti ara aláboyún obìnrin sí ọmọ inú rẹ̀. Ìkóràn sífílì láti ara ìyá-sí-ọmọ sáábà máa ń le púpọ̀ fún ọlẹ̀ ní àwọn ọ̀ràn tí wọ́n kò dá mọ̀ tàbí ṣe ìtọ́jú tó níbẹ̀rẹ̀ ìlóyún. Sífílì ìyálọ́mọ, nígbà tí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, wọ́n pẹ́ tọ́jú rẹ̀ tàbí wọn kò tọ́jú rẹ pẹlu pẹnisilíìnì, ń yọrí sí àwọn àbájáde ìbí líle nínú ìṣirò àwọn ọ̀ràn 50-80%, dá lórí ìpele sífílì.
translations.reference: Syphilis Overview: Syphilis is a bacterial sexually transmitted infection (STI) caused by Treponema pallidum which results in substantial morbidity and mortality, and it is curable. Syphilis is transmitted through sexual contact with the infectious lesions, via blood transfusion or from a pregnant woman to her fetus. Mother-to-child transmission of syphilis is usually devastating to the fetus in cases that were not detected or treated sufficiently early in pregnancy. Maternal syphilis, when untreated, treated late or not treated with penicillin, results in adverse birth outcomes (ABOs) estimated in 50−80% of cases, depending on the stage of syphilis.
translations.hypothesis: Syphilis: Syphilis is a sexually transmitted infection caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum. It can cause a variety of health problems and, if left untreated, can be fatal. Syphilis is typically spread through sexual contact with an infected person, through the use of shared needles, or from a pregnant woman to her unborn child. Congenital syphilis, which is transmitted from a mother to her child during pregnancy, can cause serious health problems for the baby, including developmental delays. Early syphilis, when treated with antibiotics, can usually be cured with penicillin, which can prevent serious complications and improve the chances of a healthy pregnancy.

translations.source: Àwọn àbájáde ìbí líle sáábà máa ń tí ó fi mọ́ àbíkú (lọ́pọ̀ ìgbà), ikú ìkókó, àìgbó, ìwọ̀n ìwúwo ìbí pínnísín, àti àwọn ìkókó tí àìsàn sọ di abiùn. Sífílì lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ àwọn àṣà ìbálòpọ̀ aláàbò tí ó fi mọ́ ìlò ọ̀rá ìdáàbòbò bí ó ti tọ́ àti déédéé. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn pẹ̀lú sífílì kò ní èyíkéyìí àmì àìsàn kankan tàbí ní àwọn àmì àìsàn kékeré tí wọ́n kì í ṣàkíyèsí. Ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ àìsàn nípasẹ̀ àwọn àyẹ̀wò láàbù àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ọ̀ràn àìsàn yóò dènà ìkóràn síwájú àti àbájáde ìlóyún líle tí ó fi mọ́ àbájáde àrùn sífílì. Àyẹ̀wò lè ṣe é ṣe nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn àyẹ̀wò ayákánkán tí wọ́n lè pèsè àbájáde ní kò tó ìṣẹ́jú 20, tí ó ń fi ààyè gba ìtọ́jú àsìkò.
translations.reference: These ABOs are often severe and include stillbirth (most commonly), neonatal death, prematurity, low birth weight, and congenitally infected infants. Syphilis can be prevented through safer sex practices including the correct and consistent use of condoms. Many people with syphilis do not have any symptoms or have only minor symptoms that go unnoticed. Identifying infection through laboratory tests and treatment of positive cases will prevent further transmission and adverse pregnancy outcomes including congenital syphilis. Screening can be done using rapid tests that can provide results in less than 20 minutes, allowing for immediate treatment.
translations.hypothesis: Sexually transmitted infections (STIs) can cause infertility (in some cases), ectopic pregnancies, pelvic inflammatory disease, complications during childbirth, and chronic illnesses. Early detection through regular screening and appropriate treatment can prevent these complications. Many people with STIs may not experience any symptoms or may have only mild symptoms that they may not recognize. Early diagnosis of STIs through screening and the management of related health conditions can prevent future complications and improve outcomes for individuals with STIs. Screening can be done by performing various tests that can detect STIs, some of which can be completed in under 20 minutes, providing timely results.

translations.source: Nígbà tí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, sífílì yóò lọ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò la onírúurú ìpele kọjá. Ìtọ́jú fún sífílì máa ń jẹ́ ṣíṣe pẹ̀lú òògùn pẹnisilíìnì benzathine tí ó ṣe é gún bí abẹ́rẹ́, pẹ̀lú ìlò 1 sí 3 èyí tí ó dá lórí ìpele àìsàn náà. Pẹnisilíìnì nìkan ló lè dènà àwọn sífílì àsìkò ìbí. Ìtọ́jú pẹ̀lú pẹnisilíìnì ti ní ìdènà láti ọwọ́ ọ̀wọ́ngógó òòrèkóòrè àti ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ ní àwọn onírúurú orílẹ̀ èdè, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi, pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn pẹ̀lú sífílì kì í ṣàfihàn àwọn àmì àìsàn tàbí wọn kì í ní ìmọ̀lára wọn.
translations.reference: When untreated, syphilis lasts many years and goes through different stages. Treatment for syphilis is done with injectable penicillin benzathine, with 1 to 3 doses depending on the stage of the disease. Only penicillin can prevent congenital syphilis. Treatment with penicillin has been hampered by periodic and prolonged shortages of benzathine penicillin in numerous countries, including high-, low- and middle-income countries. Symptoms: Many people with syphilis do not present symptoms or do not perceive them.
translations.hypothesis: Syphilis, if left untreated, can progress through several stages and cause serious health problems. Treatment for syphilis typically involves injections of benzathine penicillin, usually administered once or twice, depending on the stage of the infection. Penicillin is the only medication proven to cure syphilis. Penicillin treatment has been effective in controlling syphilis rates in many countries, but it remains a public health concern in some regions, particularly in developing countries. Symptoms: Many people with syphilis do not experience any symptoms, or they may not be aware of them.

translations.source: Sífílì, àìtọ́jú lo ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó pín sí onírúurú àwọn ìpele:. Ìkóràn ìbálòpọ̀ máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ lásìkò ìbẹ̀rẹ̀pẹ̀pẹ̀, ìyẹn ni pé, fún bí ọdún méjì lẹ́yìn àrùn. Lásìkò ìpele ìsáájú (sífílì ìpìlẹ̀), èyí tí yóò lo bí ọjọ́ 21, egbò àdáwà, aláìnírora roboto tí ó sáábà máa ń le kan (egbò) yóò farahàn ní agbègbè ìgbàsára (ìfarakanra), ní ọ̀pọ̀ ìgbà nínú òbò, okó tàbí fùrọ̀ (ṣùgbọ́n ó tún lè jẹ́ àfikún ojú ara) bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè lọ láìfarahàn. Ògòdò náà yóò gba ìwòsàn pẹ̀lú tàbí láìsí ìwòsàn láàrin ọ̀sẹ̀ 3 tàbí 10. Bí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, àìsàn náà yóò tẹ̀síwájú sí ìpele àtẹ̀lé.
translations.reference: Untreated, syphilis lasts many years and is characterized into different stages. Sexual transmission typically occurs during early stages, i.e., up to 2 years after infection. During the initial phase (primary syphilis), which lasts on average 21 days, a solitary, painless, usually hard and round sore (chancre) appears at the site of inoculation (contact), often in the vagina, penis or anus (but it may also be extra-genital) and may go unnoticed. The chancre heals with or without treatment within 3 to 10 weeks. If untreated, the disease progresses to the secondary stage.
translations.hypothesis: Shingles, also known as herpes zoster, can manifest at various stages: The initial outbreak often occurs with a rash, typically appearing two days after the onset of the illness. During the prodromal phase (the early stage of shingles), which can last up to 21 days, individuals may experience pain, tingling, or itching in a specific area of the body, often on the torso, back, or face (though it can also affect other areas). This pain can be intense and may precede the appearance of the rash. The rash itself typically appears within 1-2 days and consists of small, fluid-filled blisters that usually heal within 3 to 10 days. If the symptoms do not improve, the condition may progress to the next stage.

translations.source: Sífílì àtẹ̀lé ní máa ń ní àbùdá kúrúkùsá tí ó lè jẹ́ onírúurú kí ó sì sé ìsìnjẹ àwọn ìṣòro àrùn tàbí àìsàn mìíràn, ṣùgbọ́n ó sáábà máa ń mú àtẹ́lẹwọ́ àti àtẹ̀lesẹ̀. Kúrúkùsá náà kì í húnni bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè kéré láti jẹ́ àfojúfò. Ní àwọn ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n gbóná tí wọ́n sì ní omi, bí ihò ìdí àti ẹnu, ògòdò títóbi funfun tàbí ewú lè dàgbà ní ọ̀gangan egbò ìpìlẹ̀ (condyloma lata). Àwọn àpẹẹrẹ àti àmì sífílì àtẹ̀lé máa ń sàn fúnra rẹ̀, kódà láìsí ìtọ́jú. Láìsí ìtọ́jú, ìpele afarasin bẹ̀rẹ̀.
translations.reference: Secondary syphilis is characterized by a rash that can vary widely and mimic other infectious or non-infectious conditions, but characteristically affects the palms and soles. The rash is non-itchy and can be minimal enough to be overlooked. In warm and moist areas of the body, such as the anus and labia, large white or grey raised lesions may develop at the location of the primary chancre (condyloma lata). The symptoms and signs of secondary syphilis spontaneously resolve, even without treatment. Without treatment, the latent stage starts.
translations.hypothesis: Syphilis is a sexually transmitted infection that can have a wide range of symptoms and can lead to serious health problems if left untreated, but it can also be asymptomatic and go unnoticed. The initial symptoms, when present, can be mild and easily mistaken for other conditions. In areas of the body with moist surfaces, such as the genitals, mouth, and rectum, the primary symptom is a painless sore called a chancre. These sores typically appear within 3 weeks of exposure and heal on their own, even without treatment. However, without treatment, the infection progresses to the secondary stage.

translations.source: Sífílì afarasin kì í ṣàfihàn àwọn àmì àìsàn àti àpẹẹrẹ kankan bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìtọ́jú rẹ̀ yàtọ̀ sí ìpele ìbẹ̀rẹ̀. Lẹ́yìn àwọn ọdún tàbí ẹ̀wádún láìsí ìtọ́jú, àwọn àìsàn ajẹmọ́ ọ̀páyìnmọ́pọlọ àti àwọn àìsàn ọkàn àti àwọn mìíràn lè wáyé. Ìtọ́jú: Àyẹ̀wò ìtọ́jú sífílì máa ń dá lórí ìtàn, àyẹ̀wò ojúkojú, àyẹ̀wò láàbù ènìyàn àti nígbà mìíràn àyẹ̀wò ẹ̀rọ afẹ́wẹ́lẹ́, nítorí àmì àìsàn kò wọ́pọ̀ tàbí farahàn. Ìdámọ̀ àrùn aláìlámì àìsàn nípasẹ̀ àwọn àyẹ̀wò láàbù àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ọ̀ràn àrùn yóò dènà ìkóràn síwájú sí i àti àwọn àbájáde oyún líle, tí ó fi mọ́ sífílì amútinúwá. Àwọn àyẹ̀wò láàbù fún sífílì tí ó fi mọ́ ìdámọ̀ tààrà T. pallidum tàbí ìdámọ̀ tààrà bí àyẹ̀wò sẹ́rọ́ọ̀mù inú ẹ̀jẹ̀.
translations.reference: Latent syphilis presents no clinical symptoms or signs and the treatment is different from the early phase. After years or decades without treatment, neurological and cardiovascular diseases among others may occur. Treatment: Syphilis diagnosis is based on the person’s history, physical examination, laboratory testing and sometimes radiology, as symptoms are not common or noticeable. Identifying asymptomatic infection through laboratory tests and treatment of positive cases will prevent further transmission and adverse pregnancy outcomes, including congenital syphilis. Laboratory tests for syphilis include direct detection of T. pallidum or indirect methods such as serology.
translations.hypothesis: Syphilis does not typically present with symptoms or signs at its initial stage. However, if left untreated for years or decades, it can lead to serious complications affecting the heart, brain, and other organs. Diagnosis: Syphilis diagnosis is based on a patient's history, physical examination, and, in some cases, blood tests or other laboratory tests, as symptoms may not always be present or obvious. Early diagnosis and treatment of syphilis through physical examinations and the investigation of potential symptoms can prevent future complications and improve the patient's overall health. Specific tests for syphilis include rapid plasma reagin (RPR) or Venereal Disease Research Laboratory (VDRL) tests.

translations.source: Àwọn àgbéyẹ̀wò àti àyẹ̀wò kánkán tún wà bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n lè pèsè àwọn àbájáde ní kò tó ìṣẹ́jú 20, tí yóò sì mú ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ ìtọ́jú kánkán wá. Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé dábàá pé kí sífílì máa jẹ́ títọ́jú nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò abẹ́rẹ́ pẹnisilíìnì (benzathine penicillin G) pẹ̀lú ìlò 1 tàbí mẹ́ta, dá lórí ìpele àìsàn náà. WHO ti ṣàlàyé àwọn ìlànà ìtọ́jú fún sífílì, àti ọkàn pàtàkì fún àyẹ̀wò àti ìtọ́jú sífílì nínú oyún. Sífílì amútinúwá Kò sí àyẹ̀wò kánkán báyìí fún àwọn sífílì amútinúwá.
translations.reference: Rapid screening and diagnostic tests are also available and can provide results in less than 20 minutes, facilitating immediate treatment initiation. WHO recommends syphilis to be treated with injectable penicillin (benzathine penicillin G) with 1 or 3 doses, depending on the stage of the disease. WHO has detailed treatment guidelines for syphilis, and one specifically for the screening and treatment of syphilis in pregnancy. Congenital syphilis There are currently no diagnostic tests for congenital syphilis.
translations.hypothesis: Rapid testing and screening methods can provide results in under 20 minutes, enabling prompt treatment. The World Health Organization recommends that syphilis be treated with a single intramuscular injection of benzathine penicillin G, either once or twice, depending on the severity of the infection. The WHO has also outlined guidelines for syphilis testing and treatment in various settings. Congenital Syphilis There are currently no rapid tests for congenital syphilis.

translations.source: Gbogbo alààyè tàbí àbíkú ìkókó àwọn obìnrin tí wọ́n ní sífílì gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ àgbéyẹ̀wò fún ẹ̀rí sífílì amútinúwá. Àwọn ìkókó àbíyè gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ yíyẹ̀wò àti àyẹ̀wò lásìkò ìbí láti sọ òté ìtọ́jú. Nígbà tí wọ́n bá tọ́ka sí i, bákan náà ní fún àlàfo oṣooṣù fún oṣù 3 títí àyẹ̀wò sẹ́rọ́ọ̀mù inú ẹ̀jẹ̀ ìkókó, tí ó sì wà, láìsí àmì àìsàn, nítorí àwọn àjẹsára ìyá lè ran ọmọ náà lásìkò oyún láìṣeṣẹ́.
translations.reference: All live or stillborn infants of women with syphilis should be examined for evidence of congenital syphilis. Live-born infants should be examined and tested at birth to define treatment requirement. When indicated, also at monthly intervals for 3 months until the infant’s serology are, and remain, negative, as maternal antibodies can be passively transmitted to the baby during pregnancy.
translations.hypothesis: All pregnant or postpartum women with syphilis must be evaluated for congenital syphilis. Prenatal screening and testing are essential to prevent transmission. If syphilis is detected, treatment with a penicillin regimen for 3 weeks is recommended until the results of a cerebrospinal fluid examination are available, and the patient is asymptomatic, as penicillin can safely treat the infant in utero.

translations.source: Taeníásí àti sisikasósì Ìsọnísókí: Àìsàn pẹ̀lú aràn pẹlẹbẹ atara-ẹrankosẹ̀ lè fa àwọn ìṣòro ọ̀tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀ méjì: àrùn taẹniásì àti sisikakósì. Taeníásí jẹ́ àrùn ìfun pẹ̀lú aràn pẹlẹbẹ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó máa ń wáyé nígbà tí ènìyàn bá jẹ ẹlẹ́dẹ̀ tútù tàbí tí kò jinná, tàbí ẹlẹ́dẹ̀ tí ó ní àìsàn. Taeníásí kò ní ipa gbòógì kankan lórí ìlera ẹ̀dá. Àmọ́ sá, àwọn ẹyin aràn pẹlẹbẹ tí ẹni tí ó ní aràn pẹlẹbẹ nínú yóò jáde bọ́ síta lára ìgbẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àìsí ìmọ́tótó tí ó tọ́ lè mú àyíká di àìmọ́.
translations.reference: Taeniasis and cysticercosis Overview: Infection with the tapeworm Taenia solium can result in two distinct conditions: taeniasis and cysticercosis. Taeniasis is the intestinal infection with the tapeworm and it occurs when a person eats raw or undercooked, infected pork. Taeniasis has no major impact on human health. However, tapeworm eggs pass in the faeces of the tapeworm carrier and in the absence of a proper sanitation may contaminate the environment.
translations.hypothesis: Tinea and Yeast Infections: Skin conditions involving the scalp can lead to two distinct problems: tinea and yeast infections. Tinea is a fungal infection that occurs when a person comes into contact with a contaminated object or person, or when the scalp is damaged or irritated. Tinea does not directly affect fertility. However, the inflammation and itching associated with tinea can sometimes interfere with sexual activity, which could indirectly impact fertility.

translations.source: Nígbà tí aràn pẹlẹbẹ lè mú ẹlẹ́dẹ̀ ṣàìsàn, ẹyin T.solium náà lè mú kí ènìyàn ṣàìsàn bí ènìyàn bá jẹ wọn (nípasẹ̀ ọ̀nà ìgbẹ́-sí-ẹnu, tàbí nípa jíjẹ oúnjẹ tàbí omi tí ó ti di àìmọ́). Jíjẹ ẹyin aràn pẹlẹbẹ máa ń fa àìsàn pẹ̀lú ajọ̀fẹ́ bí ìdin nínú àwọn ìṣù ara bí isan, awọ ara, ojú àti ètò òpónà ọpọlọmẹ́yìn àárín gbùngbùn (sisikakósì ènìyàn), pẹ̀lú ipá líle lórí ìlera. Nígbà tí àwọn ògòdò paali bá dàgbà nínú ọpọlọ, àìsàn náà ni wọ́n ń pè ní nirosisikakósì. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Taeníásí sáábà máa ń ní àbùdá abọ́ọ́dé àti àwọn àbùdá aláìsọpàtó tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìsàn ìfun (inú rírun, àyà rínrìn, ìgbẹ́ gbuuru, abbl.). Àwọn àmì àìsàn wọ̀nyí lè tẹ̀síwájú títí aràn pẹlẹbẹ náà yóò fi kú lẹ́yìn ìtọ́jú, bí bẹ́ẹ̀ kọ́ ó lè wà láyé fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún.
translations.reference: While tapeworm eggs are infective for pigs, T. solium eggs may also infect humans if they are ingested by a person (via the fecal-oral route, or by ingesting contaminated food or water). Ingestion of tapeworm eggs causes infection with the larval parasite in the tissues such as muscles, skin, eyes and the central nervous system (human cysticercosis), with possible devastating effects on health. When cysts develop in the brain, the condition is referred to as neurocysticercosis. Symptoms: Taeniasis is usually characterized by mild and non-specific symptoms associated to an intestinal infection (abdominal pain, nausea, diarrhoea, etc.). These symptoms may continue until the tapeworm dies following treatment, otherwise it may live for years.
translations.hypothesis: While certain parasites can cause mild infections, *T. solium* can cause a severe condition where humans become infected by the parasite (either through ingestion of eggs or by consuming undercooked meat from an infected animal). This parasitic infection can lead to various health problems, including cysts in the brain, muscles, eyes, and other organs, which can have serious consequences. When these cysts develop in the brain, the condition is known as neurocysticercosis. Symptoms: Individuals with neurocysticercosis may experience a wide range of symptoms, including seizures, headaches, blurred vision, and hydrocephalus (fluid buildup in the brain). These symptoms can persist for years, even after the parasite dies.

translations.source: Fún sisikakósì, àsìkò ìsàba rẹ̀ máa ń yàtọ̀, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn tí wọ́n ní àìsàn náà lè máa ní àmì àìsàn kankan fún ọ̀pọ̀ ọdún. Ní àwọn àgbègbè tí àrùn yìí tí wọ́pọ̀, àwọn tí wọ́n ń ṣe àìsàn yìí lè ní àwọn ògòdò tí ó hànde tàbí ńlá (kókó tàbí ògòdò kékeré líle) tí ó wà nísàlẹ̀ awọ. Nirosisikakósì ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn onírúurú àmì àti àpẹẹrẹ tí ó dá lórí iye, ìtóbi, ìpele àti ibi àwọn àyípadà ara àti èsì àjẹsára aláìsàn àti àbùdá ajọ̀fẹ́ náà pẹ̀lú, ṣùgbọ́n ó tún lè má ní àmì àìsàn ìlera kankan. Àwọn àmì àìsàn lè ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn orí fífọ́ líle, ẹ̀fọ́jú, gìrì (wárápá bí wọ́n ba jẹ́ òòrèkóòrè), ọta orí, yínrùnyínrùn, ìgbàgbé àti àwọn àmì àìsàn tí àwọn ògòdò tí wọ́n gba àwọn ààyè ètò àkóso ibùdó ara ń fà. Ìtọ́jú: Ìtọ́jú taeníásí láti ọwọ́ Taenia solium ṣe pàtàkì láti dènà nirosisikakósì àti gẹ́gẹ́ bí irinṣẹ́ láti ṣàkóso tàbí dènà ìtànká ajọ̀fẹ́.
translations.reference: For cysticercosis, the incubation period is variable, and infected people may remain asymptomatic for years. In some endemic regions, infected people may develop visible or palpable nodules (a small solid bump or node which is solid that can be detected by touch) beneath the skin. Neurocysticercosis is associated with a variety of signs and symptoms depending on the number, size, stage and location of the pathological changes as well as the host’s immune response and the parasite’s genotype, but it can also be clinically asymptomatic. Symptoms may include chronic headaches, blindness, seizures (epilepsy if they are recurrent), hydrocephalus, meningitis, dementia and symptoms caused by lesions occupying spaces of the central nervous system. Treatment: The treatment of taeniasis by Taenia solium is important to prevent neurocysticercosis and as a tool to assist in controlling or stopping the parasite transmission cycle.
translations.hypothesis: For individuals with neurocysticercosis, the symptoms can vary, and some may not experience any symptoms for years. In areas where this disease is common, those infected may have cysts in their muscles (often in the thighs or calves) that are visible under the skin. Neurocysticercosis is characterized by a wide range of symptoms and manifestations, depending on the number, location, and stage of the cysts, as well as the inflammatory response they trigger. However, it can also present with no apparent symptoms. Symptoms can include seizures, headaches, confusion, difficulty with balance (ataxia), vomiting, and other neurological problems caused by the cysts pressing on the brain or spinal cord. Treatment: Treatment with antihelminthic drugs from Taenia solium is crucial for managing neurocysticercosis and is often necessary to control or eliminate the infection.

translations.source: Ìtọ́jú lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe lára ẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan, tàbí Àkóso Òògùn gbogbogbò èyí tí ó dá lórí àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ abẹ́lé àti àwọn ìlànà ìṣàkóso tí wọ́n ń ṣàmúlò. Taeníásí lè jẹ́ títọ́jú nípa lílo àwọn òògùn agbógunti-ajọ̀fẹ́ (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). Nínú nirosisikakósì, níwọ̀n ìgbà tí bíba àwọn ògòdò líle jẹ́ lè fa èsì ìrẹ̀dòdò, ìtọ́jú pàtàkì àìsàn tí ó ń ṣọṣẹ́ nílò bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn iṣẹ́ ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn ìlò òògùn agbógunti-ajọ̀fẹ́, àti ìwòsàn alátìlẹ́yìn pẹ̀lú corticosteroids àti/tàbí òògùn agbógunti-wárápá, àti bóyá iṣẹ́ abẹ. Ìlò àti àsìkò ìtọ́jú lè yàtọ̀ dáadáa bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè dá lórí iye, ìwọ̀n, agbègbè, àti ìpele ìdàgbàsókè oyún líle, ìrẹ̀dòdò àyíká ọṣẹ́, ìṣòro àti bí àwọn àmì àìsàn tàbí àmì ṣe lè tó.
translations.reference: The treatment can be done on an individual bases, or as Mass Drug Administration depending on the local circumstances and the control approaches being implemented. Taeniasis can be treated using anthelmintics (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). In neurocysticercosis, since the destruction of cysts may lead to an inflammatory response, specialised treatment of active disease is required and may include long courses with high doses of anthelmintic, as well as supporting therapy with corticosteroids and/or anti-epileptic drugs, and possibly surgery. The dosage and the duration of treatment can vary greatly and depend mainly on the number, size, location and developmental stage of the cysts, their surrounding inflammatory edema, acuteness and severity of clinical symptoms or signs.
translations.hypothesis: Schistosomiasis can affect individuals differently, and the overall management strategy depends on the severity of the disease and the specific symptoms present. Treatment typically involves the use of antiparasitic medications (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). In cases of severe schistosomiasis, where complications such as liver damage are present, more intensive treatment may be necessary. This can include prolonged courses of antiparasitic medication, as well as supportive care with corticosteroids and/or anti-inflammatory drugs, and potentially surgery. The specific treatment regimen, duration, and dosage will vary depending on factors such as the patient's age, weight, geographic location, the severity of the infection, and the presence of any other medical conditions.

translations.source: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì Ìsọnísókí: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì jẹ́ àìsàn líle tí ènìyàn máa ń kó láti ara ìfaragbá ọmọ àwọn bakitéríà, Clostridium tetani, èyí tí ó ń gbé nínú ilẹ̀, itọ́, eruku àti ìgbẹ́. Bakitéríà náà lè wọ inú ara nípasẹ̀ egbò ọgbẹ́ jínjìn, àwọn ìfarapa tàbí àpá iná tí wọ́n ń nípa lórí ètò àkóso ara. Àìsàn náà máa ń yọrí sí àwọn ìsúnkì isan ara, pàápàá jùlọ àgbọ̀n àti isan ọrùn, beet sì ni s mọ̀ ọ́n sí "ìwayínpọ̀". Gbogbo àwọn ènìyàn pátá lè ní tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ṣùgbọ́n àìsàn náà wọ́pọ̀ ó sì lè láàrin àwọn ọmọ tuntun àti àwọn ìyá wọn nígbà tí ìyá náà kò bá ní ààbò láti ara tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì nípasẹ̀ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára, tetanus toxoid.
translations.reference: Tetanus Overview: Tetanus is a serious illness contracted through exposure to the spores of the bacterium, Clostridium tetani, which live in soil, saliva, dust and manure. The bacteria can enter the body through a deep cuts, wounds or burns affecting the nervous system. The infection leads to painful muscle contractions, particularly of the jaw and neck muscle, and is commonly known as “lockjaw”. People of all ages can get tetanus but the disease is particularly common and serious in newborn babies and their mothers when the mother is unprotected from tetanus by the vaccine, tetanus toxoid.
translations.hypothesis: Tetanus: Tetanus is a serious infectious disease caused by the bacterium *Clostridium tetani*. This bacterium lives in soil, dust, and manure. It can enter the body through deep wounds, injections, or burns. The disease causes painful muscle spasms, often starting in the jaw and neck, and can lead to difficulty breathing, potentially resulting in death. While all people are susceptible to tetanus, it is most common in newborns whose mothers have not been vaccinated against tetanus, or in adults who have not received a tetanus booster shot.

translations.source: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì tí ó ń farahàn lásìkò oyún tàbí láàrin ọ̀sẹ̀ 6 ní ìgbẹ̀yìn oyún náà ni a ń pè ní tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìyálọ́mọ, nígbà tí tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì tí ó farahàn láàrin ọjọ́ 28 lẹ́yìn ìbí ni tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìkókó. Àìsàn náà jẹ́ ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ pàtàkì ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ẹ̀ka àgbáyé, ṣùgbọ́n paapapat jùlọ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín tàbí àdúgbò, níbi tí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára ti kéré tí àwọn àṣà ìbímọ aláìní ìmọ́tótó pọ̀ sí. Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé ṣírò pé ní 2018 (ọdún lọ́ọ́lọ́ọ́ tí àwọn ìṣirò rẹ̀ wà nílẹ̀), àwọn ìkókó 25 000 kú láti ara tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìkókó, àdínkù 88% sí ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ọdún 2000. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Ó fẹ́rẹ̀ẹ́ jẹ́ pé gbogbo ọ̀ràn tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ń ṣẹlẹ̀ láàrin àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n kò tíì gba abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára tàbí àwọn àgbàlagbà tí wọn kò tíì ṣe ìmúdójú-ìwọ̀n àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àgbàkún wọn. Àwọn àpẹẹrẹ àti àmì àìsàn tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì máa farahàn lásìkò kí àsìkò láti ọjọ́ 3 sí 21 lẹ́yìn tí bakitéríà tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì bá ti wọ inú ara nípasẹ̀ egbò kan.
translations.reference: Tetanus occurring during pregnancy or within 6 weeks of the end of pregnancy is called maternal tetanus, while tetanus occurring within the first 28 days of life is called neonatal tetanus. The disease remains an important public health problem in many parts of the world, but especially in low-income countries or districts, where immunization coverage is low and unclean birth practices are common. WHO estimates that in 2018 (the latest year for which estimates are available), 25 000 newborns died from neonatal tetanus, 88% reduction from the situation in 2000. Symptoms: Nearly all cases of tetanus occur in people who have never been vaccinated or in adults who have not kept up to date on their booster shots. Signs and symptoms of tetanus appear anytime from 3 to 21 days after tetanus bacteria enter the body through a wound.
translations.hypothesis: The period during which a person is considered contagious with tuberculosis is typically within the first 6 months of infection, while latent tuberculosis is when the infection is present but not actively causing symptoms. This disease is a major public health problem in many countries, particularly in developing nations, where access to healthcare is limited and traditional practices may increase the risk of transmission. According to the World Health Organization, in 2018 (the most recent year for which data is available), 25,000 people died from tuberculosis, an increase of 88% since the year 2000. Symptoms: It is important to note that not all people who are infected with tuberculosis will develop symptoms. However, when symptoms do occur, they are most common in people who have not been vaccinated against tuberculosis or in older adults who have weakened immune systems. Examples of tuberculosis symptoms include: The symptoms of tuberculosis typically appear within 3 to 21 days after the bacteria have entered the body through the lungs.

translations.source: Ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ọ̀ràn máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ láàrin ọjọ́ 14. Àmì àìsàn lè jẹ́: ìwayínpọ tàbí àìlágbára láti yanu ẹ̀gbọ̀n àwọn iṣu ara pàápàá jùlọ lẹ́yìn, ní inú àti àwọn òpin ara. ìnira àwọn ẹ̀gbọ̀n ìṣù ara lójijì èyí tí ariwo òjìji máa ń fà
translations.reference: Most cases occur within 14 days. Symptoms can include: jaw cramping or the inability to open the mouth muscle spasms often in the back, abdomen and extremities sudden painful muscle spasms often triggered by sudden noises
translations.hypothesis: Many problems can occur within 14 days. Symptoms may include: swelling or numbness in the limbs, particularly in the hands and feet, and tingling sensations. These symptoms can also be accompanied by pain.

translations.source: ìṣòro gbígbé nǹkan mi gìrì ẹ̀fọ́rí ibà àti òógùn lílà àyípadà nínú ìru ẹ̀jẹ̀ tàbí ìwọ̀n ọkàn tí ó ń sáré.
translations.reference: trouble swallowing seizures headache fever and sweating changes in blood pressure or fast heart rate.
translations.hypothesis: The household item I'm referring to is a humidifier and air purifier, designed to improve the air quality in a room or living space.

translations.source: Nínú tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìkókó, lára àwọn àmì aisant ni ẹ̀gbọ̀n ìṣù ara, èyí tí ó sáábà máa ń wá lẹ́yìn àìlágbára ìkókó láti fa ọyàn tàbí mu ọyàn, àti ẹkún sísun sá. Wọ́n maat ń ṣàyẹ̀wò tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì nípasẹ̀ àwọn àbùdá àìsàn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni kò nílò ìjẹ́rìí láàbù. Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì pè fún ìtọ́jú nínú ibùdó ìtọ́jú, lọ́pọ̀ ìgbà ní ilé ìwòsàn ìfiṣọwọ́sí. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n sàn láti ara tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì kò ní àjẹsára àìṣẹ̀dá bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n lè kó àìsàn náà padà nítorí náà wọn nílò láti gba abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára. Ìdènà: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára pẹ̀lú àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára tí wọ́n ní tetanus-toxoid (TTCV), èyí tí wọ́n kò pọ̀ sínú àwọn ètò àjẹsára káàkiri àgbáyé tí wọ́n sì máa ń ṣàmúlò lásìkò ìfaragbá ìtọ́jú aṣáájú ìbímọ.
translations.reference: In neonatal tetanus, symptoms include muscle spasms, which are often preceded by the newborn’s inability to suck or breastfeed, and excessive crying. Tetanus is diagnosed on the basis of clinical features and does not require laboratory confirmation. Tetanus requires treatment in a medical facility, often in a referral hospital. People who recover from tetanus do not have natural immunity and can be infected again and therefore need to be immunized. Prevention: Tetanus can be prevented through immunization with tetanus-toxoid-containing vaccines (TTCV), which are included in routine immunization programmes globally and administered during antenatal care contacts.
translations.hypothesis: In the field of medicine, one of the most important aspects is the prevention of diseases. This includes preventing infections, such as tetanus, which can cause serious complications and even death. Medical professionals use a variety of methods to prevent diseases, including vaccination. Vaccination is a process that introduces a weakened or inactive form of a disease-causing agent into the body. This helps the body to develop immunity to the disease, so that if the person is ever exposed to the disease, their body will be able to fight it off. People who are vaccinated are less likely to get sick, and if they do get sick, they are less likely to experience serious complications. Prevention: Tetanus can be prevented through vaccination with tetanus-containing vaccines (TTCVs), which are included in many global immunization programs and are typically administered during childhood.

translations.source: Láti rí ààbò títí ayé, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé dábàá pé ẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan gbọ́dọ̀ gba abẹ́rẹ́ 6 (ìlò 3 ìpìlẹ̀ 3 àgbàkún) TTCV. Àwọn abala ìwọ̀n lílo ìpìlẹ̀ 3 náà gbọ́dọ̀ bẹ̀rẹ̀ láti ọjọ́ orí ọ̀sẹ̀ 6,pẹ̀lú àwọn ìwọ̀n lílo yòókù tí wọ́n gbọ́dọ̀ fún ọmọ láàrin àlàfo gbèdéke ọ̀sẹ̀ 4 ó kéré jù láàrin àwọn ìwọ̀n lílo. Àwọn àgbàkún 3 náà gbọ́dọ̀ di fífún lásìkò ọdún kejì nílé ayé (oṣù 12-23), ní ọjọ́ orí ọdún 4-7, àti ọjọ́ orí 9-15. Ó yẹ kí àlàfo ọdún 4 ó kéré jù wà láàrin àwọn àgbàkún ìwọ̀n lílò. Àwọn onírúurú abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára wà tí wọ́n ń lò láti dáàbò bo tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì: abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára diphtheria àti tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì, tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì, àti àwúbì (ikọ́ àwúbì) (DTap) àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára vaccinetatanus àti diphtheria (Td) vacccinetatanus, diphtheria, àti àwúbì (Tdap).
translations.reference: To be protected throughout life, WHO recommends that an individual receives 6 doses (3 primary plus 3 booster doses) of TTCV. The 3-dose primary series should begin as early as 6 weeks of age, with subsequent doses given with a minimum interval of 4 weeks between doses. The 3 booster doses should preferably be given during the second year of life (12–23 months), at 4–7 years of age, and at 9–15 years of age. Ideally, there should be at least 4 years between booster doses. There are many kinds of vaccines used to protect against tetanus: diphtheria and tetanus (DT) vaccinesdiphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis (whooping cough) (DTaP) vaccinestetanus and diphtheria (Td) vaccinestetanus, diphtheria, and pertussis (Tdap) vaccines.
translations.hypothesis: Since the introduction of vaccines, the World Health Organization recommends a total of 6 (3 primary and 3 booster) doses of the DTP vaccine. The primary series should begin at 6 weeks of age, with subsequent doses given at least 4 weeks apart. The booster doses are typically given at 12-23 months of age, 4-7 years of age, and 9-15 years of age. A minimum interval of 4 weeks should be maintained between booster doses. Various combination vaccines are used to prevent these diseases: the diphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis (DTaP) vaccine, the tetanus and diphtheria (Td) vaccine, and the tetanus, diphtheria, and pertussis (Tdap) vaccine.

translations.source: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìkókó lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ fífún àwọn obìnrin tí wọ́n ti tó ọmọ bí ní abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára pẹ̀lú TTCV, yálà lásìkò ìlóyún tàbí nígbà tí wọn kò lóyún. Ní àfikún, àwọn àṣà ìṣègùn gbígbòòrò náà lè dènà àìsàn tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì tí ó fi mọ́ ìgbẹ̀bí tí ó ní ìmọ́tótó àti ìtọ́jú ìwọ́ lásìkò ìbímọ, àti ìtọ́jú egbò pípé fún àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ iṣẹ́ abẹ àti ìtọ́jú eyín.
translations.reference: Neonatal tetanus can be prevented by immunizing women of reproductive age with TTCV, either during pregnancy or outside of pregnancy. Additionally, robust medical practices can also prevent tetanus disease including clean delivery and cord care during childbirth, and proper wound care for surgical and dental procedures.
translations.hypothesis: Menopause can be managed by providing women who have reached the menopause with hormone replacement therapy (HRT), either continuously or cyclically. Furthermore, traditional medicine can help manage menopausal symptoms by promoting overall well-being, improving sleep quality, and reducing anxiety and stress.

translations.source: Ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà Ìsọnísókí: Ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà (TBE) jẹ́ okùnfà àwọn kòkòrò àrùn fáírọ́ọ̀sì líle tí ibùdó ètò ara ní ẹ̀ka ìlà oòrùn, àárín gbùngbùn, àríwá àti pàápàá jùlọ ìwọ̀ oòrùn àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè Yúróòpù, àti ní àríwá China, Mongolia, àti Orílẹ̀ èdè Russia. Ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà jẹ́ mọ̀lẹ́bí Flaviviridae. Ó sún mọ́ 10 000–12 000 àwọn ọ̀ràn ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà tí wọ́n jábọ̀ ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan ṣùgbọ́n iye yìí ni wọ́n ní ìgbàgbọ́ pé ó kéré púpọ̀ sí gbogbo iye àwọn ọ̀ràn àìsàn náà gan-an. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn àrùn láti ara kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì náà ń wáyé láti ara àwọn eégbọn tí wọ́n ti lùgbàdì àìsàn náà, tí wọ́n maat ń lẹ̀ mọ́ ara fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọjọ́.
translations.reference: Tick-borne encephalitis Overview: Tick-borne encephalitis (TBE) is an important cause of viral infections of the central nervous system in eastern, central, northern and increasingly western European countries, and in northern China, Mongolia, and the Russian Federation. Tick-borne encephalitis virus is a member of the family Flaviviridae. Approximately 10 000–12 000 clinical cases of tick-borne encephalitis are reported each year, but this figure is believed to be significantly lower than the actual total number of clinical cases. The vast majority of infections with the virus result from infected ticks, which often remain firmly attached to the skin for days.
translations.hypothesis: Tick-borne encephalitis: Tick-borne encephalitis (TBE) is a viral disease that affects the central nervous system and is found in forested areas of Central, Eastern, and Northern Europe, as well as in northern Asia, including parts of China, Mongolia, and Russia. TBE is caused by a virus belonging to the Flaviviridae family. It is estimated that there are 10,000 to 12,000 cases of TBE reported each year, but this number is likely an underestimate of the total number of infections. Many cases of TBE are mild and go undiagnosed, and some people who are infected may not develop any symptoms at all.

translations.source: Ní ìgbà mìíràn, àrùn lè wáyé láti ara jíjẹ wàrà tí wọn kò sè èyí tí wọ́n gbà láti ara àwọn ewúrẹ́, àgùntàn tàbí màlúù tí wọ́n ti lùgbàdì àìsàn náà. Àwọn ènìyàn máa ń farakó àwọn eégbọn lásìkò àwọn iṣẹ́ ìta gbangba ní àwọn agbègbè igbó títí dé ibi gíga bí mítà 2000. Kò sí ìkóràn ènìyàn-sí-ènìyàn tààrà. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn àrùn máa ń wà láìsí àmì àìsàn. Nínú ọ̀ràn àìsàn alábùdá, àsìkò ìsàba ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà máa ń tó ọjọ́ 2-28 (ní ọ̀pọ̀ ìgbà ọjọ́ 7-14) bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ọjọ́ 1-8 àwọn àmì àìsàn òtútù yóò tẹ̀lé, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀rẹ̀rẹ̀, ẹ̀fọ́rí àti ara wíwo, tí ó sáábà maat ń ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú ibà ≥ 38°C.
translations.reference: On rare occasions, infection can result from consumption of unpasteurized milk from infect goats, sheep or cows. People come in contact with the ticks during outdoor activities in forested areas up to an altitude of about 2000 meters. There is no direct person-to-person transmission. Symptoms: Most infections remain asymptomatic. In case of clinical illness, the incubation period for tick-borne encephalitis lasts 2–28 days (most commonly 7–14 days) and is followed by 1–8 days of general cold symptoms, such as fatigue, headache and general malaise, usually combined with fever of ≥38 °C.
translations.hypothesis: Sometimes, illnesses can arise from contaminated water sources, which may be exposed to bacteria, viruses, or parasites from human or animal waste. People may consume this contaminated water during outdoor activities in forested areas, even at high altitudes up to 2000 meters. There is no direct person-to-person transmission. Symptoms: Many illnesses can occur without any noticeable symptoms. In the typical disease course, the incubation period (the time between exposure and the onset of symptoms) can range from 2 to 28 days (often 7-14 days), followed by a period of 1 to 8 days of acute symptoms, such as fever, chills, and body aches, which may be accompanied by a temperature of 38°C or higher.

translations.source: Lẹ́yìn àìsí àmì àìsàn láàrin àwọn ọjọ́ 1-20 bí 15% àwọn aláàárẹ̀ ń ní ìrírí ìpele kejì àìsàn náà tí ó ń ní àbùdá ibà tí ó ju 40° C àti àwọn àpẹẹrẹ ìdásí ètò ibùdó ara, bí yínrùnyínrùn (b.a. ibà, ẹ̀fọ́rí, àti ọrùn wíwàkì), ọpọlọ wíwú (b. a. òòyì ojú, ìrújú, àti ìdíwọ́ ọpọlọ), ìrora ọ̀pá ẹ̀yìn tàbí ìrora olóbìrípo. Ọpọlọ wíwú tí ń dàgbà lásìkò ìpele kejì lè fa rọpárọsẹ̀, àpá ayérayé tàbí ikú. Bí 1% àwọn ọ̀ràn pẹ̀lú àìsàn ajẹmọ́ ètò ara lè kú; wọn tí ròyìn ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìwọ̀n ikú láti ilẹ̀ Russia, eyit tí ó lè ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú oríṣìí ẹ̀yà fáírọ́ọ̀sì mìíràn. Ọwọ́ líle àìsàn náà máa ń mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá ọjọ́ orí aláàárẹ̀, ṣùgbọ́n wọ́n ti ròyìn àwọn ikú káàkiri gbogbo àwọn ọ̀wọ́ ọjọ́ orí. Kò sí ìtọ́jú pàtàkì fún ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà.
translations.reference: After an asymptomatic interval of 1–20 days up to 15% of patients experience a second phase of the disease characterized by fever frequently exceeding 40 °C and signs of central nervous system involvement, such as meningitis (e.g. fever, headache, and a stiff neck), encephalitis (e.g., drowsiness, confusion, and sensory disturbances), myelitis or radiculitis. Encephalitis developing during this second phase may result in paralysis, permanent sequelae or death. About 1% of cases with neurological pathologies may die; higher fatality rates have been reported from the Russian federation, which may be related to a different virus subtype. Severity of illness increases with age of the patient, but fatalities have been reported from all age groups. There is no specific treatment for tick-borne encephalitis.
translations.hypothesis: Following a period of 1-20 days after infection, approximately 15% of individuals develop a second stage of the illness characterized by a fever exceeding 40°C and the emergence of systemic complications, such as rash (e.g., fever, exanthema, and maculopapular rash), conjunctivitis (e.g., redness, swelling, and discharge of the eyes), and bleeding disorders. In some cases, the second stage can lead to serious complications, including organ failure or death. Approximately 1% of individuals with severe systemic illness may die; reports have emerged from Russia of a higher mortality rate, potentially linked to other underlying health conditions. The severity of the illness tends to increase with the duration of the initial phase, although deaths have been reported across all stages. There is no known specific treatment for the rash associated with this illness.

translations.source: Ìdáàbòbò: Àwọn ènìyàn lè dáàbò bo ara wọn láti ọwọ́ eégbọn nípa wíwọ aṣọ tí ó tọ́, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ṣòkòtò gígùn àti bàtà amusẹ̀tán, nígbà tí wọ́n bá ń ṣeré tàbí pàgọ́ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tàbí agbègbè tí wọn léwu. Gbogbo ara gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ mímójútó lójoojúmọ́ kí àwọn eégbọn sì jẹ́ mímú kúrò lójú ọjọ́. Jíjẹ àwọn ẹ̀yà ara ẹran tí wọn kò sè gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ yíyàgò fún ní àwọn àgbègbè náà. Àjẹsára ń pèsè ìdáàbòbò tí ó múnádóko jùlọ. Lọ́ọ́lọ́ọ́ báyìí, àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára tí wọ́n gbajúmọ̀ 4 tí wọ́n péye ló wà:
translations.reference: Prevention: People can protect themselves from ticks by wearing appropriate clothing, including long trousers and closed footwear, when hiking or camping in countries or areas at risk. The whole body should be inspected daily and attached ticks removed as soon as possible. The consumption of unpasteurized dairy products should also be avoided in those areas. Immunization offers the most effective protection. Currently, there are 4 widely used vaccines of assured quality:
translations.hypothesis:
Fashion: People often adapt their clothing styles to suit the weather, choosing lightweight fabrics and comfortable shoes when playing sports or traveling in hot countries or regions. It's important to dress appropriately for the weather conditions, and to avoid wearing overly revealing clothing. Certain cultural norms may also dictate appropriate attire. Here are some tips for dressing appropriately in hot weather:

* Choose lightweight, breathable fabrics like cotton or linen.
* Wear light-colored clothing to reflect the sun's rays.
* Opt for comfortable shoes that won't make your feet sweat.
* Avoid wearing overly revealing clothing.
* Be mindful of cultural norms.

Here are four popular summer fashion trends:

translations.source: FSME-Imum àti Encepur, tí wọ́n ṣe ní Australia àti Germany, tí wọ́n sì dá lórí ẹ̀yà fáírọ́ọ̀sì náà ti ilẹ̀ Yúróòpù; àti TBE- Moscow àti EnceVir, tí wọ́n ṣe ní ilẹ̀ Russia tí ó sì dá lórí àwọn ẹ̀yà jìnnà réré Ìlà oòrùn. Àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára 4 ni wọ́n gbà pé wọ́n ní ààbò tí wọn sì múnádóko. Ní àwọn àgbègbè tí àwọn àìsàn náà tí wọ́pọ̀, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé dábàá pé kí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára wà fún gbogbo ènìyàn, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ọmọdé. Eégbọn tún ń tan Borreliosis (àìsàn Lyme), tí ó jẹ́ àrùn bakitéríà.
translations.reference: FSME-Immun and Encepur, manufactured in Austria and Germany respectively, and based on European strains of the virus; and TBE-Moscow and EnceVir, manufactured in the Russian Federation and based on Far-Eastern strains. The 4 vaccines are considered to be safe and effective. In areas where the disease is highly endemic, WHO recommends that vaccination be offered to all age groups, including children. Ticks also transmit Borreliosis (Lyme disease), which is a bacterial infection.
translations.hypothesis: FSME-Imum and Encepur, manufactured in Australia and Germany, are based on European tick-borne encephalitis strains. TBE-Moscow and EnceVir, produced in Russia, are designed to protect against different strains found in Eastern Europe. Four vaccines are currently available and are considered safe and effective. In areas where these diseases are prevalent, the World Health Organization recommends vaccination for all individuals, including children. Another disease transmitted by ticks is Borreliosis, also known as Lyme disease, which is a bacterial infection.

translations.source: Abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára TBE kò kájú gbígbógun ti àìsàn yìí, èyí tí ó ṣe é wò pẹ̀lú àwọn òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò.
translations.reference: TBE vaccination is not effective against this disease, which however is treatable with antimicrobials.
translations.hypothesis: The TBE (transient bacterial overgrowth) test does not accurately detect this condition, especially when it is accompanied by the use of antibiotics.

translations.source: Ewé tábà Ìsọnísókí: Nikotíìnì tí ó wà nínú ewé tábà máa ń fà ìkúndùn púpọ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìlò ewé tábà jẹ́ ewu ńlá pàtàkì fún àwọn àìsàn ajẹmọ́ ọkàn àti òpónà ìmí, ó lé ní 20 onírúurú ẹ̀yà àti oríṣìí ẹ̀yà jẹjẹrẹ, àti àwọn ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìṣòro ìlera tí wọ́n gbèrò mìíràn. Ní ọdọọdún, ó lé ní mílíọ̀nù 8 ènìyàn ń kú láti ara lílo ewé tábà. Ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ikú tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìlò ewé tábà ní wọ́n ń wáyé ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè, tí wọ́n jẹ́ àfojúsùn ìkọlù ipa àti ọjà àwọn ilé iṣẹ́ tábà. Tábà tún lè léwu fún àwọn tí wọn kì í fa sìgá.
translations.reference: Tobacco Overview: Nicotine contained in tobacco is highly addictive and tobacco use is a major risk factor for cardiovascular and respiratory diseases, over 20 different types or subtypes of cancer, and many other debilitating health conditions. Every year, more than 8 million people die from tobacco use. Most tobacco-related deaths occur in low- and middle-income countries, which are often targets of intensive tobacco industry interference and marketing. Tobacco can also be deadly for non-smokers.
translations.hypothesis: Tobacco Use: Nicotine, the substance in tobacco, is highly addictive, and tobacco use is a major risk factor for various diseases, including cancer and heart disease. It comes in many forms and varieties, and is associated with numerous health problems. Every year, millions of people die from tobacco-related illnesses. Many of these deaths occur in low- and middle-income countries, where tobacco control policies and regulations are often weaker. Tobacco can also be harmful to those who do not smoke, through secondhand smoke exposure.

translations.source: Ìsíjúsí èéfín àmukù náà tún ti fa ọ̀ràn ní àwọn àbájáde ìlera, tí ó ń fa mílíọ̀nù 1.2 àwọn ikú lọ́dọọdún. Ó tó ìdajì gbogbo àwọn ọmọdé ni wọ́n ń mí àmísínú afẹ́fẹ́ tí èéfín tábà ti bàjẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ọmọdé 65 000 ń kú lọ́dọọdún nítorí àwọn àìsàn tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú èèfín àmukù. Fífa sìgá lásìkò ìlóyún lè yọrí sí àwọn ìṣòro ìlera ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ fún àwọn ìkókó. Àwọn èròjà tábà gbígbóná (HTPs) ní tábà bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n máa ń síjú àwọn àwọn aṣàmúlò sí ìbújáde tí ó léwu, púpọ̀ àwọn èyí tí wọ́n ń fa jẹjẹrẹ tí wọ́n sì léwu fún ìlera. Àwọn ètò àgbékalẹ̀ nikotíìnì oníná (ENDS) àti àwọn ètò àgbékalẹ̀ tí kì í nikotíìnì oníná (ENNDS), tí àwọn ènìyàn mọ̀ sí sìgá oníná, kò ní tábà bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè ní tàbí kí ó má ní nikotíìnì, ṣùgbọ́n wọ́n léwu fún ìlera bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó dájú pé wọn kò ní ààbò.
translations.reference: Second-hand smoke exposure has also been implicated in adverse health outcomes, causing 1.2 million deaths annually. Nearly half of all children breathe air polluted by tobacco smoke and 65 000 children die each year due to illnesses related to second-hand smoke. Smoking while pregnant can lead to several life-long health conditions for babies. Heated tobacco products (HTPs) contain tobacco and expose users to toxic emissions, many of which cause cancer and are harmful to health. Electronic nicotine delivery systems (ENDS) and electronic non-nicotine delivery systems (ENNDS), commonly known as e-cigarettes, do not contain tobacco and may or may not contain nicotine, but are harmful to health and undoubtedly unsafe.
translations.hypothesis: The use of tobacco products continues to be a major public health problem, causing an estimated 1.2 million deaths each year. Tobacco smoke is particularly harmful to children, with 65,000 children dying each year from illnesses caused by secondhand smoke. Quitting smoking can significantly reduce the risk of long-term health problems. Heated tobacco products (HTPs), which heat tobacco instead of burning it, are also harmful and pose significant health risks. Electronic nicotine delivery systems (ENDS) and electronic non-nicotine delivery systems (ENNDS), commonly known as e-cigarettes or vapes, may or may not contain nicotine, but they are also harmful to health and should be avoided.

translations.source: Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé, ó ti yá jù láti pèsè ìdáhùn pípé lórí ipa ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ ìlò HTPs àti/tàbí àwọn sìgá oníná. Ipa: Ìṣirò ènìyàn bílíọ́nù 1.3 káàkiri àgbáyé ń ṣàmúlò àwọn èròjà tábà, 80% àwọn tí wọ́n wà ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè. Ìlò tábà ń nípa lórí òsì nípa dídarí owónàá ilé kúrò lára àwọn ìlò abẹ́lé, bí oúnjẹ àti ilégbèé, sórí tábà. Ìwà ìnáwó yìí ṣòro láti yanjú nítorí pé tábà ní àbùdá àfẹ́sódì. Ó tún máa ń fa ikú àìtọ́jọ́ àti ààbọ̀ ara fún àwọn àgbàlagbà tí wọn wà ní ọjọ́ orí iṣẹ́ nínú ilé èyí tí ó ń ṣokùnfà àdínkù èrè àti iyeó ìtọ́jú ìlera.
translations.reference: However, it is too early to provide a clear answer on the long-term impact of HTPs and/or e-cigarette use. Impact: An estimated 1.3 billion people worldwide use tobacco products, 80% of whom are in low- and middle-income countries. Tobacco use contributes to poverty by diverting household spending from basic needs, such as food and shelter, to tobacco. This spending behaviour is difficult to curb because tobacco is so addictive. It also causes premature death and disability of productive age adults in households thus leading to reduced household income and increased healthcare costs.
translations.hypothesis: While it is difficult to provide a precise figure on the impact of prolonged use of HTPs (heated tobacco products) and/or e-cigarettes, the impact is significant: An estimated 1.3 billion people worldwide use tobacco products, with 80% of them residing in low- and middle-income countries. The use of tobacco has a detrimental impact on health, leading to diseases such as cancer, heart disease, and respiratory problems. This issue is further complicated by the addictive nature of nicotine. Furthermore, exposure to secondhand smoke and vapor can also cause health problems for those nearby, particularly for vulnerable populations such as children and the elderly, leading to increased healthcare costs and reduced quality of life.

translations.source: Ní àfikún sí ìpa ìparun tábà lórí ìlera, àpapọ̀ àléébù sìgá mímu lórí ọrọ̀ ajé (láti ara àwọn ìnáwó ìlera àti àwọn àdánù lápapọ̀) ní wọ́n ṣírò pé ó tó bí tírílíọ́nù US$ 1.4 lọ́dún, èyí tí ó jẹ́ àpapọ̀ 1.8% iye ọjà abẹ́lé gbogbo àgbáyé lọ́dọọdún (GDP). Ó tó ìdá 40% gbogbo àwọn iyeó yìí ni wọ́n wáyé ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n ń gòkè àgbà, èyí tí ó ń tọ́ka sí àwọn àjàgà pàtàkì tí àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè wọ̀nyí ń kojú. Wọ́n máa ń ṣàmúlò àwọn owó orí tábà lápá kan láti gbógun ti ipa yìí bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n jẹ́ ọ̀nà tí ó dára jùlọ láti dáwọ́ ìlò tábà dúró, pàápàá jùlọ láàrin àwọn ọ̀dọ́ àti àwọn ènìyàn ní àwùjọ elérè pínnísín. Àlékún owó orí tí ó ń mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá àwọn iyeó tábà pẹ̀lú àdínkù 10% ìlò tábà láti ọwọ́ bí 4% ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi àti 5% ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè. Èsì Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: Ìwọ̀n àjálù ènìyàn àti ọrọ̀ ajé yìí yanilẹ́nu, ṣùgbọ́n ó tún ṣe é dènà.
translations.reference: In addition to the detrimental impact of tobacco on health, the total economic cost of smoking (from health expenditures and productivity losses together) are estimated to be around US$ 1.4 trillion per year, equivalent in magnitude to 1.8% of the world's annual gross domestic product (GDP). Almost 40% of this cost occurred in developing countries, highlighting the substantial burden these countries suffer. Tobacco taxes are applied in part to combat this effect and are seen as the most cost-effective way of curbing tobacco use, particularly among youth and low-income populations. A tax increase that increases tobacco prices by 10% decreases tobacco consumption by about 4% in high-income countries and about 5% in low- and middle-income countries. WHO response: The scale of this human and economic tragedy is shocking, but it’s also preventable.
translations.hypothesis: In addition to the devastating impact on health, the economic burden of tobacco (including healthcare costs and lost productivity) is estimated to be over US$1.4 trillion annually, representing 1.8% of the global gross domestic product (GDP).  Approximately 40% of these costs are borne by countries with aging populations, highlighting the significant economic strain these nations face.  Raising tobacco taxes is a proven strategy to combat this burden and is the most effective way to reduce tobacco consumption, particularly among young people and low-income populations.  Tax increases that raise tobacco prices by 10% have been shown to reduce tobacco consumption by 4% in high-income countries and 5% in low- and middle-income countries. The World Health Organization: The human and economic costs of tobacco are staggering, but they are preventable.

translations.source: Big Tobacco – pẹ̀lú gbogbo àwọn ilé iṣẹ́ olùpèsè ọjà tábà – ń gbìyànjú láti rí i pé àwọn ewu tí ó wà nínú ọjà wọn farasin, ṣùgbọ́n wọ́n ń gbógun ti èyí padà: Ní 2003, àwọn ìlú tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé fẹnukò láti ṣàmúlò Àpérò Ìhun Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé lórí Àkóso Tábà (WHO FCTC), àdéhùn ìlera àwùjọ kan ṣoṣo lábẹ́ ìdarí Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé). Nínú àṣẹ láti 2005, ó ní Ẹgbẹ́ 181 báyìí. Láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti ṣe àmúlò WHO FCTC, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ MPOWER L, ètò àwọn òdiwọ̀n àti orísun ìmọ̀, ọ̀kọ̀ọ̀kan èyí tí ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ó kéré jù ìpèsè WHO FCTC kan. MPOWER kọ́ ipa àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti ṣàmúlò àwọn òdiwọ̀n 6 láti ṣe àdínkù ìbéèrè fún àwọn ọjà tábà: ṣe àkóso ìlò tábà àti ìdènà àwọn ìlànà, dáàbò bo àwọn ènìyàn lọ́wọ́ ìlò tábà, pèsè ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti dáwọ́ ìlò tábà dúró, ṣe ìkìlọ̀ nípa àwọn ewu tábà, fi òfin de ìpolówó, ìgbéga àti ṣíṣe onígbọ̀wọ́ tábà àti ṣíṣe àlékún owó orí tábà.
translations.reference: Big Tobacco — along with all manufacturers of tobacco products — is fighting to ensure the dangers of their products are concealed, but we are fighting back: In 2003, WHO Member States unanimously adopted the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (WHO FCTC), the only public health treaty under the auspices of WHO. In force since 2005, it has currently 181 Parties. To help countries implement the WHO FCTC, WHO introduced MPOWER, a package of technical measures and resources, each of which corresponds to at least one provision of the WHO FCTC. MPOWER builds the capacity of countries to implement 6 measures to reduce the demand for tobacco products: monitor tobacco use and prevention policies, protect people from tobacco use, offer help to quit tobacco use, warn about the dangers of tobacco, enforce bans on tobacco advertising, promotion and sponsorship and raise taxes on tobacco.
translations.hypothesis: Big Tobacco – referring to all the companies involved in the tobacco industry – often tries to downplay the risks associated with their products, but there are counter-efforts: In 2003, countries that were members of the World Health Organization (WHO) agreed to the Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (FCTC), a legally binding international treaty under the auspices of the WHO. The treaty, which came into force in 2005, now has 181 parties. To help countries implement the WHO FCTC, the World Health Organization launched MPOWER, a set of six evidence-based strategies, each corresponding to a key element of the FCTC. MPOWER outlines the roles countries can play in implementing six measures to reduce tobacco demand: track tobacco use and prevalence, raise taxes on tobacco products, ban tobacco advertising, help people quit, enforce bans on smoking in public places, and warn people about the dangers of tobacco.

translations.source: Tirakómà Ìsọnísókí: Tirakómà jẹ́ àìsàn ojú arit asíwájú àìsàn tí ó ń fa ìfọ́jú káàkiri àgbáyé. Bakitéríà inú hóró ara kan tí orúkọ rẹ̀ ń jẹ́ Chlamydia trachomatis ni ó ń fà á. Àrùn náà máa ń ràn láti ara ènìyàn-sí-ènìyàn nípa ìkóràn tààrà tàbí àlùmọ̀kọ́rọ́yí ipin ojú tàbí ikun imú ẹni tí ó ti faragbá; lára ìkóràn àlùmọ̀kọ́rọ́yí ni ìgbésára àwọn ẹ̀yà esinsin kan. Àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n wà ní ọjọ́ orí jéléósinmi ní àká ńlá àrùn náà.
translations.reference: Trachoma Overview: Trachoma is a disease of the eye and the leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide. It is caused by an obligate intracellular bacterium called Chlamydia trachomatis. Infection is transmitted from person to person by direct or indirect transfer of ocular and nasal discharges of infected people; indirect transfer includes carriage on the body of species of flies. Preschool-age children harbour the principal reservoir of infection.
translations.hypothesis: Chlamydia: Chlamydia is a common sexually transmitted infection (STI) that affects people worldwide. It is caused by a bacterium called Chlamydia trachomatis. The infection is typically spread through sexual contact, including vaginal, anal, and oral sex. In some cases, it can also be spread from a mother to her baby during childbirth. Symptoms of chlamydia can vary, but many people experience no symptoms at all. However, when symptoms do occur, they can include abnormal vaginal discharge, pain during urination, and pelvic pain in women, and discharge from the penis in men. Teenagers are particularly vulnerable to this infection.

translations.source: Àwọn àwòṣe fi hàn pé ènìyàn yóò nílò àrùn tí ó lé ní 150 ní ayé kan kí ó tó ní àwọn ìṣòro ìfọ́jú tirakómà. Tirakómà jẹ́ àìsàn tí ó wọ́pọ̀ ní àárín àwọn ènìyàn kan tí wọ́n tòṣì jùlọ lágbàáyé, tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn agbègbè abúlé àti ìgbèríko tí wọn kò sì ní àǹfààní tí ó tó sí omi, ìmọ́tótó àti ìtọ́jú ìlera. Áfríkà ni ẹ̀yà tí ó faragbá jùlọ, ṣùgbọ́n òun náà ni ó ní àwọn akitiyan àkóso tí ó fọ́n ká jùlọ. Àìsàn náà tún wà ní Àárín gbùngbùn àti Gúúsù Amẹ́ríkà, Ésíà, Australia, àti Àárín gbùngbùn Ìlà oòrùn. Ní 2 Oṣù Kìíní 2020, orílẹ̀ èdè 13 jábọ̀ àṣeyọrí àwọn àfojúsùn ìfòpinsí.
translations.reference: Models suggest that an individual requires more than 150 lifetime infections to develop the blinding complications of trachoma. Trachoma is endemic in some of the world’s poorest populations, who live in rural and remote areas and have highly inadequate access to water, sanitation and healthcare. Africa is the most affected continent, but it also has the most widespread control efforts. The disease is also found in Central and South America, Asia, Australia, and the Middle East. As of 2 January 2020, 13 countries had reported achieving elimination goals.
translations.hypothesis: Studies suggest that people will need approximately 150 liters of water per day to cope with the effects of trachoma. Trachoma is a bacterial infection that is a leading cause of preventable blindness worldwide, particularly affecting people in developing countries with poor sanitation, hygiene, and access to clean water. Africa is the most affected continent, but the disease is also prevalent in Central and South America, Asia, Australia, and the Middle East. On September 2, 2020, 13 countries reported achieving success in trachoma control efforts.

translations.source: Ní 2019 nìkan, àwọn ènìyàn 92, 622 gba itojut iṣẹ́ abẹ fún tirakómà líle bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni mílíọ̀nù 95.2 ènìyàn gba ìtọ́jú pẹ̀lú òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò. Pẹ̀lú gbogbo àṣeyọrí wọ̀nyí, àìsàn náà sì jẹ́ ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ tí ó lè, pẹ̀lú ìṣirò àdánù bílíọ́nù US$8 bílíọ́nù lọ́dún nínú ìpèsè nítorí ìfọ́jú àti àléébù ojú. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Àrùn náà ni àṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn àyípadà ìrẹ̀dòdò nínú àwo inú ojú tí a mọ̀ sí "tirakómà tí ń ṣiṣẹ́". Ètò àjẹsára ẹ̀dá ènìyàn kan lè ṣe àmúkúrò ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àrùn kan ṣoṣo, ṣùgbọ́n ní àwọn àwùjọ tí àìsàn náà pọ̀ sí, àtúnkó kòkòrò naat máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ dáadáa. Lẹ́yìn ọ̀pọ̀ ọdún àṣetúnṣe àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìsàn tirakómà, inú awọ ẹyinjú lè ní àwọn àpá ńlá (àpá tirakómà awọ ojú) tí yóò sì sẹ́po sínú èyí tí yóò mú kí irun ìpéǹpéjú máa nu ẹyinjú (tirakómà tirikíásì), èyí tí yóò máa fa ìrora lemọ́lemọ́ àti àìlefarada ìmọ́lẹ̀; èyí àti àyípadà ẹyinjú mìíràn lè fa àpá nínú ẹyinjú.
translations.reference: In 2019 alone, 92 622 people received surgical treatment for advanced trachoma and 95.2 million were treated with antibiotics. Despite these successes, the disease remains a serious public health concern, with an estimated US$ 8 billion annual loss in productivity due to blindness and visual impairment. Symptoms: Infection is associated with inflammatory changes in the conjunctiva known as “active trachoma”. An individual’s immune system can clear a single episode of infection, but in endemic communities, re-acquisition of the organism occurs frequently. After years of repeated episodes of active trachoma, the inside of the eyelid can become so severely scarred (trachomatous conjunctival scarring) that it turns inwards and causes the eyelashes to rub against the eyeball (trachomatous trichiasis), resulting in constant pain and light intolerance; this and other alterations of the eye can lead to scarring of the cornea.
translations.hypothesis: In 2019 alone, 92,622 people received corneal transplants, and 95.2 million people were treated with contact lenses. Despite these successes, the condition remains a significant public health concern, with an estimated annual cost of US$8 billion in vision impairment and blindness. Symptoms: The disease is characterized by inflammation and damage to the cornea, the clear front part of the eye, often referred to as "corneal disease." While a single individual's experience with the disease can vary, in populations where the disease is prevalent, corneal scarring is a common occurrence. After years of corneal disease, the cornea can develop significant scarring (corneal opacity) that can distort vision. In some cases, the scarring can be so severe that it leads to corneal blindness, characterized by significant vision loss and light sensitivity. Additionally, other complications can arise, such as corneal ulcers or infections, which can further damage the eye.

translations.source: Bí ó bá wà láìsí ìtọ́jú, ìṣòro yìí yóò yọrí sí ìkójọ àwọn ìwò bàlùbàlù tí kò ṣe é yí padà, èyí tí yóò yọrí sí àléébù ojú tàbí ìfọ́jú. Ọjọ́ orí tí èyí yóò ṣẹlẹ̀ dá lórí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìdí tí ó fi mọ́ agbára ìkóràn abẹ́lé. Ní àwọn àwùjọ tí ó wọ́pọ̀ sí gan-an, ó lè ṣẹlẹ̀ ní ọmọdé, bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ ìṣòro ojú láàrin ọdún 30 sí 40 wọ́pọ̀ dáadáa. Àwọn obìnrin máa ń fojú ní ìlọ́po mẹ́rin bí àwọn ọkùnrin, bóyá nítorí ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú àwọn àwọn ọmọ tí wọ́n ti lùgbàdì. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn àwùjọ olósì àti ìgbèríko Áfríkà, Ésíà, Australia, Àárín gbùngbùn Ìlà oòrùn, Àárín gbùngbùn àti Gúúsù Amẹ́ríkà àti àwọn Erékùṣù Pàsífíìkì wà nínú ewu.
translations.reference: Left untreated, this condition leads to the formation of irreversible opacities, with resulting visual impairment or blindness. The age at which this occurs depends on several factors including local transmission intensity. In very highly endemic communities, it can occur in childhood, though onset of visual impairment between the ages of 30 and 40 years is more typical. Women are blinded up to four times as often as men, probably due to more frequent contact with infected children. Individuals living in the poorest and most remote communities of Africa, Asia, Australia, the Middle East, Central and South America and the Pacific Islands are at risk.
translations.hypothesis: If left untreated, this condition can lead to a cascade of problems, including corneal ulcers or even blindness. The onset of this condition can be triggered by a variety of factors that compromise the integrity of the tear film. In some cases, it can occur in children, with dry eye syndrome often developing between the ages of 30 and 40. Women are more likely to experience dry eye than men, possibly due to hormonal changes associated with pregnancy or menopause. People living in arid and semi-arid regions of Africa, Asia, Australia, the Middle East, and the southwestern United States, as well as Pacific Island nations, are at higher risk.

translations.source: Ìtọ́jú: Tirakómà ṣe é dènà nípa mímú àdínkù bá àwọn ewu àyíká. Lára àwọn wọ̀nyí ni àìtó ìmọ́tótó, àwọn ilé tó kún fún ọ̀pọ̀ èrò, àìní àǹfààní tó tó sí omi àti àìní àǹfààní tó tó sí àti ìlò ìmọ́tótó. Àwọn àrùn sáábà máa ń bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní ọjọ́ orí pínnísín bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n kì í dàgbà dáadáa àti pé àsìkò wọn kéré pẹ̀lú ọjọ́ orí tí ó ń lọ síwájú. Àwọn àrùn sáábà máa ń jẹ́ kíkó nípasẹ̀ ẹbí àti ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú àìsàn náà. Àṣetúnṣe àwọn àrùn náà máa ń fa àwọn àmì àìsàn líle àrùn náà.
translations.reference: Treatment: Trachoma can be prevented by limiting environmental risk factors. These include inadequate hygiene, crowded households, inadequate access to water and inadequate access to and use of sanitation. Infections usually begin at an early age and grow less frequent and shorter in duration with increasing age. Infections are most often acquired through family and contact with others with active disease. Repeated infections cause the most serious symptoms of the disease.
translations.hypothesis:
Headline: Climate Change Exacerbates Risks of Waterborne Diseases.

Among these risks are water contamination, inadequate sanitation facilities, lack of access to clean water, and poor hygiene practices. Waterborne diseases often begin with symptoms appearing within a few days, and can range from mild to severe, with a relatively short duration. These illnesses are typically spread through family and community contact with the infected individual. Treatment for these diseases often involves managing the symptoms of the illness.

translations.source: Wọ́n lè fòpin sí tirakómà gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò ọgbọ́n SAFE, tí ó ní i: Iṣẹ́ abẹ láti ṣe ìtọ́jú tirakómà tirikíásì, ìpele ìfọ́jú àìsàn náà.; Òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò láti pa àrùn, pàápàá jùlọ lílo ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò azithromycin, èyí tí olùpèsè rẹ̀ fi tọrẹ fún àwọn ètò orílẹ̀ èdè nípasẹ̀ Ètò Tirakómà ti Àgbáyé.; Ìmọ́tótó ojú; àti Ìdàgbàsókè àyíká, pàápàá jùlọ níní àǹfààní sí omi àti ìmọ́tótó.
translations.reference: Trachoma can be eliminated as a public health problem using the SAFE strategy, comprising: Surgery to treat trachomatous trichiasis, the blinding stage of the disease; Antibiotics to clear infection, particularly mass drug administration of the antibiotic azithromycin, which is donated by the manufacturer to national programmes through the International Trachoma Initiative; Facial cleanliness; and Environmental improvement, particularly better access to water and sanitation.
translations.hypothesis: It can be linked to the global health crisis surrounding the use of substandard and falsified medicines, specifically focusing on the SAFE (Support for Affordable and Effective Medicines) program, which includes: Ensuring the quality of essential medicines, including testing for substandard products; The use of antibiotics to treat infections, with a particular emphasis on the widespread use of the antibiotic azithromycin, which is often provided through the Global Fund's programs; Eye health; and Water, sanitation, and hygiene.

translations.source: Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé lo ọgbọ́n SAFE ní 1993 bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n tẹ̀síwájú nínú ètò wọn láti pèsè ìdarí àti àkóso àwọn akitiyan àgbáyé láti fòpin sí tirakómà gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ.
translations.reference: WHO adopted the SAFE strategy in 1993 and continues in its mandate to provide leadership and coordination of international efforts to eliminate trachoma as a public health problem.
translations.hypothesis: The World Health Organization established the SAFE program in 1993, and it has continued to develop its initiatives to prevent and control global health risks, particularly those related to public health emergencies.

translations.source: Òògùn ìbílẹ̀, àfikún àti àṣepọ̀. Ìsọnísókí: Òògùn ìbílẹ̀ Òògùn ìbílẹ̀ ní ìtàn tí ó ti pẹ̀. Ó jẹ́ àpapọ̀ gbogbo ìmọ̀, ìmọ̀-ọ́nṣe, àti àwọn iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n da lórí àwọn ìlànà, ìgbàgbọ́, àti ìrírí tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú onírúurú àṣà, yálà ó ṣe é ṣàlàyé tàbí bẹ́ẹ̀ kọ́, tí wọ́n ń lò fún àkóso ìlera àti fún ìdènà, àyẹ̀wò, ìlọsíwájú tàbí ìtọ́jú ìlera àgọ́ ara tàbí ìlera ọpọlọ. Òògùn àfikún
translations.reference: Traditional, Complementary and Integrative Medicine Overview: Traditional medicine Traditional medicine has a long history. It is the sum total of the knowledge, skill, and practices based on the theories, beliefs, and experiences indigenous to different cultures, whether explicable or not, used in the maintenance of health as well as in the prevention, diagnosis, improvement or treatment of physical and mental illness. Complementary medicine
translations.hypothesis: Medicine, supplements, and healthcare. Introduction: Medicine. Medicine encompasses the entire body of knowledge, skills, and practices related to maintaining and restoring health, preventing and treating illness, and promoting overall well-being. It includes a wide range of disciplines, from diagnosing and treating diseases to providing preventative care and managing chronic conditions. Supplements.

translations.source: Ọ̀rọ̀ ìperí "òògùn àfikún" tàbí "arọ́pò òògùn" ni àwọn onírúurú ètò iṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ikera tí wọn kì í ṣe ara àṣà ti orílẹ̀ èdè yẹn tàbí òògùn ìbílẹ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọn kò sè àmúlò wọn tán sínú ètò ìtọ́jú ìlera tí ó gbòde. Wọ́n máa ń lò wọ́n ní ìpààrọ̀ pẹ̀lú òògùn ìbílẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè kan. Àwọn òògùn egbòogi Lára àwọn òògùn egbòogi ni àgbo, èròjà egbòogi, àwọn ìpèsè egbòogi àti ọjà egbòogi tí wọ́n ti parí iṣẹ́ sí lára, tí ó ní àwọn èròjà ara àwọn ohun ọ̀gbìn, tàbí àwọn èròjà ohun ọ̀gbìn mìíràn, tàbí àwọn àpapọ̀. Ipa: Ìlànà Òògùn Ìbílẹ̀ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé 2014-2023 ni wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ tí wọ́n sì fi lólẹ̀ ní ìfèsì sí ọ̀nà àbáyọ Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé lórí òògùn ìbílẹ̀ (WHA62. 13).
translations.reference: The terms “complementary medicine” or “alternative medicine” refer to a broad set of health care practices that are not part of that country’s own tradition or conventional medicine and are not fully integrated into the dominant health-care system. They are used interchangeably with traditional medicine in some countries. Herbal medicines Herbal medicines include herbs, herbal materials, herbal preparations and finished herbal products, that contain as active ingredients parts of plants, or other plant materials, or combinations. Impact: The WHO Traditional Medicine Strategy 2014–2023 was developed and launched in response to the World Health Assembly resolution on traditional medicine (WHA62.13).
translations.hypothesis:
The term "counterfeit medicine" or "adulterated medicine" refers to substandard or falsified medical products that do not meet the required quality standards or do not contain the declared active ingredients. These products may be intentionally produced to deceive or unintentionally produced due to poor manufacturing practices. They can be found in various countries and pose a significant public health risk.

Examples of counterfeit medicines include:

*   Products that contain the wrong ingredients, or ingredients in the wrong amounts.
*   Products that contain no active ingredients at all.
*   Products that are packaged to look like genuine medicines.

Impact: The World Health Organization's Global Action Plan for the Prevention and Control of Counterfeit Medicines (2014-2023) was launched and aligned with the World Health Assembly resolution on counterfeit medicines (WHA62.13).

translations.source: Ìlànà náà fojúsun ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìlànà ìjígìrì àti àmúlò ètò ìṣe tí yóò ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ipa tí òògùn ìbílẹ̀ ń kó nínú mímú kí àwọn ènìyàn wà ní àlàáfíà. Wíwá ojútùú sí àwọn ìṣòro, fífèsì sí àìní tí àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ ṣe ìdámọ̀ àti ìtẹ̀síwájú lórí iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n ṣe lábẹ́ Ìlànà Òògùn abẹ́lé Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: 2002-2005, ìlànà àmúdójú-ìwọ̀n fún àsìkò 2014-2023 fi àsìkò púpọ̀ sílẹ̀ ju aṣáájú rẹ̀ lọ fún mímú fífi ọwọ́ pàtàkì mú àwọn iṣẹ́ àti ètò ìlera, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn èròjà òògùn ìbílẹ̀ àti àfikún, àwọn àṣà àti aṣàmúlò. Àwọn ìlànà èròǹgbà ni: Láti ṣẹ̀dá ọgbọ́n àgbékalẹ̀ fún àkóso iṣẹ́ T & CM nípasẹ̀ àwọn ìlànà orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n tọ́.; Láti ṣe ìrónilágbára fún ìdánilójú dídára, ààbò, ìlò tí ó tọ́ àti ìmúnádóko T & CM nípa fífi òfin de àwọn ọjà, àṣà àti aṣàmúlò.;
translations.reference: The strategy aims to support Member States in developing proactive policies and implementing action plans that will strengthen the role traditional medicine plays in keeping populations healthy. Addressing the challenges, responding to the needs identified by Member States and building on the work done under the WHO traditional medicine strategy: 2002–2005, the updated strategy for the period 2014–2023 devotes more attention than its predecessor to prioritizing health services and systems, including traditional and complementary medicine products, practices and practitioners. The strategic objectives are: To build the knowledge base for active management of T&CM through appropriate national policies; To strengthen the quality assurance, safety, proper use and effectiveness of T&CM by regulating products, practices and practitioners;
translations.hypothesis: The initiative focuses on providing support to member states in developing and implementing national strategies and action plans that will strengthen the role of national medicines regulatory authorities in ensuring public health. Recognizing the challenges, and building on the progress made by member states under the World Health Organization's Global Strategy for Medicines Safety: 2002-2005, the new strategic plan for 2014-2023 expands the scope and duration of activities to provide more comprehensive support for national medicines regulatory systems, including the development of national medicines policies and regulations. The key objectives are: To establish a robust framework for the management of pharmacovigilance through national strategies that are evidence-based. To strengthen the capacity of national medicines regulatory authorities to effectively monitor, assess, and manage the risks associated with medicines, through the development of appropriate policies, regulations, and procedures.

translations.source: Láti ṣe àgbéga ìpèsè ìlera káríayé nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn iṣẹ́ T & CM nínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera àti ìtọ́jú ìlera ara ẹni. Èsì Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: Ìròyìn àgbáyé Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé lórí òògùn ìbílẹ̀ àti àfikún 2019 ni wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ láti kojú àlàfo tí ó wà nínú ìṣeégbáralé, ìdára àti ojúlówó ìwífun-àlàyé láti ọwọ́ àwọn Orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ ní agbègbè T & CM. Ìròyìn náà ṣàyẹ̀wò ìlọsíwájú káríayé T & CM láàrin àwọn ogún ọdún sẹ́yìn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó dá lórí àwọn ipa láti ọwọ́ àwọn Ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé 179. Ó ń pèsè àlàyé gidi fún àwọn aṣòfin, alámọ̀já ìlera àti àwùjọ fún ṣíṣe àmúlò ipa ọjọ́ ọ̀la ìlera àti àlàáfíà T & CM.
translations.reference: To promote universal health coverage by integrating T&CM services into health care service delivery and self-health care. WHO response: The WHO global report on traditional and complementary medicine 2019 was developed to address the gap in reliable, credible and official data from Member States in the area of T&CM. This report reviews global progress in T&CM over the past two decades and is based on contributions from 179 WHO Member States. It provides valuable information for policy-makers, health professionals and the public for capitalizing on the potential contribution of T&CM to health and well-being.
translations.hypothesis:
To promote global health equity through the implementation of evidence-based practices in the areas of technology and care management in healthcare and personal health.

World Health Assembly Resolution: The World Health Assembly news release on the global strategy for neglected tropical diseases and vector control 2019 was launched to address the challenges, barriers, and opportunities for countries to implement T&CM interventions. The report highlights the progress made in global T&CM over the past years and outlines the key actions needed from World Health Assembly member states. It provides valuable information for policymakers, healthcare professionals, and communities to promote the effective and safe use of T&CM for health and well-being.

translations.source: Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara Ìsọnísókí: Ìpààrọ̀ àwọn hóró, ìṣù ara àti àwọn ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn. Ìpààrọ̀ àwọn hóró, ìṣù ara tàbí ẹ̀yà ara ń gba ẹ̀mí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ènìyàn là bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ń mú àwọn ojúṣe padà bọ̀ sípò níbi tí kò ti sí ìmúnádóko tí ó ṣe é fi wé. Ní ọdún 50, ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara ti di àṣeyọrí àṣà káàkiri àgbáyé. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé, àwọn ìyàtọ̀ ńlá wà láàrin àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè nínú àǹfààní wọn sí ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara tí ó bojúmu àti nínú ìpele ààbò, dídára, agbára ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n pèsè fún ìpààrọ̀ àti ìpààrọ̀ àwọn hóró, ìṣù ara àti ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn.
