translations.reference: Transplantation Overview: Transplantation of human cells, tissues and organs Transplantation of human cells, tissues or organs saves many lives and restores essential functions where no alternatives of comparable effectiveness exist. In 50 years, transplantation has become a successful worldwide practice. However, there are large differences between countries in access to suitable transplantation and in the level of safety, quality, efficacy of donation and transplantation of human cells, tissues and organs.
translations.hypothesis: The Concept of Beauty: A Cross-Cultural Perspective: Beauty encompasses physical appearance, personality traits, and cultural values. While beauty standards vary across cultures, certain features, such as symmetry and health, are often considered attractive universally. Over the past 50 years, the concept of beauty has become increasingly globalized. However, significant differences remain between cultures in their perceptions of beauty, particularly in terms of ideal body types, facial features, and personality traits valued for attractiveness.

translations.source: Àwọn agbọn ẹ̀kọ́ ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara síwájú. Pàápàá jùlọ, àwọn àìní aláàárẹ̀ tí kò jẹ́ bíbá pàdé àti àìtó àwọn ẹ̀yà ara fún ìpààrọ̀ máa ń yọrí ìdánwò ìjíngbéyọ-ẹ̀yà ara fún ìpààrọ̀. Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn Àwọn hóró ara àti ìṣù ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn fún ìpààrọ̀ lè gba àwọn ẹ̀mí là tàbí kí ó mú ìpadàbọ̀sípò bá àwọn ojúṣe pàtàkì. Fún àpẹẹrẹ iṣẹ́ abẹ ìpààrọ̀ ìṣù ẹyinjú lè mú ìpadàbọ̀sípò bá ìríran fún ìfọ́jú inú ẹyinjú.;
translations.reference: The ethical aspects of transplantation are at the forefront. In particular, the unmet patients’ needs and the shortage of transplants lead to the temptation of trafficking in human body components for transplantation. Human transplantation Human cells and tissues for transplantation can save lives or restore essential functions. For examplea corneal graft can restore sight in corneal blindness;
translations.hypothesis: Advanced personality assessments. More specifically, personality traits that are not easily measured and personality assessments designed for specific purposes often require more sophisticated personality assessments. Human resource and personnel assessments for employment can have significant consequences, or even lead to discrimination against certain groups. For example, a personality assessment used in the hiring process could lead to discrimination against individuals with certain personality traits.

translations.source: ìpààrọ̀ àwọn hóró ẹ̀jẹ̀ ìpìlẹ̀ aláìgbó lè ṣe ìwòsàn fún àwọn àìsàn àtìgbà èwe wá tàbí àìsàn tí ènìyàn kó bí jẹjẹrẹ inú ẹ̀jẹ̀. Ẹ rí àlàyé sí i lórí Hóró Ẹ̀jẹ̀ Ìpìlẹ̀ Aláìgbó. ìpààrọ̀ òpó ìdarí ọkàn ènìyàn sáábà máa ń ní àwọn ipò ìpààrọ̀ tí wọ́n dára jùlọ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn aláìsàn kò nílò ìwòsàn àìmẹ́jẹ̀dì ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́. Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara pẹ̀lú ti ẹranko Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara pẹ̀lú ti ẹranko, ẹranko sí ènìyàn, ni àwọn hóró alààyè, ìṣù ara tàbí ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n wá láti ara ẹranko àti àwọn omira ẹ̀dá ènìyàn, hóró, ìṣù ara tàbí ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n kò sí lára àwọn ẹ̀dá alààyè wọ̀nyí tẹ́lẹ̀, àwọn èròjà ẹ̀dá mìíràn, ní agbára láti jẹ́ arọ́pò fún àwọn èròjà tí wọ́n ti ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn wá kí wọ́n sì dí àlàfo tí ó wà nínú èròjà ẹ̀dá ènìyàn fún ìpààrọ̀.
translations.reference: the transplantation of haematopoietic stem cells can cure congenital or acquired diseases including some leukaemias See further information on Haematopoeitic Stem Cells the transplantation of a human heart valve often constitutes the best replacement situation and recipient patients do not require long term anti-coagulation therapy. Xenotransplantation Xenotransplantation, animal to human, defined as living cells, tissues or organs of animal origin and human body fluids, cells, tissues or organs that have ex vivo with these living, xenogeneic materials, has the potential to constitute an alternative to material of human origin and bridge the shortfall in human material for transplantation.
translations.hypothesis: Certain plant-based ingredients can be used to treat skin conditions or ailments that affect the human body. This is explained in more detail in the section on Plant-Based Ingredients. The human body's internal environment is constantly striving for balance, and when imbalances occur, it can lead to various health problems. Plant-based ingredients, including herbs, spices, and extracts derived from plants and animal sources, can help to restore balance and promote healing. These ingredients work by interacting with the body's systems, such as the immune system, digestive system, and nervous system. They can also help to reduce inflammation, improve circulation, and protect against damage from free radicals.

translations.source: Ìtọrẹ: Ìtọrẹ láti ara àwọn òkú gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ ṣíṣe ìdàgbàsókè fún dórí òpin agbára ìwòsàn wọn yàgò fún, nígbàkigbà tí ó bá ṣe é ṣe, àwọn ewu fún olùtọrẹ ààyè, gẹ́gẹ́ bí Ilanat Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé 3 ṣe sọ ọ́. Ìdámọ̀ àmúyẹ kan wà fún àwọn àwùjọ, àti alámọ̀já ìlera, àti kẹ́kọ̀ọ́ daradaeat sí i nípa ìtọrẹ àti ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìyẹn jẹ́ pàtàkì fún àṣeyọrí àwọn ètò ìtọrẹ òkú. Àmọ́ sá, pẹ̀lú gbogbo òòrèkóòrè ìlò àwọn èròjà tí àwọn òkú fi tọrẹ, àwọn ìtọrẹ láti ọwọ́ àwọn alààyè olùtọrẹ pọn dandan fún àwọn oríṣìí ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara kan tàbí láti pèsè fún àwọn èròjà kan tí wọ́n kò sí ní ànító láti ọwọ́ àwọn òkú olùtọrẹ láti ṣe alábàápàdé àwọn àìní aláìsàn. Nítorí náà ni wọ́n ṣe máa ń ṣe àmúlò ìtọrẹ alààyè bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ó ní àwọn ewu tí wọ́n lè má ṣe é dijú sí fún olùtọrẹ. Bí gbígba ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn fún ìpààrọ̀ láti ara òkú tàbí alààyè olùtọrẹ àti ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ọ̀tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀ tí ó tẹ̀lé lè ní ewu ẹ̀kọ́ àti ààbò fún agbàtọrẹ àti olùtọrẹ, àwọn àkóso tí wọ́n dúró dáadáa àti àbójútó tí ó múnádóko gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ gbígbé kalẹ̀ láti ọwọ́ àwọn aláṣẹ ìlera láti dáàbò bò wọ́n.
translations.reference: Donation: Donations from deceased persons should be developed to their maximum therapeutic potential avoiding, whenever possible, the inherent risks to live donors, as stated in WHO Guiding Principle 3. There is a recognized need for communities, and health professionals, to become better educated about donation and transplantation and that is the key to the success of deceased donation programmes. However, despite the frequent use of materials donated from deceased donors, the donations from living donors are necessary for some types of transplants or to compensate for the limited supply of material available from deceased donors in order to meet patient needs. Living donation is thus practised despite the fact that it involves risks for the donor that may not be negligible. As the procurement of human material for transplantation from deceased or living donors and the subsequent allogeneic transplantation may entail ethical and safety risks for both the recipient and the donor, strict controls and effective oversight should be carried out by the health authorities to protect them.
translations.hypothesis: Donation: Donations from the deceased should be a significant part of their overall estate planning, potentially including bequests to charities, as highlighted by the World Health Organization's International Day of Charity 3. There is a strong moral imperative for individuals and organizations to donate, and a growing awareness of the importance of legacy giving and charitable donations. Furthermore, donations from the deceased's estate are often crucial for funding specific programs or projects that the deceased was passionate about, or for addressing unmet needs in the community. Therefore, it is important to consider the potential risks and benefits of making a donation as part of your estate plan. The transfer of human organs for transplantation from a deceased or living donor, and the subsequent transplantation of those organs, can pose risks and benefits for the donor and recipient. Therefore, it is essential that robust and ethical guidelines are in place to ensure that all procedures are carried out safely and responsibly by qualified healthcare professionals.

translations.source: Àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà kàn ìtọ́jú tí ó dára jùlọ nípa fún àwọn olùtọrẹ àti agbàtọrẹ. Àbójútó tí ó hànde láti ọwọ́ àwọn aláṣẹ lórí ìtọrẹ àti àwọn iṣẹ́ ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara tún ṣe pàtàkì láti mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá ìgbẹ́kẹ̀lé àwùjọ nínú ètò náà. Ní àfikún, ìpinnu láti jẹ olùtọrẹ sáábà máa ń dá lórí ìlóye pé kíkó ipa nínú wíwà aowm èròjà ìpààrọ̀ lè jẹ́ àǹfààní fún àìní ìlera ẹbí olùtọrẹ ní ọjọ́ kan. Bí a bá gbáradì láti gba ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara nígbà tí s bá nílò ọ̀kan, nígbà náà ó yẹ ká ṣetán láti tọrẹ Àwọn Ẹ̀kọ́: Àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé lórí Ìpààrọ̀ Hóró Ẹ̀dá Ènìyàn, Ìṣù ara àti Ẹ̀yà ara.
translations.reference: The Guiding Principles mandate optimal care for donors and recipients. The transparent oversight of the health authorities over donation and transplantation activities is also essential to increase the trust of the public in the system. In addition, the decision to be a donor is often based on the understanding that a contribution to the availability of transplant resources may someday benefit the health needs of the donor’s family. If we are prepared to receive a transplant should we need one, then we should be ready to give Ethics: WHO Guiding Principles on Human Cell, Tissue and Organ Transplantation
translations.hypothesis: Ethical guidelines play a crucial role in ensuring the well-being of patients and healthcare professionals. The informed consent process, which involves obtaining permission from patients for medical procedures and research, is essential for promoting patient autonomy within the healthcare system. Furthermore, the decision to become a healthcare professional is often based on the belief that contributing to the health and well-being of others can be a fulfilling and rewarding experience. When faced with the need for medical care, individuals should be empowered to make informed decisions about their treatment. To this end, it is important to consult the guidelines provided by organizations such as the World Health Organization (WHO) on human milk donation, tissue, and organ transplantation.

translations.source: Ní 1987, Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé tí kò sí mọ́, lórí ìṣòwò fún èrè lórí ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn, ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ ìgbáradì àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé kìíní lórí Ìpààrọ̀ Ẹ̀yà ara, èyí tí Àjọ náà fọwọ́ sí ní 1991 nínú onat àbáyọ WHA44. 25. Àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà wọ̀nyí ti nípa ńlá lórí àwọn òfin àti ìṣe alámọ̀já àti ilé aṣòfin káàkiri àgbáyé fún bí ogún ọdún. Lẹ́yìn ìjùmọ̀sọ̀rọ̀ tí ó gba ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún, Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé Ìkẹtalélọ́gọ́ta ṣàmúlò ọ̀nà àbáyọ WHA463. 22 ní 21 Oṣù karùn-ún 2010, tí eomt sì fọwọ́ sí ìmúdójú-ìwọ̀n àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé àti ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn agbọn fún ìtẹ̀síwájú láti ṣe àmúdára àwọn àṣà ìtọrẹ àti ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara. Ìkéde Istanbul Àwọn alámọ̀já ìtọrẹ àti ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara láti gbogbo àgbègbè, nípasẹ̀ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn àjọ àti ibùdó wọn, ń fọwọ́ sí Ìkéde Istanbul lórí ìjíngbéyọ-ẹ̀yà ara àti ìrìn àjò lọ sí orílẹ̀ èdè tí ẹ̀yà ara ti rọjú tí wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ lábẹ́ ìdarí Àjọ Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara àti Àjọ Àgbáyé lórí ìmọ̀ kíndìnrín ní Oṣù Karùn-ún 2008.
translations.reference: In 1987 the fortieth World Health Assembly, concerned at the trade for profit in human organs, initiated the preparation of the first WHO Guiding Principles on Transplantation, endorsed by the Assembly in 1991 in resolution WHA44.25. These Guiding Principles have greatly influenced professional codes and practices as well as legislation around the world during almost two decades. After a consultation process that took several years, the Sixty-third World Health Assembly adopted resolution WHA63.22 on 21 May 2010, endorsing the updated WHO Guiding Principles and identifying areas of progress to optimize donation and transplantation practices. The Declaration of Istanbul Professionals of donation and transplantation from all regions, through many of their organizations and institutions, are endorsing the Declaration of Istanbul on organ trafficking and transplant tourism developed under the leadership of the Transplantation Society and the International Society of Nephrology in May 2008.
translations.hypothesis: In 1987, the World Health Organization, concerned about the issue of trafficking in human organs, established a working group to develop guidelines for the World Health Assembly on organ transplantation. These guidelines were adopted by the Assembly in 1991, during the 44th World Health Assembly, in resolution WHA44.25. These guidelines have had a significant impact on the laws and practices of transplant centers and hospitals around the world for many years. Following extensive consultations, the Second World Health Assembly working group on organ trafficking convened a session on June 21, 2010, and adopted resolution WHA63.22, which reaffirmed the guidelines of the World Health Assembly on organ transplantation and called for the development of measures to combat organ trafficking and promote ethical and safe organ donation. The Istanbul Declaration On ethical principles and guidelines for transplantation, representatives from transplant organizations and institutions from all regions, through their various organizations and institutions, adopted the Istanbul Declaration on organ trafficking and transplantation tourism at a meeting held under the auspices of the Transplantation Society and the World Health Organization in Istanbul in June 2008.

translations.source: Ìrìn àjò àti ìlera Ìsọnísókí: Ìrìn àjò àgbáyé lè mú onírúurú ewu bá ìlera, èyí dá lórí àwọn àbùdá arìnrìn-àjò àti ìrìnàjò náà. Àwọn arìnrìn-àjò lè ní àwọn àyípadà òjìji nínú ìrúkèrúdò, òtútù, kòkòrò àìfojúrí, àti ìgbóná, èyí tí ó lè yọrí sí àìlera. Ní àfikún, àwọn ewu ìlera líle lè dìde ní àwọn àgbègbè tí ilégbèé kò ti dára, ìmọ́tótó àti àyíká mímọ́ kò tó, àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera kò ní ìdàgbàsókè tó bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni kò sí omi tí ó mọ́. Ìrìnàjò àgbáyé ń pọ̀ sí i, bí ẹgbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ènìyàn ṣe ń rin ìrìn àjò fún ìdí iṣẹ́, àwùjọ, ìgbádùn àti ọ̀rọ̀ ọmọnìyàn lọ́dọọdún.
translations.reference: Travel and health Overview: International travel can pose various risks to health, depending on the characteristics of both the traveller and the travel. Travellers may encounter sudden and significant changes in altitude, humidity, microbes, and temperature, which can result in ill-health. In addition, serious health risks may arise in areas where accommodation is of poor quality, hygiene and sanitation are inadequate, medical services are not well developed and clean water is unavailable. International travel is on the rise, as millions of people travel for professional, social, recreational and humanitarian purposes each year.
translations.hypothesis: Travel and Health Risks: International travel can expose individuals to various health risks, stemming from the specific conditions and environments of the destination. Travelers may encounter infectious diseases, foodborne illnesses, injuries, and other health problems. Furthermore, health risks can arise in areas with poor sanitation, limited access to healthcare, and a lack of clean water. As international travel increases, with more people traveling for work, leisure, and cultural experiences, these risks become more prevalent.

translations.source: Gbogbo àwọn arìnrìn-àjò gbọ́dọ̀ gbáradì fún onírúurú ewu tí wọ́n lè kojú ní àwọn àyíká tí wọn kò dé rí tẹ́lẹ̀, lásìkò àti lẹ́yìn tí wọ́n rin ìrìn àjò. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbèrò láti rin ìrìn àjò gbọ́dọ̀ gbàmọ̀ràn lórí ewu tí ó lè wà ní àwọn àgbègbè tí wọ́n ń lọ kí wọ́n sì lóye ọ̀nà tí ó dára jùlọ láti dáàbò bo ìlera wọn kí wọ́n sì mú àdínkù bá ewu kíkó àìsàn. Ìṣètò ọjọ́ iwájú, àwọn òté ìdáàbòbò tí wọ́n tọ́ àti ìsọ́ra tí ó yẹ lè dáàbò bo ìlera àwọn arìnrìn-àjò, kí ó sì ṣe àdínkù ewu ìjàm̀bá àti kíkó àìsàn. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ilé-iṣẹ́ iṣẹ́ ìṣègùn àti ìrìn àjò lè pèsè ìrànlọ́wọ́ gbígbòòrò àti ìmọ̀ràn ìjìnlẹ̀, ó sì jẹ́ ojúṣe arìnrìn-àjò láti wá ìròyìn, láti lóye àwọn ewu tí wọ́n ní í ṣe àti láti gbé ìgbésẹ̀ tí ó yẹ láti dáàbò bo ìlera wọn nígbà tí wọ́n bá ń rin ìrìn àjò. Ewu: Nígbà tí a bá ń ṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn ewu ìlera fún ìrìnàjò àgbáyé, àwọn arìnrìn-àjò àti àwọn alámọ̀já ìlera gbọ́dọ̀ wo àwọn kókó pàtàkì wọ̀nyí:
translations.reference: All travellers must prepare for the variety of health risks they can be exposed to in unfamiliar environments before, during and after they travel. People planning to travel should seek advice on the potential hazards in their chosen destinations and understand how best to protect their health and minimize the risk of acquiring disease. Forward planning, appropriate preventive measures and careful precautions can protect the health of travellers, and minimize the risks of accident and of acquiring disease. Although the medical profession and the travel industry can provide extensive help and sound advice, it remains the traveller’s responsibility to seek information, to understand the risks involved and to take the necessary precautions to protect their health while travelling. Risk: When determining the health risks of international travel, travellers and health professionals should consider the following key factors:
translations.hypothesis: All travelers should be aware of the various health risks they may encounter before, during, and after their trip. People who are planning to travel should research the potential health hazards in the areas they are visiting and take appropriate steps to protect their health and minimize the risk of illness. Pre-travel consultations, vaccinations, and prophylactic medications can help protect travelers' health and reduce the risk of infectious diseases. While travel agencies and healthcare providers can offer valuable advice and information, it is ultimately the traveler's responsibility to be informed, understand the risks involved, and take the necessary precautions to protect their health while traveling. Risks: When assessing health risks for international travel, travelers and healthcare professionals should consider the following key factors:

translations.source: ọna ìrìnrìn-àjò (àwọn) ibi afẹ́dé àsìkò àti ìgbà ìrìnàjò ìdí ìrìnàjò ìkojúwọ̀n ilégbèé, ìmọ́tótó oúnjẹ àti àyíká mímọ́
translations.reference: mode of transport destination(s) duration and season of travel purpose of travel standards of accommodation, food hygiene and sanitation
translations.hypothesis: Travel essentials: essential travel documents, travel insurance, reliable transportation, healthy food, and comfortable clothing.

translations.source: ìwà arìnrìn-àjò ìlera àtẹ̀yìnwá arìnrìn-àjò Arìnrìn-àjò lè kojú àwọn ewu ìlera wọ̀nyí dá lórí ibi afẹ́dé wọn: ìgéjẹ ẹranko tàbí kòkòrò, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ẹ̀fọn, ìdun, àfòpiná, àti àwọn kòkòrò àfòká mìíràn, tí wọ́n lè tan àìsàn bí maléríà. ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú ẹ̀jẹ̀ tàbí àwọn omira mìíràn
translations.reference: behaviour of the traveller underlying health of the traveller Travellers may encounter the following health risks depending on their destination: animal and insect bites, including mosquitoes, ticks, fleas, and some flies, which can spread diseases such as malaria exposure to blood or other bodily fluids
translations.hypothesis: Travel-related illnesses: Travel illnesses can be caused by various health risks encountered during travel: food poisoning or traveler's diarrhea, which can cause vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal cramps, and other symptoms, and may lead to illnesses like malaria. Exposure to bodily fluids or other substances.

translations.source: àyípadà àyíká, bí ìrúkèrúdò, òtútù, ìgbóná àwọn àìsàn àtara-oúnjẹsẹ̀ àti àtara-omisẹ̀ àìtó àwọn iṣẹ́ ìṣègùn ìfarapa, tí ó fi mọ́ láti ara ìjàm̀bá ojú pópó tàbí àwọn iṣẹ́ ìnajú ìlera ọpọlọ, tí ó fi mọ́ wàhálà àti ìyàlẹ́nu àṣà
translations.reference: environmental changes, such as altitude, humidity, temperature foodborne and waterborne diseases inadequate medical services injuries, including from road traffic accidents or recreational activities psychological health, including stress and culture shock
translations.hypothesis: This includes the production of counterfeit goods, such as medicines and cosmetics, as well as the illegal trafficking of drugs, often linked to organized crime groups, which can lead to violence and social disruption.

translations.source: Àwọn arìnrìn-àjò tún lè faragbá onírúurú iye àwọn àìsàn, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn wọ̀nyí ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára. Àmọ́ sá, àwọn àrùn kán wà, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn àrùn tí wọ́n léwu jùlọ, èyí tí kò sí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára fún. Àwọn ibi afẹ́dé níbi tí ilégbèé, ìmọ́tótó àti àyíká mímọ́, ìtọ́jú ìṣègùn àti àbùdá omi ti dára púpọ̀ ní ewu líle níwọ̀n fún ìlera àwọn arìnrìn-àjò. Níbi tí àwọn iṣẹ́ wọ̀nyí kò ti jẹ́ pípèsè, àwọn arìnrìn-àjò gbọ́dọ̀ gbé ìgbésẹ̀ ìsọ́ra gidi láti yàgò fún àìsàn. Àwọn ikilọ ìrìnàjò láti ọwọ́ àwọn àjọ ìjọba gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ fífi ọwọ́ líle mú.
translations.reference: Travellers may also be exposed to a number of infectious diseases, and many of these can be prevented by vaccination. However, there are some infectious diseases, including some of the most dangerous, for which no vaccines exist. Destinations where accommodation, hygiene and sanitation, medical care and water quality are of a high standard pose relatively few serious risks to the health of travellers. Where these services are not provided, travellers should take stringent precautions to avoid illness. Travel warnings from governmental sources should be taken seriously.
translations.hypothesis: Travelers can be exposed to a variety of health risks, and many of these are preventable through proper precautions. Some risks are common, while others are more specific to certain destinations. Factors such as sanitation, access to clean water, food safety, and the prevalence of certain diseases can pose significant health threats to travelers. When these factors are not ideal, travelers must take proactive steps to protect their health. Government travel advisories should be carefully considered.

translations.source: Ìdáàbòbò: Síwájú ìrìnàjò: ẹ kàn sí ibùdó ìṣègùn fún ìrìnàjò tàbí oníṣègùn, bí ọ̀sẹ̀ 4-8 kí ẹ tó rin ìrìnàjò láti gba àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára tí ó yẹ. ẹ ka nípa àìsàn abẹ́lé tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ibi afẹ́dé yín; ẹ béèrè fún ìròyìn lórí ewu maléríà àti ìdènà ẹ̀fọn jíjẹ. Èyí lè mọ́ ríra òògùn idenat tí ó tọ́ àti kíkó àwọ̀n ibùsùn àti òògùn ẹ̀fọn; ẹ ṣé àyẹ̀wò ìṣègùn àti eyín;
translations.reference: Prevention: Before travel: contact a travel medicine centre or a physician, preferably 4–8 weeks before departure to receive any required vaccinations; read up on local diseases related to your destination; request information on malaria risk and prevention of mosquito bites. This may include purchasing appropriate preventive medication and packing a bednet and insect repellent; get a medical and dental check-up;
translations.hypothesis: Travel Advisory: Before you travel: Consult with a travel health professional or your doctor 4-8 weeks before your trip to receive necessary vaccinations. Discuss any potential health risks associated with your destination, including malaria and yellow fever. This will help you determine the appropriate vaccinations and medications you need. Also, get a travel health checkup and ensure you are up-to-date on all your routine vaccinations.

translations.source: ẹ gba àwọn àbá àkọsílẹ̀ fún òògùn gẹ́gẹ́ bí àsìkò tí ẹ máa lò kí ẹ sì ṣe àtòjọ irinṣẹ́ ìṣègùn tí ó yẹ; ẹ ra adójútòfo ìṣègùn tí ó yẹ pẹ̀lú ìpèsè tí ó tọ́ lẹ́yìn odi. Lásìkò ìrìnàjò: ẹ jẹ oúnjẹ tí wọ́n ṣe dáadáa àti ohun mímu tútù tí ó wà nínú ìgò tàbí ìdì nìkan. Ẹ ṣe omi mímu bí ẹ bá ń ṣiyèméjì nípa ààbò.
translations.reference: obtain prescriptions for medication according to length of stay and assemble a suitable medical kit; purchase medical insurance with appropriate cover abroad During travel: eat only thoroughly cooked food and drink only bottled or packaged cold drinks. Boil drinking-water if safety is doubtful.
translations.hypothesis: Here are some guidelines for using medication safely: always follow the prescribed dosage and schedule; carefully review the instructions for use that come with the medication. Travel tips: pack healthy snacks and essential toiletries in your carry-on or personal bag. Stay hydrated by drinking plenty of water.

translations.source: ẹ ṣàkíyèsí àwọn ìjàm̀bá tàbí ìṣòro tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìjàm̀bá, ẹranko, èèwọ̀, òòrùn àti eré ìdárayá; ẹ mú ìwé pélébé tí ó ń ṣàfihàn ẹ̀yà ẹ̀jẹ̀ yín lọ́wọ́; ẹ wọ ẹ̀gbà ọwọ́ ìtanilólobó ìlera, bí ẹ bá ní àwọn èèwọ̀ tàbí àìsàn líle. Àwọn arìnrìn-àjò gbọ́dọ̀ ṣe àyẹ̀wò ìṣègùn nígbà tí wọ́n bá padà bí wọ́n ba: padà pẹ̀lú ibà láti orílẹ̀ èdè kan tí maléríà wà tàbí lè wà
translations.reference: be aware of accidents or problems related to traffic, animals, allergies, sun and sport; carry a card showing your blood group; wear a medical alert bracelet, if you have allergies or a chronic disease Travellers should obtain a medical examination on their return if they: return with a fever from a country where malaria is or may be present
translations.hypothesis: Consider the following precautions or concerns related to travel, animals, health, safety, and entertainment; pack a first-aid kit to address minor injuries; wear appropriate protective gear, such as gloves, if you have allergies or sensitivities. Travelers should also check the regulations regarding pets when they arrive, especially if traveling from a country where malaria is prevalent or could be present.

translations.source: ní àìsàn líle ní àìsàn lẹ́yìn ọ̀sẹ̀ díẹ̀ tí wọ́n padà sí ilé gba ìtọ́jú maléríà nígbà tí wọ́n ń rin ìrìn àjò lè ti ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú àrùn líle kan lò ju oṣù mẹ́ta lọ ní orílẹ̀ èdè tí ń gòkè àgbà
translations.reference: suffer from a chronic disease experience illness in the weeks following their return home received treatment for malaria while travelling may have been exposed to a serious infectious disease spent more than 3 months in a developing country
translations.hypothesis: During a recent trip, a couple returned home and were diagnosed with malaria. They had been traveling in a country where malaria is prevalent and had contracted the disease, likely more than three months ago, in that region.

translations.source: Ikọ́ọfe Ìsọnísókí: Ikọ́ọfe ni bakitéríà (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) máa ń fà bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó daabat máa ń mú àwọn ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró. Ikọ́ọfe máa ń tàn ká nípa afẹ́fẹ́ nígbà tí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọn ní ikọ́ọfe ní ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró bá wúkọ́, sín tàbí tutọ́. Eniyan nílò láti fa àwọn kòkòrò àrùn díẹ̀ sínú láti ṣe àìsàn. Lọ́dọọdún, mílíọ̀nù 10 ènìyàn máa ń ṣàìsàn ikọ́ọfe (TB).
translations.reference: Tuberculosis Overview: TB is caused by bacteria (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) and it most often affects the lungs. TB is spread through the air when people with lung TB cough, sneeze or spit. A person needs to inhale only a few germs to become infected. Every year, 10 million people fall ill with tuberculosis (TB).
translations.hypothesis: Tuberculosis (TB): Tuberculosis is a disease caused by the bacterium Mycobacterium tuberculosis, which primarily affects the lungs. It spreads through the air when people with active TB cough, sneeze, or speak. To become infected, a person must breathe in the bacteria. Each year, approximately 10 million people become ill with TB.

translations.source: Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ó jẹ́ àìsàn tí ó ṣe é dènà tí ó sì se é wò, mílíọ̀nù 1.5 ènìyàn kú nítorí TB ní ọdọọdún – tí ó mú kí ó jẹ́ àrùn apànìyàn tí ó lékè jùlọ lágbàáyé. Ikọ́ọfe jẹ́ okùnfà ikú àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní KASA àti okùnfà ìlòdì sí òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò bakitéríà gbòógì. Púpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ṣàìsàn ikọ́ọfe ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè, ṣùgbọ́n ikọ́ọfe wà káàkiri gbogbo àgbáyé. Bí ìdajì àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé pẹ̀lú ikọ́ọfe lè wà ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè 8 : Bangladesh, China, India, Indonesia, Nigeria, Pakistan, Philippinesàti South Africa. Bí ìdá kan nínú mẹ́rin àwọn ènìyàn àgbáyé ni wọ́n ṣírò pé wọ́n ní kòkòrò bakitéríà ikọ́ọfe, ṣùgbọ́n ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ènìyàn kò ní tẹ̀síwájú láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àrùn TB bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn kan yóò san nínú àrùn náà.
translations.reference: Despite being a preventable and curable disease, 1.5 million people die from TB each year – making it the world’s top infectious killer. TB is the leading cause of death of people with HIV and also a major contributor to antimicrobial resistance Most of the people who fall ill with TB live in low- and middle-income countries, but TB is present all over the world. About half of all people with TB can be found in 8 countries: Bangladesh, China, India, Indonesia, Nigeria, Pakistan, Philippines and South Africa. About a quarter of the global population is estimated to have been infected with TB bacteria, but most people will not go on to develop TB disease and some will clear the infection.
translations.hypothesis: Although it is a preventable and curable disease, tuberculosis (TB) killed 1.5 million people last year – making it the most deadly infectious disease in the world. The incidence is particularly high in low- and middle-income countries, and the disease is a major driver of antimicrobial resistance. While the majority of people infected with TB live in developing countries, the disease is present in every country around the world. The highest rates of TB are found in eight countries: Bangladesh, China, India, Indonesia, Nigeria, Pakistan, the Philippines, and South Africa. One in four people worldwide are estimated to have latent TB infection, meaning they have the bacteria in their bodies but are not yet sick. However, many of these people will go on to develop active TB disease, and some will die from the disease.

translations.source: Àwọn tí wọ́n ní àrùn náà ṣùgbọ́n tí wọn kò tíì ṣe àìsàn kò lè ṣe ìkóràn àìsàn náà. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní bakitéríà ikọ́ọfe ní ewu ìgbésí ayé 5-10% láti ṣàìsàn ikọ́ọfe. Àwọn tí wọ́n ní ìṣòro ètò àjẹsára, bí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé pẹ̀lú KASA, àìjẹunrekánú tàbí àtọ̀gbẹ, tàbí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń fa tábà, wà nínú ewu púpọ̀ láti ṣe àìsàn. Àmì àìsàn: Lára àwọn àmì àrùn ikọ́ọfe tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ ni: Ikọ́ líle, Àyà dídùn, Rírẹ̀ tàbí ọ̀rẹ̀rẹ̀, RÍrù, Ibà, Òógùn òru Lọ́pọ̀ ìgbà, àwọn àmì àìsàn yìí yóò wà níwọ̀n fún ọ̀pọ̀ oṣù, èyí tí yóò yọrí sí àwọn ìdádúró láti wá ìtọ́jú bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò mú ìlọsíwájú bá ewu kíkó àrùn náà ran àwọn ẹlòmíràn.
translations.reference: Those who are infected but not (yet) ill with the disease cannot transmit it. People infected with TB bacteria have a 5–10% lifetime risk of falling ill with TB. Those with compromised immune systems, such as people living with HIV, malnutrition or diabetes, or people who use tobacco, have a higher risk of falling ill. Symptoms: Common symptoms of TB disease include: Prolonged cough, Chest pain, Weakness or fatigue, Weight loss, Fever, Night sweats Often, these symptoms will be mild for many months, thus leading to delays in seeking care and increasing the risk of spreading the infection to others.
translations.hypothesis: People who are infected with the disease but show no symptoms cannot transmit it. Individuals with bacterial vaginosis have a 5-10% risk of developing endometritis. People with risk factors, such as those who live with someone who has HIV, are immunocompromised, or smoke, are at a higher risk of developing the infection. Symptoms: Common symptoms of endometritis include: Fever, abdominal pain, vaginal discharge, bleeding, and pelvic pain. These symptoms can last for several months, which can lead to complications and increase the risk of spreading the disease to others.

translations.source: Bí oníṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera bá kẹ́fín pé aláìsàn kan ní àrùn ikọ́ọfe, wọn yóò rán aláìsàn náà lọ ṣe àyẹ̀wò. Ní ti ọ̀ràn ìkẹ́fín àrùn ikọ́ọfe ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró, wọn yóò sọ fún àwọn aláìsàn kí wọ́n pèsè kẹ̀lẹ̀bẹ̀ wọn fún àyẹ̀wò bakitéríà TB. Fún àwọn àrùn TB tí kò jẹ́ mọ̀ ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró, àwọn àpẹẹrẹ omira àti ìṣù ara tí ọ̀rọ̀ kan lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe àyẹ̀wò. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé dábàá àwọn àyẹ̀wò mòlíkúlà ayákánkán gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn àyẹ̀wò aṣáájú fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń ṣàfihàn àmì àti àpẹẹrẹ TB. Àwọn irinṣẹ́ ìṣàyẹ̀wò mìíràn lè jẹ́ àyẹ̀wò fínnífínní kẹ̀lẹ̀bẹ̀ àti àwòrán onítànsàn-X àyà.
translations.reference: If the healthcare provider suspects a patient to have TB disease, they will send the patient for testing. In the case of suspected lung TB disease, patients will be asked to give a sputum sample for testing for TB bacteria. For non-lung TB disease, samples of affected body fluids and tissue can be tested. WHO recommends rapid molecular diagnostic tests as initial tests for people showing signs and symptoms of TB. Other diagnostic tools can include sputum smear microscopy and chest X-rays.
translations.hypothesis: When a healthcare professional suspects a patient has tuberculosis, they will order a test to confirm the diagnosis. In the case of pulmonary tuberculosis, patients may be asked to provide a sputum sample for TB bacteria testing. For non-pulmonary TB, blood and tissue samples may be tested. The World Health Organization recommends using highly sensitive molecular tests as the primary diagnostic tool for people suspected of having TB. Other tests that may be used include microscopic examination of sputum and chest X-rays.

translations.source: Pẹ̀lú àrùn TB, ènìyàn lè ní àrùn bakitéríà TB tí yóò wà nínú ara láìṣe ọsẹ́. Àrùn yìí lè dàgbà di àrùn ikọ́ọfe bí ètò àjẹsára wọn bá káàárẹ̀. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní àrùn TB kì í ṣàfihàn èyíkéyìí àmì tàbí àpẹẹrẹ TB. Láti ṣe ìdámọ̀ àrùn TB, àwọn oníṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera yóò ṣàyẹ̀wò àwọn aláìsàn tí wọ́n nínú ewu láti wọ́gilé TB tí ó wà nínú ara, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n lè lo àyẹ̀wò awọ ara tàbí ẹ̀jẹ̀ láti ṣàyẹ̀wò àrùn TB. Ìtọ́jú: Àrùn ikọ́ọfe ṣe é wò.
translations.reference: With TB infection, a person gets infected with TB bacteria that lie inactive in the body. This infection can develop into TB disease if their immune system weakens. People with TB infection do not show any signs or symptoms of TB. To identify TB infection, healthcare providers will screen at-risk patients to rule out active TB, and they may use a skin or blood test to check for TB infection. Treatment: TB disease is curable.
translations.hypothesis: With active tuberculosis (TB), a person has a TB infection that is actively multiplying in the body. This can lead to serious illness if not treated. People with active TB are contagious and can spread the bacteria to others. To diagnose active TB, healthcare professionals will examine people who are at risk for TB to look for signs of the disease in the body. They may also use tests such as sputum cultures or chest X-rays to confirm the diagnosis. Treatment: Active TB is treated.

translations.source: Wọ́n máa ń tọ́jú rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú gbèdéke oṣù 6 plus àwọn òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò 4. Lára àwọn òògùn tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ ni rifampicin àti isoniazid. Ní ti àwọn ọ̀ràn kan bakitéríà TB kì í gbọ́ iwodsnt láti ara àwọn òògùn tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ kan. Ní ti ọ̀ràn yìí, aláìsàn náà ni TB alòdìsí-òògùn. nítorí pé TB alòdìsí-òògùn máa ń pẹ́ ó sì ṣòro púpọ̀.
translations.reference: It is treated by standard 6 month course of 4 antibiotics. Common drugs include rifampicin and isoniazid. In some cases the TB bacteria does not respond to the standard drugs. In this case, the patient has drug-resistant TB. for drug-resistant TB is longer and more complex.
translations.hypothesis: The treatment typically involves a combination of six months of medication, plus four additional drugs. The most common drugs used are rifampicin and isoniazid. In some cases of tuberculosis, bacteria can become resistant to one or more of the standard drugs. In this situation, the patient is diagnosed with multi-drug resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB), which is more serious and requires a longer and more complex treatment regimen.

translations.source: Wọn yóò pèsè ìlò àwọn òògùn náà fún aláìsàn pẹ̀lú àlàyé, àmójútó àti àtìlẹ́yìn láti ọwọ́ òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera tàbí akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ afarajìn. Láìsí àtìlẹ́yìn yẹn, ìfarajìn fún ìtọ́jú lè ṣòro. Bí ìtọ́jú náà kò bá di ṣíṣe tan, àìsàn náà lè di alòdìsí-òògùn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè ràn ká. Ní ti ọ̀ràn àrùn TB (níbi tí aláìsàn ti ní àrùn TB ṣùgbọ́n tí kò ṣàìsàn), ìtọ́jú ìdènà TB lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe láti dáwọ́ ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ àrùn náà dúró. Ìtọ́jú yìí ń ṣàmúlò àwọn òògùn kan náà fún ìgbà díẹ̀.
translations.reference: The course of TB drugs is provided to the patient with information, supervision and support by a health worker or trained volunteer. Without such support, treatment adherence can be difficult. If the treatment is not properly completed, the disease can become drug-resistant and can spread. In the case of TB infection (where the patient is infected with TB bacteria but not ill), TB preventive treatment can be given to stop the onset of disease. This treatment uses the same drugs for a shorter time.
translations.hypothesis: The doctor will prescribe the necessary medications for the patient, providing a detailed explanation, instructions, and supporting information from a healthcare professional or pharmacist. Without this support, adherence to the medication regimen can be challenging. If the medication is not taken as prescribed, the condition may worsen, and complications can arise. In the case of tuberculosis (TB), for example, TB medication can be prescribed to stop the progression of the disease. This treatment typically involves a combination of medications taken for a specific period.

translations.source: Àwọn àṣàyàn ìtọ́jú lọ́ọ́lọ́ọ́ ti kéré àsìkò fún ìtọ́jú sí oṣù 1 tàbí 3, ní àfiwé oṣù 6 ní ìgbà kan.
translations.reference: Recent treatment options have shortened the duration to treatment to only 1 or 3 months, as compared to 6 months in the past.
translations.hypothesis: The current interest rates for loans are relatively low, ranging from 1 to 3 months, with a maximum of 6 months in some cases.

translations.source: Ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò Ìsọnísókí: Ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò ni ìwọ̀n gígùn ìgbì láti 100-400m, èyí tí ó jẹ́ ìpele ńlá bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó kéré ní ìwọ̀n gígùn ìgbì ju iná tí ó ṣe é rí lọ. Ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò máa ń wá láti ara òòrùn láìṣẹ̀sá, ṣùgbọ́n ó tún lè jẹ́ ṣíṣẹ̀dá láti ọwọ́ àwọn orísun àìtọọ́dá tí wọ́n ń lò ní ilé iṣẹ́, okòwò àti ìgbafẹ́. Agbègbè ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò ni ìwọ̀n gígùn ìgbì láti 100-400m bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó pín sí ẹ̀yà mẹ́ta: UVA (315-400 nm)UVB (280-315 nm)UVC (100-280 nm). Bí òòrùn bá ṣe ń kọjá nípasẹ̀ ojú sánmọ̀, gbogbo UVC àti bí 90% ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ UVB ni òsóònù, omi, ìkùukùu àti aléèédúléjì ọ́kísíjìn máa ń fà sínú.
translations.reference: Ultraviolet radiation Overview: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation covers the wavelength range of 100–400 nm, which is a higher frequency and lower wavelength than visible light. UV radiation comes naturally from the sun, but it can also be created by artificial sources used in industry, commerce and recreation. The UV region covers the wavelength range 100-400 nm and is divided into three bands: UVA (315-400 nm)UVB (280-315 nm)UVC (100-280 nm). As sunlight passes through the atmosphere, all UVC and approximately 90% of UVB radiation is absorbed by ozone, water vapour, oxygen and carbon dioxide.
translations.hypothesis: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation: Ultraviolet radiation is a form of electromagnetic radiation with wavelengths ranging from 100 to 400 nanometers. This range is shorter than visible light and is further divided into three types: UVA (315-400 nm), UVB (280-315 nm), and UVC (100-280 nm). When sunlight passes through the atmosphere, all UVC and about 90% of UVB are absorbed by the ozone layer, water vapor, and other atmospheric particles.

translations.source: Ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ UVA kí í rí ìnira púpọ̀ láti ara òfurufú. Nítorí náà, ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò tí ó ń kan Ilé ayé máa ń ní UVA pẹ̀lú ẹ̀yà UVB kékeré. Iye ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ UV láti ara òòrùn tí ó ń dé Ilé Ayé dá lórí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn nǹkan, tí ó fi mọ́ gíga òòrùn lójú sánmọ̀, ìsọrínù, ìbòjú òfuurufú, gíga, ìnípọn awẹ́ òsóònù àti ìtànṣán ilẹ̀. Àdínkù nínú awẹ́ òsóònù nítorí ìbàyíkájẹ́ àtọọ́dá ẹ̀dá máa ń mú ìdàgbàsókè bá iye UVA àti UVB tí ó ń dé ojú náà. Èyí lè nípa lórí ìlera ènìyàn, ẹranko, àwọn ẹ̀dá inú omi àti ìgbésí ayé ohun ọ̀gbìn.
translations.reference: UVA radiation is less affected by the atmosphere. Therefore, the UV radiation reaching the Earth’s surface is largely composed of UVA with a small UVB component. The amount of UV radiation from the sun that hits the Earth’s surface depends on several factors, including the sun’s height in the sky, latitude, cloud cover, altitude, the thickness of the ozone layer and ground reflection. Reductions in the ozone layer due to human-created pollution increase the amount of UVA and UVB that reaches the surface. This can impact human health, animals, marine organisms and plant life.
translations.hypothesis: UV radiation can have significant effects on the environment. Therefore, sunscreen products designed to protect the skin from the sun typically contain UVA filters along with UVB filters. The amount of UV radiation from the sun that reaches the Earth's surface is influenced by various factors, including the time of day, season, latitude, and cloud cover. In addition to the direct effects of UV radiation on human health, animals, aquatic life, and ecosystems, the depletion of the ozone layer due to increased levels of UVA and UVB radiation can also have negative consequences. This can impact human health, wildlife, marine life, and the environment.

translations.source: Láàrin àwọn ènìyàn, ìfaragbá UV púpọ̀ lè fa jẹjẹrẹ awọ ara, owó ojú àti bíbá ètò àjẹsára jẹ́. Ìdènà: Bí iye àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń ní jẹjẹrẹ awọ ara ṣe ń pọ̀ sí i láti bí ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn ní àṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú òòrùn lásìkò àwọn iṣẹ́ ìta gbangba àti àwọn orísun àtọọ́dá ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀ bí àtùpà atànbíòòrùn àti àwọn ibùsùn onífẹ̀ẹ́wẹ́lẹ́. Àpọ̀jù ìfaragbá tún ni okùnfà àwọn ipa burúkú lórí àwọn ojú àti ètò àjẹsára. Ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn ìsọ́ra kéékèèké díẹ̀ lè mú àdínkù bá ewu àwọn ìlera wọ̀nyí: Ẹ ṣe àdínkù àsìkò gbígba òòrùn ọ̀sán láti aago 10 àárọ̀ di 4 ìrọ̀lẹ́, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn ọjọ́ tí atọ́ka UV bá ti pọ̀ jù. Nígbà tí àwọn ìtànṣán UV bá lè jùlọ tí ẹ sì gbọ́dọ̀ wà níta, ẹ gbìyànjú láti wá ibòji kí ẹ sì wọ àwọn aṣọ aláàbò, tí ó fi mọ́ àkẹtẹ̀ tí yóò pèsè ààbò lọ́wọ́ òòrùn fún orí, ojú àti ọrùn yín.
translations.reference: In humans, increased UV exposure can cause skin cancers, cataracts and immune system damage. Prevention: The rise in the number of cases of skin cancer over the past decades is strongly related to increased exposure to the sun during outdoor activities and to artificial sources of UV radiation such as sunlamps and tanning beds. Overexposure is also the underlying cause of harmful effects on the eyes and immune system. Adopting a few simple precautions can greatly reduce the risk of these health conditions: Limit time in the midday sun from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m., particularly on days when the UV index is high When UV rays are most intense and you must be outside, try to find shade and wear protective clothing, including a brimmed hat that provides sun protection for your head, face and neck.
translations.hypothesis: Excessive exposure to UV radiation can lead to skin discoloration, eye damage, and an increased risk of skin cancer. Evidence: The number of people developing skin cancer has been increasing since the 1990s, likely due to increased outdoor activities and the use of tanning beds. Furthermore, UV exposure can have negative effects on the eyes and immune system. Reducing exposure to these risks can be achieved through simple measures: Limit sun exposure between 10 am and 4 pm, especially on days when the UV index is high. When you must be outside, wear protective clothing, including a hat, sunglasses, and sunscreen with a high SPF to protect your skin from the sun's harmful rays.

translations.source: Ẹ lo ìpara agbòòrùn ọlọ́pọ̀ ìṣe ti ó kéré jù 15 SPF – ní gíga tó– kí ẹ sì máa tún lò ní wákàtí méjì méjì. Ẹ yàgò fún àtùpà atànbíòòrùn àti ilé onífẹ̀ẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ ara, nítorí pé wọ́n máa ń bá awọ ara àti àwọn ojú jẹ́. Ìfarakínra àwọn ọmọdé pẹ̀lú òòrùn gbọ́dọ̀ dín kù dáadáa nítorí pé wọ́n wà nínú ipò àyídáyidà ìdàgbàsókè nítorí náà ni wọ́n fi wà nínú ewu àyíká ju àwọn àgbàlagbà lọ. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ojúṣe pàtàkì bí ètò àjẹsára kì í dàgbà tán lásìkò ìbí, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn àyíká tí wọn kò ní ààbò lè ṣe ìdíwọ́ fún ìdàgbàsókè wọn bí ó ṣe yẹ. Èsì WHO: WHO ń ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ àti àwọn olùbádòwòpọ̀ láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlóye àwùjọ nípa àwọn ipa àpọ̀jù ìfaragbá ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò.
translations.reference: Use a broad-spectrum sunscreen of at least 15 SPF – ideally higher– and reapply every 2 hours. Avoid sunlamps and tanning parlours, as they are known to damage the skin and eyes. Children’s UV exposure should be carefully limited because they are in a dynamic state of growth and therefore more susceptible to environmental threats than adults. Many vital functions such as the immune system are not fully developed at birth, and unsafe environments may interfere with their normal development. WHO Response: WHO works with Member States and partners to increase public understanding of the effects of overexposure to ultraviolet radiation.
translations.hypothesis: Use a broad-spectrum sunscreen with an SPF of at least 15, and reapply it every two hours. Protect your skin and eyes from the sun, as they are particularly vulnerable. Children are more susceptible to the harmful effects of the sun because their skin is more delicate. Many important developmental milestones occur during childhood, and sun exposure can negatively impact their development. According to the WHO: The World Health Organization works with countries and partners to promote the responsible use of sunbeds and to raise awareness of the risks of excessive sun exposure.

translations.source: Ètò INTERSUN jẹ́ àjọṣepọ̀ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé, United Nations Environment Programme, World Meteorological Organization, International Agency on Cancer Research àti International Commission on Non-Ionizing Radiation Protection. Ó ń ṣe àgbéga àti ìgbéléwọ̀n ìwádìí lórí àwọn ipa ìlera ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ èsì tí ó tọ́ nípasẹ̀ ìtọ́nisọ́nà, àbá àti pínpín ìròyìn ká. Lára àwọn àfojúsùn ètò naa ni pípèsè ìmọ̀ràn tí ó ṣe mú lò àti ìmọ̀ràn dídára lórí àwọn ipa ìfaragbá ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò lórí ìlera àti àyíká, láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti gbé ìgbésẹ̀ láti se àdínkù àwọn ewu ìlera ajẹmọ́-ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò àti láti pèsè ìtọ́nisọ́nà fún àwọn aláṣẹ orílẹ̀ èdè nípa àwọn ètò ìpolongo òòrùn. Láti ṣe èyí, ètò náà ṣe àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn alámọ̀já àti àwọn àjọ pàtàkì láti ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ àwọn iṣẹ́ ìwádìí pàtàkì, ṣe ìdámọ̀ àti sọ iye ewu ìlera láti ara ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò, ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn àsọtẹ́lẹ̀ tí wọ́n ṣe é gbáralé nípa àwọn àbájáde ìlera àti àyíká lórí àwọn àyípadà sí ìfaragbá ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò pẹ̀lú àdínkù òsóònù ojú ọ̀run, àti ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ọ̀nà tí wọ́n ṣe é mú lò láti mójútó àyípadà àwọn ipa ìlera ajẹmọ́ ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò fún ìgbà pípẹ́. Ètò náà ń pèsè àwọn irinṣẹ́ àti ìtọ́nisọ́nà tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú atọ́ka ìfẹ́wẹ́lẹ́ awẹ́tayọ-ẹsẹ̀-àlùkò, ìdáàbòbò lọ́wọ́ òòrùn fún àwọn ọmọdé, ibùsùn olóòrùn àtọọ́dá, ìgbafẹ́ àti ìlera iṣẹ́.
translations.reference: The INTERSUN Programme is a collaboration between WHO, the United Nations Environment Programme, the World Meteorological Organization, the International Agency on Cancer Research and the International Commission on Non-Ionizing Radiation Protection. It promotes and evaluates research on the health effects of UV radiation, and develops an appropriate response through guidelines, recommendations and information dissemination. The goals of the programme including providing practical and sound advice on the health and environmental impacts of UV exposure, to encourage countries to take action to reduce UV-induced health risks and to provide guidance to national authorities about sun awareness programmes. To this end, the programme collaborates with experts and specialist agencies to implement key research activities, identifies and quantifies health risks from UV radiation, develops reliable predictions of health and environmental consequences of changes in UV exposure with stratospheric ozone depletion, and develops practical ways of monitoring change in UV-induced health effects over time. The programme provides tools and guidelines related to the UV index, sun protection for children, artificial sunbeds, tourism and occupational health.
translations.hypothesis: The INTERSUN program is a collaboration between the World Health Organization, the United Nations Environment Programme, the World Meteorological Organization, the International Agency on Cancer Research, and the International Commission on Non-Ionizing Radiation Protection. It promotes and disseminates information on the health effects of solar ultraviolet (UV) radiation, and it develops strategies for prevention through education, awareness campaigns, and media outreach. Key objectives of the program include increasing public awareness of the risks associated with exposure to solar UV radiation, providing evidence-based recommendations to countries for developing policies to reduce the health burden of UV-related diseases, and providing guidance to policymakers on effective sun protection strategies. To achieve these goals, the program collaborates with experts and organizations to conduct research, assess the health risks associated with UV exposure, develop educational materials, and promote sun-safe behaviors. The program also provides information on sun protection measures, including sunscreen use, protective clothing, sunglasses, shade, and limiting sun exposure during peak hours.

translations.source: Iṣẹ́ ìlera Káríayé Ìsọnísókí: Iṣẹ́ ìlera káríayé (IIK) túmọ̀ sí pé gbogbo ènìyàn ní àǹfààní sí gbogbo agbègbè àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera pípéye tí wọ́n nílò, nígbà tàbí níbi tí wọ́n ti nílò wọn, láìsí ìṣòro owó. Ó pèsè fún gbogbo ìtẹ̀síwájú àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì, láti orí ìpolongo ìlera dórí ìdáàbòbò, ìtọ́jú, ìwòsàn àti ìrànwọ́ ìtọ́jú. Láti pèsè àdéhùn yìí, àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè nílò láti ní àwọn ètò ìlera alágbára, tí wọ́n kájúẹ̀ àti aláìṣojúsàjú tí wọ́n ta egbò nínú àwọn àwùjọ tí wọ́n ń pèsè fún. Ìtọ́jú ìlera abẹ́lé (IIA) ni ọ̀nà tí ó kójúwọ̀n jùlọ tí kò sì gunpá láti dé ibẹ̀.
translations.reference: Universal health coverage Overview: Universal health coverage (UHC) means that all people have access to the full range of quality health services they need, when and where they need them, without financial hardship. It covers the full continuum of essential health services, from health promotion to prevention, treatment, rehabilitation and palliative care. To deliver on this promise, countries need to have strong, efficient and equitable health systems that are rooted in the communities they serve. Primary health care (PHC) is the most effective and cost-efficient way to get there.
translations.hypothesis: Universal Health Coverage: Universal health coverage (UHC) means that all people have access to the full range of quality health services they need, when and where they need them, without financial hardship. It includes a comprehensive set of essential health services, from primary care to specialized treatments, diagnostics, and rehabilitation. To achieve this, countries need to have strong health systems that are efficient, equitable, and that prioritize the needs of the populations they serve. Primary health care (PHC) is the most effective and efficient way to achieve UHC.

translations.source: Orílẹ̀ èdè kọ̀ọ̀kan ní ọ̀nà tí ó yàtọ̀ láti ṣe àṣeyọrí IIK àti láti pinnu ohun tí wọn yóò bò dá lórí àwọn àwọn àìní àwọn ènìyàn àti àwọn ohun èlò tí ó wà ní àrọ́wọ́tó. Ìdókòwò nínú IIK ń rí i dájú pé àwọn àìsàn wọ̀nyẹn ń jẹ́ ìdámọ̀, ní pàtàkì àti ṣíṣe àmójútó ní ọ̀nà àṣepọ̀; pé ikọ̀ òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera àti ìtọ́jú tí ó tóbi tí ó sì ní irinṣẹ́ wà; àti pé gbogbo ẹ̀ka àwùjọ ń kópa lórí kíkojú àwọn ìṣòro àyíká àti àwùjọ ọrọ̀ ajé tí wọ́n ń nípa lórí ìlera àti àlàáfíà, tí ó fi mọ́ gbígbáradì fún, ìfèsì sí àti ìwòsàn láti ara àwọn pàjáwìrì. Èsì Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: Gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìpìlẹ̀ fún IIK, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé dábàá ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ètò ìlera nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò ìlànà ìtọ́jú ìlera abẹ́lé (IIA). Ṣíṣe àṣeyọrí IIK ni ó jẹ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé lógún jùlọ, pẹ̀lú àfojúsùn àwọn ènìyàn bílíọ́nù 1 sí i láti jẹ àǹfààní iṣẹ́ ìlera àgbáyé ní 2025. Ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí pẹ̀lú ètò ìlera tí kò lágbára, a ń fojúsun ìrànlọ́wọ́ ìmọ̀ láti kọ́ àwọn ibùdó orílẹ̀ èdè àti ìpèsè iṣẹ́ láti dí àwọn àlàfo pàtàkì ní àsìkò àwọn pàjáwìrì.
translations.reference: Every country has a different path to achieving UHC and to decide what to cover based on the needs of their populations and the resources at hand. Investing in PHC ensures that all those needs are identified, prioritized and addressed in an integrated way; that there is a robust and equipped health and care workforce; and that all sectors of society contribute to confronting the environmental and socio-economic factors that affect health and well-being, including preparing for, responding to and recovering from emergencies. WHO response: As a foundation for UHC, WHO recommends reorienting health systems using a primary health care (PHC) approach. Achieving UHC is a WHO strategic priority, with the goal of 1 billion more people benefitting from universal health coverage by 2025. In countries with fragile health systems, we focus on technical assistance to build national institutions and service delivery to fill critical gaps in emergencies.
translations.hypothesis: Each country has its own unique approach to achieving Universal Health Coverage (UHC) and determining what services should be included for its population and workforce. A successful UHC system is characterized by financial risk protection, access to a comprehensive range of quality health services, and the active participation of all stakeholders in addressing health challenges and promoting health and well-being. World Health Organization: As a key driver of UHC, the World Health Organization promotes the development of national health policies by providing technical guidance on health system strengthening. Achieving UHC is a top priority for the World Health Organization, with the goal of ensuring that 1 billion more people have access to essential health services by 2025. In countries with weak health systems, the focus is on providing technical assistance to build capacity and address key challenges in the context of emergencies.

translations.source: Nínú àwọn ètò ìlera tí wọ́n tóbi, a ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ipa ìlera àwùjọ sí ìpèsè ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn nípasẹ̀ ìlànà ìtàkùrọ̀sọ fún àwọn ètò ọjọ́ iwájú àti àtìlẹ́yìn ọlọ́gbọ́n láti mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá iṣẹ́. Ìtọ́nisọ́nà àṣà àti àwọn àdéhùn, ìwífún-àlàyé, ìwádìí àti ìṣẹ̀dá ohun ọ̀tun; àti ìdarí níbi àwọn ìjíròrò, àgbàwí, ìdọ́gba takọtabo, ìlera àìṣojúsàjú àti àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn, ìṣe ọlọ́pọ̀ agbọn, àti ìṣúná ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún iṣẹ́ yìí. Àlàkalẹ̀ 2030 fún àwọn Àfojúsùn Ìdàgbàsókè Kánrinkése iṣẹ́ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ṣiṣẹ́ pọ̀ pẹ̀lú ìpinnu Àfojúsùn Ìdàgbàsókè Kánrinkése (AIK) 3.8, èyí tí ó fojúsun ṣíṣe àṣeyọrí iṣẹ́ ìlera káríayé, tí ó fi mọ́ ewu ààbò, àǹfààní sí àwọn iṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera pàtàkì tí wọ́n péye àti àǹfààní sí àwọn òògùn àti abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára tí wọ́n ní ààbò, tí wọ́n múnádóko, tí wọ́n péye tí wọn kò sì gunpá fún gbogbo ènìyàn. Káríayé, àṣeyọrí nípa iṣẹ́ ìlera káríayé jẹ́ ìṣòro. Ìtẹ̀síwájú nínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ti há nígbà tí iye àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń kojú ìnáwó ńlá àìlera ń pọ̀ sí i sá.
translations.reference: In more robust health system settings, we drive public health impact towards health coverage for all through policy dialogue for the systems of the future and strategic support to improve performance. This work is supported by normative guidance and agreements; data, research and innovation; and leadership in the realms of diplomacy, advocacy, gender equality, health equity and human rights, multisectoral action, and finance. 2030 Agenda for SDGs WHO’s work is aligned with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) target 3.8, which focuses on achieving universal health coverage, including financial risk protection, access to quality essential health-care services and access to safe, effective, quality and affordable essential medicines and vaccines for all. Globally, progress towards universal health coverage is a challenge. Progress in service coverage has stalled while the proportion of the population facing catastrophic out-of-pocket health spending increases continuously.
translations.hypothesis: Within large-scale health programs, the impact of social determinants of health is addressed by tailoring interventions to the specific needs of individuals through targeted outreach, community engagement, and evidence-based strategies. Factors such as culture and beliefs, education, income and employment, housing, access to healthcare, transportation, and social support systems all contribute to this work. The 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, specifically Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 3.8, focuses on achieving universal health coverage, which includes access to essential health services and quality, safe, effective, affordable, and accessible medicines and vaccines for all. Globally, achieving universal health coverage remains a significant challenge. Progress in improving health outcomes has been hampered by the increasing burden of non-communicable diseases.

translations.source: Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ń mójútó ìdàgbàsókè bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n ń ṣe àtẹ̀jáde àwọn ìròyìn káríayé láti ṣàfihàn ẹ̀rí tuntun ní ọdún méjì méjì. Ẹ wò àwọn ìròyìn náà níhìn-ín kí ẹ sì ṣe àbẹ̀wò sí ìwé òtítọ́ IIK fún àlàyé sí i.
translations.reference: WHO monitors progress and publishes global reports to reveal the latest evidence every two years. See the reports here and visit the UHC fact sheet for more information.
translations.hypothesis: The World Health Organization is closely monitoring reports and is investigating potential new cases of the disease that have emerged in the past two years. Please review the reports and refer to the IIK document for more information.

translations.source: Ìlera ìlú ńlá Ìsọnísókí: Ìsọ̀gbẹ́dìgboro jẹ́ ọ̀kan nínú àwọn asíwájú ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àgbáyé ní ọ̀rúndún 21 tí ó ní ipa ńlá lórí ìlera. Ó lé ní ìdá 55% àwọn ènìyàn àgbáyé ni wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn agbègbè ìgboro – iye tí ìrètí wà pé yóò tó 68% ní 2050. Gẹ́gẹ́ bí púpọ̀ ọjọ́ iwájú ìdàgbàsókè ìgboro yóò ṣe wáyé ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n ń gòkè àgbà, ilé ayé lónìí ní àǹfààní tí ó yàtọ̀ láti ṣe ìtọ́nisọ́nà ìsọ̀gbẹ́dìgboro àti àwọn ìdàgbàsókè ìgboro abágbàmu mìíràn gbòógì ní ọ̀nà tí yóò dáàbò bo àti ṣe ìgbéga ìlera. Èyí ṣe pàtàkì, kì í ṣe nítorí pé ìlera àti àlàáfíà àwọn ará ìlú jẹ́ ọrọ̀ ikut tí ó ṣe pàtàkì jùlọ.
translations.reference: Urban health Overview: Urbanization is one of the leading global trends of the 21st century that has a significant impact on health. Over 55% of the world’s population live in urban areas – a proportion that is expected to increase to 68% by 2050. As most future urban growth will take place in developing countries, the world today has a unique opportunity to guide urbanization and other major urban development trends in a way that protects and promotes health. This is important, not least because the health and well-being of citizens is perhaps a city’s most important asset.
translations.hypothesis: Urban Health Crisis: Urbanization is one of the defining global trends of the 21st century, with a significant impact on health. Currently, 55% of the world's population lives in urban areas – a figure projected to reach 68% by 2050. As urbanization continues, particularly in aging countries, it is crucial to develop innovative strategies for urban planning and public health interventions that address the unique challenges and opportunities presented by urban environments. This is particularly important because the health and well-being of urban populations are a critical priority.

translations.source: Àmọ́ sá, púpọ̀ nínú àwọn ènìyàn bílíọ́nù 4.2 tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn ìlú ńlá ń kojú ilégbèé àti ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ tí kò tó, àìsí ìmọ́tótó àti àkóso ìdọ̀tí, àti àbùdá afẹ́fẹ́ tí ó kùnà àwọn àlàkalẹ̀ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé. Àwọn ẹ̀yà ìbàyíkájẹ́ mìíràn, bí ariwo, bíba omi àti ilẹ̀ jẹ́, àti èyí tí wọ́n ń pè ní àwọn erékùṣù gbígbóná ìgboro, àti àìsí ààyè fún ìrìn, kẹ̀kẹ́ gígùn àti ìgbé ayé iṣẹ́ túbọ̀ ń parapọ̀ láti mú kí àwọn ìlú ńlá jẹ́ ibùdó ìfọ́nkálé àìsàn tí kì í ràn àti àwọn awakọ̀ àyípadà ojú ọjọ́. Àká Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé lórí Ìlera Ìlú ńlá Ipa: Bí 40% ìdàgbàsókè ìgboro wà nínú àwọn ibi èérí tí wọn kò ní omi àti ìmọ́tótó, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni 91% awomt ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ní agbègbè ìgboro ń mí afẹ́fẹ́ burúkú. Nígbà ti ó bá di ọ̀rọ̀ oúnjẹ tí ó dára, ìsọ̀gbẹ́dìgboro máa ń mú ìgbéga bá kí ọ̀nà oko jìn fún jíjẹ, èyí tí ó ń mú ìgbéga bá àwọn oúnjẹ tí kò dára tí wọ́n ti tún ṣe.
translations.reference: However, most of the 4.2 billion people living in cities suffer inadequate housing and transport, poor sanitation and waste management, and air quality that fails WHO guidelines. Other forms of pollution, such as noise, water and soil contamination, so-called urban heat islands, and a lack of space for walking, cycling and active living further combine to make cities epicentres of a noncommunicable disease epidemic and drivers of climate change. WHO Repository on Urban Health Impact: Around 40% of urban growth is in slums that lack safe water and sanitation, and 91% of people in urban areas breathe polluted air. When it comes to healthy diets, urbanization increases the distance from farm to fork, driving demand for unhealthy, processed foods.
translations.hypothesis: Globally, an estimated 4.2 billion people live in urban areas, facing challenges such as inadequate housing, limited access to transportation, poor sanitation, and increased exposure to air pollution, all of which are major concerns for the World Health Organization. Other factors, including noise, water scarcity, and land degradation, as well as the prevalence of sedentary lifestyles, obesity, and chronic diseases, contribute to making large cities centers of preventable illness and premature mortality. The World Health Organization's Urban Health Initiative: Approximately 40% of urban populations live in areas with inadequate water and sanitation, and 91% of people living in urban areas are exposed to air pollution. While access to nutritious food is important, urbanization often leads to increased consumption of processed foods and unhealthy diets.

translations.source: Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ìlú ńlá tún wà nínú ewu àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ nítorí gbígbé ara lé àwọn epo rọ̀bì fún ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀, iná dídá àti ìmúlémóoru. Àwọn ìlú ńlá ni wọ́n ń lo ìdá méjì nínú mẹ́ta àwọn agbára àti yíyọ 60% èéfín àwọn gáàsì, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn torí ilẹ̀ lè ní ìrírí ìmóoru 3-5° C púpọ̀ ju àwọn àyíká agbègbè ìgbèríko nítorí ìgbòòrò kànnkéré àti àwọn ààyè koríko tí kò tó. Gbogbo èyí ni ó fi àwọn olùgbé ìlú ńlá sínú ewu ìlera. Púpọ̀ àwọn okùnfà ikú 10 ni wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìsọ̀gbẹ́dìgboro kankan àti aláìlétò, àti ìhun àti ètò ìgboro tí kò dára. Àwọn ìlú ńlá ń kojú ìlọ́po mẹ́ta àjàgà àwọn àrùn bí KASA/Ìsọdọ̀lẹ Àjẹsára, ikọ́ọfe, òtútù àyà, ibà oriìke àti ìgbẹ́ gbuuru;
translations.reference: Urban dwellers are also highly vulnerable to the effects of climate change because of their dependence on fossil fuels for transport, cooking and heating. Cities account for over two thirds of the world’s energy and emit 60% of greenhouse gases, and those inland may experience temperatures 3–5º C higher than surrounding rural areas because of their large expanses of concrete and limited open green spaces. All of this puts urban dwellers’ health at risk. Most the top 10 causes of death are closely related to rapid and unplanned urbanization, and poor urban design and planning. Cities face the triple health burden of infectious diseases like HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis, pneumonia, dengue and diarrhoea;
translations.hypothesis: People living in large cities are also at risk of heatstroke due to the prevalence of dark surfaces for transportation, industry, and construction. Large cities absorb two-thirds of the sunlight and release 60% of the heat as gas, which can lead to temperatures that are 3-5°C higher than in surrounding rural areas due to the lack of vegetation and water bodies. This puts city dwellers at increased risk of health problems. Many deaths are linked to social isolation and lack of access to healthcare, as well as poor urban planning and infrastructure. Large cities also contribute to three major environmental problems: air pollution, water pollution, and noise pollution.

translations.source: àwọn àìsàn tí kì í ràn bí àìsàn ọkàn, ẹ̀gbà, àìsàn sẹ́mìísẹ́mìí, jẹjẹrẹ, àtọ̀gbẹ àti ìbànújẹ́ ọkàn; àti ìwà ipá àti ìfarapa, tí ó fi mọ́ ìfarapa ojht pópó. Èsì Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: Pẹ̀lú gbogbo ìdojúkọ wọn, awomt ìlú ńlá lè ṣẹ̀dá àwọn àǹfààní fún ìlera dídára, àyíká mímọ́ àti iṣẹ́ ojú ọjọ́. Àwọn ìlànà ìlú ńlá tí wọ́n lágbára gbọ́dọ̀ pàtàkì ìlera, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ó ṣe ṣe pàtàkì fún ìdàgbàsókè igbe ayé dídára ní ìgbòòrò, ṣíṣe ẹ̀dá àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ tí wọ́n lè ṣiṣẹ́ dáadáa, ṣíṣe ẹ̀dá àwọn àwùjọ alágbára tí wọ́n sì dára, fífi ayé gba ìyanká, ìgbéga ìbánisọ̀rọ̀ ní àwùjọ àti ìdáàbòbò bo àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu. Ìlànà Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé fún ìlera ìlú ńlá fojúsun afẹ́fẹ́ tí ó dára, omi àti ìmọ́tótó, àti àwọn atọ́ka àyíká mìíràn; ìṣètò ìlú ńlá tí ó ní ìlera; àwọn àyíká tí wọ́n ní ìlera tí wọn kò sì ní èéfín; ìyanká tí ó ní ààbò àti ìlera; ìdènà ìwà ipá àti àwọn ìfarapa; àgbéga ètò oúnjẹ àti oúnjẹ tí ó ní ètò; àkóso àyíká àwọn àìsàn tí ajọ̀fẹ́ ń fà; àti ìgbáradì ìgboro fún àwọn pàjáwìrì.
translations.reference: noncommunicable diseases like heart disease, stroke, asthma, cancer, diabetes and depression; and violence and injuries, including road traffic injuries. WHO response: Despite their challenges, cities can create opportunities for better health, cleaner environments and climate action. Strong urban policies must prioritize health, as it is essential for fostering good urban livelihoods, building a productive workforce, creating resilient and vibrant communities, enabling mobility, promoting social interaction and protecting vulnerable populations. WHO’s approach to urban health focuses on better air quality, water and sanitation, and other environmental determinants; healthy urban planning; healthier and smoke-free environments; safe and healthy mobility; preventing violence and injuries; promoting healthy food systems and diets; environmental management of vector-borne diseases; and urban preparedness for emergencies.
translations.hypothesis: Non-communicable diseases, such as heart disease, stroke, cancer, diabetes, chronic respiratory diseases, and mental health disorders, are a major public health concern. They are also a leading cause of death and disability worldwide. The World Health Organization: With the right policies, large cities can create environments that promote health, well-being, and economic productivity. Urban policies should prioritize health, promoting healthy lifestyles, creating jobs, building strong communities, reducing inequalities, fostering social connections, and protecting vulnerable populations. The World Health Organization's urban health framework focuses on air quality, water and sanitation, and other environmental factors; healthy urban planning; health promotion and disease prevention; access to health services; mental health and well-being; healthy food environments and nutrition; control of infectious diseases; and emergency preparedness.

translations.source: Ṣíṣe iset káàkiri àwọn agbọn àti rírí i dájú pé àwọn ìlànà báramu káàkiri àwọn agbègbè jẹ́ pàtàkì fún ṣíṣẹ̀dá àtìlẹ́yìn àti àwọn àyíká tí ó dára fún ìlera, èyí tí yóò rí i dájú pé ìpinnu ìlera àti ìdọ́gba jẹ́ ṣíṣe àmúlò káàkiri ìgbésẹ̀ ètò, ìdókòwò àti àwọn ìpinnu ìlànà ní ìpele abẹ́lé. Láti ran àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ lọ́wọ́ láti kojú àwọn kókó pàtàkì òkè yìí, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ń ṣiṣẹ́ láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ibùdó ẹ̀rí láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn abẹ́núgan láti ṣe àwọn ìpinnu tí wọ́n dára nígbà tí wọ́n bá ń kojú ewu ìlera. Ó ń pèsè àwọn irinṣẹ́ àti ìtọ́ni lórí ohun tí ó ṣiṣẹ́, àti àtìlẹ́yìn àkóso àwọn atọ́ka kókó ajẹmọ́ ìlera. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ń síwájú bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n ń ṣe iṣẹ́ láti ṣe àwọn ìpààrọ̀ ìlú-sí-ìlú kí wọ́n sì ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìhun àti ilé iṣẹ́ àti ìlànà fún ìdarí ìlú ńlá dáadáa fún ìlera àti àlàáfíà àwọn ìlú ńlá.
translations.reference: Working across sectors and ensuring the coherence of policies across different areas is key to creating supportive and enabling environments for health, which ensures that health and equity considerations are integrated throughout the planning process, investments and policy decisions at the local level. To help Member States address the above priorities, WHO works to strengthen the evidence base to help policy-makers make informed decisions when addressing health risks. It provides tools and guidance on what works, and supports monitoring of key health-related indicators. WHO leads and engages in fostering city-to-city exchanges and helps develop institutional and policy frameworks for good urban governance for health and well-being in cities.
translations.hypothesis: It is crucial to understand the impact of environmental factors and the importance of public health policies in promoting health and well-being. This highlights the need for integrated health and social policies that address the root causes of health problems at the local, national, and global levels. To support member states in addressing these critical issues, the World Health Organization is working to strengthen health systems and provide guidance to individuals to make informed decisions about their health. It also promotes research and innovation in public health, and supports the development of evidence-based policies. The World Health Organization is committed to working with partners to address health challenges and to promote healthy cities and communities that are conducive to the health and well-being of their residents.

translations.source: Ìwà ipá sí àwọn Ọmọdé Ìsọnísókí: Lára àwọn ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé ni gbogbo ẹ̀yà ìwà ipá sí àwọn ènìyàn lábẹ́ ọdún 18. Fún àwọn ìkókó àti ọmọdé, ìwà ipá ní í ṣe fífi ìyà jẹ ọmọdé (ìyẹn ìyà ara, ìdàpọ̀ àti ìmọ̀lára àti àìbìkítà) ní ọwọ́ àwọn òbí àti àwọn aláṣẹ mìíràn. Àwọn ọmọkùnrin àti ọmọbìnrin wà nínú ewu adọ́gba ìdíyàjẹ ojúkojú àti ìmọ̀lára àti àìbìkítà, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ọmọbìnrin wà nínú ewu ìdíyàjẹ nípa ìbálòpọ̀. Bí àwọn ọmọdé ṣe ń di àgùnbánirọ̀, ìwà ipá àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àti ìwà ipá alábàárìn, ní àfikún sí ìdíyàjẹ ọmọdé, pọ̀ sí i.
translations.reference: Violence against children Overview: Violence against children includes all forms of violence against people under 18 years old. For infants and younger children, violence mainly involves child maltreatment (i.e. physical, sexual and emotional abuse and neglect) at the hands of parents and other authority figures. Boys and girls are at equal risk of physical and emotional abuse and neglect, and girls are at greater risk of sexual abuse. As children reach adolescence, peer violence and intimate partner violence, in addition to child maltreatment, become highly prevalent.
translations.hypothesis: Child Abuse: Child abuse encompasses all forms of harm to individuals under the age of 18. For infants and children, abuse often involves physical harm (e.g., hitting, shaking, and burning) inflicted by parents or caregivers. Boys and girls are at risk of physical, emotional, and neglectful abuse, while girls are also at risk of sexual abuse. As children grow older, peer abuse and bullying, in addition to child abuse, become more prevalent.

translations.source: Ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé lè ṣe é dènà. Ìdènà àti ìfèsì sí ìwà ipá tako àwọn ọmọdé pè fún kí àwọn akitiyan jẹ́ gbígbé kalẹ̀ láti kojú àwọn ewu àti àwọn okùnfà ààbò ní gbogbo àwọn ìpele ewu mẹ́rin tí wọ́n farakọ́ra (ènìyàn, ìbáṣepọ̀, ìlú, àwùjọ). Oṣù Karùn-ún 2016 ọ̀nà àbáyọ Àjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé fọwọ́ sí iṣẹ́ ètò káríayé Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé àkọ́kọ́ irúu rẹ̀ lórí ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ojúṣe ètò ìlera láàrin ìfèsì ọlọ́pọ̀ ẹ̀ka orílẹ̀ èdè láti kojú ìwà ipá ibáraẹniṣepọ̀, pàápàá jùlọ sí àwọn obìnrin àti ọmọbìnrin, àti sí àwọn ọmọdé. A Gẹ́gẹ́ bí ètò yìí, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ àti olùbádòwòpọ̀ mìíràn, farajìn sí: Ṣíṣe àmójútó títóbi àti àwọn àbùdá ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé káríayé àti ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn akitiyan orílẹ̀ èdè láti ṣe àkọsílẹ̀ àti òdiwọ̀n irúfẹ́ ìwà ipá bẹ́ẹ̀. Ṣíṣe àkóso àkóso àlàyé ètò orí ẹ̀rọ tí ó ṣe ìsọnísókí àwọn ìwífun-àlàyé lórí àjàgà náà, àwọn okùnfà ewu àti àbájáde ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé, àti ẹ̀rí fún ìdènà rẹ̀.
translations.reference: Violence against children can be prevented. Preventing and responding to violence against children requires that efforts systematically address risk and protective factors at all four interrelated levels of risk (individual, relationship, community, society). May 2016 World Health Assembly resolution endorsed the first ever WHO Global plan of action on strengthening the role of the health system within a national multisectoral response to address interpersonal violence, in particular against women and girls, and against children. A According to this plan, WHO in collaboration with Member States and other partners, is committed to: Monitoring the global magnitude and characteristics of violence against children and supporting country efforts to document and measure such violence. Maintaining an electronic information system that summarizes the scientific data on the burden, risk factors and consequences of violence against children, and the evidence for its preventability.
translations.hypothesis: Violence against children is a serious problem. Awareness and prevention of violence are crucial to ensuring the safety and well-being of children at all levels of society (individual, family, community). In June 2016, the World Health Organization launched a global initiative, the first of its kind, to promote and support national action plans for violence prevention across multiple sectors, particularly focusing on intimate partner violence, violence against women and girls, and violence against children. As part of this initiative, the World Health Organization is working with member states and other partners to: Raise awareness of the prevalence and consequences of violence against children globally and to support national efforts to prevent and respond to this form of violence. Develop and implement evidence-based interventions to address the root causes of violence against children, and to promote positive parenting practices. Establish a global online platform to share information and resources on violence prevention, including best practices, data, and tools.

translations.source: Ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àti àpínká àwọn ìwé ìmọ̀ ìtọ́nisọ́nà adálórí-ẹ̀rí, àṣà àti òṣùwọ̀n fún ìdènà àti ìfèsì sí ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé. Ṣíṣe àtẹ̀jáde ìròyìn ipò káríayé lórí akitiyan orílẹ̀ èdè láti kojú ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé nípasẹ̀ àwọn ìlànà orílẹ̀ èdè àti ètò iṣẹ́, òfin, àwọn ètò ìdènà àti àwọn iṣẹ́ ìfèsì. Ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè àti àwọn olùbádòwòpọ̀ nínú ṣíṣe àmúlò ìdènà adálórí-ẹ̀rí àti àwọn ọgbọ́n ìfèsì, bí àwọn tí wọ́n wà nínú INSPIRE: Ọgbọ́n méje fún fífi òpin sí ìwà ipá tako àwọn ọmọdé. Ṣíṣe àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn àjọ àti ilé-iṣẹ́ àgbáyé láti ṣe àdínkù àti ìfòpinsí ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé káàkiri àgbáyé, nípasẹ̀ àwọn ètò bí Àjọṣepọ̀ Káríayé lati fi òpin sí Ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé. àjọṣepọ̀ Káríayé lati fi òpin sí Ìwà ipá sí àwọn ọmọdé.
translations.reference: Developing and disseminating evidence-based technical guidance documents, norms and standards for preventing and responding to violence against children. Regularly publishing global status reports on country efforts to address violence against children through national policies and action plans, laws, prevention programmes and response services. Supporting countries and partners in implementing evidence-based prevention and response strategies, such as those included in INSPIRE: Seven strategies for ending violence against children. Collaborating with international agencies and organizations to reduce and eliminate violence against children globally, through initiatives such as the Global Partnership to End Violence against Children, the Global Partnership to End Violence against Children,
translations.hypothesis:
Promote and disseminate educational, cultural, and ethical resources for fostering character development and promoting positive values in children.
Develop and share best practices for national strategies to promote character development in children through national policies, programs, laws, educational initiatives, and social services.
Provide support to countries and partners in developing and implementing character education and positive value programs, such as the "Seven Pillars of Character" program for children.
Collaborate with organizations and institutions worldwide to enhance and expand character development initiatives for children globally, through programs like the Global Initiative to Promote Character in Children.

translations.source: Wíwà papọ̀ fún àwọn Ọmọbìnrin àti Ìdènà Ìwà ipá parapọ̀. Ìlòkulò ọmọdé Ìlòkulò ọmọdé ni ìlòkulò àti àìbìkítà tí ó ṣẹlẹ̀ sí ọmọdé lábẹ́ ọdún 18. Ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú gbogbo àwọn oríṣìí ìwà ojúkojú àti/tàbí ìlòkulò ìmọ̀lára, ìlòkulò ajẹmọ́-ìbálòpọ̀, àìbìkítà, ìdẹ́yẹsí àti okòwò tàbí ìyànjẹ mìíràn, èyí tí ó yọrí sí ìpalára tàbí ìpalára ọjọ́ iwájú fún ìlera, ìyè, ìdàgbàsókè tàbí iyì ọmọ náà nínú ọ̀gangan ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ojúṣe ìbáraẹniṣpọ̀, ìfọkàntán tàbí agbára. Ìfaragbá ìwà ipá ọ̀rẹ́ alábàárìn tún ṣe é kì ó wà lára ẹ̀yà ìlòkulò ọmọdé.
translations.reference: Together for Girls and the Violence Prevention Alliance. Child maltreatment Child maltreatment is the abuse and neglect that occurs to children under 18 years of age. It includes all types of physical and/or emotional ill-treatment, sexual abuse, neglect, negligence and commercial or other exploitation, which results in actual or potential harm to the child’s health, survival, development or dignity in the context of a relationship of responsibility, trust or power. Exposure to intimate partner violence is also sometimes included as a form of child maltreatment.
translations.hypothesis: Comprehensive Assessment for Girls and Boys with Behavioral Problems. Child assessment is the evaluation and analysis of a child's development and behavior. It addresses all areas of concern, including emotional and behavioral issues, learning difficulties, social skills, communication, and any other challenges that may impact the child's well-being, health, safety, or success in school, family, or community settings. A thorough assessment of the child's social and emotional functioning is also an important part of the child assessment process.

translations.source: Ìlòkulò ọmọdé ní àwọn àtúbọ̀tán ńlá yàjóyàjó àti ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́. Ju ikú, ìfarapa àti ààbọ̀ ara lọ, ìwà ipá lè yọrí sí wàhálà tí yóò nípa lórí ìdàgbàsókè ọpọlọ àti ìbàjẹ́ àwọn ètò ojúṣe ara àti àjẹsára. Èyí náà tún ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìdádúró ìdàgbàsókè ìlóye, ìṣe ilé ẹ̀kọ́ tí kò dára àti ààbọ̀ ẹ̀kọ́, àwọn ìṣòro ìlera ìròrí, ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀míaraẹni, àlékún àwọn ìwà tí wọ́n léwu fún ìlera, àṣetúnṣe ìbàjẹ́ ọkàn àti ṣíṣe ìwà ipá. Iroyin ayọ̀ ibẹ̀ ni pé lílo ọmọdé ní ìlòkulò lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ àwọn ìdásí tí wọ́n ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn òbí àti olùtọ́jú, ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn èrè àti ààbò ọrọ̀ ajé àwọn ẹbí, pèsè èsì dídára àti àwọn iṣẹ́ àtìlẹ́yìn, ṣẹ̀dá àti ṣàkóso àwọn àyíká aláàbò fún awomwt ọmọdé, atit ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àti àmúlò àwọn òfin tako ìlòkulò ọmọdé. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé, ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn olùbádòwòpọ̀:
