translations.reference: Child maltreatment has enormous immediate and long-term repercussions. Beyond death, physical injury and disability, violence can lead to stress that impairs brain development and damages the nervous and immune systems. This in turn is associated with delayed cognitive development, poor school performance and dropout, mental health problems, suicide attempts, increased health-risk behaviours, revictimization and the perpetration of violence. The good news is that child maltreatment can be prevented through interventions that support parents and caregivers, promote non-violent norms and values, provide education and life skills training, strengthen families’ income and economic security, offer high quality response and support services, create and sustain safe environments for children, and implement and enforce laws against child maltreatment. WHO, in collaboration with a number of partners:
translations.hypothesis: Child labor, characterized by long hours and hazardous conditions, is a serious problem. Beyond the physical dangers of injury, illness, and exploitation, it can lead to significant social and psychological harm. This includes the disruption of education, poor academic performance, increased vulnerability to exploitation, mental health issues, the development of unhealthy behaviors, and long-term psychological trauma. The good news is that child labor can be addressed through a variety of strategies that support families and communities, promote economic stability, provide access to education and healthcare, combat harmful social norms, and offer mental health services and support for victims of child labor. These strategies often involve: providing support for parents and caregivers, addressing the root causes of poverty and economic hardship, providing access to quality education and healthcare, tackling harmful social norms, and offering mental health services and support for victims of child labor. The World Health Organization, in collaboration with various partners:

translations.source: pèsè ìtọ́nisọ́nà ìmọ̀ àti àṣà fún ìdènà ìlòkulò ọmọdé adálórí-ẹ̀rí; ṣíṣe agbẹnusọ fún ìlọsíwájú àtìlẹ́yìn káríayé fún ati ìdókòwò nínú ìdènà ìlòkulò ọmọdé adálórí-ẹ̀rí.; pípèsè àtìlẹ́yìn ìmọ̀ fún àwọn ètò ìdènà ìlòkulò ọmọdé adálórí-ẹ̀rí ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè. Ìwà ipá ọ̀dọ́ Ìwà ipá ọ̀dọ́ jẹ́ ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ káríayé.
translations.reference: provides technical and normative guidance for evidence-based child maltreatment prevention; advocates for increased international support for and investment in evidence-based child maltreatment prevention; provides technical support for evidence-based child maltreatment prevention programmes in several low- and middle-income countries. Youth violence Youth violence is a global public health problem.
translations.hypothesis: Promoting science and cultural education for children with special needs; fostering social inclusion and well-being within special education programs; and providing scientific and cultural resources for special education programs in diverse and underserved communities. Child development is a crucial aspect of overall social well-being.

translations.source: Ó ní àwọn onírúurú ìṣe láti orí ìpániláyà àti ìjà ojúkojú, dórí àwọn ìkọlù ìbálòpọ̀ àti ojúkojú dórí ìpànìyàn. Ìwà ipá àwọn ọ̀dọ́ ń mú kí iyeó ìlera, ìtọ́jú àti ìdájọ́ ọ̀daràn pọ̀ sí i; ń ṣe àdínkù iṣẹ́ ṣíṣe; bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ń ba ètò àwùjọ jẹ́. Yàtọ̀ sí àwọn ikú, ìfarapa àti ìpalára ọkàn, ìwà ipá ọ̀dọ́ lè yọrí sí kí àwọn ìwà tó léwu fún ìlera pọ̀ sí i bí fífa nǹkan, ìlòkulò òògùn olóró, ìbálòpọ̀ aláìláàbò, àti ìwà ipá síwájú sí i. Ìwọ̀nyí bákan náà ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn àìsàn òpónà ìmí líle, jẹjẹrẹ, àwọn àìsàn ọkàn, àtọ̀gbẹ, oyún oge àti awont àìsàn ìbálòpọ̀. Lára àwọn ìdènà tí ó tó àti àwọn ọgbọ́n èsì ni àwọn tí wọ́n ń ṣe àgbéga jíjẹ́ òbí àti ìdàgbàsókè ọmọdé láti kékeré.;
translations.reference: It includes a range of acts from bullying and physical fighting, to more severe sexual and physical assault to homicide. Youth violence dramatically increases health, welfare and criminal justice costs; reduces productivity; and generally undermines the fabric of society. Beyond deaths, injuries and psychological harm, youth violence can lead to increased health risk behaviours such as smoking, substance abuse, unsafe sex, and further violence. These in turn are associated with chronic respiratory diseases, cancer, cardiovascular diseases, diabetes, early pregnancy and sexually transmitted diseases. Effective prevention and response strategies include those that promote parenting and early childhood development;
translations.hypothesis: It encompasses a wide range of activities, from recreational and competitive sports to physical education and exercise. The active lifestyle of children contributes to improved health, fitness, and well-being; it enhances cognitive function; and it benefits society as a whole. Unlike injuries, accidents, and mental health issues, physical activity can promote the development of healthy habits, such as regular exercise, proper nutrition, teamwork, and a positive attitude. These benefits can help prevent chronic diseases, obesity, mental health problems, and other health issues. Among the most significant and common issues are childhood obesity and developmental delays in children.

translations.source: ìdènà ìpániláyà ajẹmọ́-ilé-ẹ̀kọ́, akadá, àti ìgbésí ayé àti ìdàgbàsókè àwọn ètò ìmọ̀-ọ́nṣe àwùjọ; àwọn ìlànà ìwòsàn pẹ̀lú àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu púpọ̀, àti àwọn ìlànà adálórí-ìpele ìlú àti àwùjọ bí mímú àdínkù bá níní àǹfààní sí àti àsìlò ọtí líle àti ìbọn, àwọn àgbègbè ewu àti ṣíṣe àwọn ìlànà adálórí-ìṣòro, ìmúdójú-ìwọ̀n ìgboro, àti àdínkù òsì. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé àti àwọn olùbádòwòpọ̀ ṣe àdínkù ìwà ipá ọ̀dọ́ nípasẹ̀ àwọn ètò tí wọ́n ń ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti ṣe ìdámọ̀, àpèjúwe àti fọ èsì sí ìṣòro náà, lára wọn ni: ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò fún àwọn ètò ìdènà ìwà ipá adálórí-ilé-ẹ̀kọ́; pípe àkíyèsí sí iye ìwà ipá ọ̀dọ́ àti ìdí tí wọ́n fi nílò ìdènà;
translations.reference: school-based bullying prevention, academic, and life and social skills development programmes; therapeutic approaches with high-risk individuals, and community- and society-level approaches like reducing access to and misuse of alcohol and firearms, hotspots and problem-oriented policing, urban upgrading, and poverty de-concentration. WHO and partners decrease youth violence through initiatives that help to identify, quantify and respond to the problem, these include: developing a package for schools-based violence prevention programmes; drawing attention to the magnitude of youth violence and the need for prevention;
translations.hypothesis: The fields of education, social sciences, and public health and social welfare are all relevant to addressing the issue of youth violence. This includes understanding the social determinants of health, such as poverty and inequality, as well as the risk factors and protective factors associated with violence. It also includes developing and implementing effective prevention and intervention strategies, promoting healthy communities, and addressing the root causes of violence. The World Health Organization and its partners are working to promote youth well-being through programs that aim to prevent, reduce, and address violence, including: developing and implementing evidence-based programs for youth violence prevention in schools; increasing awareness of the prevalence of youth violence and the factors that contribute to it; and promoting policies and practices that support healthy youth development.

translations.source: ṣíṣẹ̀dá ẹ̀rí lórí ààlà àti àwọn oríṣiríṣi ìwà ipá ní àwọn onírúurú ibùdó; ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìtọ́nisọ́nà fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ àti àwọn ẹ̀ka tí ọ̀rọ̀ kan láti dènà ìwà ipá ọ̀dọ́ àti ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ńlá fún àwọn èsì sí i; ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn akitiyan orílẹ̀ èdè láti dènà ìwà ipá ọ̀dọ́; àti ṣíṣe àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn àjọ àti ilé-iṣẹ́ àgbáyé láti dènà ìwà ipá ọ̀dọ́ káríayé.
translations.reference: building evidence on the scope and types of violence in different settings; developing guidance for Member States and all relevant sectors to prevent youth violence and strengthen responses to it; supporting national efforts to prevent youth violence; and collaborating with international agencies and organizations to prevent youth violence globally.
translations.hypothesis: Promoting ethical behavior in online spaces and addressing various forms of online harm; developing educational resources for communities and organizations to combat cyberbullying and providing support for victims; implementing national strategies to address cyberbullying; and fostering partnerships with international organizations to promote global efforts to combat cyberbullying.

translations.source: Ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin Ìsọnísókí: Ìwà ipá tako àwọn obìnrin – pàápàá jùlọ ìwà ipá ọ̀rẹ́ tímọ́tímọ́ àti ìwà ipá ìbálòpọ̀ – jẹ́ ìṣòro àwùjọ àti ìlera ìṣègùn gbòógì àti rírúfin àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn. Ó gbilẹ̀ nínú bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ń ṣàmúlò àìdọ́gba takọtabo. Káríayé 1 nínú àwọn obìnrin 3 ni wọ́n ní ìrírí ìwà ipá ara àti ìwà ipá ìbálòpọ̀ ní ayé wọn, lọ́pọ̀ ìgbà láti ọwọ́ alábàáṣepọ̀ tímọ́tímọ́. Èyí jẹ́ ìrántí gidi fún ìwọ̀n àìdọ́gba takọtabo àti ìyàsọ́tọ̀ sí àwọn obìnrin. Ìsọ̀kan àwọn Orílẹ̀ èdè Àgbáyé ki ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin gẹ́gẹ́ bí "èyíkéyìí ìwà ipá adálórí-ìjákọjábo tí ó yọrí sí, tàbí ṣe é ṣe kí ó yọrí sí, ìpalára ara, ìbálòpọ̀, tàbí ìròrí tàbí ìjìyà fún àwọn obìnrin, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ìpániláyà ìṣe bẹ́ẹ̀, ipá tàbí ìdènà òmìnira láìnídìí, yálà ó ṣẹlẹ̀ ní ìgbé ayé àwùjọ tàbí ìkọ̀kọ̀.
translations.reference: Violence against women Overview: Violence against women – particularly intimate partner violence and sexual violence – is a major public and clinical health problem and a violation of women's human rights. It is rooted in and perpetuates gender inequalities.Globally 1 in 3 women experience physical and/or sexual violence in their lifetime, mostly by an intimate partner. This is a stark reminder of the scale of gender inequality and discrimination against women. The United Nations defines violence against women as "any act of gender-based violence that results in, or is likely to result in, physical, sexual, or mental harm or suffering to women, including threats of such acts, coercion or arbitrary deprivation of liberty, whether occurring in public or in private life.
translations.hypothesis: Violence against women: Violence against women – including physical, sexual, psychological, and economic violence – is a pervasive and harmful social problem that violates human rights. It is a major cause of injury and death. One in three women worldwide experience physical or sexual violence, mostly from an intimate partner. This is a serious public health and human rights issue. The United Nations defines violence against women as "any act of gender-based violence that results in, or is likely to result in, physical, sexual, or psychological harm or suffering for women, including threats of such acts, coercion or arbitrary deprivation of liberty, whether occurring in public or in private."

translations.source: Ìwà ipá alábàárìn tímọ́tímọ́ ni ìwà láti ọwọ́ alábàárìn tímọ́tímọ́ tàbí alábàárìn ìgbà kan tí ó ń fa ìpalára ara, ìbálòpọ̀ tàbí ìròrí, tí ó fi mọ́ ìbínú, ìwà ipá ìbálòpọ̀, ìlòkulò ajẹmọ́-ìròrí àti àwọn ìwà ìpàsẹwàá. Ìwà ipá ìbálòpọ̀ ni "èyíkéyìí ìṣe ìbálòpọ̀, ìgbìyànjú láti ṣe ní ìbálòpọ̀, tàbí ìṣe mìíràn tí ó lòdì sí ipò ìjákọ̀jábo ènìyàn nípa lílo ipá, láti ọwọ́ ẹnikẹ́ni bí ó ti wù kí àjọṣepọ̀ wọn rí sí afaragbá, ní ibùdó èyíkéyìí. Lára rẹ̀ ni ìfipábánilọpọ̀, tí wọ́n kì bí ipá ara tàbí nígbà mìíràn fífi ipa wọ inú òbò tàbí fùrọ̀ pẹ̀lú okó, ẹ̀yà ara tàbí nǹkan mìíràn. Ipa: Ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin jẹ́ ìṣòro káríayé bí àwọn ìwọ̀n àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn. Ó máa ń fa ìparun, ìpalára sí ayé àwọn obìnrin àti ti àwọn ọmọdé.
translations.reference: Intimate partner violence refers to behaviour by an intimate partner or ex-partner that causes physical, sexual or psychological harm, including physical aggression, sexual coercion, psychological abuse and controlling behaviours. Sexual violence is "any sexual act, attempt to obtain a sexual act, or other act directed against a person’s sexuality using coercion, by any person regardless of their relationship to the victim, in any setting. It includes rape, defined as the physically forced or otherwise coerced penetration of the vulva or anus with a penis, other body part or object." Impact: Violence against women is a global problem of pandemic proportions. It causes devastating, harm to women’s lives and that of their children.
translations.hypothesis: Aggressive behavior, whether from a child or an adult, is characterized by actions that cause harm, conflict, or distress, often stemming from anger, frustration, or a desire for control. Aggressive behavior can manifest in various ways, including physical violence, verbal abuse, intimidation, and other harmful actions. A key element of aggressive behavior is the intent to cause harm or exert power over another person, often in a way that disregards the other person's feelings or well-being. This can include hitting, kicking, pushing, or other forms of physical assault, as well as verbal insults, threats, or manipulation. Aggression: Aggression towards women is a serious global issue. It can cause physical and emotional harm to women and girls.

translations.source: Ó tún ń fa ìpalára fún ọrọ̀ ajé àti ìlera àwùjọ àwọn ẹbí, àwùjọ àti orílẹ̀ èdè wọn. Nígbà tí àwọn obìnrin kan wà nínú ewu ńlá ju àwọn mìíràn lọ, ìwà ipá lè ṣẹlẹ̀ sí obìnrin èyíkéyìí, ní èyíkéyìí orílẹ̀ èdè – láì fi àṣà, ẹ̀sìn tàbí ipò ọrọ̀ ajé ṣe. Lára àìdọ́gba ìjákọ̀jábo ni àwọn àṣà takọtabo tí wọ́n léwu jẹ́ adarí pàtàkì fún ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin. Ìwà ipá lè nípa burúkú lórí ìlera ara, ìròrí, ìbálòpọ̀, àti ìbísí àwọn obìnrin. Ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìlọsíwájú ewu àwọn ìfarapa, ìbànújẹ́ ọkàn, ìrújú ìpayà, oyún àìnídìí, àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀, KASA àti ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ìṣòro ìlera mìíràn.
translations.reference: It also hurts the economic and social health of their families, communities, and countries. While some women are more at risk than others, violence can happen to any woman, in any country – regardless of culture, religion or economic status. Gender inequality including harmful gender norms are key drivers of violence against women. Violence can negatively affect women’s physical, mental, sexual, and reproductive health. It is associated with increased risk of injuries, depression, anxiety disorders, unplanned pregnancies, sexually transmitted infections, HIV and many other health problems.
translations.hypothesis: It also contributes to disparities in wealth and health outcomes across families, communities, and countries. When certain groups face greater risks than others, discrimination can manifest in various ways, including through language, customs, or economic opportunities. Among the complex factors contributing to discrimination against women, cultural norms that perpetuate harmful practices are a significant driver. Discrimination can manifest in negative attitudes towards women's health, appearance, education, and participation in society. It can lead to increased exposure to violence, mental health issues, substance abuse, risky behaviors, chronic diseases, cancer, and other health problems.

translations.source: Àwọn ọmọdé máa ń dàgbà nínú àwọn ẹbí níbi tí ìwà ipá wọ́n sì lé ní ìrírí onírúurú àwọn ìdíwọ́ ìwà àti ìmọ̀lára. Àwọn wọ̀nyí lè yọrí sí ìṣòro orí àti ìdàgbàsókè bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n tún ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú wíwọ́lé kanlẹ̀ tàbí níní ìrírí ìwà ipá ní ìgbésí ayé ọjọ́ iwájú. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé sapá ńlá nínú pípé àkíyèsí sí àti fífèsì sí ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìlera àwùjọ àti ọ̀rọ̀ ìdọ́gba ìṣakọṣabo nípasẹ̀: Ìwádìí àti ìṣẹ̀dá ẹ̀rí láti sọ iye ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin, àwọn ewu àti àbájáde rẹ̀, àti láti ṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn ìdásí pípéye fún ìdènà àti ìfèsì. Ṣíṣẹ̀dá àwọn ìlànà àti irinṣẹ́, àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn àṣà àti òṣùwọ̀n fún fífèsì sí ìlera pípéye sí ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin.
translations.reference: Children who grow up in families where there is violence may suffer a range of behavioural and emotional disturbances. These can lead to mental and development problems and also be associated with perpetrating or experiencing violence later in life. WHO response: WHO plays a key role in bringing attention to and responding to violenceagainst women as a public health and gender equality issue through: Research and evidence-building to highlight the magnitude of violence against women, its risk factors and consequences, and to identify effective interventions for prevention and response. Developing guidelines and tools, setting norms and standards for an effective health response to violence against women.
translations.hypothesis:
Children grow up within families, and their character is shaped by a variety of moral and ethical values. These values can lead to resilience and coping mechanisms, and they can also contribute to a sense of well-being and a positive outlook on life.

The World Health Organization: The World Health Organization plays a significant role in raising awareness and promoting health related to violence against women through:

*   Raising awareness of the prevalence of violence against women, its causes, and its consequences, and promoting research to better understand the issue.
*   Developing guidelines and recommendations for preventing and responding to violence against women.
*   Supporting the development of national policies and programs to address violence against women.

translations.source: Ṣíṣe ìrónilágbára fún ipa àwọn ètò orílẹ̀ èdè àti olùpèsè àti fífèsì sí ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin. Ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún adarí nínú ètò ìlera àti ṣíṣẹ̀dá ìfẹ́ òṣèlú láti kojú ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin nípasẹ̀ ṣíṣe agbẹnusọ àti àwọn ìbádòwòpọ̀. Lára àwọn Ètò Gbogbogbò fún Iṣẹ́ (EGbI) Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé 13 ni ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin gẹ́gẹ́ bí ara ipa wọn fún àwọn Àfojúsùn Ìdàgbàsókè Kánrinkése (AIK) 5 lórí ìdọ́gba takọtabo àti àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn obìnrin (pàápàá jùlọ, AIK 5.2 lórí fífi òpin sí ìwà ipá sí àwọn obìnrin àti ọmọbìnrin). Ní Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé ní Oṣù Karùn-ún 2016, àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ fọwọ́ sí ètò káríayé lórí ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ojúṣe àwọn ètò ìlera nínú wíwá ojútùú sí ìwà ipá ara ẹni, pàápàá jùlọ sí àwọn obìnrin àti ọmọbìnrin àti sí àwọn ọmọdé.
translations.reference: Strengthening country capacity of health systems and providers to prevent and respond to violence against women. Encouraging leadership in health systems and building political will to address violence against women through advocacy and partnerships. The WHO 13th General Programme of Work (GPW) includes violence against women as part of its contribution to Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 5 on gender equality and women’s empowerment (specifically, SDG 5.2 on the elimination of violence against women and girls). At the World Health Assembly in May 2016, Member States endorsed a global plan of action on strengthening the role of the health systems in addressing interpersonal violence, in particular against women and girls and against children.
translations.hypothesis: Promote the role of language and communication and address gender stereotypes. Advocate for leadership in health programs and create a supportive environment to promote gender equality for women through education and awareness. Among the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) of the United Nations, gender equality is a key priority, specifically Goal 5 on achieving gender equality and empowering all women and girls (particularly, Target 5.2 on eliminating all forms of discrimination against women and girls). At the World Health Assembly in May 2016, member states committed to a global action plan to promote health equity, particularly for women and girls, and for children.

translations.source: Iná igbó aláìlákòóso Ìsọnísókí: Iná igbó aláìlákòóso ń pọ̀ sí i káàkiri àgbáyé ní iye, líle àti àsìkò, tí ó ń mú kí ìdí láti lóye àwọn ipa ìlera ìfaragbá iná igbó aláìlákòóso pọ̀ sí i. Ewu iná igbó aláìlákòóso pọ̀ ní àwọn àsìkò gbígbẹ, bí ọ̀dà, àwọn ìgbì ooru ari lásìkò afẹ́fẹ́ ńlá. Èéfín iná igbó aláìlákòóso jẹ́ àpòpọ̀ àwọn èròjà abafẹ́fẹ́jẹ́ tí wọ́n léwu, bí PM2. 5, NO2, òsóònù, àwọn èédú-áídírò aromátíìkì, tàbí òjé. Ní àfikún sí bíbá afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn abafẹ́fẹ́jẹ́ burúkú, iná igbó aláìlákòóso tunt ń yára nípa lórí ojú ọjọ́ nípa ṣíṣe ìtújáde ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn èédú àti afẹ́fẹ́ oníbejì àti àwọn afẹ́fẹ́ adènà ooru mìíràn sínú afẹ́fẹ́. Pẹ̀lú àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ tí ó ń yọrí sí àwọn ìgbóná ojú ọjọ́ àti àwọn ọ̀gbẹlẹ̀ pẹ̀lú ìsọ̀gbẹ́dìgboro àwọn ìgbèríko tí ó ń tẹ̀síwájú, àsìkò iná náà ń tètè bẹ̀rẹ̀, ó sì ń pẹ́ parí.
translations.reference: Wildfires Overview: Wildfires are increasing around the globe in frequency, severity and duration, heightening the need to understand the health effects of wildfire exposure. The risk of wildfires grows in extremely dry conditions, such as drought, heat waves and during high winds.Wildfire smoke is a mixture of hazardous air pollutants, such PM2.5, NO2, ozone, aromatic hydrocarbons, or lead. In addition to contaminating the air with toxic pollutants, wildfires also simultaneously impact the climate by releasing large quantities of carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases into the atmosphere. With climate change leading to warmer temperatures and drier conditions and the increasing urbanization of rural areas, the fire season is starting earlier and ending later.
translations.hypothesis: The Global Wildfire Crisis: Wildfires are increasing in frequency, intensity, and extent around the world, leading to a growing need to understand and address the health impacts of wildfire smoke. The risks associated with wildfires are particularly high during certain seasons, such as the dry season and periods of prolonged drought. Wildfire smoke contains a complex mixture of harmful pollutants, including particulate matter (PM2.5), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), ozone, volatile organic compounds, and carbon monoxide. In addition to the direct health effects of exposure to these pollutants, wildfire smoke can also exacerbate existing respiratory and cardiovascular conditions. With increasing temperatures and longer periods of drought, wildfires are becoming more frequent and severe, leading to longer and more intense smoke seasons. This poses a significant threat to public health, particularly for vulnerable populations such as children, the elderly, and people with pre-existing health conditions.

translations.source: Àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ iná igbó aláìlákòóso ń le sí i ní agbègbè sarè ilẹ̀ tí ó ń jó, àsìkò àti agbára, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n lè ṣe ìdíwọ́ ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀, ìbáraẹnisọ̀rọ̀, ìpèsè omi, àti àwọn iṣẹ́ iná mọ̀nàmọ́ná àti gáàsì. Ipa: Iná igbó aláìlákòóso tí ó jó sún mọ́ àwọn àgbègbè tí ènìyàn pọ̀ sí lè nípa ńlá lórí àyíká, dúkìá, ẹran ọ̀sìn àti ikú ẹ̀dá ènìyàn àti ikú èyí dá lórí ìtóbi, ìyára àti agbègbè sí iná náà, àti bóyá àwọn ènìyàn náà gba ìkìlọ̀ siwaju láti kúrò. Èéfín iná igbó aláìlákòóso jẹ́ àpòpọ̀ àwọn èròjà abafẹ́fẹ́jẹ́ èyí tí ìdọ̀tí afẹ́fẹ́ (IA) jẹ́ olórí ewu ìlera àwùjọ. PM2.5 láti ara èéfín iná igbó aláìlákòóso ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn ikú àìtọ́jọ́ nínú iye ènìyàn gbogbogbò, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè fa àti kí ó fẹjú àwọn àìsàn ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró, ọkàn, ètò ọpọlọ/ara, awọ ara, òpónà oúnjẹ, kíndìnrín, ojú, inú àti ẹ̀dọ̀. Wọ́n tunt tí fìdí rẹ̀ múlẹ̀ pé ó ń yọrí sí idenat ìlóye àti àdánù ìrántí.
translations.reference: Wildfire events are getting more extreme in terms of acres burned, duration and intensity, and they can disrupt transportation, communications, water supply, and power and gas services. Impact: Wildfires that burn near populated areas can have significant impact on the environment, property, livestock and human mortality and morbidity depending on the size, speed and proximity to the fire, and whether the population has advanced warning to evacuate. Wildfire smoke is a mixture of air pollutants of which particulate matter (PM) is the principal public health threat. PM2.5 from wildfire smoke is associated with premature deaths in the general population, and can cause and exacerbate diseases of the lungs, heart, brain/nervous system, skin, gut, kidney, eyes, nose and liver. It has also been shown to lead to cognitive impairment and memory loss.
translations.hypothesis: Uncontrolled wildfires can occur in areas with dry vegetation, high temperatures, and strong winds, and they can cause significant damage to property, infrastructure, water resources, and natural ecosystems. Impacts: Uncontrolled wildfires that spread to populated areas can have devastating consequences, including loss of life, injuries, displacement of people, and damage to homes and businesses. The rapid spread of wildfires can also create hazardous conditions, making evacuation difficult and increasing the risk of exposure to smoke and flames. The smoke from uncontrolled wildfires is a complex mixture of pollutants, with particulate matter (PM) being a major public health concern. PM2.5 from wildfire smoke is associated with increased mortality rates and can exacerbate respiratory illnesses, cardiovascular disease, and other health problems. Studies have also linked wildfire smoke exposure to increased rates of hospital admissions and emergency room visits.

translations.source: Àwọn panápaná àti òṣìṣẹ́ afèsìsí pàjáwìrì tún máa ń ní àwọn ìfarapa, àpá iná àti fífa èèfún sínú, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn ìwọ̀n líle. Ìwádìí ajẹmọ́ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ẹ̀ka ìwádìí ni ó dájú pé yóò lóye àwọn ipa ìfaragbá iná igbó aláìlákòóso afarasin àti ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ lórí ìlera lórí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu (àwọn ọmọdé, àgbàlagbà, àwọn aláboyún, àwọn tí wọ́n ń ṣe àìsàn ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́), pàápàá jùlọ fún àwọn agbègbè tí wọn ń faradà ìfaragbá àṣetúnṣe àti olóbìrípo sí awokt ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ iná igbó aláìlákòóso wọ̀nyí. Èsì Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: Títóbi ipá ìnáwó ara àti ẹ̀dá ènìyàn láti àwọn iná igbó aláìlákòóso lè ṣe é dínkù bí ìdènà, ìgbáradì, ìfèsì sí pàjáwìrì tí ó tó àti àwọn òṣùwọ̀n fún imúpàdàbọ̀sípò bá jẹ́ ṣíṣe àmúlò ní ọ̀nà kánrinkése àti lójú ọjọ́. Ṣíṣe iṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti ṣẹ̀dá àwọn ètò ìlera alágbára tí ó sì ní ìdarí tí ó lè fojúsun àwọn àìní àti ìṣòro lásìkò àwọn pàjáwìrì, kí ó lè ṣe é ṣe fún wa láti ṣe àdínkù àwọn ewu àti ìfèsì pípé lásìkò tí ó nílò. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ń ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn olùbádòwòpọ̀ láti ṣe ìgbáradì, ìdènà, àyẹ̀wò, ìfèsìsí àti imúpàdàbọ̀sípò láti ara àwọn pàjáwìrì àti àjálù, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ìfaragbá àyíká, bí ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ètò ìkìlọ̀ ìsáájú àti pípèsè ìmọ̀ràn lórí ìlera àti àbùdá afẹ́fẹ́;
translations.reference: Firefighters and emergency response workers are also greatly impacted by injuries, burns and smoke inhalation, particularly at high concentrations. More interdisciplinary research is warranted to understand the latent and long-term health effects of wildfire exposure on vulnerable populations (children, older people, pregnant people, chronically ill people), particularly for geographic areas enduring repeated and cyclical exposure to these wildﬁre events. WHO Response: The magnitude of the physical and human costs from wildfires can be reduced if adequate emergency prevention, preparedness, response and recovery measures are implemented in a sustainable and timely manner. Working with Member States, WHO helps to build resilient and proactive health systems that can anticipate the needs and challenges during emergencies, so that we can be more likely to reduce risks and respond effectively when needed. WHO works with partners in preparing, preventing, detecting, responding and recovering from emergencies and disasters, including environmental exposures, such asimplementing early warning systems and issuing health and air quality advisories;
translations.hypothesis: Hospitals and emergency services often experience power outages, equipment failures, and disruptions to essential services, particularly during natural disasters. These events can have a significant impact on patient care, especially for vulnerable populations such as children, the elderly, individuals with disabilities, and those with chronic illnesses. The World Health Organization (WHO) recognizes the critical importance of ensuring continuity of essential health services during and after emergencies. To this end, the WHO provides guidance and resources to help countries develop and implement robust health emergency preparedness plans. These plans should address the potential impacts of various hazards, including power outages, equipment failures, and disruptions to supply chains. The WHO also works with countries to strengthen their health systems' capacity to respond to emergencies, including providing training and technical assistance to healthcare workers. The WHO collaborates with partners to provide essential health services during and after emergencies, including setting up temporary clinics, providing medical supplies, and conducting public health surveillance. This work helps to minimize the impact of emergencies on health and well-being, and to ensure that essential health services are available to those who need them.

translations.source: tí ó ní ìtújáde àwọn èròjà burúkú; ìgbéléwọ̀n àwọn àìní ìlera ní ìlú àti ìbàjẹ́ àwọn ohun amáyédẹrùn; ìdápadà àwọn iṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú abẹ́lé, bí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára, ìlera ọmọ àti ìyálọ́mọ, àti ìlera ọpọlọ.; ṣíṣe ìrónilágbára fún ìpèsè ẹ̀dá ènìyàn fún àkóso àjálù; ṣíṣe àgbàsílẹ̀, ìyànnàná àti àpínká ìròyìn tí ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn pàjáwìrì àti àwọn àjálù tí wọ́n ṣe é ṣe láti wáyé ní agbègbè kan; ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àti àmójútó àwọn ìpèsè àti irinṣẹ́ ìṣúra ìtura;
translations.reference: containing the release of hazardous materials; assessing health needs of the community and infrastructure damage; restoring primary care services, like immunization, child and maternal health, and mental health; strengthening human resources for disaster management; collecting, analysing and disseminating information related to emergencies and disasters that are likely to occur in a region; establishing and managing stocks of relief supplies and equipment;
translations.hypothesis: It includes the trafficking of illicit goods; the spread of infectious diseases in urban areas; the destruction of public property; the disruption of essential services, such as transportation, healthcare, and education; the use of violence to intimidate or control the population; the establishment, support, or promotion of extremist ideologies and groups that seek to incite violence or commit atrocities; and the recruitment and training of individuals for terrorist or criminal activities.

translations.source: ìpè fún owó ìrànwọ́ pàjáwìrì láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn iṣẹ́ ìlera; àti ṣíṣẹ̀dá àwọn ìlànà orílẹ̀ èdè, ọ̀nà àbáyọ àti àwọn ètò ìfèsìsí pàjáwìrì orílẹ̀ èdè.
translations.reference: calling for emergency funding to support health action; and developing national policies, recommendations and national emergency response plans.
translations.hypothesis: Funding for research projects in public health; and the development of national, regional, and local public health policies, strategies, and programs.

translations.source: Ìlera obìnrin Ìsọnísókí: Jíjẹ́ ọkùnrin tàbí obìnrin ní ipa pàtàki lórí ìlera, gẹ́gẹ́ bí àbájáde àpapọ̀ àwọn ìyàtọ̀ ìṣẹ̀dá àti ajẹmọ́-takọtabo. Ìlera àwọn obìnrin àti ọmọbìnrin ṣe pàtàkì nítorí, ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwùjọ, wọ́n kò ní àǹfààní nítorí ìyàsọ́tọ̀ tí ó tagbò nínú àwọn àbùdá àwùjọ-máṣà. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, àwọn obìnrin àti ọmọbìnrin ń kojú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ewu KASA/Àrùn Ìsọdọ̀lẹ Àjẹsára. Lára àwọn àbùdá àwùjọ-máṣà kan tí wọ́n ń dènà àwọn obìnrin àti ọmọbìnrin láti jẹ àǹfààní láti ara iṣẹ́ ìlera dídára àti dídé ìpele ìlera tí ó dára jùlọ ni:
translations.reference: Women's health Overview: Being a man or a woman has a significant impact on health, as a result of both biological and gender-related differences. The health of women and girls is of particular concern because, in many societies, they are disadvantaged by discrimination rooted in sociocultural factors. For example, women and girls face increased vulnerability to HIV/AIDS. Some of the sociocultural factors that prevent women and girls to benefit from quality health services and attaining the best possible level of health include:
translations.hypothesis: Women's Health: The impact of gender on health is significant, particularly concerning the prevalence of certain diseases and health conditions. Women's and girls' health is crucial because, in many societies, they face unique challenges and barriers that affect their access to healthcare and overall well-being. For example, women and girls are disproportionately affected by certain cancers and other serious health issues. Some of the social factors that prevent women and girls from accessing quality healthcare and achieving optimal health include:

translations.source: àìdọ́gba ìbáṣepọ̀ agbára láàrin ọkùnrin àti obìnrin; àṣà àwùjọ tí ń ṣe àdínkù ẹ̀kọ́ àti àwọn àǹfààní iṣẹ́ owó; àfojúsùn lórí àwọn ojúṣe ìbísí àwọn obìnrin; àti agbára tàbí ìrírí ara, ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìmọ̀lára ìwà ipá. Nígbà tí òsì jẹ́ ìdènà ńlá sí àwọn ọ̀nà àbáyọ ìlera dídára fún tọkùnrin tobìnrin, ó dàbí ẹni pé òsì jẹ́ àjàgà ńlá lórí ìlera àwọn obìnrin àti ọmọbìnrin nítorí, fún àpẹẹrẹ, àṣà oúnjẹ jíjẹ (àìjẹunrekánú) àti ìlò àwọn èpò ìdáná tí wọ́n léwu (COPD).
translations.reference: unequal power relationships between men and women; social norms that decrease education and paid employment opportunities; an exclusive focus on women’s reproductive roles; and potential or actual experience of physical, sexual and emotional violence. While poverty is an important barrier to positive health outcomes for both men and women, poverty tends to yield a higher burden on women and girls’ health due to, for example, feeding practices (malnutrition) and use of unsafe cooking fuels (COPD).
translations.hypothesis: The interplay of power dynamics between men and women; social norms that promote education and economic opportunities; the impact of gender on health outcomes; and the influence of gender on self-esteem, relationships, and moral development. While there are significant advancements in specific health areas for women, there remains a substantial gap in overall women's and girls' health, for example, due to issues like unhealthy eating habits (malnutrition) and the prevalence of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD).

translations.source: Yáàsì (Endemic treponematoses) Ìsọnísókí: Yáàsì jẹ́ àrùn awọ ara ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ tí ó ní àbùdá ògòdò (ògòdò aláìní jẹjẹrẹ) àti àwọn egbò. Bakitéríà Treponema pallidum subspecies pertenue ni ó ń fà á, eyit tí ó wà ọ̀wọ́ kan náà pẹ̀lú bakitéríà tí ń fa àwọn sífílì ìbálòpọ̀. Yáàsì máa ń sáábà mú àwọn ọmọdé lábẹ́ ọjọ́ orí ọdún 15 tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn ìlú ooru, ọlọ́pọ̀ omi nínú kùrukùru àti àwọn ìlú ijù ipa òòrùn agbègbè Áfríkà, Látìn Amẹ́ríkà àti àwọn erékùṣù Pàsífíìkì. Púpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn tí àìsàn náà ń ṣe ń gbé ní àwọn agbègbè ìgbèríko, tí wọ́n jìnnà sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera.
translations.reference: Yaws (Endemic treponematoses) Overview: Yaws is a chronic skin infection characterized by papillomas (noncancerous lumps) and ulcers. It is caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum subspecies pertenue, which belongs to the same group of bacteria that causes venereal syphilis. Yaws primarily affects children aged under 15 years who live in poor communities in warm, humid and tropical forested areas of Africa, Asia, Latin America and the Pacific islands. The majority of affected populations live rural areas, far from health services.
translations.hypothesis: Yaws (Endemic treponematoses): Yaws is a chronic skin disease characterized by localized lesions (nodules) and ulcers. It is caused by the bacterium *Treponema pallidum subspecies pertenue*, which is closely related to the bacterium that causes syphilis. Yaws primarily affects children under the age of 15 living in tropical, humid areas, particularly in rural communities in Africa, Latin America, and the Pacific Islands. Many of those affected live in impoverished areas with limited access to healthcare.

translations.source: Òsì, àwọn ipò àwùjọ-mọ́rọ̀-ajé àti ìmọ́tótó ara ẹni tí kò dára dákun ìtànká yáàsì. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé àwọn ọ̀ràn yáàsì 80 000 wà ní ọdọọdún, àwọn alámọ̀já gbàgbọ́ pé àìsàn náà lè ṣe é ṣàkóso kí ó sì di ìfòpinsí pátápátá nítorí onírúurú àwọn ìdí. Ní àkọ́kọ́, ó maat ń ṣe àwọn ẹ̀dá ènìyàn nìkan, kì í ṣe ẹranko, tí ó mú mi àkóso rẹ̀ rọrùn dáadáa. Ó tún rọrùn láti tọ́jú pẹ̀lú àwọn òògùn tí wọ́n wà nílẹ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè kan, tí ó fi mọ́ India ti rẹ́yìn rẹ̀. Àwọn àlàfo àrùn yáàsì tí ó kù, bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ó wọ́pọ̀ ní àwọn ìgbèríko, túmọ̀ sí pé ìtànká kò ṣe é ṣe kó wáyé pẹ̀lú àmójútó tí ó yẹ àti àwọn òṣùwọ̀n ìṣàkóso.
translations.reference: Poverty, low socio-economic conditions and poor personal hygiene facilitate the spread of yaws. Although there are over 80 000 cases of yaws each year, experts believe the disease can be controlled and ultimately eradicated for several reasons. First, it only occurs in humans, not animals, making control much easier. It is also easy to treat with readily available drugs and has already been eliminated in some countries, including India. The remaining pockets of yaws infection, although usually in remote places, means further spread is less likely with proper surveillance and control measures.
translations.hypothesis: Yeast infections, social stigmas, and poor self-esteem are all factors that contribute to the suffering caused by this condition. While there are an estimated 80,000 cases of yeast infections each year, many people believe that the condition is easily treatable and should not be a source of embarrassment. However, the reality is that yeast infections can be difficult to manage and can have a significant impact on a person's quality of life. In some cases, yeast infections can be caused by a variety of factors, including hormonal changes, medications, and underlying medical conditions. This can make it difficult to determine the cause of the infection and to find the most effective treatment. Furthermore, some people may experience recurring yeast infections, which can be frustrating and time-consuming to manage. The fact that yeast infections are often associated with sexual activity can also lead to feelings of shame and embarrassment. This can make it difficult for people to seek treatment and to talk openly about their condition.

translations.source: Àmì àìsàn: Ìpele méjì àrùn yáàsì ni ó wà:. Ìpele ìbẹ̀rẹ̀pẹ̀pẹ̀ ni nígbà tí aláìsàn bá kó àrùn náà tí ó sì lè kó àìsàn náà ran àwọn ẹlòmíràn. Ní ìpele yìí, ògòdò kan (kókó tí ó ń dàgbà kan, aláìní jẹjẹrẹ) yóò dàgbà níbi àrùn náà. Ògòdò náà kún fún àwọn ajọ̀fẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè wà níbẹ̀ fún oṣù 3-6 lẹ́yìn ìtọ́jú aláìtọ́ọ́dá. Láìsí ìtọ́jú, ohun tí yóò tẹ̀lé èyí ni ìtànká àwọn ogido káàkiri ara.
translations.reference: Symptoms: There are two stages of yaws infection. The early stage is when the patient is infectious and can spread the disease to others. In this stage, a papule (a noncancerous, outward-growing lump) develops at the site of infection. This papule is full of the organisms and may persist for 3–6 months followed by natural healing. Without treatment, this is followed by disseminated skin lesions over the body.
translations.hypothesis: Disease Transmission: The disease has two stages. The initial stage occurs when a person contracts the disease and can potentially transmit it to others. In this stage, a rash (small, red bumps) will appear at the site of infection. This rash is often itchy and can last for 3-6 weeks after the initial infection. Without treatment, the next stage involves the spread of the rash throughout the body.

translations.source: Ìrora eegun àti kókó inú eegun lè wáyé. Ìpele kejì ni aláìní àrùn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò farahàn lẹ́yìn ọdún márùn-ún àrùn. Ó ní àbùdá sísọ àwọn ojúṣe imú, egungun àti fífa palma/plantar hyperkeratosis (ara yíyi). Lẹ́yìn àyẹ̀wò lẹ́nu ìtọ́jú dá lórí àwọn àmì àìsàn wọ̀nyí, aisant náà lè jẹ́ jíjẹ́rìí nípa ṣíṣe àyẹ̀wò àpẹẹrẹfáyẹ̀wò láti inú ògòdò awọ ara lábẹ́ oríṣìí máíkírósíkòòpù pàtó kan (àyẹ̀wò agbọn dúdú). Àwọn àyẹ̀wò ẹ̀jẹ̀ kì í sáábà jẹ́ lílò nítorí pé yáàsì tan pẹ̀lú bakiteria tí wọ́n ń fa sífílì bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn àìsàn méjèèjì náà yóò ṣàfihàn èsì kan náà.
translations.reference: Bone pain and bone lesions may also occur. The second stage is noninfectious and typically appears five years after infection. It is characterized by disabling consequences of the nose, bones and palmar/plantar hyperkeratosis (thickening). After a field diagnosis based on these symptoms, the disease can be confirmed by examining a sample from a skin legion under a special type of microscope (darkfield examination). Blood tests are not typically used because yaws is closely related to the bacterium that causes syphilis and the two diseases will show the same result.
translations.hypothesis: Skin lesions and internal organ complications can develop. The second stage of the disease typically appears after about five years. It is characterized by the development of skin lesions, nodules, and palmoplantar hyperkeratosis (thickening of the skin on the palms and soles). After a thorough examination of these skin lesions, a biopsy may be performed to examine a sample of tissue taken from the affected area under a microscope (a biopsy). Blood tests are not typically used because the disease is caused by a virus and blood tests will not reveal this.

translations.source: Ìtọ́jú: Kò sí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára fún ìtọ́jú yáàsì báyìí. Ẹ̀kọ́ ìlera àti ìdàgbàsókè ìmọ́tótó ara ẹni jẹ́ àwọn ọgbọ́n tí wọ́n ṣe pàtàkì jùlọ fún ìdènà. Òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò méjì lè ṣe é lò láti tọ́jú yáàsì. Àtẹnugbà ìlò azithromycin kan (30mg/kg, ó pọ̀ jù 2g) ni wọ́n fọwọ́ sí fún ìtọ́jú. Benzathine penicillin (ìlò àgbàsínú-isan kan) ní àwọn ìwọ̀n mílíọ̀nù 0.6 (àwọn ọmọdé lábẹ́ ọjọ́ orí 10) àti àwọn ìwọ̀n mílíọ̀nù 1.2 (fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ti ju ọdún 10 lọ) lè ṣe é lò fún àwọn aláìsàn pẹ̀lú afurasí ìtọ́jú tí ó kùnà lẹ́yìn azithromycin, tàbí àwọn aláìsàn tí wọ́n kò ṣe é tọ́jú pẹ̀lú azithromycin.
translations.reference: Treatment: There is currently no vaccine for yaws. Health education and improved personal hygiene are the most important tactics for prevention. Two antibiotics can be used to treat yaws. A single oral dose of azithromycin (30 mg/kg, maximum 2 g) is the recommended treatment. Benzathine penicillin (single intramuscular dose) at 0.6 million units (children aged under 10 years) and 1.2 million units (people aged over 10 years) can be used for patients with suspected clinical treatment failure after azithromycin, or patients who cannot be treated with azithromycin.
translations.hypothesis: Treatment: There are no specific guidelines for treating this particular infection. Health education and accurate self-assessment are the most important factors for prevention. A combination of two antibiotics may be used to treat the infection. A single dose of azithromycin (30mg/kg, up to a maximum of 2g) is often prescribed for treatment. Benzathine penicillin (a single intramuscular injection) at a dose of 0.6 million units (for children under 10 years old) and 1.2 million units (for individuals over 10 years old) can be used for patients with more severe infections that do not respond to azithromycin, or for patients who cannot tolerate azithromycin.

translations.source: Ìtọ́jú yìí yoo yọrí sí 95% ìwòsàn pátápátá ṣùgbọ́n àwọn aláàárẹ̀ gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ ṣíṣe àtúnyẹ̀wò lẹ́yìn ọ̀sẹ̀ 4 tí wọ́n ti gba òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò. Àtúnyẹ̀wò àwọn ìwé ìtàn láti àwọn ọdún 1950 fi hàn pé ó lé ní orílẹ̀ èdè àti agbègbè 90 ni wọ́n ní àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn yáàsì. 14 péré nínú àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè àti agbègbè 90 ni wọ́n ní ìwífun-àlàyé abágbàmu lórí yáàsì dá lórí ètò àmójútó àgbékalẹ̀; àmọ́ sá, àwọn iye wọ̀nyí lè jẹ́ ìtọ́ka wíwà àìsàn náà tí kì í sì ṣe gbogbo wíwàa rẹ̀. Ghana, Papua New Guinea àti Solomon Islands jábọ̀ ó lé ní àwọn ọ̀ràn 10 000 lọ́dún. Ecuador àti India dà bí ẹni pé wọ́n ti rẹ́yìn ìkóràn.
translations.reference: This treatment will result in complete clinical healing in 95% of cases but patients should be re-examined 4 weeks after receiving the antibiotics. A review of the historic documents from 1950s shows that over 90 countries and territories were endemic for yaws. Only 14 out of the 90 countries and territories have recent data on yaws based on the routine surveillance system; however, these figures may just be an indication of the presence of the disease and not its full extent. Ghana, Papua New Guinea and the Solomon Islands report over 10 000 cases per year. Ecuador and India appear to have interrupted transmission.
translations.hypothesis: This study showed a 95% success rate in completely eliminating the disease, but follow-up checks are necessary after 4 weeks of treatment with the anti-parasitic drug. Historical data from the 1950s indicates that the disease was present in 90 countries and regions. In 14 of those 90 countries and regions, the disease was linked to a significant increase in infant mortality rates, likely due to the lack of effective treatment; however, these figures may not reflect the full extent of the disease's impact. Ghana, Papua New Guinea, and the Solomon Islands reported over 10,000 cases annually. Ecuador and India appear to have eradicated the disease.

translations.source: Àìsàn ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ Ìsọnísókí: Àìsàn ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ jẹ́ àìsàn fáírọ́ọ̀sì tí ó máa ń ran ènìyàn nípasẹ̀ ẹnu ẹ̀fọn tí ó ti lùgbàdì. Ó ṣe é ṣe kí ó di àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára. Àwọn ẹ̀yà ẹ̀fọn ajẹni lóòjọ́ láyìíká ilé (abẹ́lé), nínú igbó tàbí ijù (aginjù), tàbí ní àwọn ibi méjèèjì (kòṣabẹ́lé-kòṣejù). Ibà apọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ jẹ́ àìsàn onípa ńlá, tí ó léwu púpọ̀, pẹ̀lú ewu ìtànká káàkiri àgbáyé, èyí tí ó jẹ́ ewu fún ààbò ìlera àgbáyé.
translations.reference: Yellow fever Overview: Yellow fever is a viral disease that is transmitted to humans by the bites of infected mosquitoes. It is prone to epidemics and is preventable with a vaccine. These day-biting mosquitoes breed around houses (domestic), in forests or jungles (wild), or in both habitats (semi-domestic). Yellow fever is a high-impact, high-threat disease, with a risk of international spread, representing a potential threat to global health security.
translations.hypothesis: Malaria: Malaria is a serious infectious disease transmitted to humans through the bites of infected mosquitoes. It can cause severe illness and even death if left untreated. Mosquitoes that transmit malaria live in tropical and subtropical regions, including forests, swamps, and areas with standing water. Malaria is a major public health problem, causing significant morbidity and mortality worldwide, and poses a threat to global health security.

translations.source: Ìpele ìkóràn 3 ló wà: Sylvatic (tàbí ijù) ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ máa ń ràn nígbà tí àwọn ẹ̀fọn ijù bá jẹ àwọn ọ̀bọ tí wọ́n kó fáírọ́ọ̀sì náà ran àwọn ọ̀bọ àti ènìyàn mìíràn. Ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ agbedeméjì máa ń tàn nígbà tí àwọn ẹ̀fọn kògbélé-kògbéjù bá kó àìsàn náà ran àwọn ọ̀bọ àti ènìyàn. Èyí ni oríṣìí ẹ̀yà ìbújáde tí ó wọ́pọ̀ ní Áfíríkà. Ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ ìgboro lè fa àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn ńlá nígbà tí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n bá lùgbàdì àrùn náà bá kó fáírọ́ọ̀sì náà ran àwọn àgbègbè tí àwọn ènìyàn pọ̀ sí pẹ̀lú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ẹ̀fọn àti níbi tí àjẹsára àwọn ènìyàn kò ti tó nǹkan. Ní ìgbà mìíràn, àwọn arìnrìn-àjò tí wọ́n ti lùgbàdì àìsàn náà tí kó àwọn ọ̀ràn wọ àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n kò ti ní ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀.
translations.reference: There are 3 types of transmission cycles: Sylvatic (or jungle) yellow fever spreads when monkeys are bitten by wild mosquitoes that pass the virus on to other monkeys and humans. Intermediate yellow fever spreads when semi-domestic mosquitoes infect both monkeys and people. This is the most common type of outbreak in Africa. Urban yellow fever can cause large epidemics when infected people introduce the virus into heavily populated areas with high mosquito density and where people have little immunity. Occasionally, infected travellers have exported cases to countries that are free of yellow fever.
translations.hypothesis: There are three levels of risk: sylvatic (or jungle) transmission, where the virus circulates among non-human primates and occasionally spills over to humans. Zoonotic transmission occurs when infected monkeys transmit the virus to humans. This is the most common mode of transmission in Africa. Urban transmission can lead to large outbreaks when infected humans transmit the virus to other people in densely populated areas with poor sanitation. In other cases, travelers who have been infected with the virus can introduce it to areas where it is not normally found.

translations.source: Àmọ́ sá, àìsàn yìí lè tàn ká ní ìrọ̀rùn wọ orílẹ̀ ede tuntun bí àwọn oríṣìí ẹ̀fọn tí wọ́n lè ṣe ìtànká rẹ̀, ipò ojú ọjọ́ pàtó, àti ẹranko ajọ̀fẹ́ tí ó nílò láti gbé e ka bá wà nìkan. Àmì àìsàn: Ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ lè ní àwọn onírúurú àmì àìsàn àti ọwọ́ líle onírúurú. Ní kété tí ènìyàn bá ti kó o, fáírọ́ọ̀sì ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ yóò sàba nínú ara fún ọjọ́ 3-6. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ènìyàn kì í ní ìrírí àmì àìsàn, ṣùgbọ́n nígbà tí àwọn wọ̀nyí bá ṣẹlẹ̀, àwọn tí ó wọ́pọ̀ jùlọ ni ibà, isan ríro pẹ̀lú ẹ̀yìn dídùn, orí fífọ́, àìlèjẹun, àti àyà rínrìn tàbí èébì. Fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ọ̀ràn, àmì àìsàn máa ń pòórá lẹ́yìn bí ọjọ́ 3-4.
translations.reference: However, the disease can only spread easily to a new country if there are mosquito species able to transmit it, specific climatic conditions, and the animal reservoir needed to maintain it. Symptoms: Yellow fever can present with a wide range of symptoms and severity. Once contracted, the yellow fever virus incubates in the body for 3–6 days. Many people do not experience symptoms, but when these do occur, the most common are fever, muscle pain with prominent backache, headache, loss of appetite, and nausea or vomiting. In most cases, symptoms disappear after 3–4 days.
translations.hypothesis: Unfortunately, this condition can manifest in various ways, ranging from mild symptoms like a slight rash or itching to more severe symptoms such as specific skin conditions, localized swelling, and allergic reactions that require immediate attention. Symptoms: Allergic reactions can present with a wide range of symptoms and varying degrees of severity. Typically, symptoms of an allergic reaction appear within 3-6 hours of exposure. While many people experience mild symptoms, others may experience more severe reactions, including hives, itching, swelling, dizziness, difficulty breathing, and a rapid heartbeat. In many cases, the symptoms will subside within 3-4 days.

translations.source: Ìdá kékeré àwọn aláàárẹ̀ máa ń wọnú ìpele kejì, tí ó lè gan-an láàrin wákàtí 24 tí wọ́n bá ti padà bọ̀ sípò láti ara àwọn àmì àìsàn ìsáájú. Ibà líle yóò padà bẹ́ẹ̀ sit ni yóò nípa lórí àwọn onírúurú ètò ara, lọ́pọ̀ọ̀gbà ẹ̀dọ̀ àti kíndìnrín. Ní ìpele yìí, ó ṣe é ṣe kí àwọn ènìyàn ní ibà pọ́njúpọ́nra (ara àti ojú pípọ́n, nítorí náà ni orúkọ rẹ̀ ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀), ìtọ̀ pípọ́n, àti inú rírun pẹ̀lú èébì. Ẹ̀jẹ̀ lè sẹ́ láti ẹnu, imú, ojú, tàbí ikùn. Ìdajì àwọn aláàárẹ̀ wọ̀nyí ń kú láàrin ọjọ́ 7-10, ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ ṣòro láti ṣàyẹ̀wò, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn ìpele ìbẹ̀rẹ̀pẹ̀pẹ̀.
translations.reference: A small percentage of patients enter a second, more toxic phase within 24 hours of recovering from initial symptoms. High fever returns and several body systems are affected, usually the liver and kidneys. In this phase, people are likely to develop jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes, hence the name yellow fever), dark urine, and abdominal pain with vomiting. Bleeding can occur from the mouth, nose, eyes, or stomach. Half of these patients die within 7–10 days.Yellow fever is difficult to diagnose, especially during the early stages.
translations.hypothesis: The initial stage of chickenpox typically lasts for about 24 hours and is characterized by the appearance of small, itchy blisters that develop from the underlying skin. These blisters will then break open and form a yellowish crust. During this stage, individuals may also experience a fever, headache, and general malaise. In some cases, people may also develop a rash on their face and scalp. The blisters typically appear on the trunk, face, and scalp, and can also be found on the mucous membranes of the mouth and throat. These blisters usually heal within 7-10 days, although the scabs may take longer to disappear, especially in the early stages.

translations.source: Àwọn ọ̀ràn líle púpọ̀ lè rújú ní ìdámọ̀ pẹ̀lú maléríà líle, lẹ́pitosipirósì, àrùn fáírọ́ọ̀sì jẹ̀dọ̀jẹ̀dọ̀ (pàápàá jùlọ àwọn ẹ̀yà òjìji), àwọn ibà ajẹmẹ́jẹ́ mìíràn, àrùn pẹ̀lú àwọn fáírọ́ọ̀sì ẹbí Flaviviridae mìíràn (bí ibà oríike inú ẹ̀jẹ̀) àti májèlé jíjẹ. Ẹ̀wọ́n Ìṣesí Ploymerase (EIS) àyẹ̀wò nínú ẹ̀jẹ̀ àti ìtọ̀ lè ṣe ìdámọ̀ fáírọ́ọ̀sì náà ní àwọn ìpele ìbẹ̀rẹ̀pẹ̀pẹ̀ àìsàn náà. Ní àwọn ìpele ìgbẹ̀yìn, àyẹ̀wò láti ṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn ẹ̀sọ́ ètò ara ṣe pàtàkì (IgM). Ìtọ́jú: Àrùn ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ lè fa àìsàn líle bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè gbẹ̀mí ní ti àwọn ọ̀ràn líle. Àtìlẹ́yìn ìtọ́jú kíákíá ní àwọn ilé ìwòsàn ń mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìwọ̀n yíyè.
translations.reference: More severe cases can be confused with severe malaria, leptospirosis, viral hepatitis (especially fulminant forms), other haemorrhagic fevers, infection with other flaviviruses (such as dengue haemorrhagic fever) and poisoning. Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) testing in blood and urine can sometimes detect the virus in early stages of the disease. In later stages, testing to identify antibodies is needed (IgM). Treatment: Yellow fever infections can cause serious illness and can be fatal in severe cases. Early supportive treatment in hospitals improves survival rates.
translations.hypothesis: Various viral infections can manifest with fever, including malaria, influenza (especially during flu season), other viral infections, infections caused by other Flaviviridae family viruses (such as dengue fever), and even bacterial infections. Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) tests in blood and other samples can be used to detect these viruses in the early stages of illness. In later stages, tests to detect antibodies (IgM) are important for diagnosis. Conclusion: Fever can be a symptom of many different infections, and it can also be a symptom of non-infectious conditions. Accurate diagnosis is crucial for appropriate treatment. Evidence-based guidelines in healthcare settings are essential for advancing this field.

translations.source: Kò sí òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì kankan pàtó báyìí fún ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀, ṣùgbọ́n ìtọ́jú àyàsọ́tọ̀ láti tọ́jú omi ara gbígbẹ, ibà, àti ìkùnà ẹ̀dọ̀ àti kíndìnrín ń ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn àbájáde. Àwọn àrùn bakitéríà tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú rẹ̀ lè ṣe é tọ́jú pẹ̀lú òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò bakitéríà. Aláàárẹ̀ ní latit dúró lábẹ́ àpò ẹ̀fọn lásìkò ojú mọmọ láti ṣe àdínkù ewu ṣíṣe ìtànká sí àwọn mìíràn nípa àwọn ẹnu ẹ̀fọn. Abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára nìkan ni òṣùwọ̀n pàtàkì jùlọ fún ìdènà ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀. Ìdènà àwọn ìbújáde lè jẹ́ àṣeyọrí bí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn bá ní àjẹsára.
translations.reference: There is currently no specific anti-viral drug for yellow fever, but specific care to treat dehydration, fever, and liver and kidney failure improves outcomes. Associated bacterial infections can be treated with antibiotics. Patients need to stay under mosquito nets during the day to limit the risk of spreading to others through mosquito bites. Vaccination is the single most important measure for preventing yellow fever. The prevention of outbreaks can only be achieved if the majority of the population is immunized.
translations.hypothesis: Currently, there is no specific over-the-counter medication approved for the treatment of hemorrhoids. However, various remedies aimed at relieving pain, itching, and inflammation associated with hemorrhoids can be helpful. Bacterial infections associated with hemorrhoids can be treated with topical antibiotic creams. It is important to avoid prolonged sitting on the toilet to reduce the risk of straining, which can exacerbate hemorrhoid symptoms. Fiber is the most important dietary factor for preventing hemorrhoids. Increasing fiber intake can help prevent constipation, which is a common cause of hemorrhoids.

translations.source: Abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ dára kò sì wọ́n, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìlò ẹ̀ẹ̀kan péré yóò pèsè àjẹsára títí ayé fún àìsàn náà. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n gba ìwòsàn láti ara ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ náà ti ní ààbò títí ayé. Kà sí i nípa abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀. Àkóso ẹ̀fọn náà lè ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti dènà ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ṣe pàtàkì ní àwọn ìgbà tí ìpèsè abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára bá kéré tàbí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára kò tètè wà nílẹ̀. Lára àwọn àkóso ẹ̀fọn ni mímú àwọn ibi tí àwọn ẹ̀fọn ti lè bímọ kúrò àti pípa àwọn àgbà ẹ̀fọn àti ọ̀dọ́ wọn nípa lílo àwọn òògùn apakòkòrò ní àwọn àgbègbè tí ẹ̀fọn pọ̀ sí.
translations.reference: The yellow fever vaccine is safe and affordable, and a single dose provides life-long immunity against the disease. People who recover from yellow fever infection are also protected for life. Read more about the yellow fever vaccine. Mosquito control can also help to prevent yellow fever and is vital in situations where vaccination coverage is low or the vaccine is not immediately available. Mosquito control includes eliminating sites where mosquitoes can breed and killing adult mosquitoes and larvae by using insecticides in areas with high mosquito density.
translations.hypothesis: The spread of infectious diseases is a serious problem, and a single outbreak can have devastating consequences for communities. People who are infected with infectious diseases can suffer from a range of symptoms, and some diseases can be fatal. Here are some facts about the spread of infectious diseases. Control measures can help to prevent the spread of infectious diseases, and they are especially important in situations where the risk of infection is high, or where there is a limited supply of vaccines. Some control measures include identifying and isolating infected individuals, educating the public about how to prevent the spread of disease, and providing access to vaccines and other medical care in areas where disease is prevalent.

translations.source: Ìdásí àwùjọ nípa àwọn iṣẹ́, bí nínu àwọn kòtò ilé àti bíbo àwọn koto omi níbi tí àwọn ẹ̀fọn lè dàgbà sí jẹ́ ọ̀nà pàtàkì tí ó sì múnádóko láti ṣàkóso àwọn ẹ̀fọn.
translations.reference: Community involvement through activities, such as cleaning household drains and covering water containers where mosquitoes can breed is an important and effective way to control mosquitoes.
translations.hypothesis: Proper sanitation practices, including maintaining clean homes and water sources, are crucial and effective ways to control the spread of diseases.

translations.source: Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n nílò ìtọ́jú ni wọ́n "fi sílẹ̀ nínú ìnira" ìjábọ̀ tuntun ló gbé e jáde Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Lágbàyé (WHO) ti ṣe àtẹ̀jáde ìjábọ̀ tuntun lórí ànfààní sí mọ́fínì fún lílò nínú ìṣègùn, tí ó ń ṣàpèjúwe bí pínpín mọ́fínì káàkiri àgbáyé, gẹ́gẹ́ bí oògùn ara ríro tó ṣe pàtàkì, ò ṣe dọ́gba tí kò sì pé ìlò rẹ̀ nínú ìṣègùn. Ìjábọ̀ náà, tí àkòrí rẹ̀ jẹ́ "Left behind in pain", ṣàfihàn àwọn ìṣòro nínú níní ànfààní sí oògùn tó ṣe pàtàkì yìí tí ó sì tún pèsè iṣẹ́ láti mú àyípadà bá níní ànfààní aláìléwu nípasẹ̀ òfin tó dọ́gba. Pẹ̀lu bí mọ́fínì ṣe jẹ́ oògùn tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ tí owó rẹ̀ sì ṣe bí ẹni kéré fún ìtura ìrora tó lágbára, tí wọ́n gbé jáde láti ọdún 1977 nínú àtúnṣe àkọ́kọ́ àkọsílẹ Àwòṣe WHO fún Àwọn Oògùn Pàtàkì, ìyàtọ̀ tó wà nínú ànfààní káàkiri àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lágbára. Ìyàtọ̀ bí ìlọ́po 5- sí 63- ni àpapọ̀ agbede-méjì ìlò mọ́fínì tó wà láàrin àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n ìpawówọlé wọn pọ̀ àti àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré.
translations.reference: People with medical needs are “left behind in pain” reveals new report The World Health Organization (WHO) has published a new report on access to morphine for medical use, which describes how the global distribution of morphine, as a vital pain medicine, is unequal and does not fulfil the medical need. The report, titled “Left behind in pain”, highlights the problems in access to this essential medicine and offers actions to improve safe access through balanced policy. Despite morphine being an effective and relatively low-cost medicine for relieving strong pain, listed since 1977 in the first edition of the WHO Model list of Essential Medicines, the disparity in access across countries is stark. There is a 5- to 63-fold difference in the estimated median consumption of morphine between high-income countries and lower income countries.
translations.hypothesis: People in need of pain relief are "left behind" by a new report. The World Health Organization (WHO) has released a new report on access to essential medicines, highlighting the disparities in access to pain medication around the world, particularly opioids, which are crucial for managing severe pain, but are often unavailable or unaffordable. The report, titled "Left behind in pain," reveals the challenges in ensuring safe and effective pain management through policy and regulation. Despite the fact that opioids are effective medications for managing severe pain, they have been largely excluded from essential medicines lists since the WHO's initial list of essential medicines was established in 1977, leading to significant disparities in access across countries. The report found that the average number of essential pain medications available in low-income countries is just 5-6, compared to 25-30 in high-income countries.

translations.source: Bátánì lílo yátọ̀ gidi gan-an káàkiri àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ọrọ̀ wọn dọ́gba; Kò dọ́gba pẹ̀lú ohun tí ìṣègùn nílò, gẹ́gẹ́ bí àpapọ iye ònkà ọjọ́ tí àwọn ènìyàn fi wà nínú ìrora tàbí tí wọ́n kérora pẹ̀lú àìlè mí dáadáa tó le fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní àìlera àtìgbàdégbà ṣe fi hàn. Dátà náà tẹnumọ́ ti 2018 Lancet Commission tí ó ṣàpèjúwe àìsí ànfààní sí àwọn oògùn ara ríro gẹ́gẹ́ bí 'ọ̀kan nínú àwọn àìdọ́gba burúkú, tó farasin nínú ìlera àgbáyé' pẹ̀lú bí 10% àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tó lówó jùlọ ṣe ní 90% àwọn ópọ́ídì tó dọ́gba pẹ̀lú mọ́fínì. "Tí ó fi àwọn ènìyàn sílẹ̀ nínú ìnira nígbà tí àwọn oògùn tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ wà fún ìtọ́jú ìrora, pàápàá jùlọ ní ọ̀gangan ipò ìtọ́jú òpin-ìṣẹ̀mí, ní láti jẹ́ nǹkan àpérò fún àwọn aṣòfin," Dr Yukiko Nakatani, Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún Àwọn Èlò Oògùn àti Ìlera WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "A gbọ́dọ̀ tètè jà fún ànfààní tí ò léwu tó sì ṣe déédé àsìkò fún mọ́fínì fún àwọn tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀ fún ìlera nípasẹ òfin tó dọ́gba, níbi gbogbo."
translations.reference: Consumption pattern varies significantly across countries of similar wealth; it does not correspond to medical need, as indicated by the estimated number of days people are in pain or suffer with severe shortness of breath for people with a terminal illness. This data echoes that of the 2018 Lancet Commission which described the lack of access to pain relief medication as ‘one of the most heinous, hidden inequities in global health’ with the richest 10% of countries possessing 90% of distributed morphine-equivalent opioids. “Leaving people in pain when effective medicines are available for pain management, especially in the context of end-of-life care, should be a cause of serious concern for policy-makers,” says Dr Yukiko Nakatani, WHO Assistant Director-General for Medicines and Health Products. “We must urgently advocate for safe and timely access to morphine for those in medical need through balanced policy, everywhere.”
translations.hypothesis:
Significant disparities in access to essential medicines exist across countries, particularly concerning the availability of pain relief medications. This issue is highlighted by a 2018 Lancet Commission report, which described the lack of access to essential medicines as "one of the most pressing global health challenges," with the wealthiest countries accounting for 90% of the opioid consumption despite representing only 10% of the world's population.

"Keeping people in pain when effective pain relief medications are available, especially in areas like palliative care, is simply unacceptable," said Dr. Yukiko Nakatani, Assistant Director-General for Access to Medicines at the World Health Organization. "We need to ensure equitable access to safe and effective pain relief for all who need it, through appropriate regulation and access policies."

translations.source: Àwọn èrèdí fún ìyàtọ̀ náà ànfààní sí mọ́fínì fún lílò nínú ìṣègùn ni oríṣiríṣi nǹkan tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ pọ̀ ṣe òkùnfà rẹ̀ tó fi mọ́ àwọn aṣokùnfa tó jẹ mọ́ ìṣèjọba tó dára, àwọn ìlànà fún rírà àti pínpín kiri tó ṣe é gbáralé/ṣiṣẹ́, àwọn ohun èlò tó wà àti àwọn iṣẹ́ ìdagbàsókè ilé-iṣẹ́, tó fi mọ́ àwọn ìdènà tó tan mọ́ àwọn òfin àti ìlànà-iṣẹ́ aṣèdíwọ́ lápapọ̀, ìpèsè iṣẹ́ tí ò tó. Pẹ̀lú wíwo ọ̀gangan ipò oríṣiríṣi orílẹ̀-ède, àwọn aṣokùnfa, àwọn ìdènà àti kàn-án-pá fún iṣẹ́ yàtọ̀; ìwádìí alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ kan tí wọ́n gbékalẹ̀ nínú ìjábọ̀ yìí, pẹ̀lú àwọn olùjábọ̀ láti 105 Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ WHO, fi ìdí ìyàtọ̀ yìí múlẹ̀. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, àìsí déédé pínpín mọ́fínì àti àwọn ópọ́ídì alágbára mìíràn ní àwọn ilé-ìwòsàn nítorí àìtó owó ni wọ́n ṣe àkíyèsí rẹ̀ gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìdènà ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré àti àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn pọ̀. Ó lé ní ìdá mẹ́ta àwọn olùjábọ̀ káàkiri gbogbo ẹkùn agbègbè tí wọ́n tún ṣe àkíyèsí àwọn ìdènà tó ń wáyé látara àwọn aṣokùnfa òfin àti ìlànà-iṣẹ́ nígbà tí wọ́n tún wo pàtàkì ìlànà òfin àti ìlànà-iṣẹ́ nínú ṣíṣe àṣeyọrí ànfààní aláìléwu.
translations.reference: Reasons for the disparity Access to morphine for medical use is influenced by many interacting factors including enablers related to good governance, reliable/efficient procurement and supply processes, resource availability and capacity-building activities, along with barriers related to overly restrictive legislation and policies, inadequate service provision, and misinformed attitudes and perception. Given different country contexts, enablers, barriers and priorities for action vary; a stakeholder survey presented in this report, with respondents from 105 WHO Member States, confirms this variation. For example, irregular supply of morphine and other strong opioids at health facilities due to limited financing were commonly noted as a barrier in low- and middle-income countries compared to high-income countries. More than a third of respondents across all regions also noted barriers arising from legislative and regulatory factors while recognizing the importance of a legislative and regulatory framework in achieving safe access.
translations.hypothesis: The factors contributing to health inequities are multifaceted and include a range of social determinants of health, such as access to quality education, safe and affordable housing, healthy food, and employment opportunities, as well as systemic discrimination and historical injustices. These factors can create significant barriers to health for certain populations, leading to disparities in health outcomes. This report examines various country contexts, determinants, and indicators of health inequities, drawing on evidence from a comprehensive review of the literature. The report was developed by a working group of experts from 105 WHO Member States, who identified key areas for action to address health inequities. For example, the report highlights the importance of addressing poverty and other social determinants of health in both low- and high-income countries. The report also includes case studies from around the world that illustrate the impact of policy and programmatic interventions on health inequities, and it emphasizes the importance of evidence-based policies and programs in achieving health equity.

translations.source: Àwọn ìbéèrè ìdènà tí ò yẹ lè ṣe ìdènà fún ànfààní àwọn aláìsàn nítorí wọ́n máa ń ṣe ìdíwọ́ fún pínpín tàbí jẹ́ kí ìjúwe lílo oògùn àti fífún ni lóògùn nira fún àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ètò-ìlera. Ó ṣe pàtàkì láti mọ̀ pé ìrònú nípa àwọn ipa ewu ópọ́ídì tó ṣeéṣe jẹ́ tòótọ́, gẹ́gẹ́ bíi agbára wọn láti yọrí sí àìsàn àṣìlo ópọ́ídì. Fún ìdí èyí, òdiwọn ìṣọ́ra díẹ̀ nípa àwọn ewu lílo ópọ́ídì tó ṣeéṣe (b.a lílò fún ìrora aláìṣe-jẹjẹrẹ tó lágbára) ṣe pàtàkì fún ìlera àwùjọ lódiwọ̀n ìgbà tí lílo rẹ̀ bá ti di mímọ̀ dáadáa tó sì jẹ́ ìwọ̀nba fún ewu. Irú ìrònú bẹ́ẹ̀ ò gbọdọ̀ fojú réna àwọn ànfààní lílo ópọ́ídì nígbà tí ó bá ti fojú hàn nínú ìṣègùn àti tí àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ bá lò ó láìséwu. Àwọn ànfààní fún iṣẹ́ Ìjábọ̀ náà ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò aṣèrànwọ́ fún àwọn agbègbè fún iṣẹ́ tí ó ń gbèrò láti mú àyípadà bá anfààní aláìléwu sí mọ́fínì.
translations.reference: Unduly restrictive requirements could hamper patient access because they impede the flow of supply or make prescribing and dispensing difficult for health-care professionals. It is important to note that concerns about the potential harmful effects of opioids are valid, such as their potential to lead to opioid use disorder. For this reason, a certain amount of caution about the potential harms of opioid use (e.g. use in chronic non-cancer pain) is important for public health insofar as its use is well-informed and proportionate to risks. Such concerns should not undermine the benefits of opioid use when clinically indicated and when used safely by trained professionals. Opportunities for actionThe report presents a complementary set of areas for action that aim to improve safe access to morphine.
translations.hypothesis: Certain medical conditions that may be treated with opioids require careful consideration due to the potential for addiction or the need for long-term medication, which can be challenging for healthcare providers. It is crucial to understand that the risks associated with using strong opioids are significant, especially concerning the potential for developing chronic pain conditions. Therefore, a cautious approach to prescribing strong opioids (e.g., for acute, non-cancer pain) is essential for public health, and their use should be reserved for situations where the benefits clearly outweigh the risks. This type of caution should not discourage the appropriate use of opioids when they are clearly indicated and used safely under medical supervision. The medical community is developing programs to educate patients about the risks and benefits of opioid use and to provide support for those who may be struggling with addiction. These programs aim to improve patient safety and minimize the potential for harm.

translations.source: Lára rẹ̀ lati rí ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn ètò agbègbè oníwọ̀n-kékeré tàbí jákèjádò-ìpínlẹ̀ lórí mímú àyípadà bá ànfààní sí mọ́fínì fún lílò ìṣègùn pẹ̀lú àpapọ àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ tó ṣe pàtàkì àti àwọn ohun èlò fún ìtọ́jú ìtura tí wọ́n ṣe ní ìbámu pẹ̀lú Àkọsílẹ Àwòṣe Àwọn Oògùn Pàtàkì WHO àti Àwọn Àkójọpọ̀ Ìtọ́jú Ìtura WHO tó ṣe Pàtàkì. Lára rẹ̀ la tún ti rí mímú àyípadà bá ìṣèjọba, ìsọdìrọ̀rùn ìlànà rírà àti pínpín láti kojú èyíkéyìí àìṣedéédé, mímú àyípadà bá wíwá ohun èlò bíi owó ìrànwọ́ tó dúróore, ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera tó kájú òṣùwọ̀n, àti ṣíṣe ìkéde nípa àwọn ànfààní àti ewu tó ṣeéṣe látara lílo ópọ́ídì. Àṣeyọrí àwọn iṣẹ́ wọ̀nyìí máa gbára lé àjọṣepọ̀ àti ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ láàrín gbogbo àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ ní ìpele orílẹ̀-èdè, ẹkùn agbègbè, àti lágbàyé.
translations.reference: These include implementing small-scale regional or state-wide programmes on improving access to morphine for medical use with a package of essential services and products for palliative care that is formulated according to the WHO Model List of Essential Medicines and the WHO Essential Package of Palliative Care. They also include improving governance, streamlining procurement and supply processes to address any inefficiency, improving resourcing such as stable funding, enhancing competent skill sets of the health workforce, and raising awareness about both the benefits and potential harms of opioid use. The success of these actions will hinge on collaboration and cooperation among all stakeholders at national, regional, and global levels.
translations.hypothesis: The initiative focuses on improving access to essential medicines and health technologies, prioritizing the procurement of critical drugs and medical supplies in accordance with the World Health Organization's (WHO) Model List of Essential Medicines and the WHO's Priority Medical Products List. It also aims to strengthen governance, promote rational drug use, and reduce substandard and falsified medicines, utilizing tools such as sustainable financing mechanisms, capacity building for healthcare workers, and public awareness campaigns about the benefits and risks associated with the use of various medications. The success of these efforts relies on collaboration and cooperation among all stakeholders at the national, regional, and global levels.

translations.source: Àwọn Erékùṣù Kékeré gba láti gbé ìgbésẹ̀ akin lórí àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn àti ìlera ọpọlọ Lónì, Ìkéde Bridgetown lórí Àwọn Ààrùn tí kìí ràn (NCDs) àti Ìlera Ọpọlọ ni wọ́n ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ rẹ̀, láti kojú àwọn ààrùn aṣekúpani jùlọ lágbàyé ní Àwọn Ìpínlẹ̀ Onídágbàsókè Erékùṣù Kékeré (SIDS), tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu jùlọ. Ìkéde náà jẹ́ àbájáde pàtàkì fún Àpérò Mínísítà SIDS lórí Àwọn Ààrùn tí kìí ràn (NCDs) àti Ìlera Ọpọlọ, tó bẹ́rẹ̀ láti 14 sí 16 oṣù Òkudù, tí Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Lágbàyé (WHO) jọ ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Ajẹmọ́ Ilẹ̀ Amẹ́ríkà (PAHO) àti Ìjọba orílẹ̀-ède Barbados. NCDs ń kóbá SIDS lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀ - tí ó ń ṣokùnfà 74% gbogbo ikú lágbàyé - nítorí ìgbẹ́kẹ̀lé wọn lórí àwọn oúnjẹ tí wọ́n ń kó wọlé, àwọn ipa okòwò àti rògbòdìyàn ojú ọjọ́. Ìjábọ̀ WHO tuntun fi hàn pé 8 nínú àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 15 tí wọ́n ní ju 30% ewu ikú àìtọ́jọ́ látara ààrùn ọkàn, ààrùn jẹjẹrẹ, àtọ̀gbẹ, tàbí ààrùn ọ̀fìnkì tó le ni SIDS.
translations.reference: Small islands agree bold plan of action on noncommunicable diseases and mental health Today, the 2023 Bridgetown Declaration on Noncommunicable Diseases (NCDs) and Mental Health was launched, to address some of the world’s deadliest diseases in Small Island Developing States (SIDS), which are especially at risk. The Declaration is a key outcome of the SIDS Ministerial Conference on Noncommunicable Diseases (NCDs) and Mental Health, running from 14 to 16 June, co-hosted by the World Health Organization (WHO), the Pan American Health Organization (PAHO) and the Government of Barbados. SIDS are disproportionately impacted by NCDs – which cause 74% of all deaths globally – due to their reliance on imported food, commercial influences and the climate crisis. A new WHO report shows that 8 of the 15 countries with more than a 30% risk of premature death from cardiovascular disease, cancer, diabetes, or chronic respiratory disease are SIDS.
translations.hypothesis: Small Island Developing States (SIDS) are taking steps to address the growing burden of non-communicable diseases (NCDs) and other chronic illnesses. The Bridgetown Declaration on NCDs and Health, launched today, aims to tackle the most prevalent diseases globally within the SIDS, which are particularly vulnerable. The declaration is a key outcome of the Ministerial Conference on NCDs and Health for SIDS, held from October 14th to 16th, and co-organized by the World Health Organization (WHO), the Pan American Health Organization (PAHO), and the government of Barbados. NCDs are a major challenge for SIDS, accounting for 74% of all deaths globally, due to factors such as unhealthy diets, lack of physical activity, and tobacco use. A recent WHO report found that 8 of the 15 SIDS with the highest rates of premature mortality from cardiovascular diseases, cancers, diabetes, or chronic respiratory diseases.

translations.source: Àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 10 tí wọ́n ní ònkà iye ènìyàn sísanra tó pọ̀ jùlọ lágbàyé ni gbogbo wọ́n jẹ́ SIDS ní Pàsífíkì, níbi tí 45% àwọn àgbàlagbà ti sanra. Àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ ìlera ọpọlọ wọ́pọ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè SIDS, tó sì ń kóbá àpapọ 15.2% àwọn ènìyàn ní Caribbean àti 11.2% àwọn ènìyàn ní Pàsífíkì. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní ìpèníjà ìlera ọpọlọ ń kojú ọ̀pọ̀ ewu ikú àìtọ́jọ́, tó fi mọ́ látara àwọn ìpèníjà ara tí wọn kò tọ́jú àti látara ìgbẹ̀mí ara ẹni. Àbùku, àìtó àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ pàtàkì àti ipa àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ ń ṣe àfikún sí ìṣẹ̀lẹ ìpèníjà ní SIDS tí ó nílò ìgbésẹ̀ ojú ẹsẹ̀. Ìkéde Bridgetown 2023 ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ ìgboyà láti kojú oríṣiríṣi àwọn ìṣòro àwùjọ, àyíká, ọrọ̀-ajéàti okòwò tó ń yọrí sí àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ NCDs àti ìlera ọpọlọ.
translations.reference: The 10 countries with the highest obesity rates globally are all SIDS in the Pacific, where over 45% of adults live with obesity. Mental health conditions are common in SIDS countries, affecting an estimated 15.2% of the population in the Caribbean and 11.2% of the population in the Pacific. People with mental health conditions face a higher risk of premature mortality, including from unaddressed physical health conditions and from suicide. Stigma, specialized staff shortages and the impact of climate change contribute to a challenging situation in SIDS that requires immediate attention. The 2023 Bridgetown Declaration outlines bold steps to address the range of social, environmental, economic and commercial issues that lead to NCDs and mental health conditions.
translations.hypothesis: The 10 countries with the highest rates of non-communicable diseases (NCDs) globally are all Small Island Developing States (SIDS) in the Pacific, where 45% of adults suffer from these conditions. NCDs are prevalent in SIDS, accounting for approximately 15.2% of deaths in the Caribbean and 11.2% of deaths in the Pacific. People with NCDs face a higher risk of premature death due to complications from their conditions and related health issues. Factors such as limited access to healthcare, a shortage of essential medical personnel, and the impact of climate change exacerbate the burden of NCDs in SIDS, requiring urgent action. The Bridgetown Initiative 2023 outlines concrete steps to address various social challenges, including climate change, debt relief, and sustainable development, all of which contribute to the prevalence of NCDs and related health issues.

translations.source: Èyí tí wọ́n gbékalẹ̀ nípasẹ̀ ìlànà aláfibọ̀ tí SIDS léwájú rẹ̀ 'fun' ara wọn, ìkéde náà ṣe àfihàn pé àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ NCDs àti ìlera ọpọlọ ò ṣe kojú bó ṣe yẹ láì dáhùn sí rògbòdìyàn ojú ọjọ́. "Ìgbésẹ̀ ìgboyà fún ojú ọjọ́, ìlera tó dára, àti ìgbáyé-gbádùn wa gbára lé kíkojú àti àtúntò ìpèsè owó lágbàyé láti ṣílẹ́kun bílíọ̀nù nínú ìdókówò, tí kò sì ní jẹ́ kí ìyà púpọ̀ jẹ àwọn fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè onídágbàsókè láti san gbèse wọn," Ọlọ́lá jùlọ Mia Amor Mottley, Alákòso Ìjọba fún orílẹ̀-èdè Barbados ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Ìpèsè owó fún àmuyẹ àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ àti àdínkù ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n kángun séwu jùlọ tún ṣe pàtàkì, pẹ̀lú àlàyé lórí àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn àti ìlera ọpọlọ "Àwọn ìpèníjà tí Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Onídágbàsókè Erékùṣù Kékeré farakínra ó sì pín sí oríṣiríṣi. Àyípadà ojú ọjọ́, ìbàjẹ́ àyíká, àìdọ́gba àwùjọ àti ọrọ̀ ajé, àti àwọn okòwò aṣokùnfa ìlera tó ń mú kí ẹrù NCDs àti ìlera ọpọlọ ó burú síi," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀.
