translations.reference: Developed through an inclusive process led ‘for’ and ‘by’ SIDS, the declaration highlights that NCDs and mental health conditions cannot be properly addressed without responding to the climate crisis. “Bold action for our climate, good health, and well-being relies on redressing and reorganizing global financing to unlock billions in investment, while making it less punishing for developing countries to pay their debts,” said Her Excellency Mia Amor Mottley, Prime Minister of Barbados. “Funding for climate change adaptation and mitigation in the most vulnerable countries is also key, with noncommunicable diseases and mental health accounted for.” "The challenges faced by Small Island Developing States are interconnected and multifaceted. Climate change, environmental degradation, social and economic inequalities, and the commercial determinants of health exacerbate the burden of NCDs and mental health conditions," said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General.
translations.hypothesis: Initiated through a policy brief released by the SIDS, the statement highlights that the burden of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) and multiple health challenges is disproportionately high in small island developing states. "Progress towards healthy lives, well-being, and quality of life requires a fundamental shift and a global commitment to address the root causes of ill-health, and to ensure that no one is left behind," stated Mia Amor Mottley, Prime Minister of Barbados. "Increased investment in health promotion and prevention in vulnerable countries is crucial, along with addressing the underlying social determinants of health." The priorities identified by the Small Island Developing States are diverse. They include: climate change, natural disasters, food security, economic instability, and the social determinants of health that exacerbate NCDs and multiple health challenges. Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the WHO, stated, "These are complex and interconnected challenges."

translations.source: "Pẹ̀lú àìtó ohun èlò àti ìdíwọ́ agbègbè wọn, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè wọ̀nyìí ti ṣàfihàn ìfaradà tó lápẹẹrẹ lójú ìpọ́njú. Lápapọ̀, a gbọ́dọ̀ wá ọ̀nà àbáyọ tó dá lórí ìdọ́gba, ìfaradà, àti alálòpẹ́. A gbọ́dọ̀ ró ètò ìlera lágbára, ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn ìlànà fún ìdènà àti ìtètè dámọ̀, kí wọ́n sì mú ìṣàmúlò ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera NCD àti ìlera ọpọlọ sínú ètò ìlera alábọ́dé. "Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ tún jẹ́jẹ̀ẹ́ pé WHO máa ṣiṣẹ́ láti ṣe àkójọ ìpèsè owó láti mú ìdàgbàsókè bá ìfaradà ojú ọjọ́, àwọn ilé-ìwòsàn alálòpẹ́ láwùjọ ní SIDS. WHO máa tẹ̀síwájú láti pè fún ìpèsè owó 'ìpàdánù àti bíbàjẹ́' fún ìṣàmúlò àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ àti ìdènà ìdókówò ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré.
translations.reference: "Despite their limited resources and geographical constraints, these nations have shown remarkable resilience in the face of adversity. Together, we must forge a path that is based on equity, resilience, and sustainability. We must strengthen health systems, enhance prevention and early detection measures, and prioritize the integration of NCD and mental health services into primary health care. "The Director-General also pledged that WHO would work to mobilize financial resources to develop climate resilient, environmentally sustainable health-care facilities in the SIDS. WHO will also continue to advocate for ‘loss and damage’ funding for climate change adaptation and mitigation investments in lower income countries.
translations.hypothesis: "With their unique challenges and regional contexts, these countries have demonstrated remarkable resilience in the face of adversity. Overall, a sustainable approach is needed that addresses poverty, inequality, and vulnerability. We must strengthen health systems, promote policies for prevention and early detection, and integrate non-communicable disease and maternal health services into primary healthcare." The report also emphasized that the WHO will work to mobilize resources to support the fight against preventable diseases and improve healthcare infrastructure in Small Island Developing States. The WHO will continue to advocate for increased funding for "prevention and care" to address preventable diseases and improve health outcomes in low-income countries.

translations.source: Gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀kan lára gbígba Ìkéde náà, SIDS forúkọsílẹ̀ láti gbé ìgbésẹ̀ ìpẹ̀jọ́, tí yóò kárí àwọn agbègbè bíi bíbá gbogbo àwùjọ sọ̀rọ̀, sísáré ṣe àmúlò rẹ̀, wíwá àwọn ìdókówò, Ìbádọ́gba káàkiri àwọn ìjọba àti ṣíṣe àlàyé fún ìtẹ̀síwájú. Àpérò náà tún ṣàfihàn àwọn ìfọkànsìn pàtó láti ọ̀dọ SIDS lórí NCDs àti ìlera ọpọlọ, tó fi mọ́ láti mú àyípadà bá ànfààní sí ìtọ́jú ìlera ọpọlọ ní àwọn ilé-ìwòsàn ètò ìlera alábọ́dé, ṣe ìsọdọ̀tun òfin ìdarí tábà láti kárí siga-oníná àti ìfàsímú, kí wọ́n sì mú àdínkù bá lílo iyọ̀ àti ṣúgà àwọn ènìyàn. Àwọn ìfọkànsìn wọ̀nyìí dọ́gba pẹ̀lú àwọn tí WHO 'rà jùlọ' - ètò àwọn ìdásí tí ò ga ju ara lọ, adálórí-ẹ̀rí láti kojú NCDs. Àkọsílẹ̀ àwọn tí wọ́n rà julọ ni wọ́n ṣe ìsọdọ̀tun rẹ̀ ní àìpẹ́ yìí níbi Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé ní oṣù Èbìbí láti ṣe àfikún ìdènà onípele kejì fún ibà aromọléegun, àmójútó alágbára àti onígbà-pípẹ́ fún ikọ́fe àti ààrùn aṣèdíwọ́ òpó ìpínjẹ̀ tó lágbára bákan náà ni oríṣiríṣi ìdásí fún jẹjẹrẹ àbáwọlé ilé ọmọ, ọyàn, abọ́dìí, ẹ̀dọ̀ àti ìgbà èwe, àti ìtọ́jú ààrùn jẹjẹrẹ tó péye fún àwọn tí wọ́n ní KASA. "SIDS ní ilẹ̀ Amẹ́ríkà ṣì kángun séwu gidi gan-an sí NCDs.
translations.reference: As part of agreeing the Declaration, SIDS signed up to undertake a suite of actions, covering areas such as engaging all of society, accelerating implementation, securing investments, aligning across governments and accounting for progress. The conference also showcased specific commitments by SIDS on NCDs and mental health, including to improve access to mental health care in primary care facilities, update tobacco control legislation to cover e-cigarettes and vaping, and reduce populations’ salt and sugar intake. These commitments align with WHO’s ‘best buys’ – a set of cost-effective, evidence-based interventions to tackle NCDs. The list of best buys was recently updated at the World Health Assembly in May to include secondary prevention for rheumatic fever, acute and long-term management of asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease as well as several interventions for cervical, breast, colorectal, liver and childhood cancer, and the comprehensive treatment of cancer for those living with HIV. “SIDS in the Americas remain overwhelmingly vulnerable to NCDs.
translations.hypothesis: As one of the key outcomes of the High-Level Meeting on SIDS, a commitment was made to accelerate progress on a range of issues, including promoting inclusive social dialogue, strengthening resilience, addressing vulnerabilities, fostering partnerships, and creating an enabling environment for sustainable development. The commitment also highlighted specific priorities for SIDS related to non-communicable diseases (NCDs) and health, including strengthening primary healthcare systems, implementing evidence-based policies to reduce tobacco and alcohol consumption, and promoting healthy diets and physical activity. These priorities align with the WHO's "top priorities" – addressing the social determinants of health and promoting integrated approaches to NCDs. The commitment was further reinforced at the Global Health Security Summit in February, where a second phase of the SIDS Partnership Action Plan was launched, focusing on strengthening health systems and addressing infectious and non-communicable diseases. This included specific actions for improving maternal and child health, addressing malnutrition, preventing and controlling HIV, tuberculosis, and malaria, and strengthening health systems to respond to emerging health threats. "SIDS in the Americas face a significant and growing burden of NCDs."

translations.source: Àpérò Mínísítà yìí wáyé ní àkókò aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ yóò sì ṣe kókó láti fi kún ohùn àti àwọn ohun tí SIDS nílò, mímú NCDs àti ìlera ọpọlọ wá sí iwájú àkọsílẹ àwọn ohun tó ṣe pàtàkì jùlọ fún wa," Dr Jarbas Barbosa, Olùdarí PAHO, àti Olùdarí Ẹkùn Agbègbè fún Ilẹ̀ Amẹ́ríkà WHO. "Láti gùn lé ìlọsíwájú yìí, a gbọ́dọ̀ gbé ìgbésẹ̀ báyìí. PAHO ti ṣetán láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìtètè ṣàmúlò àwọn ìdásí ní ẹkùn agbègbè wa, tó fi mọ́ ìtọ́jú ojoojúmọ́ fún àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ wọ̀nyìí sínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ètò ìlera alábọ́dé àti lílo ànfààní àwọn ìmọ̀ tuntun bíi àwọn irinṣẹ́ ìlera àkósódù." Àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè SIDS ní àkọsílẹ̀ àti máa ṣe bọ̀ fún ìdarí ìpinnu-ṣíṣe lágbàyé; àdéhùn ojú ọ̀na Port of Spain àti SAMOA ló ṣínà fún ìkéde òṣèlú UN àkọ́kọ́-irú-rẹ̀ lórí NCDs ní ọdún 2018. Àpérò àti Ìkéde náà máa ṣe ìfitóni yóò sì ṣàpẹẹrẹ Ìpàdé Onípele-Gíga Àpérò Àpapọ UN tó ń bọ̀ lórí kíkárí ìlera lágbàyé ní oṣù Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2023 àti lórí NCDs ní ọdún 2025.
translations.reference: This Ministerial Conference comes at a unique time and will be key to amplifying the voice and needs of SIDS, bringing NCDs and mental health to the forefront of our priority list,” said Dr Jarbas Barbosa, PAHO Director, and WHO Regional Director for the Americas. “To build on this momentum, we must act now. PAHO is ready to support accelerated implementation of interventions in our region, including mainstreaming care for these conditions into primary health services and taking advantage of innovations such as digital health tools.” SIDS countries have a track record of driving global decision-making; their Port of Spain and SAMOA pathway agreements paved the way for the first-ever UN political declaration on NCDs in 2018. The conference and the Declaration will inform and shape forthcoming UN General Assembly High-Level Meetings on universal health coverage in September 2023 and on NCDs in 2025.
translations.hypothesis: This Ministerial Meeting, held at a critical time, will focus on addressing the urgent needs of Small Island Developing States (SIDS), including the rising burden of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) and promoting overall health. "To build on this progress, we must take concrete steps forward," said Dr. Jarbas Barbosa, Director of PAHO and Regional Director for the Americas of the World Health Organization. "PAHO is committed to supporting the development of national strategies in our region, integrating the prevention and control of these diseases into primary health care programs and leveraging new technologies, such as telemedicine." SIDS play a vital role in global governance; the Port of Spain and SAMOA pathways provide a framework for understanding the unique challenges faced by these nations in addressing NCDs. The Meeting and the Pathways will inform the upcoming High-Level Meeting on Global Health at the UN General Assembly in September 2023 and the High-Level Meeting on NCDs in 2025.

translations.source: Ìpàdé Ètò Ààrùn Ìgbóná Tí Wọ́n Ti Patì Lágbàyé WHO parí pẹ̀lu ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìpè fún ìgbésẹ̀ lágbàyé Àwọn aṣojú ìjọba àti àwọn aṣojú kúrò níbi Ìpàdé Àwọn Alájọṣepọ̀ Ètò Ààrùn Ìgbóná Tí Wọ́n Ti Patì Lágbàyé pẹ̀lú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìpè láti ṣiṣẹ́ láti ọ̀dọ Olùdarí rẹ̀ Dr Ibrahim Socé Fall. Pẹ̀lú bí ìpàdé oríṣiríṣi orílẹ̀-èdè náà, tí àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ṣànkò-ṣànkò 300 wà níbẹ̀ náà, ṣe wá sópin ní 13 ọjọ́ Ìṣẹ́gun oṣù Òkudù, Dr Fall rọ àwọn akópa láti ṣàmúlò àkọmọ̀nà pé "tí a ò bá pe ara wa níjà, a ò lè dàgbà sókè a ò sì lè yípadà" . Nínú ètò tó fẹjú tó wáyé láti ṣe àtúnyẹwò ìlọsíwájú tako ìlànà-iṣẹ́ WHO fún àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì 2021-2030 àti láti pèsè òye tó dára lórí àwọn iṣẹ́ NTD lágbàyé láti àpapọ ojú ìwòye adálórí-ààrùn àti ààlà-fibọ̀, àwọn alájọṣepọ̀, àwọn olùpèsè owó, àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ onímọ̀ ẹ̀rọ, àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ àti àwọn aṣojú orílẹ̀-èdè gbọ́dọ̀ ṣe àtúnṣe sí ìfọkànsìn wọn láti ṣe àṣeyọrí àfojúsùn èròngbà kan: ilé-ayé tí kò sí àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì tí yóò bá fi di ọdún 2030. Àkòrí àkọ́kọ́ ìpàdé náà dá lórí àfojúsun "Àwùjọ kan tó ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ Ètò NTD Lágbàyé kalẹ̀".
translations.reference: WHO’s Global Neglected Tropical Diseases Programme Partners’ Meeting ends with stirring call for worldwide action Delegates and representatives left the Global Neglected Tropical Diseases Programme Partners’ Meeting with a stirring call to action from its director Dr Ibrahima Socé Fall. As the multinational meeting, attended by some 300 key partners, came to an end in Geneva on Tuesday 13 June, Dr Fall urged participants to take on board the message that, “if we do not challenge ourselves, we cannot evolve and improve”. In a wide-ranging event held to review progress against the WHO road map for neglected tropical diseases 2021–2030 and generate a better understanding of global NTD operations from both disease-specific and cross-cutting perspectives, partners, donors, technical partners, institutions and country representatives reinforced their commitment to achieving an ambitious target: a world free from neglected tropical diseases by 2030. The meeting’s primary theme centred around the vision of “One community delivering one Global NTD Programme”.
translations.hypothesis: The World Health Organization's (WHO) high-level meeting on neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) concluded with a series of commitments for global progress. Government representatives and partners participating in the meeting received numerous pledges from its chairman, Dr. Ibrahim Socé Fall. With a diverse range of countries represented, and approximately 300 participants present over the 13-day event, which concluded on July 13th, Dr. Fall urged delegates to embrace a shared vision: "If we don't act now, we cannot go back, and we cannot afford to wait." The meeting, which focused on reviewing the WHO's strategy for NTDs for the period 2021-2030 and promoting a better understanding of global NTD efforts to improve health outcomes and reduce disease transmission, saw partners, donors, researchers, industry representatives, and government officials reaffirm their commitment to achieving a common goal: a world free of NTDs by 2030. A key outcome of the meeting was the launch of the "Global NTD Alliance."

translations.source: Wọ́n mú ọgbọ́n tiwọn látara àwọn àyípadà ọlọgbọ́n pàtàkì mẹ́ta tí wọ́n ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún nínú ìlànà-iṣẹ́ - ìsọdi torílẹ̀-èdè lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀, wíwọn àwọn ipa kìí ṣe àwọn ìlànà, àti ìfọkànsìn fún àwọn iṣẹ́ ààlà-fibọ̀ àti àjọṣepọ̀ fún iṣẹ́ tó lágbára - àwọn ìjírórò náà jẹ́ ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tòótọ́, ó ń ṣíni-lójú ó sì ń tàn káàkiri bákan náà pẹ̀lú ìdí fún ìlera adọ́gba àti ìdájọ́ òdodo láti kojú àwọn NTD. Irú ìlànà bẹ́ẹ̀ nìkan, tí ìpàdé náà gbọ́, lè mú àyípadà tí àwọn àwùjọ kọ̀ọ̀kan tí wọ́n kúṣẹ̀ jùlọ lágbàyé nílò láti ṣe àṣeyọrí Àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ 2030 Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé. Nínú irú ìpàdé àgbáyé àkọ́kọ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ láti ìgbà tí wọ́n ti ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ náà ní oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2021, àwọn aṣojú ìjọba tún gbọ́ láti ọ̀dọ Olùdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́-papọ̀ WHO Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. Nígbà tí ó ń kí àwọn akópa káàbọ̀ níbi ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ àpèjẹ ọlọ́jọ́-méjì náà, Dr Tedros sọ wí pé, "Ìjókòó yìí ṣàpèjúwe nǹkan tí WHO wà fún - kí àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ máa kóra jọ láti ṣàmúlò agbára àjọni wọn láti kojú àwọn ìpèníjà ìlera lágbàyé tí ìkankan nínú wa ò lè kojú lóun nìkan. "Ìpèníjà oríṣiríṣi bíi àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì nílò ìlànà oríṣiríṣi, nǹkan tí a sì mú wá nìyẹn, lápapọ̀ ".
translations.reference: Taking their cues from the three key strategic shifts advocated in the road map – greater country ownership, measuring impacts not process, and a commitment to cross-cutting activities and collaboration for greater efficiency – discussions were pragmatic, clear-sighted and suffused also with the need for health equity and justice to tackle NTDs. Only such an approach, the meeting heard, can bring about the change that is needed by some of the world’s poorest communities to achieve the United Nations 2030 Sustainable Development Goals. In the first such global meeting since the launch of the road map in January 2021, delegates heard also from WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. Welcoming participants at the beginning of the two-day symposium, Dr Tedros said, “This gathering illustrates what WHO is all about – partners coming together to leverage their collective strength to address global health challenges that none of us could address alone. “A multifaceted challenge like neglected tropical diseases takes a multifaceted approach, and that is what we bring, together”.
translations.hypothesis: The initiative, driven by three key pillars – expanded multilingualism, a focus on impact rather than processes, and increased funding for neglected tropical diseases and global health security – represents a significant shift. These changes are crucial for improving global health and promoting equity in addressing Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs). This type of approach, as highlighted at the meeting, is essential for achieving the goals of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and the World Health Organization's (WHO) agenda. During this important global meeting, which followed the launch of the initiative in September 2021, government representatives also heard from WHO Director-General Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. In his opening remarks at the two-day event, Dr. Tedros stated, "This meeting exemplifies what the WHO is all about – bringing partners together to leverage their collective power to address global health challenges that none of us can solve alone." He added, "Different challenges require different approaches, and that is what we are seeing here."

translations.source: Àwọn aṣojú ìjọba àti àwọn akópa lo ọjọ́ méji tó tẹ̀lé e láti ṣàwárí àwọn àlàfo pàtàkì àti àwọn ìpèníjà láti ṣe àṣeyọrí àwọn èròngbà onípele-gíga àti abẹ́lé, àwọn ìpèníjà nílé-lóko. Lára wọn ìpèníjà nílé-lóko wọ̀nyẹn lati rí ríríi dájú pé àwọn ènìyàn tí àwọn NTD bá ṣe àkóbá fún tààrà, àti àwọn tí wọ́n ní ojúṣe tààrà fún ṣíṣe àwọn ìdásí tako àwọn NTD, ni wọ́n bèèrè fún tí wọ́n sì gbà wọlé, pàápàá jùlọ níbi èyí tó níṣe pẹ̀lú ìbánisọ̀rọ̀ àti ìfọ̀rọ̀wérọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwùjọ. Ìpàdé náà tún ṣe àkọsílẹ̀ pàtàkì ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn àwùjọ ìfikọ́ra bákan náà, láti lè mú ìdàgbàsókè bá ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe àti ìgboyà àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ NTD lágbàyé. Nínú àfikún ìjírórò, àwọn aṣojú ìjọba fẹnukò lórí àwọn ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àti àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ tó ṣe é ṣe láti ṣàmúlò iṣẹ́ fún àwọn àfojúsùn tí wọ́n là kalẹ̀ tí wọ́n sì jọ ṣètò papọ̀ fún Ètò Amúṣẹ́ṣe Àgbàyé fún 2025-2030. Àwọn igbéṣẹ̀ pàtàkì àti ìpinnu tó ń wáyé látara ìpàdé náà la ti rí àwọn àbá fún ìrólágbára àti ìṣàmúlò àwọn ìkànnì ìdarí orílẹ̀-èdè láti ṣe àrídájú ìdarí fún ètò àjọṣe àti fún àmójútó àti ìgbéléwọ̀n, àti láti ṣe àrídájú àmójútó ojú-òpóìpèsè tó ṣiṣẹ́ lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀ láàrín àwọn ètò orílẹ̀-èdè.
translations.reference: Delegates and participants spent the subsequent two days identifying the major gaps and challenges to achieving high-level goals and local, on-the-ground challenges. Those on-the-ground challenges include ensuring that people directly affected by NTDs, and those directly responsible for administering interventions against NTDs, are solicited and acknowledged, particularly in terms of communication and community engagement. The meeting noted also the importance of developing communities of practice, in order to build the capacity and confidence of the global NTD workforce. In further discussions, delegates agreed on principles and practical steps to implement action towards the stated targets and planned together for a Global Action Plan for 2025–2030. Key actions and decisions arising from the meeting included recommendations for strengthening and operationalizing national coordination platforms to ensure coordination for joint planning and for monitoring and evaluation, and to ensure more efficient supply chain management within national systems.
translations.hypothesis: Government officials and partners convened for two days to discuss key priorities and strategies for achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and addressing challenges in the workplace. Among the topics discussed was the need to ensure that individuals affected by Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs), and those with disabilities who are working to implement NCD programs, have equal access to opportunities and are treated with respect, particularly through inclusive communication and engagement with communities. The meeting also emphasized the importance of strengthening national health systems to improve the effectiveness and efficiency of NCD programs worldwide. Furthermore, government representatives shared best practices and steps that can be taken to promote employment for people with disabilities, and they collaborated on the development of the Global Action Plan for NCDs 2025-2030. The key outcomes and decisions from the meeting are expected to inform national policies and strategies to promote health and well-being, and to address health inequities across various national programs.

translations.source: Ìpàdé Àwọn Alájọṣepọ̀ náà wáyé bákan náà ní ìparí ìlànà àtúnyẹwò iṣẹ́ pàtàkì láàrín Ẹ̀ka Ìdènà Àwọn Ààrùn Ìgbóná Tí Wọ́n Ti Patì WHO. Láti bíi oṣù mẹ́rin kọjá, WHO ti ń gbọ́ àfikún àti ìjẹ́rìsí láti ọ̀dọ gbogbo àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ àti àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ rẹ̀, láti lè rí i dájú pé òun fúnra rẹ̀ ní àwọn àwòmọ́ ṣíṣe ìṣirò, kíkó àkóyawọ́ àti ìṣe déédé tí ó gbà wọlé tó sì ń bèèrè fún. Lára àwọn àwòmọ́ náà ni rírí i dájú pé wọ́n ṣe àdápadà NTD nínú àgbékalẹ̀ ìlera lágbàyé. Ṣíṣe ìpàdé pẹ̀lú àwọn akópa jẹ́ ìkíni káàbọ̀ fún àtẹnumọ́ lórí àwọn ojúṣe pàtàkì tí àwùjọ NTD lè ṣe tí ó sì gbọdọ̀ ṣe níbi ìdásí àwọn ìpèníjà káàkiri bíi Kíkárí Ìlera Lágbàyé, Ìlera Kan àti idúkokò àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ lágbàyé tó ń lékún. Àwọn aṣojú ìjọba tí wọ́n wá fúnra wọn ni wọ́n tún ráàyè gbádùn ìpàtẹ ọjà tí wọ́n fi sọrí àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì ní olú ilé-iṣẹ́ WHO.
translations.reference: The Partners’ Meeting took place also at the end of a major functional review process within WHO’s Department of Control of Neglected Tropical Diseases. Over the course of the past four months, WHO has been hearing contributions and testimony from all its partners and stakeholders, in order to ensure that it itself embodies the values of accountability, transparency and equity that it espouses and demands. Those values also include ensuring that NTDs are repositioned in the global health architecture. Meeting participants welcomed the emphasis on the key roles that the NTD community can and must play in contributing to wider challenges such as Universal Health Coverage, One Health and the increasing threat of global climate change. Delegates attending in person were also able to enjoy an exhibition dedicated to neglected tropical diseases at WHO headquarters.
translations.hypothesis: The meeting of partners took place following a comprehensive review of the World Health Organization's (WHO) neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) program. For the past four months, the WHO has been gathering feedback and recommendations from all partners and stakeholders to ensure that the program is effective, accountable, and responsive to the needs of the communities it serves. A key focus of the review was to assess the impact of NTDs on global health. The meeting with partners was crucial for discussing the key priorities for the NTD program and how it can best address the challenges posed by global health crises such as the COVID-19 pandemic. Government representatives who attended the meeting also discussed market-based solutions for addressing the diseases that are covered by the WHO's NTD program.

translations.source: Wọ́n ṣíṣọ lójú àtò àwọn pánẹ́lì onírin kan láti ṣàpèjúwe àwọn májẹ̀mú 20 tí ó parapọ̀ di ìpínsísọ̀rí NTD, àwọn wọ̀nyìí ni wọ́n sì tẹ̀lé pẹ̀lú fọ́tò àti ìròyìn tó ń ṣàpèjúwe àwọn iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n ti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ láti bíi àìmọye ọdún sẹ́yìn láti kojú wọn.
translations.reference: A set of metal panels was commissioned to illustrate the 20 conditions that make up the NTD grouping, and these were accompanied by photographs and information illustrating the actions that have been put in place over the course of many decades to tackle them.
translations.hypothesis: They presented a series of panels featuring 20 individuals who have overcome Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs). Each panel included a photograph and a story detailing their journey and the steps they took to manage their condition and live healthy lives.

translations.source: Ìpàdé Àwọn Alájọṣepọ̀ Ètò Ààrùn Ìgbóná Tí Wọ́n Ti Patì Lágbàyé, 12-13 oṣù Òkudù ọdún 2023 Àwọn aṣojú àwùjọ NTD lágbàyé máa kóra jọ ní Geneva ní ọ̀sẹ̀ tó ń bọ̀ láti kópa nínú Ìpàdé Àwọn Alájọṣepọ̀ Ètò Ààrùn Ìgbóná Tí Wọ́n Ti Patì, tí yóò wáyé ní gbọ̀gan olú ilé-iṣẹ́ náà ní 12-13 oṣù Òkudù ọdún 2023. Àwọn èròngbà ìpàdé náà ni láti ṣe àtúnyẹwò ìtẹ̀síwájú lórí ìlànà-iṣẹ́ WHO fún àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì 2021-2030 àti láti pèsè òye tó dára fún àwọn iṣẹ́ NTD lágbàyé láti àpapọ ojú ìwòye adálórí-ààrùn àti ààlà-fibọ̀, bákan náà ni ṣíṣe ìjírórò tó fẹjú káàkiri àfojúsùn fún ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ One WHO ní orí Ètò NTD Lágbàyé kan. Nígbà tó ṣe wí pé WHO ló pe ìpàdé àgbáyé àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ NTD ní ọdún 2007 àti 2017, ètò kẹ́ta tó ń bọ̀ lọ́nà yìí ni àkọ́kọ́ láti ìgbà tí wọ́n ti ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ náà ní oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2021. ìlànà-iṣẹ́ WHO fún àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì 2021-2030 jẹ́ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ tó kún fọ́fọ́ tí ó sàlàyé àwọn àfojúsùn, àwọn ìlànà fún ìbámu pẹ̀lú Àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ àti àwọn èròngbà àgbáyé mìíràn àti ìpè fún àyípadà ọlọgbọ́n nínú àwọn ìlànà NTD pẹ̀lú èròngbà láti fi òpin sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì ní ọdún 2030.
translations.reference: Global Neglected Tropical Diseases Programme Partners’ Meeting, 12–13 June 2023 Representatives of the global NTD community will gather in Geneva next week to participate in the Global Neglected Tropical Diseases Programme Partners’ Meeting, which takes place in headquarters’ auditorium on 12-13 June 2023. The objectives of the meeting are to review progress on the WHO road map for neglected tropical diseases 2021–2030 and generate a better understanding of global NTD operations from both disease-specific and cross-cutting perspectives, as well as to engage in wide-ranging discussions around the vision for One WHO delivering on one Global NTD Programme. While previous global meetings of NTD partners were convened by WHO in 2007 and 2017, this third upcoming event is the first since the launch of the road map in January 2021. The WHO road map for neglected tropical diseases 2021–2030 is a comprehensive framework that outlines targets, mechanisms for alignment with the Sustainable Development Goals and other global agendas and calls for strategic shifts in NTD approaches with the aim of ending the epidemic of neglected tropical diseases by 2030.
translations.hypothesis:
Global Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) Partnership Meeting, October 12-13, 2023.

Global NTD community representatives will convene in Geneva next week to participate in the Global NTDs Partnership Meeting, which will be held at the organization's headquarters on October 12-13, 2023.

The meeting's agenda includes a review of progress on the WHO's 2021-2030 framework for NTDs, as well as a discussion of strategies to improve the global response to NTDs, including efforts to reduce the burden of disease and disability, and to promote health equity. The meeting will also focus on the implementation of the One WHO approach to the global NTD program.

The WHO has convened global NTD partnership meetings in the past, in 2007 and 2017. This meeting is the first since the launch of the WHO's 2021-2030 framework for NTDs in April 2021.

The WHO's 2021-2030 framework for NTDs is a comprehensive plan that outlines the organization's priorities, strategies, and targets for the next decade. It includes a focus on reducing the burden of NTDs by 75% by 2030, as well as on improving access to essential medicines and other interventions.

translations.source: Ìpàdé náà máa dá lórí: àtúnyẹwò ìtẹ̀síwájú lọ́wọ́lọ́wọ́ láti ṣe àṣeyọrí àwọn àfojúsùn ọdún 2030; mímọ àwọn àlàfo pàtàkì àti àwọn ìpèníjà láti débi àwọn àfojúsùn wọ̀nyẹn; gbígbà àwọn ìpìlẹ̀-òye iṣẹ́ àti àwọn igbéṣẹ̀ tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ fún bí a ṣe lè ṣàmúlò iṣẹ́ tọ àwọn àfojúsùn náà; ṣíṣe ìwúrí fún àtìlẹ́yìn àti ìfọkànsìn láti ọ̀dọ àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ NTD láti ṣiṣẹ́ lórí àwọn àyípadà tí wọ́n bèèrè fún nínú ìlànà-iṣẹ́ náà àti àwọn àfojúsùn rẹ̀; àti
translations.reference: The meeting will focus on: reviewing current progress to achieving the 2030 targets; identifying the major gaps and challenges to reaching those targets; agreeing principles and practical steps for how to implement action towards the targets; galvanizing support and commitment from NTD partners to operationalize the shifts advocated in the road map and to achieve its targets; and
translations.hypothesis: The meeting will focus on: reviewing progress made towards achieving the 2030 Sustainable Development Goals; identifying key priorities and challenges in reaching those goals; discussing practical steps and strategies for effectively implementing the goals; and mobilizing support and resources from NTD (Neglected Tropical Diseases) partners to address the challenges outlined in the work plan and achieve the goals.

translations.source: ṣíṣe ètò papọ̀ fún Ètò Amúṣẹ́ṣe Àgbàyé 2025-2030. 300 lé àwọn akópa nínú ìpàdé náà ni ìrètí wà pé wọn yóò kópa, yálà ní-tara tàbí lórí ẹ̀rọ ayélujára. Lára wọn ni a ti máa rí àwọn aṣojú àwùjọ NTD lágbàyé, láti àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè àti àwọn olùpèsè tó fi dórí àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ aṣàmúlò àti ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ, láti ilé-ẹ̀kọ́ tó fi dórí àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àwọn aláìsàn àti gbogbo àwọn nǹkan tókù àti àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n lọ́wọ́ sí gbígbógun ti àwọn NTD. Ìpàtẹ ọjà lórí àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì máa wáyé ní ilé pẹpẹ àárín ilé B, èyí tó wà lókè ilé oúnjẹ náà. Àtò àwọn pánẹ́lì onírin máa ṣe àpèjúwe àwọn májẹ̀mú 20 tí wọ́n pín sísọ̀rí lábẹ́ àbùradà NTD tí yóò sì pèsè àlàyé lórí ìgbésẹ̀ tí wọ́n ṣàmúlò láti kojú ẹrù wúwo wọn.
translations.reference: planning together for a Global Action Plan 2025–2030. Over 300 participants in the meeting are expected to attend, either in-person or online. They will include representatives of the global NTD community, from countries and donors to implementing and technical partners, from academia to patients’ associations and all other entities and institutions involved in the fight against NTDs. An exhibition on neglected tropical diseases will take place in the mezzanine of the B building, just above the cafeteria. A set of metal panels will illustrate the 20 conditions grouped under the NTD umbrella and provide information on actions implemented to tackle their burden.
translations.hypothesis: Comprehensive strategy for the Global Health Sector Strategy 2025-2030. It is expected that approximately 300 participants will attend the meeting, either in person or virtually. These participants will include representatives from global NTD organizations, governments, and partner organizations, as well as researchers, academics, representatives from patient advocacy groups, and all relevant stakeholders and industry partners committed to combating NTDs. A market analysis on neglected tropical diseases will be presented in the main conference hall, located on the second floor of the building. The panel discussions will focus on the 20 key priorities outlined under the NTD strategy and will explore the progress made in achieving these goals.

translations.source: WHO kéde ẹni tó jáwé olúbori níbi Ayẹyẹ Fíìmù Ìlera fún Gbogbo èèyàn kẹrin àwọn fíìmù kékèké tó jáwé olúbori 7, àwọn ìkéde àṣàyàn 4 láti ọ̀dọ̀ àwọn adájọ́ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Lágbàyé ti kéde yíyan àwọn fíìmù tó jáwé olúbori fún ti ọdún yìí níbi Ayẹyẹ Fíìmù Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn Ọlọ́dọọdún kẹrin, tó wáyé ní Olú ilé-iṣẹ́ WHO, Geneva. Ètò náà, tí àwọn òṣìṣẹ́, àwọn olùpèsè àti àwọn gbajúmọ̀ láwùjọ wá ní-tara àti lórí ẹ̀rọ ayélujára, rí i tí wọ́n kéde fíìmù tó jáwé olúbori fún ìṣọ̀rí 7 ọ̀tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀, nígbà tí fíìmù 4 gba ìkéde àṣàyàn láti ọ̀dọ̀ àwọn adájọ́.
translations.reference: WHO announces winners of the 4th Health for All Film Festival 7 winning short films, 4 special mentions from the jury The World Health Organization has announced the official selection of this year’s winning films at its 4th Annual Health for All Film Festival, held at WHO Headquarters, Geneva. The event, attended in person and online by actors, producers and public figures, saw winning films announced for 7 different categories, while 4 films received special mentions from the jury.
translations.hypothesis: The WHO announced the winners of the short film competition for health promotion at the fourth annual Health for All Film Festival, recognizing seven films that effectively promote health. Four films also received special mentions from the judges, who are members of the World Health Organization's expert panel. The awards ceremony, which took place at the WHO headquarters in Geneva, celebrated the work of filmmakers, health professionals, and community members who used film to address a variety of health issues. The winning films were selected from a pool of seven finalists, while the four films receiving special mentions were chosen by the judges.

translations.source: Ọdún kẹrin ayẹyẹ fíìmù náà rè é ìdíje náà ò sì ní àdínkù agbára rárá, pẹ̀lú bí àwọn fíìmù 93 kọ̀ọ̀kan tí wọ́n gbé jáde tó kárí àwọn ìṣòro tó bẹ̀rẹ̀ láti ìbẹ̀rù àti ìrẹ̀wẹ̀sí ọkàn nípasẹ̀ àwọn ipa àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ lórí ìlera, bákan náà ni àwọn ìpèníjà ìlera àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní ìpèníjà ara. Ìgbìmọ̀ àṣàyàn àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́, àwọn òṣèré àti àwọn ajàfẹ́tọ ọmọnìyàn, tó fi mọ́ àwọn gbajúmọ̀ òṣèré, Sharon Stone àti Alfonso Herrera, àwọn adarí ijó, Sherrie Silver, ajàfẹ́tọ ojú ọjọ́, Sophia Kianni àti sọ̀rọ̀sọ̀rọ̀, Adelle Onyango ni wọ́n gbé ìdájọ́ kalẹ̀ lórí àwọn fíìmù náà. Àwọn lọ́gà-lọ́gà nínú àwọn olùdarí Àjò Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé àti àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ WHO náà darapọ̀ mọ́ wọn. "Ayẹyẹ Fíìmù Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn náà gbé ojú ènìyàn wọ àwọn iṣẹ́ sáyẹnsì WHO, "Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO, Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Títẹ́tí sí ohùn àwọn ènìyàn tí àwọn ìṣòro ìlera náà ṣe àkóbá fún jẹ́ ọ̀nà tó lágbára láti ṣe ìkéde àti láti mú àyípadà bá òye wa nípa ìrírí àwọn ènìyàn èyí sì lè ràn wá lọ́wọ́ láti sún mọ́ ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn."
translations.reference: This is the fourth year of the film festival and competition was no less fierce, with some 93 shortlisted films covering issues ranging from anxiety and depression through to the effects of climate change on health, as well as the health challenges of people with disabilities. The films were judged by a panel of distinguished professionals, artists and activists, including the renown actors, Sharon Stone and Alfonso Herrera, dance choreographer, Sherrie Silver, climate activist, Sophia Kianni and media personality, Adelle Onyango. They were joined by senior United Nations officials and WHO staff. “The Health for All Film Festival brings a human face to WHO’s scientific work,” said WHO Director-General, Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “Listening to the voices of people affected by health issues is a powerful way to raise awareness and improve our understanding of people’s experiences and this can help us advance towards health for all.”
translations.hypothesis: The fourth annual film festival was a powerful event, featuring 93 films that explored a range of issues, from the challenges of living with chronic illness and mental health struggles to the experiences of people with disabilities. The judging panel, comprised of prominent actors, directors, and activists, including renowned actress Sharon Stone and actor Alfonso Herrera, as well as directors Sherrie Silver, activist Sophia Kianni, and journalist Adelle Onyango, carefully evaluated the films. Experts from the World Health Organization and other international organizations also participated. "The Film Festival for Global Health has brought attention to the important work of the WHO," said WHO Director-General Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "By sharing the stories of people affected by health challenges, the festival provides a powerful way to raise awareness and understanding of the human impact of these issues, and can help to promote health for all."

translations.source: Láti inú àṣàyàn náà wọ́n máa sàmi "ÌDÍJE ÀMÌ-Ẹ̀YẸ" fún ìkọ̀ọ̀kan àwọn ojúlówó ìsọ̀rí ìdíje mẹ́tẹ̀ta náà: kíkárí Ìlera Lágbàyé, àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera, àti Ìlera tó dára àti ìgbáyégbádùn, tí ó wà ní ìbámu pẹ̀lú Àfojúsùn Bílíọ̀nù Nílọ̀po Mẹ́ta WHO. Sharon Stone, òṣèrébìnrin alámì-ẹ̀yẹ Golden Globe àti Emmy láti United States of America, olùpèsè àti ajàfẹ́tọ fún okùnfà ìlera àti ọmọnìyàn náà sọ wí pé: "Inú mi dùn láti darapọ̀ mọ́ Ayẹyẹ Fíìmù Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn. Èyí jẹ́ nípa ṣíṣe ìpolongo tó dára lórí àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ pàtàkì tí a nílò fún dídé ipò ìgbésí ayé ìlera káàkiri àgbáyé. Àwọn ìtàn tí wọ́n yàn báwa sọ̀rọ̀ nípa pàtàkì ojúlówó ìlera tó dára àti bí a ṣe lè lò ó, wọ́n sì tún pè fún kíkárí ìlera lágbàyé.
translations.reference: From the official selection one “GRAND PRIX” is attributed for each of the three main competition categories: Universal Health Coverage, Health emergencies, and Better health and well-being, which align with WHO’s Triple Billion Targets. Sharon Stone, Golden Globe and Emmy Award-winning actress from the United States of America, producer and activist for health and humanitarian causes said: "I am delighted to be part of the Health for All Film Festival. This is about creating better awareness on crucial actions needed for reaching healthier living conditions around the world. The stories selected talk to us about the intrinsic value of good health and its access, and they advocate for universal health coverage.
translations.hypothesis: From the selection, three films were chosen as "Health for All" award recipients: "Global Health," which addresses global health challenges; "Health Stories," focusing on health issues; and "Healthy and Happy," promoting well-being, all in line with the World Health Organization's (WHO) Sustainable Development Goals. Sharon Stone, a Golden Globe and Emmy-winning actress from the United States of America, and a health advocate, stated: "I am delighted to join the Health for All Film Festival. This festival highlights the crucial steps needed to improve global health outcomes. The selected films shed light on the importance of good health and how it can be achieved, and they also promote global health initiatives."

translations.source: Kíkárí Ìlera Lágbàyé jẹ́ ẹ̀tọ́ tó ṣe pàtàkì gidi gan-an, ó jẹ́ ẹ̀tọ ọmọnìyàn fún gbogbo ènìyàn káàkiri àgbáyé. "Àwọn ẹ̀bùn pàtàkì mẹ́rin ni wọ́n tún fún fíìmù tí akẹ́kọ̀ọ́ darí, fíìmù tó dá lórí àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ àti ìlera, fíìmù lórí Ìlera Ìbálòpọ̀ àti Ìbímọ àti Ẹ̀tọ́ àti Fíìmù Tó Kéré gidi gan-an. Àkòrí ìlera ọpọlọ jẹyọ lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀ nínú àwọn ìgbaniwọlé tó jáwé olúbori lọ́dún yìí, tó fi mọ́ fíìmù kékeré tó lágbára tó sì bani nínú jẹ́ láti Sweden nípa ìbẹ̀rù àti ìrẹ̀wẹ̀sí ọkàn. Fíìmù náà, tí wọ́n ṣe sórí fọ́rán orin náà, ṣe àfihàn oríṣiríṣi àwọ̀n òṣèré tí ń ṣàpèjúwe oríṣiríṣi ìpele ìbẹ̀rù àti bí ó ṣe lè fojú hàn fúnra rẹ̀. Gbogbo àlàyé náà ni wí pé ìwọ nìkan kọ́ ni ò ń ní ìrírí èrò òdì àti wí pé ó dára láti gba irú ìmọ̀lára bẹ́ẹ̀. Fíìmù tó jáwé olúbori mìíràn, láti Bangladesh, ṣàfihàn ọmọdékùnrin kan tí wọ́n ti kọ́ ọ láti lo òjé ní ilé-iṣẹ́ ìbílẹ̀ kan.
translations.reference: Universal Health Coverage is a very important right, it’s a human right for everyone around the world. "Four special prizes were also given for a Student-produced film, a film on Climate Change and Health, a film on Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights and a Very Short Film. The theme of mental health featured heavily in this year’s winning entries, including a powerful and moving short film from Sweden about anxiety and depression. The film, set to a stirring soundtrack, featured a series of actors expressing different stages of anxiety and how it can manifest itself. The overall message was that you are not alone in experiencing negative thoughts and that it is okay to acknowledge such feelings.Another winning film, from Bangladesh, features a young boy who was exposed to lead from a local factory.
translations.hypothesis: Global Health is a fundamental right and a crucial concern for people around the world. "Four key themes were highlighted in the showcased films: maternal and child health, universal health coverage, human rights, and short films. Several films addressed the issue of mental health, with a particularly powerful short film from Sweden focusing on anxiety and depression. This film, set to music, featured various actors portraying different levels of anxiety and how it can manifest. The overall message was that everyone experiences anxiety at some point and that it's important to seek help when needed. Another impactful film, from Bangladesh, depicted a young boy who was forced to work in a factory instead of attending school."

translations.source: Fíìmù tó bani nínú jẹ́ náà ṣàlàyé àwọn ipa tí èyí ní lórí ìdagbàsókè ẹ̀kọ́ rẹ̀ àti iṣẹ́ tí Ilé-iṣẹ́ ìbílẹ̀ tí kìí ṣe ti ìjọba ti ṣe láti ṣàfihàn ìṣòro náà àti láti gbé ìgbésẹ̀ láti dènà májèlé òjé nínú àwùjọ tí ó kàn náà. Àkọsílẹ̀ àwọn fíìmù tó gbàmì-ẹ̀yẹ: UHC "Ìdíje Àmì-Ẹ̀yẹ": "Jonathan's Miracle Feet" - Sierra Leone / Ìpèníjà ara, ẹsẹ̀ akàn Mamihasina Raminosoa àti Nantenaina Rakotondranivo láti Madagascar ló darí rẹ̀ fún NGO Miracle Feet / Ìwé ìtàn - Iye àkókò 3'19" Àwọn Pàjáwírì Ìlera "Ìdíje Àmì-Ẹ̀yẹ": "Nurses facing Covid/ Na Lihna de Frente" - Brazil / COVID-19 àti ànfààní sí ìtọ́jú
translations.reference: The moving film details the effects this has had on his educational development and the work that a local non-governmental organization has done to both highlight the issue and take action to prevent lead poisoning in the affected community. List of films awarded: UHC "Grand Prix": “Jonathan’s Miracle Feet” – Sierra Leone / Disability, clubfoot Directed by Mamihasina Raminosoa and Nantenaina Rakotondranivo from Madagascar for the NGO Miracle Feet / Documentary – Duration 3’19’’ Health Emergencies "Grand Prix": “Nurses facing Covid / Na Lihna de Frente” – Brazil / COVID-19 and access to care
translations.hypothesis:
The film highlights the impact of this initiative on children's education and the work that the non-governmental organization has done to address the problem and promote inclusion within the community.

Here are the descriptions of the award-winning films:
UHC "Award of Excellence": "Jonathan's Miracle Feet" - Sierra Leone / This film tells the story of Mamihasina Raminosoa and Nantenaina Rakotondranivo from Madagascar, who were supported by the NGO Miracle Feet. / Documentary - Length: 3'19"
Health Advocates "Award of Excellence": "Nurses facing Covid/ Na Lihna de Frente" - Brazil / This film focuses on COVID-19 and its impact on healthcare.

Short Film Showcase for Very Short Films: "I am naked / Je suis nue" - France / Raising awareness of sexual violence; Promoting women's health; Combating gender inequality by Alexander Mignien (France) / Narrative film - Duration: 2'20"

For a longer explanation of the themes, a list of the films, and to watch the films and participate in the event, please visit: https://www.who.int/film-festival

translations.source: Ànfààní sí agbára ìpìlẹ̀ tún rẹ́yìn pẹ̀lú àwọn ànfààní ìsọdọ̀tun, ìjábọ̀ tuntun ṣàfihàn rẹ̀ Àlàfo tó wà láàrin ànfàní sí agbára lágbàyé ṣì ń tẹ̀síwájú: 675 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn tí ò ní iná agbára, 2.3 bílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n gbára lé àwọn epo ìdáná tó léwu Ìjábọ̀ tuntun láti ọwọ́ Àjọ tó ń rí sí Agbára Ilẹ̀ òkèèrè (IEA), Àjọ tó ń rí sí Agbára Ìsọdọ̀tun Ilẹ̀ òkèèrè (IRENA), Ẹ̀ka Ìṣirò Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé (UNSD), ilé-ìfowópamọ́ Àgbáyé, àti Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Lágbàyé (WHO), tí wọ́n gbéjáde lónì, ṣàwarí rẹ̀ pé ayé ò sí lórí ojú ọ̀nà láti ṣe àṣeyọrí Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ (SDG) 7 fún agbára ní ọdún 2030. Ọdún yìí ló sámì ìdajì ọ̀nà fún ṣíṣe àṣeyọrí SDG ní ọdún 2030. SDG 7 ni láti ṣe àrídájú ànfààní sí agbára tó rọjú, tó ṣe é gbáralé, alálòpẹ́ àti ti ìgbàlódé.
translations.reference: Basic energy access lags amid renewable opportunities, new report shows Global energy access gap persists: 675 million people without electricity, 2.3 billion people reliant on harmful cooking fuels A new report by the International Energy Agency (IEA), the International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), the United Nations Statistics Division (UNSD), the World Bank, and the World Health Organization (WHO), released today, finds that the world is not on track to achieve the Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 7 for energy by 2030. This year marks the halfway point for achieving SDGs by 2030. SDG 7 is to ensure access to affordable, reliable, sustainable and modern energy.
translations.hypothesis:
Despite significant progress in electrification and renewable energy, the global energy landscape still faces major challenges. 675 million people still lack access to electricity, and 2.3 billion rely on polluting fuels for cooking.

A new report released today by the International Energy Agency (IEA), the International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), the United Nations Statistics Division (UNSD), the World Bank, and the World Health Organization (WHO), warns that the world is not on track to achieve Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 7 for energy by 2030. This year marks a critical turning point for achieving the SDG by 2030. SDG 7 aims to ensure universal access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy.

translations.source: Lára èròngbà náà ni níní ànfààní àgbáyé sí iná agbára àti ìdáná tó mọ́, sísọ àwọn ìpele ìdagbàsókè tó ṣe é lò nínú ìtàn di ìlọ́po méjì, àti ṣíṣe àfikún alálòpẹ́ pínpín àwọn ìsọdọ̀tun nínú àpapọ̀ agbára lágbàyé. Níní èròngbà yìí máa ní ipa tó jinlẹ̀ lórí ìlera àwọn ènìyàn àti ìgbáyégbádùn, tí yóò ràn wọ́n lọ́wọ́ láti dáàbò bò wọ́n kúrò níbi àwọn ewu agbègbè àti àwùjọ bíi bíba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́, àti fífẹ ànfààní sí ètò ìlera alábọ́dé àti ìpèsè iṣẹ́ lójú. Àtúnṣe 2023 Ìtọpa SDG 7: Ìjábọ̀ Ìtẹ̀síwájú Agbára kílọ̀ pé àwọn ìgbìyànjú lọ́wọ́lọ́wọ́ ò tó láti ṣe àṣeyọrí SDG 7 ní àkókò. Àwọn ìtẹ̀síwájú kọ̀ọ̀kan ti wà lórí àwọn ohun èlò èròngbà SDG 7 kan pàtó - fún àpẹẹrẹ, àlékún iye àwọn tó ń lo ìsọdọ̀tun ní ẹ̀ka iná agbára - ṣùgbọ́n ìlọsíwájú ò tó láti dé ibi àfojúsùn tí wọ́n ṣe síwájú nínú SDG náà. Rògbòdìyàn agbára lágbàyé ni ìrètí wà pé yóò ṣe ìwúrí fún ìṣàmúlò ìsọdọ̀tun àti àyípadà agbára tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn òfin ìjọba tó ń tọ́ka sí àlékún ìdókówò.
translations.reference: The goal includes reaching universal access to electricity and clean cooking, doubling historic levels of efficiency improvements, and substantially increasing the share of renewables in the global energy mix. Attaining this goal will have a deep impact on people’s health and well-being, helping to protect them from environmental and social risks such as air pollution, and expanding access to primary health care and services. The 2023 edition of Tracking SDG 7: The Energy Progress Report warns that current efforts are not enough to achieve the SDG 7 on time. There has been some progress on specific elements of the SDG 7 agenda – for example, the increased rate of using renewables in the power sector – but progress is insufficient to reach the targets set forth in the SDGs. The global energy crisis is expected to stimulate the deployment of renewables and improve energy efficiency with several government policies pointing to increasing investment.
translations.hypothesis: The global energy and electricity sector has undergone significant transformations over the past two decades, marked by technological advancements and shifts in global power dynamics. This sector plays a crucial role in human health and well-being, enabling access to essential services and contributing to economic development. However, the 2023 Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 7 progress report indicates that current efforts are insufficient to achieve SDG 7 by the target date. While progress has been made in specific areas of SDG 7, such as the number of people with access to electricity, overall progress towards the goal has been slow. Global energy demand is projected to continue rising, posing challenges for the transition to sustainable energy sources and the deployment of renewable energy technologies, particularly in the face of increasing government regulations.

translations.source: Síbẹ̀síbẹ̀, ìṣirò IRENA fi hàn pé àwọn ìpèsè owó àwùjọ ilẹ̀ òkèèrè fún àtìlẹ́yìn agbára tó mọ́ ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré àti agbede-méjì ti ń dínkù tipẹ́ síwájú àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 owó ìrànwọ́ náà sì wà fún iye àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè kékèké kan. Láti dọ́gba pẹ̀lú àwọn àfojúsùn SDG 7 àti láti rí i dájú pé àwọn ènìyàn jẹ ànfààní kíkún lára àwọn èrè ọrọ̀-ajé-àwùjọ fún àyípadà sí agbára alálòpẹ́, ó ṣe pàtàkì láti ṣe àtúnṣe ìṣúná gbogbo ilẹ̀ òkèèrè pẹ̀lú àgbékalẹ̀ àti láti ṣàlàyé àwọn ànfààní tuntun láti ṣí àwọn ìdókówò tuntun. Ìjábọ̀ náà tún rí i pé àlékún gbèsè àti owó agbára tó ń lọ sókè ti ń mú ìbàjẹ́ bá ìfojúsọ́nà fún ṣíṣe ànfààní àgbáyé sí ìdáná tó mọ́ àti iná agbára. Àsọtẹ́lẹ̀ lọ́wọ́lọ́wọ́ ṣírò wí pé 1.9 bílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn ni kò ní ní ìdáná tó mọ́ tí 660 mílíọ̀nù ò sì ní ní ànfààní sí iná agbára ní ọdún 2030 tí a ò bá gbé ìgbésẹ̀ síwájú kí á sì tẹ̀síwájú pẹ̀lú àwọn ìgbìyànjú lọ́wọ́lọ́wọ́. Àwọn àlàfo wọ̀nyìí máa ní ipa burúkú lórí ìlera ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn wa tó kángun séwu jùlọ tí yóò sì mú kí àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ yá.
translations.reference: However, IRENA estimates show that international public financial flows in support of clean energy in low- and middle-income countries have been decreasing since before the COVID-19 pandemic and funding is limited to a small number of countries. To meet SDG 7 targets and to ensure that people fully benefit from the socio-economic gains of the shift to sustainable energy, it is necessary to structurally reform international public finance and define new opportunities to unlock investments. The report also finds that mounting debt and rising energy prices are worsening the outlook for reaching universal access to clean cooking and electricity. Current projections estimate that 1.9 billion people will be without clean cooking and 660 million without electricity access in 2030 if we do not take further action and continue with current efforts. These gaps will negatively impact the health of our most vulnerable populations and accelerate climate change.
translations.hypothesis: However, a recent IRENA report revealed that public investment in renewable energy in developing countries and emerging economies remains insufficient to meet the growing demand for electricity, particularly in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic. This funding is crucial for these smaller nations. To align with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and ensure that everyone has access to affordable and reliable energy, it is essential to scale up public investment and explore new financing mechanisms to address these challenges. The report also highlighted that increasing electricity demand and the rising cost of fossil fuels are posing significant challenges to achieving global energy access goals. Current projections indicate that by 2030, 1.9 billion people will still lack access to electricity, and 660 million will not have access to clean cooking fuels, unless significant progress is made and current trends are reversed. These disparities will have a detrimental impact on the health and well-being of vulnerable populations and hinder sustainable development.

translations.source: Gẹ́gẹ́ bí WHO ṣe sọ, 3.2 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn ló ń kú lọ́dún kọ̀ọ̀kan látara àìsàn tí lílo àwọn epo àti ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ tó ń ba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́, tí ó ń mú àlékún bá ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú àwọn ìpele májèlé bíba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ nínú ilé. Àwọn kókó ìwádìí nínú ìjábọ̀ náà Ní ọdún 2010, 84% àwọn ènìyàn lágbàyé ti ní ànfààní sí iná agbára. Èyí lékún sí 91% ní ọdún 2021, èyí túnmọ̀ sí wí pé ó lé ní bílíọ̀nù kan àwọn ènìyàn tó ní ànfààní yíká àkókò náà. Síbẹ̀síbẹ̀, eré ìdagbàsókè ànfààní dínkù ní 2019-2021 yàtọ̀ sí àwọn ọdún tó ti ré kọjá.
translations.reference: According to WHO, 3.2 million people die each year from illness caused by the use of polluting fuels and technologies, which increase exposure to toxic levels of household air pollution. Key findings of the report In 2010, 84% of the world’s population had access to electricity. This increased to 91% in 2021, meaning more than a billion people gained access over that period. However, the growth pace of access slowed in 2019–2021 compared to previous years.
translations.hypothesis: According to the WHO, 3.2 million people die each year from diseases caused by air pollution, including household air pollution from cooking with solid fuels and technologies that release harmful pollutants. Key findings in the report include: In 2010, 84% of the world's population was exposed to household air pollution. This increased to 91% in 2021, meaning that over a billion people were exposed to this risk at that time. However, the rate of increase in household air pollution exposure slowed down between 2019 and 2021 compared to previous years.

translations.source: Ìgbìyànjú iná agbára ní ìgbèríko tún ṣàfikún sí ìlọsíwájú yìí, ṣùgbọ́n àlàfo tó fẹ̀ láàrín àwọn agbègbè ìlú ṣì wà níbẹ̀. Ní ọdún 2021, 567 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn ní ìwọ̀-oọ̀rùn ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ ò ní ànfààní sí iná agbára, èyí tó dúró fún 80% lé nínú àwọn ènìyàn lágbàyé tí kò ní ànfààní. Àìtó ànfààní ní àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè náà wà bákan náà gẹ́gẹ́ bó ṣe wà ní ọdún 2010. Ayé ṣì wà lójú ọ̀nà láti ṣe àṣeyọrí ànfààní àgbáyé sí ìdáná tó mọ́ ní ọdún 2030. Ó fẹ́rẹ̀ tó 2.3 bílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ṣì ń lo àwọn epo àti ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ tó ń ba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́, púpọ̀ ní ìwọ̀-oọ̀rùn ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ àti Asia.
translations.reference: Rural electrification efforts contributed to this progress, but a large gap within urban areas remains. In 2021, 567 million people in sub-Saharan Africa did not have access to electricity, accounting for more than 80% of the global population without access. The access deficit in the region stayed almost the same as in 2010. The world remains off track to achieve universal access to clean cooking by 2030. Up to 2.3 billion people still use polluting fuels and technologies for cooking, largely in sub-Saharan Africa and Asia.
translations.hypothesis: The progress in electricity access is encouraging, but significant disparities still exist between urban and rural areas. In 2021, 567 million people in sub-Saharan Africa lacked access to electricity, representing 80% of the global population without access. This lack of access is particularly prevalent in rural areas. However, there is still a path to achieving universal electricity access by 2030. Approximately 2.3 billion people still rely on traditional fuels and technologies for cooking, many of whom live in sub-Saharan Africa and Asia.

translations.source: Lílo àwọn báómáásì ìbílẹ̀ tún túnmọ̀ sí wí pé àwọn ilé ń lò tó wákàtí 40 ní ọ̀sẹ̀ fún àkójọ igi ìdáná àti oúnjẹ sísè, tí ó ń dènà àwọn obìnrin láti wá iṣẹ́ tàbí kíkópa nínú àwọn ẹgbẹ́ tó ń ṣe ìpinnu ní agbègbè àti àwọn ọmọdé láti lọ sí ilé-ẹ̀kọ́. Gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìṣirò WHO ti ọdún 2019, 3.2 mílíọ̀nù ikú àìtọ́jọ́ lọ́dún kọ̀ọ̀kan ni wọ́n fi sun bíba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ nínú ilé tí ó wáyé látara lílo àwọn epo àti ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ tó ń ba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ fún ìdáná. Lílo iná agbára ìsọdọ̀tun nínú ìlò àgbáyé ti lọ sókè láti 26.3% ní ọdún 2019 sí 28.2% ní ọdún 2020, àlékún tó tóbi jùlọ ní ọdún kan ṣoṣo láti ìgbà tí ìtọpa ìtẹ̀síwájú ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ fún àwọn SDG. Àwọn ìgbìyànjú láti mú àlékún bá pínpín ìsọdọ̀tun fún ìdáná àti iṣẹ́ ọkọ̀ wíwà, tí ó dúró fún ìdá mẹ́ta nínú ìdá mẹ́rin lé iná agbára lílò lágbàyé, sì dúró ní ẹ̀bá ọ̀nà láti ṣe àwọn èròngbà ojú ọjọ́ 1.5oC. Ìkúnra agbára - wíwọn iye agbára tí ọrọ̀-ajéàgbáyé ń lò fún dọ́là kan nínú GDP - yípadà láti ọdún 2010-2020 pẹ̀lú 1.8% lọ́dọọdún.
translations.reference: The use of traditional biomass also means households spend up to 40 hours a week gathering firewood and cooking, which prohibits women from pursuing employment or participating in local decision-making bodies and children from going to school. According to the 2019 WHO estimates, 3.2 million premature deaths each year were attributable to household air pollution created by using polluting fuels and technologies for cooking. Renewable electricity use in global consumption has grown from 26.3% in 2019 to 28.2% in 2020, the largest single-year increase since the start of tracking progress for the SDGs. Efforts to increase renewables’ share in heating and transport, which represent more than three quarters of global energy consumption, remain off target to achieve 1.5oC climate objectives. Energy intensity – the measure of how much energy the global economy uses per dollar of GDP – improved from 2010–2020 by 1.8% annually.
translations.hypothesis: The use of biomass also contributes to indoor air pollution, with households using an average of 40 hours per week of solid fuels for cooking and heating, which exposes women and children to harmful pollutants and prevents them from participating in education or employment. According to the 2019 WHO report, 3.2 million deaths each year are attributed to household air pollution caused by the use of solid fuels and other polluting technologies for cooking. The share of renewable energy in the global energy mix increased from 26.3% in 2019 to 28.2% in 2020, the largest annual increase since the adoption of the Sustainable Development Goals. Efforts to reduce the share of fossil fuels in energy consumption, with three out of four watts of global energy consumption still reliant on fossil fuels, are crucial to achieving the goal of limiting global warming to 1.5°C. Energy intensity – the amount of energy used per unit of GDP – decreased by 1.8% per year between 2010 and 2020.

translations.source: Èyí pọ̀ ju àyípadà 1.2% láti àìmọye ọdún tó ti kọjá. Síbẹ̀síbẹ̀, iye àyípadà ìkúnra agbára ti dínkù láti bí ọdún díẹ̀ sẹ́yìn ó sì ti wálẹ̀ sí 0.6% ní ọdún 2020. Èyí jẹ́ kí ó jẹ́ ọdún tó burú jùlọ fún àyípadà ìkúnra agbára láti ìgbà tí rògbòdìyàn ìṣúná àgbáyé ti ṣẹlẹ̀, yàtọ̀ sí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ nítorí àwọn ìdíwọ́ ajẹmọ́-àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn, tí ó lè ṣàfihàn ìfàsẹ́yìn fún ìgbà díẹ̀ nìkan. Àyípadà ọdọọdún di ọdún 2030 gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ àpapọ̀ 3.4% báyìí láti bá àfojúsùn SDG 7. Àwọn ìpèsè owó àwùjọ ilẹ̀ òkèèrè fún àtìlẹ́yìn agbára tó mọ́ ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè onídágbàsókè dúró sí US$ 10.8 bílíọ̀nù ní ọdún 2021, ó fi 35% kéré sí àpapọ ti 2010-2019 tí ó sì jẹ́ bí 40% òpin 2017 ti US$ 26.4 bílíọ̀nù. Ní ọdún 2019, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 19 gba 80% àwọn owó náà. Ijábọ̀ náà ni wọn yóò gbé síwájú àwọn aṣòfin tó ga jùlọ níbi ètò ìfilọ́lẹ̀ àkànṣe kan ní ọjọ́ 11 oṣù Agẹmọ ọdún 2023 ní Àpérò Òṣèlú Onípele-Gíga (HLPF) lórí Ìdagbàsókè Alálòpẹ́, ṣíwájú Àpérò SDG kejì ní oṣù Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2023 ní New York.
translations.reference: This is higher than the 1.2% improvement from the previous decades. However, the rate of energy intensity improvement has slowed in recent years and dropped to 0.6% in 2020. This makes it the worst year for energy intensity improvement since the global financial crisis, albeit largely due to pandemic-related restrictions, which may indicate only a temporary setback. Annual improvements through 2030 must now average 3.4% to meet the SDG target 7.3. International public financial flows in support of clean energy in developing countries stand at US$ 10.8 billion in 2021, 35% less than the 2010–2019 average and only about 40% of the 2017 peak of US$ 26.4 billion. In 2021, 19 countries received 80% of the commitments.The report will be presented to top decision-makers at a special launch event on 11 July 2023 at the High-Level Political Forum (HLPF) on Sustainable Development, ahead of the second SDG Summit in September 2023 in New York.
translations.hypothesis: This represents an increase of 1.2% compared to the previous year. However, the growth in renewable energy capacity has been declining in recent years and reached only 0.6% in 2020. This was the lowest growth rate for renewable energy capacity since the global financial crisis, largely due to the disruptions caused by the pandemic, which temporarily halted some projects. To achieve the Sustainable Development Goal 7, which aims to ensure access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all, annual renewable energy growth needs to average 3.4% by 2030. Public investment in renewable energy in developing countries remained high in 2021, reaching US$10.8 billion, which is 35% less than the average for 2010-2019, but still 40% higher than the US$26.4 billion recorded in 2017. In 2019, 19 countries received 80% of this funding. The report will be presented to high-level officials at a special session of the High-Level Political Forum (HLPF) on Sustainable Development on July 11, 2023, in New York, ahead of the second SDG Summit in September 2023.

translations.source: Àwọn ònkọ̀wé rọ àwọn àwùjọ ilẹ̀ òkèèrè àti àwọn aṣòfin láti dáàbò bo àwọn èrè tí wọ́n rí láti lè ṣe àṣeyọrí SDG 7, láti tẹ̀síwájú àtúnṣe àgbékalẹ̀, àti láti ni àfojúsùn ọlọgbọ́n lórí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tó kángun séwu tí wọ́n nílò àtìlẹyìn náà jùlọ. Àgbàsọ: "Rògbòdìyàn agbára tí ìkọlù orílẹ̀-èdè Russia sí Ukrain fà ń tẹ̀síwájú láti ní ipa tó lágbára lórí àwọn ènìyàn káàkiri àgbáyé. Owó agbára tó pọ̀ ti bá àwọn tó kángun séwu jùlọ kíkan-kíkan, pàápàá jùlọ àwọn tí wọ́n wà nínú àwọn ọrọ̀-ajéonídágbàsókè. Nígbà tí ṣíṣílọ sí agbára tó mọ́ ń sára gidi gan-an ju bí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ṣe lérò lọ, iṣẹ́ tó pọ̀ ló ṣì wà láti ṣe láti lè ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ànfààní alálòpẹ́, tí ò léwu tó sì rọjú sí ìpèsè agbára ìgbàlódé fún bílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé láì ní i. Àwọn ìṣípòrọpò agbára aláṣeyọrí gbára lé àwọn òfin tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ àti ìmọ̀-ọ̀tunìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ tó papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àkójọ owó ìdókówò òní òṣùwọ̀n-ńlá.
translations.reference: The authors urge the international community and policymakers to safeguard the gains made toward achieving SDG 7, to advance structural reforms, and to maintain a strategic focus on the vulnerable countries needing the most support. Quotes: “The energy crisis sparked by Russia’s invasion of Ukraine continues to have a profound impact on people all around the world. High energy prices have hit the most vulnerable hard, particularly those in developing economies. While the clean energy transition is moving faster than many think, there is still a great deal of work needed to deliver sustainable, secure and affordable access to modern energy services for the billions of people who live without it. Successful energy transitions rely on effective policies and technological innovation combined with large-scale mobilisation of investment capital.
translations.hypothesis: Researchers are urging governments and policymakers to address the energy crisis to achieve SDG 7, promote sustainable development, and ensure energy security for vulnerable countries that need it most. Key points: "The ongoing conflict between Russia and Ukraine continues to have a significant impact on people around the world. The energy crisis is disproportionately affecting the most vulnerable, particularly those living in poverty. While the immediate crisis may be subsiding faster than many expected, significant work remains to be done to build a more resilient and sustainable energy future for the billions of people who currently lack access to it. Policy failures and technological shortcomings, combined with today's high energy prices, are exacerbating the problem."

translations.source: Àwùjọ ilẹ̀ òkèèrè gbọ́dọ̀ ṣàmúlò gbogbo àwọn irinṣẹ́ wọ̀nyìí láti dọ́gba pẹ̀lú àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ náà tí yóò bá fi di ìparí ọdún mẹ́wàá yìí." Fatih Birol, Olùdarí Aláṣẹ, Àjọ tó ń rí sí Agbára Ilẹ̀ òkèèrè "Agbára ìsọdọ̀tun tó ń ṣe ìfigagbága-iye owó tún ti ṣàpèjúwe ìfaradà tó lápẹẹrẹ lẹ́ẹ̀kan sí i, ṣùgbọ́n àwọn tí wọ́n kúṣẹ̀ jùlọ ní àgbáyé ò sì tíì lè jẹ ànfààní kíkún lára rẹ̀ lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀. Láti ṣe SDG7 láì yẹ àdéhùn àwọn èròngbà ojú ọjọ́, a gbọ́dọ̀ mú àyípadà tó létò bá ọ̀nà tí ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ ilẹ̀ òkèèrè ṣe ń ṣiṣẹ́. Ó ṣe pàtàkì pé kí àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ ìṣúná oríṣiríṣi darí ìpèsè owó ní dọ́gba-dọ́gba lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀ káàkiri àgbáyé láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún pínpín ìsọdọ̀tun àti àwọn ìdagbàsókè ohun amáyédẹrùn arídìmú tó tan mọ́ ọ." Francesco La Camera, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀, Àjọ tó ń rí sí Agbára Ìsọdọ̀tun Ilẹ̀ Òkèèrè "Pẹ̀lú gbogbo ìgbésẹ̀ lọ sí ibi àfojúsùn agbára alálòpẹ́ ní àárín gbùngbùn Ìpinnu 2030, Èròngbà 7 jọ bí ẹni le láti ṣe ju bó ṣe wà ní ọdún 2015 bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìgbésẹ̀ ìfẹ̀lójú ṣe pàtàkì tí a bá fẹ́ ṣe àrídájú ànfààní agbára tó rọjú, tó ṣe é gbára lé, alálòpẹ́ tó sì jẹ́ ìgbàlódé fún gbogbo ènìyàn ní ọdún 2030.
translations.reference: The international community must leverage all these tools to meet the Sustainable Development Goals by the end of this decade.” Fatih Birol, Executive Director, International Energy Agency “Cost-competitive renewable energy has yet again demonstrated remarkable resilience, but the poorest in the world are still largely unable to fully benefit from it. To realise SDG7 without compromising climate goals, we must bring about systemic change in the way international cooperation works. It is crucial that multilateral financial institutions direct financial flows more equitably around the world to support renewables deployment and related physical infrastructure development.” Francesco La Camera, Director-General, International Renewable Energy Agency “Despite advances towards sustainable energy targets at the mid-point of Agenda 2030, Goal 7 seems harder to reach than it was in 2015 and scaled-up action is necessary if we are to ensure access to affordable, reliable, sustainable and modern energy for all by 2030.
translations.hypothesis: "Countries must accelerate the implementation of all these measures to achieve the Sustainable Development Goal 7 by 2030," said Fatih Birol, Executive Director of the International Energy Agency. "The recent price volatility has highlighted the vulnerability of many countries to energy shocks, and it is crucial that the most vulnerable are protected from these impacts. To achieve SDG7, we need to transform the way energy is produced and consumed globally. It is essential that all countries prioritize investments in renewable energy and energy efficiency to ensure a sustainable and affordable energy future for all." Francesco La Camera, Director-General of the International Renewable Energy Agency, added: "With just over a decade to go to achieve the goals of the 2030 Agenda, SDG7 is more important than ever. Progress towards affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all is essential for achieving sustainable development and improving the lives of millions of people."

translations.source: Ànfààní sí iná agbára àti ìdáná tó mọ́ ṣàfihàn ìyàtò ẹkùn agbègbè tó lágbára tó sì gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ àfojúsùn iṣẹ́ láti rí i dájú pé ẹnikẹ́ni ò kù lẹ́yìn mọ́. Ìdókòwò nílò láti dé àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìdagbàsókè wọn kéré jùlọ àti àwọn ìwọ̀-oòrùn ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ láti ṣe àrídájú ọ̀pọ̀ ìtẹ̀síwájú tó dọ́gba tọ Èròngbà 7." Stefan Schweinfest, Ẹ̀ka Ìṣirò Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé "Pẹ̀lú àdínkù àìpẹ́ yìí nínú eré agbára fún iná agbára, iye àwọn ènìyàn tí kò ní iná agbára fẹ́rẹ̀ jẹ́ ààbò láti bí ọdún mẹ́wàá, láti 1.1 bílíọ̀nù ní ọdún 2010 wálẹ̀ sí 675 mílíọ̀nù ní ọdún 2021. Síbẹ̀síbẹ̀, àfikún ìgbìyànjú àti àwọn ìlànà ni wọ́n gbọ́dọ̀ ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ lójú ẹsẹ̀ láti rí i dájú pé àwọn tó kúṣẹ̀ jùlọ àti àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ṣòro láti rí ò kù lẹ́yìn mọ́. Láti débi ànfààní àgbáyé ní ọdún 2030, àwùjọ ìdagbàsókè gbọ́dọ̀ fẹ ìdókówò agbára tó mọ́ àti àtìlẹyìn òfin lójú." Guangzhe Chen, Igbákejì Ààrẹ fún Ohun Amáyédẹrùn, ilé-ìfowópamọ́ Àgbáyé
translations.reference: Access to electricity and clean cooking still display great regional disparities and should be the focus of action to ensure that no one is left behind. Investment needs to reach the least-developed countries and sub-Saharan Africa to ensure more equitable progress toward Goal 7.” Stefan Schweinfest, United Nations Statistics Division “Despite a recent slowdown in the global pace of electrification, the number of people without electricity almost halved over the past decade, from 1.1 billion in 2010 down to 675 million in 2021. Nonetheless, additional efforts and measures must urgently be put in place to ensure that the poorest and hardest-to-reach people are not left behind. To reach universal access by 2030, the development community must scale up clean energy investments and policy support.” Guangzhe Chen, Vice President for Infrastructure, World Bank
translations.hypothesis: Access to electricity and clean cooking solutions are crucial for improving the quality of life in vulnerable communities and are essential components of sustainable development to ensure that no one is left behind. Efforts are needed to reach the least developed countries and the most vulnerable populations, as highlighted in Sustainable Development Goal 7. "With this additional investment in clean energy technologies, the number of people without access to electricity has decreased significantly, from 1.1 billion in 2010 to 675 million in 2021. However, further progress and innovative approaches are needed to ensure that the most vulnerable and those living in poverty are not left behind. To achieve universal access to electricity by 2030, increased investment in clean energy technologies and supportive policies are essential." Guangzhe Chen, Vice President for Programs, World Bank.

translations.source: "A gbọ́dọ̀ dáàbò bo àwọn ìran tó ń bọ̀ pẹ̀lú gbígbé ìgbésẹ̀ báyìí. Ìdókòwò nínú ọ̀nà àbáyọ tó mọ́ tó sì ní ìsọdọ̀tun láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ànfààní sí agbára lágbàyé ni bí a ṣe lè ṣe àyípadà tòótọ́. Àwọn ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ ìdáná tó mọ́ nínú ilé àti iná agbára tó ṣe é gbára lé ní àwọn ilé-ìwòsàn lè kó ipa pàtàkì nínú ìdáàbò bo ìlera àwọn ènìyàn wa tó kángun séwu jùlọ." Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Lágbàyé Nípa ìjábọ̀ náà Àwọn àjọ tó ń sọ́ SDG 7, Àjọ tó ń rí sí Agbára Ilẹ̀ Òkèèrè (IEA), Àjọ tó ń rí sí Agbára Ìsọdọ̀tun Ilẹ̀ Òkèèrè (IRENA), Ẹ̀ka Ìṣirò Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé (UNSD), ilé-ìfowópamọ́ Àgbáyé, àti Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Lágbàyé (WHO) ló tẹ Ìjábọ̀ yìí jáde wọ́n sì ń gbèrò láti pèsè dáṣíbọọ̀dù fún àwùjọ àgbáyé láti ṣe àkọsílẹ̀ ìtẹ̀síwájú lórí ànfààní sí agbára, agbára tó ṣe é lò, agbára tó ṣe é sọ dọ̀tun àti ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ ilẹ̀ òkèèrè láti tẹ̀síwájú SDG 7.
translations.reference: “We must protect the next generation by acting now. Investing in clean and renewable solutions to support universal energy access is how we can make real change. Clean cooking technologies in homes and reliable electricity in health-care facilities can play a crucial role in protecting the health of our most vulnerable populations.” Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General, World Health Organization About the report This report is published by the SDG 7 custodian agencies, the International Energy Agency (IEA), the International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), the United Nations Statistics Division (UNSD), the World Bank, and the World Health Organization (WHO) and aims to provide the international community with a global dashboard to register progress on energy access, energy efficiency, renewable energy and international cooperation to advance SDG 7.
translations.hypothesis:
"We must accelerate our efforts to address the health challenges associated with air pollution. The potential for clean energy to contribute to improved global health is immense, and we must seize this opportunity to make a real difference. Technological advancements in areas such as indoor air quality and renewable energy sources can play a crucial role in protecting the health of our most vulnerable populations." - Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the World Health Organization.

In a joint report, the organizations working on Sustainable Development Goal 7, including the International Energy Agency (IEA), the International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), the United Nations Statistics Division (UNSD), the World Bank, and the World Health Organization (WHO), have called for increased action to accelerate progress on energy, energy efficiency, renewable energy, and access to electricity to advance SDG 7.

translations.source: Àtúnṣe kẹsàn-án ìjábọ̀ yìí rè é, tí a mọ̀ tẹ́lẹ̀ sí ìlànà-iṣẹ́ Ìtọpinpin Àgbayé (GTF). Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Lágbàyé (WHO) ló ṣe alága àtúnṣe ọdún yìí. Ìjábọ̀ náà lè di gbígbà silẹ̀ níbí Ètò Ìrànwọ́ Àmójútó Ẹ̀ka Agbára ilé-ìfowópamọ́ Àgbáyé (ESMAP) ló pèsè owó ìrànwọ́ fún ìjábọ̀ náà.
translations.reference: This is the ninth edition of this report, formerly known as the Global Tracking Framework (GTF). This year’s edition was chaired by the World Health Organization (WHO). The report can be downloaded here Funding for the report was provided by the World Bank’s Energy Sector Management Assistance Program (ESMAP).
translations.hypothesis: This year's health summit focused on the Global Fund's operating model. The World Health Organization (WHO) led the summit. The event was supported financially by the Energy Sector Management Assistance Program (ESMAP).

translations.source: Ìgbìmọ Ilẹ̀ Yúróópù àti WHO ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ ìlera àkósódù tó lápẹẹrẹ láti ró ààbò ìlera lágbàyé lágbára Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Lágbàyé (WHO) àti Ìgbìmọ Ilẹ̀ Yúróópù ti kéde ìfilọ́lẹ̀ àjọṣepọ̀ ìlera àkósódù tó lápẹẹrẹ lónì. Ní oṣù Òkudù ọdún 2023, WHO máa gba àkóso ètò Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Ilẹ̀ Yúróópù (EU) lórí ìwé-ẹ̀rí COVID-19 àkósódù láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò àgbáyé tí yóò ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti kó àwọn ènìyàn jọ lágbàyé tí yóò sì dáàbò bo àwọn ènìyàn káàkiri àgbáyé níbi àwọn ewu ìlera lọ́wọ́lọ́wọ́ àti lọ́jọ́ iwájú, tó fi mọ́ àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn. Èyí ni ojúlé àkọ́kọ́ ojú-òpóÌwé-ẹ̀rí Ìlera Àkósódù Lágbàyé WHO (GDHCN) tí yóò pèsè ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ohun èlò àkósódù láti pèsè ètò ìlera tó dára fún gbogbo ènìyàn. "Gígùn lé àṣeyọrí ńlá ojú-òpóìwé-ẹ̀rí àkósódù EU, WHO gbèrò láti fún gbogbo àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ WHO ní ànfààní sí irinṣẹ́ ìlera àkósódù tí yóò wà fún lílò gbogbo ènìyàn, tí ó dá lórí àwọn ìpìlẹ̀-òye ìdọ́gba, ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun, kíkó àkóyawọ́ àti ìdáàbò bo dátà àti àṣírí," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀.
translations.reference: The European Commission and WHO launch landmark digital health initiative to strengthen global health security The World Health Organization (WHO) and European Commission have announced today the launch of a landmark digital health partnership. In June 2023, WHO will take up the European Union (EU) system of digital COVID-19 certification to establish a global system that will help facilitate global mobility and protect citizens across the world from on-going and future health threats, including pandemics. This is the first building block of the WHO Global Digital Health Certification Network (GDHCN) that will develop a wide range of digital products to deliver better health for all. “Building on the EU’s highly successful digital certification network, WHO aims to offer all WHO Member States access to an open-source digital health tool, which is based on the principles of equity, innovation, transparency and data protection and privacy,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General.
translations.hypothesis: The European Union and the World Health Organization (WHO) have announced the launch of a groundbreaking health initiative aimed at strengthening global health security. Today, the WHO and the European Union announced the launch of a collaborative health initiative designed to address global health challenges. In October 2023, the WHO will take over the management of the European Union's COVID-19 vaccine portfolio to create a global program that will help protect people around the world from current and future health threats, including pandemics. This initiative is a key component of the WHO's Global Digital Health Certificate Network (GDHCN), which will provide a secure and interoperable platform for sharing health information. "Building on the success of the EU's digital certificate, the WHO is committed to ensuring that all WHO member states have access to a secure and reliable digital health certificate system that is based on privacy, security, and interoperability," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the WHO.

translations.source: "Àwọn ohun èlò ìlera àkósódù nínú ìdàgbàsókè gbèrò láti ran àwọn ènìyàn lọ́wọ́ níbi gbogbo láti gba ìtọ́jú ìlera kíákíá tí ó sì ṣiṣẹ́ dáadáa". Ó dá lórí ìlànà-iṣẹ́ Ìlera Àgbáyé EU àti ìlànà-iṣẹ́ WHO Àgbáyé lórí ìlera àkósódù, ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ náà tẹ̀lé àdéhùn ọjọ́ 30 oṣù Bẹlu ọdún 2022 láàrín Kọmíṣọ́na Kyriakides àti Dr Tedros láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ ọlọ́gbọ́n lórí àwọn ìṣòro ìlera lágbàyé. Èyí túnbọ̀ ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ètò oríṣiríṣi tó lágbára pẹ̀lú WHO ní pàápàá ẹ̀ gan-an, tí EU tó lágbára ṣe onígbọ̀wọ́ rẹ̀. "Àjọṣepọ̀ yìí jẹ́ ìgbésẹ̀ pàtàkì fún ètò iṣẹ́ àkósódù ìlànà-iṣẹ́ Ìlera Àgbáyé EU. Láti lo àwọn àṣà Yúróópù tó dára jùlọ a máa ṣe àfikún sí ojúlówó ìlera àkósódù àti ìṣiṣẹ́ káàkiri àgbàyé-fún ànfààní àwọn tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀ jùlọ. Ó tún jẹ́ àpẹẹrẹ tó lágbára fún bí idọ́gba láàrín EU àti WHO lè ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìlera tó dára fún gbogbo ènìyàn, ní EU àti káàkiri àgbáyé.
translations.reference: “New digital health products in development aim to help people everywhere receive quality health services quickly and more effectively”. Based on the EU Global Health Strategy and WHO Global strategy on digital health, the initiative follows the 30 November 2022 agreement between Commissioner Kyriakides and Dr Tedros to enhance strategic cooperation on global health issues. This further bolsters a robust multilateral system with WHO at its core, powered by a strong EU.“This partnership is an important step for the digital action plan of the EU Global Health Strategy. By using European best practices we contribute to digital health standards and interoperability globally—to the benefit of those most in need. It is also a powerful example of how alignment between the EU and the WHO can deliver better health for all, in the EU and across the world.
translations.hypothesis: "The joint health action plan aims to support people everywhere in accessing quality healthcare and improving health outcomes." It is based on the EU's Global Health Strategy and the World Health Organization's (WHO) global health strategy for infectious diseases, following a commitment made on March 30, 2022, between Commissioner Kyriakides and Dr. Tedros to strengthen scientific cooperation on global health challenges. This commitment specifically supports various initiatives led by the WHO, which the EU is proud to support. "This partnership is a crucial step for the EU's Global Health Action Plan. By leveraging the best European practices, we will contribute to strengthening health security and global health systems, particularly for those who need it most. It is also a powerful example of how collaboration between the EU and the WHO can improve health for all, both within the EU and globally."

translations.source: Gẹ́gẹ́ bí aláṣẹ tó ń darí tó sì ń ṣàkóso iṣẹ́ ìlera ilẹ̀ òkèèrè, kò sí alájọṣepọ̀ tó dára jùlọ ju WHO láti ṣe ìtẹ̀síwájú iṣẹ́ tí a bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní EU àti láti túnbọ̀ pèsè àwọn ọ̀nà àbáyọ ìlera àkósódù lágbayé," Stella Kyriakides, Kọmíṣọ́na fún Ètò Ìlera àti Ààbò Oúnjẹ ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. Nínú àjọṣepọ̀ yìí lati máa rí àjọṣepọ̀ tó gúnmọ́ nínú ìdàgbàsókè, àmójútó àti àmúlò ètò GDHCN WHO, tí yóò jẹ ànfààní látara àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ Ìgbìmò Ilẹ̀ Yúróópù tí ó tó ní ẹ̀ka náà. Ìgbésẹ̀ àkọ́kọ́ ni láti rí i pé ìwé-ẹ̀rí àkósódù EU lọ́wọ́lọ́wọ́ tẹ̀síwájú láti ṣiṣẹ́ dáadáa." Pẹ̀lú àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè àti agbègbè 80 tí wọ́n sopọ̀ mọ́ ìwé-ẹ̀rí COVID-19 àkósódù EU, EU ti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ òdiwọ̀n àgbáyé. Kìí ṣe wí pé ìwé-ẹ̀ri EU ti di irinṣẹ́ pàtàkì nínú ìgbógun ti àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn wa nìkan, ṣùgbọ́n ó tún ti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìrìnàjò àti ìrìnàjò-afẹ́ ilẹ̀ òkèèrè.
