translations.reference: As the directing and coordinating authority on international health work, there is no better partner than the WHO to advance the work we started at the EU and further develop global digital health solutions,” said Stella Kyriakides, Commissioner for Health and Food Safety. This partnership will include close collaboration in the development, management and implementation of the WHO GDHCN system, benefitting from the European Commission’s ample technical expertise in the field. A first step is to ensure that the current EU digital certificates continue to function effectively.“ With 80 countries and territories connected to the EU Digital COVID-19 Certificate, the EU has set a global standard. The EU certificate has not only been an important tool in our fight against the pandemic, but has also facilitated international travel and tourism.
translations.hypothesis: "As a leading global health organization, the WHO is the best partner to advance the work initiated by the EU and to address global health challenges," said Stella Kyriakides, Commissioner for Health and Food Safety. This partnership, which will build on the strengths of the WHO's Global Health Security Programme, will be supported by experts from the European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control. The initial focus will be to ensure the continued effective operation of the EU's COVID-19 vaccination program. With 80 countries and regions connected to the EU's COVID-19 vaccination program, the EU has established a global platform. Not only has the EU's vaccine program been crucial in the fight against the pandemic, but it has also facilitated travel and mobility within the European region.

translations.source: Inú mi dùn wí pé WHO máa ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìpìlẹ̀-òye adáábòbo-àṣírí àti àwọn ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ ìwé-ẹ̀ri EU alágbára láti pèsè irinṣẹ́ àgbáyé kojú àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn ọjọ́ iwájú," Thierry Breton, Kọmíṣọ́na fún Ọjà Ilẹ̀ Òkèèrè fi kún-un. Ètò WHO àgbáyé tó ń gùn lé ipa EU Ọ̀kan lára àwọn ohun tó ṣe pàtàkì nínú iṣẹ́ Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Ilẹ̀ Yúróópù kojú àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 ni àwọn ìwé-ẹ̀ri COVID-19 àkósódù. Láti pèsè lílọ-bíbọ̀ nírọ́rùn láàrín ààlà rẹ̀, EU tètè ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ ìwé-ẹ̀rí iṣẹ́ COVID-19 (tó ní àkòrí 'Ìwé-ẹ̀ri COVID-19 Àkósódù EU' tàbí 'EU DCC'). Ó dá lórí àwọn ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ tó wà fún gbogbo ènìyàn àti ojúlówó tó fááyè gbà bákan náà fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí kìí ṣe EU tí ó ń ṣe àgbéjáde àwọn ìwé-ẹ̀rí gẹ́gẹ́ bí àlàkalẹ̀ EU DCC, tí ó sì ti di ọ̀nà àbáyọ tí wọ́n ń lò jùlọ káàkiri àgbáyé.
translations.reference: I am pleased that the WHO will build on the privacy-preserving principles and cutting-edge technology of the EU certificate to create a global tool against future pandemics,” added Thierry Breton, Commissioner for Internal Market. A global WHO system building on EU legacy One of the key elements in the European Union’s work against the COVID-19 pandemic has been digital COVID-19 certificates. To facilitate free movement within its borders, the EU swiftly established interoperable COVID-19 certificates (entitled ‘EU Digital COVID-19 Certificate’ or ‘EU DCC’). Based on open-source technologies and standards it allowed also for the connection of non-EU countries that issue certificates according to EU DCC specifications, becoming the most widely used solution around the world.
translations.hypothesis: "I am pleased that the WHO will be using the EU's digital COVID certificate and other digital health credentials to facilitate international travel and combat future pandemics," said Thierry Breton, the European Commissioner for the Internal Market. The WHO's global initiative, which builds on the EU's experience with COVID-19 certificates, is a key part of the European Union's efforts to address the COVID-19 pandemic. To ensure interoperability and ease of use, the EU has developed a digital COVID certificate (known as the 'EU Digital COVID Certificate' or 'EU DCC'). This system is based on open standards and is accessible to all citizens and travelers. It has also been adopted by many non-EU countries as a model for their own digital health certificate systems, and has become a widely used standard around the world.

translations.source: Láti ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà, WHO ti ń sọ̀rọ̀ papọ̀ pẹ̀lú gbogbo àwọn Ẹkùn Agbègbè WHO láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìtọ́nisọ́nà àpapọ̀ fún irú ìwé-ẹ̀rí bẹ́ẹ̀. Láti lè báni ró ìgbaradì ìlera àgbáyé lágbára ní ojú àwọn ewu ìlera tó ń gbòòrò si, WHO ti ń ń ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ ọjú òpó ìwé-ẹ̀rí ìlera àkósódù àgbáyé tí yóò gùn lé àwọn ìpìlẹ̀ lílé ìlànà-iṣẹ́ EU DCC, ìpìlẹ̀-òye àti àwọn ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ tó ṣí sílẹ̀ fún gbogbo ènìyàn. Pẹ̀lú àjọṣepọ̀ yìí, WHO máa ṣe ìlànà yìí káàkiri àgbáyé lábẹ́ àgbékalẹ̀ tirẹ̀ pẹ̀lú èròngbà láti jẹ́ kí gbogbo ayé lè jẹ ànfààní látara àkójọpọ̀ àwọn ìwé-ẹ̀rí àkósódù. Lára èyí la ti rí ètò-ojúlówó àti ìfọwọ́sí àwọn ìbuwọ́lù àkósódù láti dènà ìwa jìbìtì. Láti ṣe bẹ́ẹ̀, WHO kò ní ní ànfààní sí èyíkéyìí dátà àdáni lábẹ́nú, tí yóò tẹ̀síwájú láti jẹ́ ibùgbé ìyàsọ́tọ̀ àwọn ìjọba.
translations.reference: From the onset of the pandemic, WHO engaged with all WHO Regions to define overall guidelines for such certificates. To help strengthen global health preparedness in the face of growing health threats, WHO is establishing a global digital health certification network which builds upon the solid foundations of the EU DCC framework, principles and open technologies. With this collaboration, WHO will facilitate this process globally under its own structure with the aim to allow the world to benefit from convergence of digital certificates. This includes standard-setting and validation of digital signatures to prevent fraud. In doing so, WHO will not have access to any underlying personal data, which would continue to be the exclusive domain of governments.
translations.hypothesis: Since the outbreak of the disease, the WHO has been working closely with all WHO regional offices to develop a common framework for such vaccines. To effectively address the growing global health threats, the WHO is developing a global vaccine strategy that will align with the EU's DCC action plan, knowledge sharing, and technological advancements for the benefit of all. Through this collaboration, the WHO will implement this strategy worldwide under its own framework, with the aim of ensuring that everyone can benefit from the collective efforts in vaccine development. This includes the development of a robust and reliable supply chain, as well as the establishment of quality control mechanisms. To achieve this, the WHO will need access to reliable data, which will be crucial in guiding the development and deployment of vaccines. This will also help to build trust and confidence in the vaccines, which is essential for their successful implementation.

translations.source: Ojúlé àkọ́kọ́ ètò WHO àgbáyé yóò bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí ṣiṣẹ́ ní oṣù Òkudù ọdún 2023 ó sì ń gbèrò láti ní ìdagbàsókè ìlọsíwájú ní àwọn oṣù tó ń bọ̀. Àjọṣepọ̀ àkósódù ọlọ́jọ́-pípẹ́ yóò ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìlera tó dára fún gbogbo ènìyàn Láti lè ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún gbígba àkóso EU DCC fún WHO àti láti dásí àwọn iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ àti ìdàgbàsókè síwájú sí i, WHO àti Ìgbìmọ Ilẹ̀ Yúróópù ti gbà latí dòwòpọ̀ nínú ìlera àkósódù. Àjosoyépò yìí máa ṣiṣẹ́ láti mú ìdàgbàsókè ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ bá ètò WHO pẹ̀lú ìlànà tí wọ́n ti gbé kalẹ̀ láti kárí àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àlékún lílò, níbi tí a ti lè rí, fún àpẹẹrẹ, ṣíṣe àkósódù Ìwé-ẹ̀rí Ilẹ̀ Òkèèrè fún Àjẹsára tàbí Ìdèna ààrùn. Fífẹ irú ọ̀nà àbáyọ àkósódù bẹ́ẹ̀ lójú máa ṣe pàtàkì láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò ìlera tó dára fún àwọn aráàlú káàkiri àgbáyé.
translations.reference: The first building block of the global WHO system becomes operational in June 2023 and aims to be progressively developed in the coming months. A long-term digital partnership to deliver better health for all To facilitate the uptake of the EU DCC by WHO and contribute to its operation and further development, WHO and the European Commission have agreed to partner in digital health. This partnership will work to technically develop the WHO system with a staged approach to cover additional use cases, which may include, for example, the digitisation of the International Certificate of Vaccination or Prophylaxis. Expanding such digital solutions will be essential to deliver better health for citizens across the globe.
translations.hypothesis: The first phase of the WHO's global initiative will begin in October 2023 and is expected to yield significant progress in the coming months. This long-term partnership will focus on improving health outcomes for all people. To support the EU's Digital COVID Certificate (DCC) for the WHO and to facilitate its use and further development, the WHO and the European Union have agreed to collaborate in the area of digital health. This partnership will leverage the EU's expertise in digital technology to enhance the WHO's programs, including the development of digital health certificates for vaccination or disease testing. This type of innovative approach is crucial for building effective health programs for communities around the world.

translations.source: Ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ yìí dá lórí àwọn àṣà àjọni àti àwọn ìpìlẹ̀-òye kíkó àkóyawọ́ àti híhànde, aláfibọ̀, ìṣirò ṣíṣe, ìdáàbò bo dátà àti àṣírí, ètò ààbò, gbígbóórò ní ìpele àgbáyé, àti ìdọ́gba. WHO àti Ìgbìmọ Ilẹ̀ Yúróópù máa ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti ṣe ìwúrí fún gbígbà á lágbàyé pátápátá àti kíkópa. Àkíyèsí pàápàá máa wà lórí àwọn ànfààní tó dọ́gba fún kíkópa àwọn tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀ jùlọ: àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré àti agbede-méjì.
translations.reference: This cooperation is based on the shared values and principles of transparency and openness, inclusiveness, accountability, data protection and privacy, security, scalability at a global level, and equity. The WHO and the European Commission will work together to encourage maximum global uptake and participation. Particular attention will be paid to equitable opportunities for the participation by those most in need: low and middle-income countries.
translations.hypothesis: This initiative focuses on daily practices and key areas such as hygiene, sanitation, water, food safety, disease prevention, health promotion, global health security, and emergency preparedness. The World Health Organization (WHO) and the European Union work together to provide support for these efforts worldwide, with a particular focus on vulnerable populations: low-income countries and young people.

translations.source: Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin - Ìròyìn ojoojúmọ́: 30 oṣù Èbìbí ọdún 2023 Àpéjọ ọlọ́dọọdún WHO parí pẹ̀lu àdéhùn lórí ìpèsè owó, àti oríṣiríṣi àwọn àkòrí-ọ̀rọ̀ ìlera Àpéjọ ìlera 76th WHO parí lónì, lẹ́yìn tí wọ́n ti yanjú oríṣiríṣi àwọn ìṣòro tó tóbi, tó fi mọ́ àwọn sáyẹnsì ìhùwàsí; àwọn tí wọ́n rà julọ fún àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn; àyẹwò ààrùn; ìpèníjà ara; ìdènà rírì; pàjáwírì, ìtọ́jú pàtàkì tó sì ń ṣiṣẹ́; àwọn aṣara lóore inú oúnjẹ; ètò ìlera ìbílẹ̀; ìdènà àti ìtọ́jú ìkóràn; ètò ìlera ìyá àti ọmọ; èémí-àmísínú ètò ìlera; ètò ìlera alábọ́dé; ìlera àwọn asásàálà àti aṣíkiri;
translations.reference: Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly – Daily update: 30 May 2023 WHO’s annual assembly ends with agreement on funding, and array of health topics WHO’s 76th health assembly closed today, having addressed a vast array of issues, including behavioural sciences; best buys for non-communicable diseases; diagnostics; disabilities; drowning prevention; emergency, critical and operative care; food micronutrients; indigenous health; infection prevention and control; maternal and child health; medical oxygen; primary health care; refugee and migrant health;
translations.hypothesis: World Health Assembly 76th Session - Daily News: March 30, 2023. The annual WHO meeting concluded today with a focus on financial contributions and various health priorities. The 76th World Health Assembly concluded today, following discussions on a range of pressing global health issues, including: neglected tropical diseases; emerging and re-emerging infectious diseases; non-communicable diseases; mental health; road safety; the social determinants of health; health systems; prevention and control of communicable diseases; maternal and child health; universal health coverage; health in emergencies; and health workforce.

translations.source: ìtúnirọ; ìṣègùn ìbílẹ̀, àti iṣẹ́ WHO ní ìdáhùn sí dọ́sínì àwọn pàjáwírì nígbà tí wọ́n tún ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti túnbọ̀ múra sílẹ̀ dáadáa láti kojú àwọn nǹkan tuntun. Síwájú àkókò yìí lónì, àwọn aṣojú ìjọba ní Ìgbìmọ A fẹnukò láti ṣe àkọsílẹ̀ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ tọ Ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Ìlera àti Àlàáfíà Àgbáyé (papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àyípadà díẹ̀ nínú orúkọ láti "Ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Ìlera Àgbáyé fún Àlàáfíà"), wọ́n sì tún bèèrè pé kí Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ ó jábọ̀ lórí ìtẹ̀síwájú nínú ìrólágbára ìlànà-iṣẹ́ náà. Nínú ìjókòó ilé, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìgbésẹ̀ ìfọwọ́sí ìgbẹ̀yìn àpéjọ náà, àwọn aṣojú ìjọba gba àwọn ìfẹnukò àti ìpinnu àwọn ìgbìmọ̀ méjèèjì wọlé, wọ́n sì gba ìjábọ̀ wọn. Lára rẹ̀ la ti rí ìfọwọ́sí ètò ìṣúná fún 2024-25, àti àlékún 20% nínú àwọn owó àjọ tí wọ́n yẹ̀wò. Àwọn alága ìgbìmọ̀ náà àti àwọn aṣojú láti àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè méjèèjì sọ̀rọ̀ láti mọ rírì iṣẹ́ àti ìlọsíwájú Àpéjọ yìí.
translations.reference: rehabilitation; traditional medicine, and WHO’s work in responding to dozens of emergencies while working with Member States to be better prepared to face new ones. Earlier in the day, delegates in Committee A agreed to note the roadmap towards the Global Health and Peace Initiative (along with a slight change in name from “Global Health for Peace Initiative”), and requested that the Director-General report on progress in strengthening the roadmap. In plenary, as the final approval step of the assembly, delegates adopted the resolutions and decisions of the two committees, and adopted their reports. This included approval of the budget for 2024-25, and a 20% increase in assessed contributions. The committee chairs and representatives from two regions spoke to recognize the work and progress of this Assembly.
translations.hypothesis: The resolution; the amendment, and the WHO's work in addressing health emergencies, as well as its collaboration with member states to effectively respond to new challenges. Prior to today's meeting, government representatives from the General Assembly were invited to present the draft resolution on the World Health Organization (with a slight modification to the name, now "World Health Organization for Health"), and they were also asked to provide feedback on the proposed amendments. During the plenary session, following a brief presentation by the rapporteur of the committee, the government representatives formally introduced the draft resolution and the amendments, and they responded to questions. Among the items discussed was the proposed budget for 2024-25, including a 20% increase in the organization's core funding. The leaders of the committee and representatives from both regional groups expressed their support for the work and progress of the organization.

translations.source: Nínú ọ̀rọ̀ ìparí rẹ̀, Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO, sọ wí pé "àlékún nínú owó àjọ tí wọ́n yẹ̀wò àti owó ìdókówò jẹ́ mánigbàgbé àti àṣeyọrí tó tóbi." Ó sọ̀rọ̀ nípa ọdún tó ń bọ̀ níwájú, pẹ̀lú àwọn ìpàdé onípele-gíga lórí kíkárí ìlera lágbàyé, ikọ́-ife àti ìmúrasílẹ̀ àti ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn níbi Àpéjọ Àpapọ̀ UN fún ti ọdún yìí. Ó tọ́ka sí ìtẹ̀síwájú ìdúnàdúrà lórí ìṣọ̀kan àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àtúnṣe sí Àwọn Òfin Ìlera Ilẹ̀ Òkèèrè gẹ́gẹ́ bí nǹkan tí wọn ò lérò - "ìran-díran"-àwọn ànfààní láti kọ́ ẹ̀kọ́ látara àwọn àṣìṣe àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 àti láti rí i dájú pé wọn ò tún wáyé mọ́. Àwọn aṣojú ìjọba tún ní ànfààní láti gbọ́ láti ọ̀dọ òṣìṣẹ́ WHO tẹ́lẹ̀ rí Gwen Carnelley, tí ó pé 100 ní ọdún yìí, tí ó bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú WHO ní ọdún 1949, ọdún kan péré lẹ́yìn tí wọ́n dá WHO sílẹ̀ ní ọdún 75 sẹ́yìn. Fónrán amóhùnmáwòrán fún Ìparí Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé 76.
translations.reference: In his closing remarks, Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO’s Director-General, noted that “the increase in assessed contributions and the investment round are historic and a huge milestone.” He spoke about the year ahead, with high-level meetings on universal health coverage, tuberculosis and pandemic preparedness and response at this year’s UN General Assembly. He pointed to the continuing negotiations on the pandemic accord and amendments to the International Health Regulations as unprecedented—“generational”—opportunities to learn from the mistakes of the COVID-19 pandemic and ensure they are not repeated. Delegates also had the chance to hear from former WHO staff Gwen Carnelley, who turned 100 this year, who began working with WHO in 1949, just a year after WHO was founded 75 years ago. Video of Closing of the 76th World Health Assembly.
translations.hypothesis: In his closing remarks, Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, the Director-General of the WHO, stated that "the past year has been marked by significant challenges and remarkable achievements." He spoke about the year ahead, highlighting upcoming high-level meetings on global health, innovation, and pandemic preparedness, as well as discussions on addressing health emergencies at this year's United Nations General Assembly. He emphasized the progress made in strengthening global health security and revising the International Health Regulations, calling them "bold" steps taken to learn from the mistakes of the COVID-19 pandemic and to ensure they are not repeated. Government representatives also paid tribute to Gwen Carnelley, a former WHO staff member who is celebrating her 100th birthday this year. She began working with the WHO in 1949, just one year after the organization was established 75 years ago. Photo for the 76th World Health Assembly closing.

translations.source: Wọ́n ṣàfihàn Gwen Carnelley ní 54:37 ó sì sọ̀rọ̀ ní àkókò díẹ̀ lẹ́yin rẹ̀. Bẹ̀rẹ̀ láti ọ̀la, ìpàdé 153rd Ìgbìmọ̀ Alàkóso máa bẹ̀rẹ̀, níbi tí wọn ó ti jíròrò nípa àwọn àbájáde Àpéjọ náà, pẹ̀lú àwọn nǹkan mìíràn.
translations.reference: Gwen Carnelley is introduced at 54:37 and speaks shortly afterwards. Starting tomorrow, the 153rd meeting of Executive Board begins, where the outcomes of the Assembly will be discussed, among other things.
translations.hypothesis: Gwen Carnelley was announced at 54:37, and she spoke for a short time afterward. Following her, the 153rd Board Meeting is scheduled to begin, where discussions will focus on the Assembly's agenda, along with other matters.

translations.source: Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin - Ìròyìn ojoojúmọ́: 29 oṣù Èbìbí ọdún 2023 Ìpinnu tó lápẹrẹ fún ìrólágbára ìlera àwọn ọmọ ìlú káàkiri àgbayé Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé lónì ti buwọ́lù ìpinnu àìròtẹ́lẹ̀ lórí ìlera àwọn ọmọ ìlú, tí ó bèèrè fún kí Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ ó ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò amúṣẹ́ṣe àgbáyé fún ìlera àwọn ọmọ ìlú kí ó sì ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ níbi Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé kọkàn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin ní ọdún 2026. Àwọn Ọmọ Ìlú, bó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ wí pé wọ́n jẹ́ oríṣiríṣi ìran ènìyàn àti àwùjọ, ní àpapọ̀ wọ́n ní ìṣẹ̀mí tó kéré nínú èrò ju àwọn ènìyàn tí kìí ṣe ọmọ ìlú lọ. Wọ́n tún ní ọ̀nkà àpapọ ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìsan tó pọ̀ fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ààrùn àti ipò ìlera tí ò dára, tó fi mọ́ àtọ̀gbẹ, ikú ìyá àti ọmọ àti àìtó èròjà purotéénì.
translations.reference: Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly – Daily update: 29 May 2023 Landmark resolution for strengthening Indigenous Peoples’ health globally The World Health Assembly today approved an unprecedented resolution on the health of Indigenous Peoples, which requests the Director-General to develop a global action plan for the health of indigenous peoples and to present it to the Seventy-ninth World Health Assembly in 2026. Indigenous Peoples, although representing diverse population groups and communities, in general have considerably lower life expectancy than non-indigenous populations. They also have a higher prevalence of many diseases and adverse health conditions, including diabetes, maternal and infant mortality and malnutrition.
translations.hypothesis: World Health Assembly - Daily News: April 29, 2023. Landmark decision to strengthen child health globally. The World Health Assembly today adopted a resolution on child health, urging Member States to develop and implement national action plans for child health and to report on their progress at the World Health Assembly in 2026. Children, despite their diversity, share common vulnerabilities. They are disproportionately affected by poverty and have a higher risk of developing many diseases and health conditions, leading to disability, premature death, and reduced life expectancy.

translations.source: Àpéjọ náà pè fún wí pé kí ètò amúṣẹ́ṣe náà jẹ́ ṣíṣe ní ìfọ̀rọ̀wérọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn Ọmọ Ìlú; pé kí WHO ó pèsè àtìlẹ́yìn fún Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́, tí wọ́n bá bèèrè fún-un, fún àyípadà ìlera ìbílẹ̀; àti pé kí àyípadà ìlera àwọn Ọmọ Ìlú di àfikún sínú àgbékalẹ̀ Ètò Iṣẹ́ Àpapọ WHO Kẹrìnlá. Nínú ìpinnu kan náà, Àpéjọ Ìlera náà rọ Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti, pẹ̀lú àwọn iṣẹ́ yòókù, ní òye nípa ipò ìlera Àwọn Ọmọ Ìlú, pẹ̀lú ìfọwọ́sí ọ̀fẹ́, ṣíwájú àti ìfitónilétí wọn; ṣe àgbékalẹ̀, owó kí wọ́n sì ṣàmúlò àwọn ètò ìlera orílẹ̀-èdè, àwọn ìlànà-iṣẹ́ tàbí àwọn ètò mìíràn fún Àwọn Ọmọ Ìlú;
translations.reference: The Assembly requested the action plan be developed in consultation with Indigenous Peoples; that WHO provide support to Member States, upon request, for improving indigenous health; and that the improvement of Indigenous Peoples’ health be included in the development of the Fourteenth WHO General Programme of Work. In the same resolution, the Health Assembly urged Member States to, among other tasks, develop knowledge about the health situation of Indigenous Peoples, with their free, prior and informed consent; develop, fund and implement national health plans, strategies or other measures for Indigenous Peoples;
translations.hypothesis: The assembly called for the implementation of health programs in a sustainable manner with community involvement; for the WHO to provide support to member states, upon request, for the development of community health; and for community health to be integrated into the framework of the WHO's Fourth Strategic Plan. In a related decision, the Health Assembly urged member states, in conjunction with other relevant actors, to promote awareness of the health status of communities, including their social, economic, and environmental determinants; to develop, fund, and implement national community health programs, policies, or other initiatives.

translations.source: ṣe ìwúrí fún ìfanimọ́ra, ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́, ìgbani síṣẹ́ àti gbígba àwọn Ọmọ Ìlú gẹ́gẹ́ bí òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera pẹ̀lú ìbojú wo ìmọ̀ ìbílẹ̀ àti àsà. Àwọn ìwé to fara jọ ọ́: A76/A/CONF./1 - Ìlera Àwọn Ọmọ Ìlú Ìpinnu àkọ́kọ́-irú-rẹ̀ láti mú iṣẹ́ yá lórí ìdènà rírì Àwọn aṣojú ìjọba tí wọ́n wà níbi Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin fẹnukò lórí ìpinnu láti mú iṣẹ́ yá lórí ìdènà rírì. Ìpinnu náà pè fún kí Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ ó ṣe àyẹ̀wò ipò rírì ní orílẹ̀-èdè wọn kí wọ́n sì ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àti àmúlò àwọn ètò ìdènà rírì ọlọ́pọ̀-ìsọ̀rí. Rírì ń fa ikú 236 000 ní ọdọọdún.
translations.reference: encourage the attraction, training, recruitment and retention of Indigenous Peoples as health workers taking into account the traditional knowledge and practices. Related document: A76/A/CONF./1 - Health of Indigenous Peoples First-ever resolution to accelerate action on drowning preventionThe delegates at the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly agreed a resolution to accelerate action on global drowning prevention. The resolution requests Member States to assess their national drowning situation and to develop and implement multisectoral drowning prevention programmes. Drowning causes 236 000 deaths every year.
translations.hypothesis: Promoting health, preventing diseases, and improving the well-being of urban populations are crucial aspects of public health, especially considering the cultural context. Relevant documents: A76/A/CONF./1 - Urban Health: Initial steps to address the issue. The resolution, adopted at the Forty-sixth World Health Assembly, calls for action to address the issue. The resolution urges Member States to assess the health situation in their urban areas and to develop and implement appropriate health programs. Poor health leads to 236,000 deaths annually.

translations.source: Ó jẹ́ okùnfà ikú àwọn ọmọdé ajẹmọ́-ìfarapa tó ń léwájú lágbayé. Láti bí ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn, 2.5 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn ló kú látara rírì, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni 90% lé nínú rẹ̀ ló ṣẹlẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré àti agbede-méjì. Níbi ìfìwé peni Àpéjọ Àpapọ̀ Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé (UN), WHO máa darí àwọn iṣẹ́ láàrín ètò UN lórí ìdènà rírì yóò sì ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ṣíṣe Àyájọ́ Ọjọ́ Ìdènà Rírì Lágbàyé ní ọjọ́ 25 oṣù Agẹmọ ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan. WHO máa tún ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ Ìdènà Rírì fún Ìsọ̀kan Àgbáyé pẹ̀lú àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ ètò UN, àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ìdagbàsókè ilẹ̀ òkèèrè àti àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ tí kìí ṣe ti ìjọba. Láti túnbọ̀ ní òye ẹrù tòótọ́ àti ipa rírì, ìpinnu náà tún tẹ̀síwájú láti pè fún WHO láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìjábọ̀ ipò àgbáyé lórí ìdènà rírì.
translations.reference: It is a leading global cause of injury-related child deaths. Over the past decade, 2.5 million people died from drowning, and over 90% of those occurred in low- and middle-income countries. At the invitation of the United Nations (UN) General Assembly, WHO will coordinate actions within the UN system on drowning prevention and facilitate the observance of World Drowning Prevention Day on 25 July each year. WHO will also set up a Global Alliance for Drowning Prevention with organizations of the UN system, international development partners and nongovernmental organizations. To better understand the true burden and impact of drowning, the resolution further requests WHO to prepare a global status report on drowning prevention.
translations.hypothesis: It is a tragic reality that childhood injuries are a leading cause of death and disability worldwide. For the past decade, 2.5 million people have died from injuries, with 90% of these deaths occurring in low- and middle-income countries. Within the United Nations system, the World Health Organization (WHO) leads efforts to address injury prevention and will support the implementation of the Decade of Action for Road Safety, launched on May 25th each year. The WHO also collaborates with other UN agencies, international organizations, and non-governmental organizations to promote injury prevention globally. To better understand the scope and impact of injuries, the resolution also calls for the WHO to develop a global status report on injuries.

translations.source: Àwọn ìwé tó fara jọ ọ́: EB152/CONF./2 - Mímú iṣẹ́ yá lórí ìdènà rírì lágbàyé Kà sí i nípa ìpinnu náà Àyájọ́ Ọjọ́ Ìdènà Rírì Lágbàyé 2023 Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ tò sẹ́yìn ìpinnu láti kojú kẹ́míkà, ìdọ̀tí àti bíba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ gba ìpinnu tó ń yanjú àwọn aṣokùnfà àwùjọ wọlé, tó fi mọ́ àmójútó àwọn kẹ́míkà àti ìdọ̀tí. Àwọn ìgbìyànjú náà lè ṣe ìrànwọ́ láti dènà tó ìdá kan nínú ìdá márùn-ún àwọn ikú ajẹmọ-ìgbẹ̀mí-ara-ẹni látara àwọn oògùn apa kòkòro tó léwu gidi gan-an.
translations.reference: Related document: EB152/CONF./2 - Accelerating action on global drowning prevention Read more about the resolution World Drowning Prevention Day 2023 Member States mobilize behind resolution to tackle chemicals, waste and pollution Member States welcomed the resolution addressing environmental determinants, including management of chemicals and waste. The efforts can help prevent up to one fifth of all suicide-related deaths from highly hazardous pesticides.
translations.hypothesis: Subject: EB152/CONF./2 - Follow-up on the implementation of the Global Strategy to Reduce Tobacco Demand Regarding the Decision Taken by the World Health Assembly 2023. Member States that have committed to reducing tobacco use, including tobacco control policies, have adopted a decision that promotes the reduction of tobacco consumption and the prevention of tobacco use. These measures can help to prevent one of the four major non-communicable diseases, namely cancer, which is a serious public health concern.

translations.source: Wọ́n rọ Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ìṣàmúlò àwọn ìlànà-iṣẹ́ WHO tó ti wà tẹ́lẹ̀ tó fi mọ́ ìṣàmúlò ìlànà-iṣẹ́ kẹ́míkà WHO tí ó ṣe àlàkalẹ̀ àwọn ojúṣe pàtàkì fún ẹ̀ka ètò ìlera nínú ìṣàmúlò Ìlànà Ọlọ́gbọ́n fún Àmójútó Àwọn Kẹ́míkà Ilẹ̀ Òkèèrè. Ìpinnu náà tún ṣe ìwúrí fún àwọn Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba tó ń rí sí Ètò Ìlera láti máa ṣe àwọn ìgbìyànjú láti pèsè àdéhùn fún ìgbìmọ̀ adájọ́ lórí òfin sáyẹ́nsì ìjọba-sí-ìjọba àti ìdúnàdúrà fún àdéhùn láti fi òpin sí bíba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ ike. Ìpinnu náà ké sí Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ náà láti gbé oríṣiríṣi ìgbésẹ̀, tó fi mọ́: ṣíṣe àtẹ̀jáde ìjábọ̀ lórí àwọn àkóbá kẹ́míkà fún ìlera, ìdọ̀tí àti bíba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ láti ojú ìwòye "One Health"; ìsọdọ̀tun ìwé 2012 lórí Ipò Sáyẹnsì fún Àwọn Kẹ́míkà Tó ń ṣe ìdíwọ́ fún ẹsẹ́ ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú Ètò Agbègbè Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé; àti
translations.reference: Member States were urged to bolster the implementation of existing WHO strategies including implementation of the WHO Chemicals Roadmap which outlines key roles of the health sector in implementing the Strategic Approach to International Chemicals Management. The resolution also encourages the Ministries of Health to engage in efforts to prepare proposals for an intergovernmental science policy panel and negotiations for a treaty to end plastic pollution. The resolution called upon the Director-General to undertake several actions, including: publishing a report on the health implications of chemicals, waste and pollution from a "One Health" perspective; updating the 2012 document on the State of the Science of Endocrine Disrupting Chemicals jointly with the United Nations Environment Programme; and
translations.hypothesis: The member states were urged to support the implementation of the WHO's existing guidelines, including the WHO guidelines on chemical safety, which establish essential requirements for the health sector in the implementation of the Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals. The decision also encourages government agencies involved in health to develop strategies to ensure compliance with national science-based regulations and to promote the adoption of best practices for chemical safety. The decision calls for a range of actions, including: developing guidance documents on chemical safety for health, promoting the "One Health" approach to chemical safety from a public health perspective; updating the 2012 report on the State of Science on Chemicals in Products in collaboration with the United Nations Environment Programme; and

translations.source: ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè nínú àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ètò aṣòfiwọ̀n-kẹ́míkà nínú ọmọnìyàn ní orílẹ̀-èdè tàbí ẹkùn agbègbè fún àwọn kẹ́míkà tí wọ́n jẹ́ ẹ̀dun ọkàn. Àwọn ìwé tó fara jọ ọ́: A76/A/CONF./2 - Ipa àwọn kẹ́míkà, ìdọ̀tí àti bíba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ lórí ìlera ènìyàn Iṣẹ́ WHO lórí ààbò kẹ́míkà àti ìlera Ṣíṣe Àṣeyọrí ìgbáyégbádùn: ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àgbáyé Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ fẹnukò láti gba "ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àgbáyé fún ṣíṣe àfikún ìgbáyé-gbádùn sínú ìlera àwùjọ pẹ̀lú lílo ìlànà ṣíṣe ìpolongo ìlera", tó ń ṣe ìgbìyànjú láti jẹ́ kí gbogbo ènìyàn gbérí àti láti ṣe àṣeyọrí agbára ìlera ara àti ọpọlọ wọn ní kíkún ní gbogbo ọjọ́ ayé wọn àti láti ìran-díran. ìlànà-iṣẹ́ Àgbáyé náà dábàá àwọn ojú ọ̀nà ìlànà-iṣẹ́ pàtàkì mẹ́fà tó dá lórí: kíkárí ìlera lágbàyé, ọrọ̀-ajétó dọ́gba, dídáábò bo ayé, àwọn ètò ìdáàbò bo àwùjọ, àwọn ètò àkósódù láti fún ìlera láàyè, bákan náà ni ìgbéléwọ̀n àti àmójútó ìgbáyégbádùn.
translations.reference: supporting countries in developing national or regional human biomonitoring programmes for chemicals of concern. Related document: A76/A/CONF./2 - The impact of chemicals, waste and pollution on human health WHO’s work on chemical safety and health Achieving well-being: a global frameworkMember States agreed to adopt the "Global framework for integrating well-being into public health utilizing a health promotion approach", which strives to enable all people to flourish and achieve their full physical and mental health potential throughout their lives and across generations. The Global framework recommends six key strategic directions that focus on: universal health coverage, equitable economies, protecting the planet, social protection systems, digital systems to enable health, and measuring and monitoring well-being.
translations.hypothesis: Action and support for countries in the implementation of chemical safety programs in healthcare settings, at the national or regional level, for healthcare workers who are at risk. Relevant documents: A76/A/CONF./2 - The impact of chemicals, waste and air pollution on human health. WHO work on chemical safety and health. Action for improved well-being: A global framework. Member States are encouraged to adopt the "global framework for improving well-being in health systems through the use of health promotion strategies," which aims to ensure that all people are healthy and can achieve their full potential in all aspects of life and at all stages of development. This framework focuses on six key areas of action: health promotion, disease prevention, healthy living, social determinants of health, health systems, and well-being and resilience.

translations.source: ìlànà-iṣẹ́ náà dábàá àjọṣepọ̀ tó gúnmọ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn ẹ̀ka ní ìta ẹ̀ka ètò ìlera láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àti láti dáàbò bo ètò ìlera. Ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìtọ́nisọ́nà fún gbogbo àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ láti kópa ní ọ̀nà tó ní ìṣọ̀kan àti ètò yíká èròngbà àjọni: ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìlera àwọn ènìyàn àti àgbáyé ní ọ̀nà alálòpẹ́ àti ìdọ́gba. ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìlera àwọn ènìyàn àti àgbáyé ní ọ̀nà alálòpẹ́ àti ìdọ́gba. Àpéjọ náà bèèrè pé kí Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ náà jábọ̀ lórí ìṣàmúlò ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àgbáyé ní ọdún 2024, 2026 àti 2031. Àwọn ìwé tó fara jọ ọ́: A76/7 Add.2 - àtìlẹ́yìn ìgbáyé-gbádùn àti ìlera
translations.reference: The framework proposes close collaboration with sectors outside the health sector to promote and protect health. It serves as a guide for all stakeholders to engage in a coherent and coordinated manner around a common purpose: promoting the health of people and planet in a sustainable and equitable manner. promoting the health of people and planet in a sustainable and equitable manner. The Assembly requests the Director-General to report on implementation of the global framework in 2024, 2026 and 2031. Related document: A76/7 Add.2 - Well-being and health promotion
translations.hypothesis: The initiative aims to establish a partnership with various sectors outside the health sector to support and strengthen the health system. It functions as a platform for all stakeholders to participate in a coordinated and effective manner, promoting human and global health in a sustainable and equitable way. The initiative promotes human and global health in a sustainable and equitable way. The Assembly requests the Secretary-General to report on the implementation of the global initiative in 2024, 2026, and 2031. Relevant documents: A76/7 Add.2 - Global health and well-being.

translations.source: Àwọn ìpinnu tuntun láti mú kí ìgbìyànjú lórí ìrólágbára àwọn aṣara lóore inú oúnjẹ yá Àwọn aṣojú ìjọba náà fọwọ́sí ìpinnu kan lórí mímú kí àwọn ìgbìyànjú láti dènà àwọn àìtó aṣara lóore nípasẹ̀ ìrólágbára oúnjẹ tí ò léwu tó sì ń ṣiṣẹ́ ó yá. Àìtó iye fítáámì àti aṣara lóore, pàápàá jùlọ ti fólétì, áyọ́ọ́nù, Fítáámì A, àti zínkì, ń kóbá 50% gbogbo àwọn ọmọ tí ọjọ́ orí wọn wà lábẹ́ ilé-ẹ̀kọ́ àṣẹ̀ṣẹ̀ bẹ̀rẹ̀ àti 67% gbogbo àwọn obìnrin tí ọjọ́ orí wọn wà ní ipò ìbímọ kárí ayé. Àìtó èròjà aṣara lóore lè ní ìpadàbọ̀ tó lágbára, tó fi mọ́ wíwọ́ ọ̀pá ẹ̀yìn àti àwọn àlébù túúbù ìmọ̀lára mìíràn. Ìrólágbára oúnjẹ lórí òṣùwọ̀n-ńlá jẹ́ ọ̀kan lára ọ̀nà àbáyọ náà. Pẹ̀lú ṣíṣe àfikún àwọn fítáámì àti aṣara lóore tó ṣe pàtàkì sí àwọn oúnjẹ ojoojúmọ́ àti àwọn ìsebẹ̀, bíi àlìkámà àti àgbàdo ìyẹ̀fun, ìrẹsì, òróró ìsebẹ̀ àti iyọ̀ ní ìbámu pẹ̀lú bátánì àti lílò orílẹ̀-èdè àti àìtó, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lè ṣe àtúnṣe kí wọ́n sì tún tẹ̀síwájú ìdènà àìtó èròjà aṣara lóore tí wọ́n ṣàpèjúwe.
translations.reference: New resolution to accelerate efforts on food micronutrient fortification The delegates approved a resolution on accelerating efforts to prevent micronutrient deficiencies through safe and effective food fortification. Deficiencies in vitamin and mineral status, particularly of folate, iron, vitamin A, and zinc, affect 50% of all preschool aged children and 67% of all women of reproductive age worldwide. Micronutrient deficiencies can have serious consequences, including spina bifida and other neural tube defects.Large scale food fortification is part of the solution. By adding essential vitamins and minerals to staple foods and condiments, such as wheat and maize flours, rice, cooking oil and salt in accordance with national consumption patterns and deficiencies, countries can correct and further prevent a demonstrated micronutrient deficiency.
translations.hypothesis: New regulations aim to reduce the levels of harmful additives in food. Government officials have approved a measure to require manufacturers to disclose the presence of certain additives in food products that are considered safe and effective. These additives, including artificial sweeteners and preservatives, such as aspartame, sodium benzoate, and zinc oxide, are found in approximately 50% of all food products consumed by children and 67% of all food products consumed by women of childbearing age. The presence of these additives has been linked to a number of health problems, including allergies, digestive issues, and developmental delays. Reducing the levels of these additives in food is one way to protect public health. By requiring manufacturers to disclose the presence of these additives, and by encouraging the use of more natural and less harmful alternatives, governments can help to reduce the exposure of consumers to these potentially harmful substances.

translations.source: Ìpinnu náà rọ Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn òfin lórí ìrólágbára oúnjẹ pẹ̀lú àwọn èròjà aṣara lóore àti/tàbí àfikún, láti láti gbèrò àwọn ọ̀nà láti ró ìpèsè owó lágbára àti àwọn ètò ìṣàmójútó. Wọ́n fẹnukò lórí ìpinnu náà lábẹ́ àbùradà ìjábọ̀ Ọdún Mẹ́wàá Iṣẹ́ lórí Èròjà Aṣara lóore Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé (2016-2025). Ìwé tó fara jọ ọ́: EB152/CONF./5 Mímú kí àwọn ìgbìyànjú fún ìdènà àìtó èròjà aṣara lóore àti ìpadàbọ̀ wọn ó yá, tó fi mọ́ wíwọ́ ọ̀pá ẹ̀yìn àti àwọn àlébù túúbù ìmọ̀lára mìíràn, nípasẹ̀ ìrólágbára oúnjẹ tí ò léwu tó sì ṣe é lò Àwọn ojú-òpótó fara jọ ọ́
translations.reference: The resolution urges Member States to develop policies on food fortification with micronutrients and/or supplementation, and to consider ways of strengthening financing and monitoring mechanisms. The resolution was agreed under the umbrella of the United Nations Decade of Action on Nutrition (2016-2025) report. Related document: EB152/CONF./5 Accelerating efforts for preventing micronutrient deficiencies and their consequences, including spina bifida and other neural tube defects, through safe and effective food fortification Related links
translations.hypothesis: The decision encourages member states to develop policies on food safety related to genetically modified organisms (GMOs) and/or their derivatives, in order to enhance traceability and promote sustainable development. It was adopted under the framework of the Decade of Action on Food Security (2016-2025). The document in question is: EB152/CONF./5. The recommendation emphasizes the need for transparency in the labeling of GMOs and their reduction, promoting consumer rights and other relevant scientific information through safe and effective food safety measures. Key points include:

translations.source: Kà sí i nípa ìpinnu náà Iṣẹ́ WHO lórí ìrólágbára oúnjẹ Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ fọwọ́sí ìgbésẹ̀ láti kojú àwọn oògùn tí kìí ṣe ojúlówó àti ayédèrú Ní 27 oṣù Èbìbí, Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin fọwọ́sí ìpinnu kan láti ṣe àtúnyẹwò olómìnira Ètò Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ (MSM) fún àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀, àwọn oògùn tí kìí ṣe ojúlówó àti ayédèrú, tí ó ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ìpè oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2023 Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún àlékún ìṣọ́ àti iṣẹ́ ní ààyè pàtàkì yìí. Àtúnyẹwò náà ni wọ́n máa ṣe níbi ìpàdé ìgbìmọ̀ àwọn adarí MSM tó ń bọ̀ ní àkókò kan ní ọdún 2023.
translations.reference: Read more about the resolution WHO's work on food fortification Member States approve actions to counter substandard and falsified medicines On 27 May, the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly approved a resolution to conduct an independent review of the Member State Mechanism (MSM) for incidents, substandard and falsified medicines, reinforcing the Director-General’s January 2023 call for increased vigilance and action in this important area. The review will be initiated at the next MSM steering committee meeting later in 2023.
translations.hypothesis: Regarding the decision: WHO's work on food safety. Member States approve steps to regulate counterfeit and substandard medicines. On February 27, the Forty-eighth World Health Assembly approved a decision to review the implementation of the Member States Mechanism (MSM) for reporting incidents, substandard and falsified medicines, in line with the September 2023 deadline. The Secretary-General welcomed this important initiative. The review will be conducted at a meeting of MSM leaders to be held sometime in 2023.

translations.source: Àpéjọ náà ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ MSM ní ọdún 2012 láti kojú àwọn ohun èlò oògùn tí kìí ṣe ojúlówó àti ayédèrú ní ojú ìwòye ìlera àwùjọ àti ní ọ̀nà àkóyawọ́ àti aláfibọ̀. Èròngbà MSM ni láti dáàbò bo ìlera àwùjọ àti láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ànfààní sí àwọn ohun èlò oògùn tó rọjú, aláìléwu, tó múnádóko tó sì jẹ́ ojúlówó. Ìtọ́kasí ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àwọn oògùn tó ti bàjẹ́ tí wọ́n rí ní oríṣiríṣi ọjà láìpẹ́ yìí àti àwọn tó fara jọ ikú tó ṣe é dènà, Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ tẹnu mọ́ àtìlẹ́yìn tí wọ́n nílò láti mú àyípadà bá ìkápá wọn láti dènà, ṣàwarí àti láti dáhùn sí àwọn ohun èlò oògùn tí kìí ṣe ojúlówó àti ayédèrú nípasẹ̀ ànfààní sí àwọn ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ tó yẹ, tó fi mọ́ àwọn ohun èlò àyẹ̀wò ààyè fún ìmọ̀ ìjìnlẹ̀, pẹ̀lú àtúnṣe ìfojúrénà lórí àwọn ọjà àti ìpolówó gbẹ̀fẹ́, àti títa àwọn ohun èlò oògùn lórí ẹ̀rọ ayélujára. Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ pè fún gbígbà àti ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ tọpa kí o sì wá káàkiri fún ìdarí àti ìtọpinpin ọjà bákan náà ni ìnílò àjọṣepọ̀ aláìgbẹ̀fẹ́ láti ọ̀dọ̀ oríṣiríṣi àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ìbílẹ̀ àti lágbàyé bíi ìṣàkóso àwọn agbófinró àti àwọn oníbodè. Wọ́n rọ Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti wà ní ìdọ́gba pẹ̀lú àwọn àpéjọpọ̀ tí wọ́n gbèrò fún àyípadà òfin àti ṣíṣe àwọn kàn-án-pá ìṣelééwọ̀ fún àwọn ìṣòwò tí ò tọ́ nínú àwọn ohun èlò oògùn tí kìí ṣe ojúlówó àti ayédèrú.
translations.reference: The Assembly established the MSM in 2012 to tackle substandard and falsified medical products from a public health perspective and in a transparent and inclusive way. The goal of the MSM is to protect public health and promote access to affordable, safe, efficacious and quality medical products. Noting recent events of contaminated medicines found on various markets and related preventable deaths, Member States emphasized the support needed to improve their capacity to prevent, detect and respond to substandard and falsified medical products through access to appropriate technologies, including laboratory testing facilities, along with improved oversight on informal markets and advertising, and the online sale of medical products. Member States called for embracing and implementing track and trace technologies for market control and surveillance, as well as the need for formal collaboration by relevant local and global partners such as law enforcement and customs administrations. Member States were encouraged to align with conventions aimed at improvement of legislation and imposition of prohibitive sanctions for wrongful dealing in substandard and falsified medical products.
translations.hypothesis: The organization was established in 2012 to promote the responsible use of medicines and medical devices in the public health sector, as well as through ethical and transparent practices. The organization's mission is to improve public health and to ensure that medicines and medical devices are safe, effective, and accessible. In response to recent incidents of substandard and falsified medicines found in various markets, and the resulting deaths, Member States recognized the need to strengthen their regulatory frameworks to combat these threats and to promote the responsible use of medicines and medical devices through the application of appropriate technologies, including track-and-trace systems for medicine identification, improved market surveillance and enforcement, and the use of digital technologies for medicine supply chain management. Member States called for the development and implementation of appropriate technologies to support market oversight and control. This requires a collaborative effort involving various stakeholders, including regulatory authorities, manufacturers, and distributors. The organization is working with Member States to develop and implement policies and procedures to address the challenges of substandard and falsified medicines in the supply chain.

translations.source: Wọ́n ní kí Ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba náà jábọ̀ lórí ìtẹ̀síwájú ní agbègbè yìí fún Ìgbìmọ̀ Alàkóso ní ọdún 2025. Ìwé tó fara jọ ọ́: EB152/9 -àwọn ohun èlò oògùn tí kìí ṣe ojúlówó àti ayédèrú Àwọn ojú-òpótó fara jọ ọ́ Ètò Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ WHO lórí àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀, àwọn ohun èlò oògùn tí kìí ṣe ojúlówó àti ayédèrú Iṣẹ́ WHO lórí àwọn ohun èlò oògùn tí kìí ṣe ojúlówó àti ayédèrú
translations.reference: The Secretariat is requested to report on progress in this area to the Executive Board in 2025. Related document: EB152/9 - Substandard and falsified medical products Related links WHO Member State Mechanism on incidents, substandard and falsified medical products WHO's work on substandard and falsified medical products
translations.hypothesis: The government agency is required to report on progress in this area to the Board of Directors in 2025. The relevant documents are: EB152/9 - Non-essential medicines and supplies; Optics; WHO Programme on Essential Medicines, Vaccines and Diagnostics; WHO work on essential medicines and diagnostics.

translations.source: Ìpinnu àkọ́kọ́-irú-rẹ̀ lórí sáyẹnsì ìhùwàsí fún ìlera tó dára ti di gbígbà wọlé Lónì Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ gba ìpinnu sáyẹ́nsì ìhùwàsí fún ìlera tó dára wọlé pẹ̀lú bí Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ ṣe ń ṣe àfihàn ìṣọ̀kan tó gbòòrò fún ìdí fún àfikún tíọ́rì sáyẹnsì ìhùwàsí pẹ̀lú ọgbọ́n, àwọn ìlànà àti ìgbésẹ̀ káàkiri àwọn àkòrí-ọ̀rọ̀ ìlera àti àwọn ojúṣe ìlera àwùjọ. Ìpinnu náà rọ Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti mọ rírì sáyẹ́nsì ìhùwàsí láti ṣe àṣeyọrí àbájáde ìlera tó dára, láti ṣàwárí àwọn ànfààní fún àlékún ìlò àti, láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ojúṣe àti ẹ̀ka fún ìṣẹ̀dá àti ìtumọ̀ ẹ̀rí láti ṣe àwọn òfin àti ètò. Ó tún bèèrè pé kí Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ láti sọ lílo sáyẹ́nsì ìhùwàsí láàrín ilé-iṣẹ́ náà di nǹkan àti láti pèsè àtìlẹ́yìn fún Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ nípasẹ̀ àgbékalẹ̀ ìtọ́nisọ́nà àti ìpèsè ìrànlọ́wọ́ omímọ̀ ẹ̀rọ. Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ mọ rírì àṣeyọrí Ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Sáyẹ́nsì Ìhùwàsí fún Ìlera Tó Dára tí Ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba léwájú rẹ̀, kí Malaysia kú orí-ire, onígbọ̀wọ́ ìpinnu náà, bákan náà ni àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 19 tí ó kù tí wọ́n darapọ̀ gẹ́gẹ́ bíi onígbọ̀wọ́-àjọṣe, wọ́n sì dúpẹ́ lọ́wọ́ Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún ìjábọ̀ náà.
translations.reference: First ever resolution on behavioural sciences for better health is adopted Today Member States adopted the behavioural sciences for better health resolution with Member States showing broad consensus towards the need for integrating systematically behavioural science theory, methods and approaches across health topics and public health functions. The resolution urges Member States to acknowledge the role of behavioural science in achieving better health outcomes, to identify opportunities for increased use and, to establish functions and units for generating and translating evidence to inform policies and programmes. It also requests the Director-General to mainstream the use of behavioural science within the organization and to provide support to Member States through the development of guidance and the provision of technical assistance. Member States recognized the achievement of the Behavioural Sciences for Better Health Initiative led by the secretariat, congratulated Malaysia, sponsor of the resolution, as well as the other 19 countries that joined as co-sponsors, and thanked the Director-General for the report.
translations.hypothesis: The initial phase of the Science-Based Policy for Good Health initiative has been officially launched today by Member States. The Member States have adopted the science-based policy for good health, which aims to promote a stronger link between scientific evidence, policy, and practice in the areas of health and public health indicators. This policy encourages Member States to utilize scientific evidence to improve health outcomes, to understand the benefits of different interventions, and to develop and implement effective policies and programs. It also calls for the leadership to prioritize the use of scientific evidence within their organizations and to support Member States through the development of capacity and the provision of technical assistance. The Member States are confident that the successful implementation of the Science-Based Policy for Good Health will lead to improved health outcomes, and Malaysia, as the current chair, along with the other 19 participating Member States, have expressed their commitment to the initiative and thanked the leadership for their support.

translations.source: Wọ́n tẹnu mọ́ pàtàkì ṣíṣe ìdásílẹ̀ ilé-iṣẹ́ ní agbègbè yìí, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn ọ́fíísì ẹkùn agbègbè, àti ìṣẹ̀dá àkójọpọ̀ ẹ̀rí àti àjọṣepọ̀ láàrín àwọn ẹ̀ka, tó fi mọ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn ilé-ẹ̀kọ́ àti ẹ̀ka aládàni. Ìwé tó fara jọ ọ́: EB152(23) -Sáyẹnsì ìhùwàsí fún ìlera tó dára EB152/25 - Sáyẹnsì Ìhùwàsí fún ìlera tó dára, Ìjábọ̀ láti ọwọ́ Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ Sáyẹnsì Ìhùwàsí fún Ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Ìlera Tó Dára Àwọn ohun tó wà lókè yìí ni wọ́n jíròrò lé lórí gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀kan lára àwọn ìwé A76/7 Rev.1 - Ìjábọ̀ ìṣọ̀kan láti ọwọ́ Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀.
translations.reference: They stressed the importance of building capacity in this area, particularly in regional offices, and of creating a repository of evidence and synergies between sectors, including with academia and the private sector. Related documents: EB152(23) - Behavioural sciences for better health EB152/25 - Behavioural sciences for better health, Report by the Director-General Behavioural Sciences for Better Health Initiative The above items were discussed as part of the document A76/7 Rev.1 - Consolidated report by the Director-General.
translations.hypothesis: They are concerned about the implementation of the company's policies in this area, particularly regarding the local offices, and the creation of a harmonious and collaborative environment between departments, in conjunction with schools and community organizations. Relevant documents include: EB152(23) - Guidelines for Promoting Workplace Wellness EB152/25 - Guidelines for Promoting Workplace Wellness, Report from the Chief Human Resources Officer on the Implementation of Policies for a Healthy Workplace. The following documents are also particularly relevant: A76/7 Rev.1 - Report of the Chief Human Resources Officer.

translations.source: Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin - Ìròyìn ojoojúmọ́: 27 oṣù Èbìbí ọdún 2023 ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àgbáyé àkọ́kọ́ lórí ìdíwọ́ àti ìdènà àkóràn Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé ti fẹnukò lónì lórí ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àgbéṣe lórí ìdíwọ́ àti ìdènà àkóràn (IPC) tó jẹ́ àkọ́kọ́-irú-rẹ̀, tí ó gùn lé àwọn ìgbìyànjú bíi ògún ọdún tí WHO àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ rẹ̀ léwájú rẹ̀. ìlànà-iṣẹ́ náà pèsè àwọn ọ̀nà ọlọ́gbọ́n fún Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti mú àdínkù alálòpẹ́ bá ewu àwọn àkóràn ajẹmọ́-ìlera (HAIs) tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́, tó fi mọ́ àwọn tí wọ́n ń ṣàfihàn ẹ̀yàwuuru jáàgùn. àwọn HAI wà lára àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìfẹ́ gbogbo ìgbà tó pọ̀ jùlọ tó ń wáyé ní ọ̀gangan ipò ìpèsè ètò ìlera. Àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 àti àwọn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn ńlá láìpẹ́ yìí bíi ààrùn kòkòrò Ẹ̀bọ́là, àìsàn èémí Àárín Ìlà-oòrùn àti kòkòrò ààrùn Sudan ti tú àṣírí àlàfo tó wà nínú ètò IPC ní gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè kedere.
translations.reference: Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly – Daily update: 27 May 2023 First global strategy on infection prevention and controlThe World Health Assembly agreed today on the first-ever global strategy on infection prevention and control (IPC), which builds on almost two decades of efforts led by WHO and partners. The strategy provides Member States with strategic directions to substantially reduce the ongoing risk of health care-associated infections (HAIs), including those that exhibit antimicrobial resistance. HAIs are among the most frequent adverse events occurring in the context of health service delivery. The COVID-19 pandemic and recent major disease outbreaks such as Ebola virus disease, the Middle East respiratory syndrome and the Sudan virus disease have clearly exposed the existing gaps in IPC programmes in all countries.
translations.hypothesis: World Health Assembly – Daily News: February 27, 2023. Global strategy for infection prevention and control. The World Health Assembly today endorsed a global strategy on infection prevention and control (IPC), a critical area for action led by the World Health Organization (WHO) and its partners. The strategy outlines evidence-based approaches for member states to enhance infection prevention and control practices, particularly in healthcare settings. Healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) are a major cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide. They are among the most frequent adverse events in healthcare settings. The COVID-19 pandemic and other recent outbreaks, such as Ebola, Middle East Respiratory Syndrome (MERS), and the Sudan virus outbreak, have highlighted the critical importance of strong IPC programs in all countries.

translations.source: ìlànà-iṣẹ́ náà ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àfojúsùn tó hàn: ní ọdún 2030, gbogbo ènìyàn tó ń lo tàbí pèsè ètò ìlera rí ìdáàbòbò kúrò níbi àwọn àkóràn tó so mọ́ ọ. Àwọn kókó èròngbà rẹ̀ mẹ́tẹ̀ẹ̀ta ni: láti dènà àkóràn nínú ètò ìlera; ṣiṣẹ́ láti rí i dájú pé àwọn ètò IPC wà ní ìkàlẹ̀ wọ́n sì ń ṣàmúlò rẹ̀; kí ó sì darí àwọn iṣẹ́ IPC pẹ̀lú àwọn agbègbè àti ẹ̀ka mìíràn. ìlànà-iṣẹ́ náà ni wọ́n fi sun èyíkéyìí ààyè tí wọ́n ti ń pèsè ètò ìlera, káàkiri ètò ìlera; ó dá lórí ìpìlẹ̀-òye ìtọ́jú tó mọ́ tí kò sì léwu gẹ́gẹ́ bí òpó ìpìlẹ̀ fún ẹ̀tọ́ sí ìlera, tí ìdọ́gba ń darí, tí ó sì gbọdọ̀ ṣe àrídájú ìṣirò àti alálòpẹ́. ìlànà-iṣẹ́ IPC lágbayé ni wọ́n máa ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún pẹ̀lú, tí wọn ó sì lò ó papọ̀ pẹ̀lú, ètò amúṣẹ́ṣe àgbáyé àti ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àmójútó tó ní nǹkan ṣe pẹ̀lú ẹ̀, tí wọn yóò ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní ọdún 2023-2024. Ìwé tó fara jọ ọ́ EB152/9 - Àkọsílẹ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àgbáyé lórí ìdíwọ́ àti ìdènà àkóràn, Ìjábọ̀ ìsọníṣókí Aláṣẹ láti ọwọ́ Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀
translations.reference: The strategy sets a clear vision: by 2030, everyone accessing or providing health care is safe from associated infections. Its three key objectives are: to prevent infection in health care; act to ensure IPC programmes are in place and implemented; and coordinate IPC activities with other areas and sectors. The strategy is focused on any setting where health care is delivered, across the health system; it is based on the principle of clean and safe care as a fundamental component of the right to health, which is equity driven, and which should ensure accountability and sustainability. The global IPC strategy will be complemented by, and used in conjunction with, an associated global action plan and monitoring framework, that will be developed in 2023–2024. Related document EB152/9 - Draft global strategy on infection prevention and control, Executive summary Report by the Director-General
translations.hypothesis: The strategy aims to achieve the following: by 2030, all individuals using or affected by health programs will be free from the diseases associated with them. Its three main pillars are: to eliminate diseases within health programs; to ensure the sustainability of IPC (Infection Prevention and Control) programs and their effective implementation; and to integrate IPC activities with other relevant sectors and areas. This strategy builds upon existing global health initiatives, encompassing the entire health system. It is based on evidence-based practices, is relatively low-risk compared to other approaches for improving health outcomes, is leadership-driven, and promotes equity and sustainability. Global IPC strategies will be supported and implemented in collaboration with global health initiatives and related strategies, with a planned launch in 2023-2024. Document reference: EB152/9 - Report on the global strategy for the prevention and control of noncommunicable diseases, Report by the Director-General.

translations.source: Iṣẹ́ WHO lórí ìdíwọ́ àti ìdènà àkóràn Ìpinnu mánigbàgbé ṣínà fún ìrólágbára ìtúnirọ nínú ètò ìlera Lónì Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé fẹnukò lórí ìpinnu tó lápẹrẹ lórí ìrólágbára ìtúnirọ nínú ètò ìlera. Ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìtúnirọ kó ipa pàtàkì láti rí i dájú pé ìgbádùn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn tó fi mọ́ ojúlówó ìlera ara àti ọpọlọ tó ga jùlọ tí ènìyàn lè ní. Ó tún ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìbímọ, ó sì mọ rírì ẹ̀tọ́ sí iṣẹ́ àti ẹ̀tọ́ sí ètò ẹ̀kọ́. Ìpinnu tó lápẹrẹ yìí gbèrò láti yanjú àwọn ìpèníjà nínú ìtúnirọ gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìdí láti: mú àlékún bá ìpolongo ìtúnirọ nígbà tí wọ́n bá ń ṣe ètò àwọn kàn-án-pá ìlera àti èròngbà ìwádìí, pínpín ohun èlò, ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ àti gbígba ìfiránṣẹ́ ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ;
translations.reference: WHO's work on infection prevention and control Historic resolution paves the way for strengthening rehabilitation in health systemsToday the World Health Assembly agreed a landmark resolution on strengthening rehabilitation in health systems. Rehabilitation services play a key role in ensuring the enjoyment of human rights including the highest attainable standard of physical and mental health. It also promotes sexual and reproductive health, and recognizes the right to work and the right to education. This landmark resolution aims to address the challenges in rehabilitation such as the need to: increase awareness of rehabilitation when setting health priorities and research agendas, allocating resources, promoting cooperation and enabling technology transfer;
translations.hypothesis: The WHO's work on promoting and preventing non-communicable diseases (NCDs) is crucial for strengthening health systems. The World Health Assembly recently reaffirmed its commitment to strengthening health systems. Investing in prevention is essential to ensuring the well-being of individuals and reducing the burden of NCDs. It also supports overall health and development, and contributes to economic productivity and educational attainment. This commitment includes addressing key challenges in NCD prevention, such as: integrating NCD prevention and control into health promotion and disease management programs, increasing access to essential medicines and technologies, promoting healthy lifestyles, and strengthening surveillance and monitoring systems.

translations.source: rí i dájú pé àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ní ohun èlò tó dára láti dáhùn sí àlékún òjijì nínú ìnílò ìtúnirọ tó fi mọ́ ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ aṣèrànwọ́ nítorí àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera; rí i dájú pé àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n wà nípò ìdẹ́yẹsí tí wọ́n sì kángun séwu ní ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìtúnirọ tó rọjú, tó jẹ́ ojúlówó tó sì péye tó fi mọ́ àwọn ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ aṣèrànwọ́; sọ́ra fún ìnáwó àpò-ara-ẹni fún àwọn ènìyàn láti ní ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìtúnirọ àti ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ aṣèrànwọ́ tí ó lè fa ìnira owó; àti yanjú ìpele àìtó àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìtúnirọ lọ́wọ́lọ́wọ́ láti ṣíṣe fún ìnílò àwọn ará ìlú. Ìpinnu yìí ṣe àkọsílẹ̀ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìgbésẹ̀ tí Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba WHO ní láti gbé bíi:
translations.reference: ensure countries are better equipped to respond to the sudden increase in rehabilitation needs including assistive technology due to health emergencies; ensure persons in marginalized and vulnerable situations have access to affordable, quality and appropriate rehabilitation services including assistive technology; avoid high out-of-pocket costs for people to access rehabilitation services and assistive technology that can cause financial hardships; and address the current insufficient level of rehabilitation workforce to serve the needs of the population. The resolution lists a range of actions to be taken by the WHO Secretariat such as:
translations.hypothesis: It is evident that countries possess valuable resources to contribute to the global effort of combating infectious diseases through technological innovation. It is also clear that individuals in low-resource settings, who are often most vulnerable, can benefit significantly from access to relevant and affordable technological solutions in the field of medical technology. Furthermore, there is a need to address the financial barriers that prevent people from accessing these technologies and medical innovations, and to ensure that the skills of healthcare workers are adequately developed to meet the needs of urban populations. This conclusion highlights several key steps that the World Health Organization (WHO) should take:

translations.source: ṣíṣe àtẹ̀jáde ìjábọ̀ ní ìparí ọdún 2026 pẹ̀lú àlàyé lórí ìkápá Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti dáhùn sí àwọn ohun tí ìtúnirọ nílò; ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn àfojúsùn àti atọ́ka fún kíkárí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìtúnirọ tó ṣe é lò ní ọdún 2023; ṣíṣe àrídájú pé wọ́n pèsè àwọn ohun èlò tó yẹ fún WHO láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ nínú ìṣàmúlò ìtọ́nisọ́nà ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ àti ohun èlò; àti ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti ṣe àfikún ìtúnirọ àti ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ aṣèrànwọ́ nínú ètò ìgbaradì àti ìdáhùn pàjáwírì wọn. Ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba WHO máa jábọ̀ lórí ìtẹ̀síwájú nínú ìṣàmúlò ìpinnu yìí fún Àpéjọ Ìlera ní ọdún 2026, 2028 àti 2030.
translations.reference: publishing a baseline report by the end of 2026 with information on the capacity of Member States to respond to rehabilitation needs; developing targets and indicators for effective coverage of rehabilitation services by 2030; ensuring appropriate resources are allocated at WHO to support Member States in implementing technical guidance and resources; and supporting Member States to integrate rehabilitation and assistive technology in their emergency preparedness and response plan. The WHO Secretariat will report on progress in the implementation of this resolution to the Health Assembly in 2026, 2028 and 2030.
translations.hypothesis: Implement a comprehensive review of the progress made by Member States in addressing the needs identified in the global health priorities by the end of 2026; develop a set of indicators and benchmarks to measure the effectiveness of health interventions implemented in 2023; ensure that Member States have access to the essential health products and services needed to support their efforts in promoting health innovation and technology; and provide support to Member States to strengthen their health systems and improve their capacity for research and development. The WHO governing bodies will review the progress made in implementing these recommendations at the World Health Assembly in 2026, 2028, and 2030.

translations.source: Ìwé tó fara jọ ọ́: EB152/8 - Ìrólágbára ìtúnirọ nínú ètò ìlera, Ìjábọ̀ láti ọwọ́ Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ ojú-òpótó fara jọ ọ́ Iṣẹ́ WHO lórí ìtúnirọ Àwọn nǹkan tó wà lókè yìí ni wọ́n jíròrò lé lórí gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ọ̀kan lára ìwé A76/7 Rev.1 - Ìjábọ̀ ìṣọ̀kan láti ọwọ́ Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀. Ìpinnu lórí ìrólágbára ìkápá ìdámọ̀ àisàn
translations.reference: Related document: EB152/8 - Strengthening rehabilitation in health systems, Report by the Director-General Related link WHO's work on rehabilitation The items above were discussed as part of the document A76/7 Rev.1 - Consolidated report by the Director-General. Resolution on strengthening diagnostics capacity
translations.hypothesis: Document Number: EB152/8 - The Role of Technology in Health, Report from the Director-General, World Health Organization, regarding Technology. The following is a summary of a key document: A76/7 Rev.1 - Report of the Director-General. Decision on the strengthening of health information systems.

translations.source: Ní 26 oṣù Èbìbí Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ buwọ́lù ìpinnu kan láti ró ìkápá ìdámọ̀ àisàn ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lágbára àti láti mú àyípadà bá ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìdámọ̀ àìsàn. Ìpinnu oríṣiríṣi tó gbòòrò náà mọ̀ wí pé ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìdámọ̀ àìsàn ṣe pàtàkì fún ìdènà, ìtọpinpin , ìdámọ̀ àìsàn, àmójútó ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀, ṣíṣe àmójútó àti ìtọ́jú ààrùn tí kìí ràn, àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná àti èyí tó ṣọ̀nwọ́n tí wọ́n ti patì, ìfarapa, àti ìpèníjà ara. Ìdámọ̀ àìsàn fi ààyè gba ìdánimọ̀ àìsàn ní pàtó, fún ìdí èyí ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ àwọn ìtọ́jú tó péye fún àbájáde ìlera tó dára lásìkò. Ìpinnu náà ro gbogbo ìpìlẹ "ìdámọ̀ àisàn", torí náà a rí àpapọ̀ àwọn àyẹ̀wo ààyè fún ìmọ̀ ìjìnlẹ̀ "nínú ìgò" b.a àyẹ̀wò ìdámọ̀ àìsàn ojú ẹsẹ̀ àti pínpín DNA sí ìṣọ̀rí (PVR) àti ìdánimọ̀ àìsàn "tí kìí ṣe nínú ìgò" b.a. àwọn ẹ̀rọ ayàwòrán tàbí wíwọn ìfúnpá. Ó kárí àwọn igbéṣẹ̀ fún ìwádìí àti ìdàgbàsókè, ìpèsè nǹkan (tó fi mọ́ ìpèsè lábẹ́lé àti ìfiránṣẹ́ ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ), òfin, yíyàn àti rírà, ìpolongo, ìgbẹnusọ àti kíkojú àwọn ìdíwọ́ ànfààní lápapọ̀.
translations.reference: On 26 May Member States endorsed a resolution to strengthen diagnostics capacity in countres and to improve access to diagnostic services. The broad ranging resolution recognizes that diagnostic services are vital for the prevention, surveillance, diagnosis, case management, monitoring and treatment of communicable, noncommunicable, neglected tropical and rare diseases, injuries, and disabilities. Diagnostics allow for the precise identification of diseases, and therefore the timely initiation of the correct treatments for better health outcomes. The resolution considers the full spectrum of “diagnostics”, thus including both “in vitro” laboratory tests e.g. rapid diagnostic tests and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and “non in vitro” diagnostics e.g. imaging or blood pressure measurement devices. It covers actions for research and development, manufacturing (including local production and technology transfer), regulation, selection and procurement, awareness, advocacy and addressing access barriers in general.
translations.hypothesis: On June 26, the United Nations passed a resolution to promote research into diseases and to ensure that research benefits are accessible to all. The various resolutions recognized the importance of research for development, innovation, disease prevention, emergency response, the development of new treatments, the prevention and control of infectious diseases, non-communicable diseases, and mental health. Research provides the basis for evidence-based policies that promote better health outcomes. The resolution covers all aspects of "research," including both "in vitro" (laboratory-based) research, such as cell and DNA analysis and the development of new diagnostic tools, and "in vivo" (non-laboratory) research, such as clinical trials. It also addresses issues related to ethics, data sharing, intellectual property, access to medicines, and the overall benefits of research.

translations.source: Ìṣàmúlò ìpinnu náà máa gùn lé yóò sì tún fẹ àwọn iṣẹ́ àtẹ̀yìnwá àti lọ́wọ́lọ́wọ́ ní àwọn ìpele mẹ́tẹ̀ẹ̀ta WHO lójú láti ran àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lọ́wọ́ láti mú àyípadà bá ànfàní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìdámọ̀ àìsàn. Wọ́n ní kí Ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba náà jábọ̀ lórí ìtẹ̀síwájú àmúlò ní ọdún 2025. Ìwé tó fara jọ ọ́ EB152/6 ríro ìkápá ìdámọ̀ àisàn lágbára
translations.reference: The implementation of the resolution will build and expand on previous and current work at three levels of WHO to help countries improve access to diagnostic services. The Secretariat is requested to report on implementation progress in 2025. Related document EB152/6 Strengthening diagnostics capacity
translations.hypothesis: The decision aims to accelerate and expand the implementation of essential health services at all levels of the WHO, supporting countries in achieving their health goals. The organization has set a target to accelerate progress in this area by 2025. The document, EB152/6, outlines a plan for strengthening health systems.

translations.source: Àmì-ẹ̀yẹ fún ìgbìyànjú aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ sí ìlera àwùjọ tí wọ́n gbékalẹ̀ lásìkò Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin Lásìkò tí ayẹyẹ ń lọ lọ́wọ́ níbi Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin ní Geneva, wọ́n pín àmì-ẹ̀yẹ fún àwọn ènìyàn àti àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ káàkiri àgbáyé fún ìgbìyànjú aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ wọn sí ìlera àwùjọ. "Ẹ̀bùn àti àmì-ẹ̀yẹ ìlera àwùjọ ọdún 2023 náà gbóríyìn fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n kópa ribiribi tí wọ́n kó nínú ìlera àwùjọ, àti àwọn tí iṣẹ́ wọn ti ré kọjá ìpè fún iṣẹ́ lásán," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. *Ní àkókò tí gbogbo ayé ń kojú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìpèníjà, ìkọ̀ọ̀kan wọn jẹ́ ìwúrí àti ìtanilólobó nípa ìtẹ̀síwájú tí ó ṣe é ṣe láti mú àyípadà bá ìlera àti ìgbáyégbádùn fún gbogbo ènìyàn." Ìpè fún yíyan àwọn akópa fún ẹ̀bùn kọ̀ọ̀kan ni wọ́n máa ń fi ránṣẹ́ síta ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan lẹ́yìn ìparí Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé náà.
translations.reference: Awards for outstanding contributions to public health presented during the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly During a moving ceremony at the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly in Geneva, awards were presented to persons and institutions from around the world for their outstanding contributions to public health. “The 2023 public health prizes and awards celebrate people who have made a significant contribution to global health, and whose work has extended far beyond the call of normal duty,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “At a time when the world faces many challenges, each is an inspiration and a reminder of the progress that can be made to improve health and wellbeing for all.” The call for nominations of candidates for each prize is sent out each year after closure of the World Health Assembly.
translations.hypothesis:
Award for Outstanding Contribution to Public Health during the 48th World Health Assembly.

The award ceremony, held during the 48th World Health Assembly in Geneva, recognized individuals and organizations from around the world for their exceptional contributions to public health. "The 2023 Public Health Award celebrates the individuals and organizations who have made significant contributions to public health, and whose work has gone above and beyond," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the WHO. "In a world facing many challenges, their dedication and innovation are an inspiration to us all."

The criteria for selecting the recipients of each award are announced annually after the World Health Assembly.

translations.source: Yíyàn lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe láti ọ̀dọ àwọn alákòso ìlera orílẹ̀-èdè fún Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ WHO àti láti ọ̀dọ ẹni tí ó ti gba ẹ̀bùn náà tẹ́lẹ̀. Níbi ìjókòó 152nd rẹ̀ ní oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2023, Ìgbìmọ̀ Alàkóso yan àwọn tí wọ́n gba ẹ̀bùn náà ní ọdún 2023, tó dá lórí àwọn àbá tí ìgbìmọ̀ àwọn adájọ́ aṣẹ àṣàyàn tí wọ́n ní àfọkànsìn náà ṣe fún ẹ̀bùn kọ̀ọ̀kan. Wọ́n ti yan àwọn ẹni iyì méjèèje náà láti ṣe àjọyọ̀ ipa aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ wọn àti àfikún wọn sí ìlera àwùjọ ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè wọn àti káàkiri àgbáyé. Wọ́n wá láti Àwọn Ẹkùn agbègbè WHO mẹ́rin - fún àwọn Amẹ́ríkà, Eastern-Mediterranean, Gúúsù Ìlà-oòrùn Asia àti Western Pacific. Àwọn olóríire márùn-ún nínú wọn jẹ́ ẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan, mẹ́ta nínú wọn sì jẹ́ obìnrin.
translations.reference: Nominations can be made by national health administrations of a WHO Member State and by any former recipient of the prizes. At its 152nd session in January 2023, the Executive Board designated the 2023 winners of the prizes, on the basis of proposals made by the dedicated selection panels for each prize. The seven laureates have been selected to celebrate their unique role and contribution for public health in their countries and globally. They come from four WHO Regions – for the Americas, the Eastern-Mediterranean, South-East Asia and the Western Pacific. Five winners are individuals, and three among them are women.
translations.hypothesis: The awards were presented by health ministers from WHO member states and by individuals who have previously received the award. During its 152nd session in September 2023, the Board of Governors selected the recipients for 2023, based on the nominations submitted by the selection committee. The seven individuals were chosen for their outstanding contributions and dedication to public health in their countries and around the world. They represent four WHO regions: the Americas, the Eastern Mediterranean, South-East Asia, and the Western Pacific. Among the awardees, there are three men and four women.

translations.source: Ní àfikún, àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ méjì tí wọ́n ti gba àmì-ẹ̀yẹ ọdún 2023 ló jẹ́ wí pé obìnrin ló léwájú wọn. Àwọn tí wọ́n gba ẹ̀bùn ọdún 2023 Ẹ̀bùn Ìlera Sasakawa Àwọn ẹni iyì ọdún 2023: Nick Simons Institute láti Nepal, àti Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Vichai Tienthavorn láti Thailand Nick Simons Institute jẹ́ ilé-iṣẹ́ tí kìí ṣe ti ìjọba tó ń gbèrò láti mú àyípadà bá ètò ìlera ní àwọn agbègbè ìgbèríko Nepal. Ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ pẹ̀lú Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba tó ń rí sí Ètò Ìlera àti Ònkà ènìyàn ní Nepal, Ilé-iṣẹ́ náà ti kọ́ 7000 àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ètò ìlera (bíi àwọn agbẹ̀bí àti àwọn amúgbá lẹ́gbẹ̀ apèsè abẹ́rẹ́ apa-ìrora) ní àwọn agbègbè ìgbèríko, tí wọ́n lè pèsè àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n nílò jùlọ fún àwọn àwùjọ ìbílẹ̀ níbi tí ò bá ti sí orísun ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera àti akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́. Ilé-iṣẹ́ náà ti pèsè àwọn irinṣẹ́ pàtàkì ó sì ti pèsè àfikún àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ìlera láti mú àdínkù bá àìsí àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ìlera ní ilé-ìwòsàn 44, ó sì ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àgbékalẹ̀ ètò amúṣẹ́ṣe fún ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ojúlówó tó dára ní àwọn ilé-ìwòsàn ìgbèríko tó lé ní 100.
translations.reference: In addition, the two institutions that have received a 2023 award are led by women. Winners of the 2023 prizes Sasakawa Health PrizeThe 2023 laureates: The Nick Simons Institute from Nepal, and Professor Vichai Tienthavorn from Thailand The Nick Simons Institute is a nongovernmental organization aiming to improve health care in the rural areas of Nepal. Working closely with the Ministry of Health and Population of Nepal, the Institute has trained 7000 medical professionals (such as midwives and anaesthetic assistants) in rural areas, who now provide most-needed services to local communities in the absence of a sufficient pool of general practitioners and specialists. The Institute has supplied essential equipment and provided additional health personnel to alleviate the lack of medical professionals in 44 hospitals, and supported the design of an action plan for better quality services in more than 100 rural hospitals.
translations.hypothesis:
In addition, two organizations that received awards in 2023 were led by women. The recipients of the 2023 Sasakawa Health Prize are: Nick Simons Institute from Nepal, and Dr. Vichai Tienthavorn from Thailand.

The Nick Simons Institute is a non-governmental organization that works to improve healthcare in rural Nepal. It partners with the Ministry of Health and Population in Nepal to train and support community health workers, such as female community health volunteers (FCHVs), who provide essential health services to remote communities. The organization has trained over 7,000 FCHVs, who provide a range of services, including maternal and child health care, family planning, and disease prevention. The Institute has also provided essential equipment and supplies to health posts, and has supported the development of community-based health programs to address specific health needs in rural communities. It has also supported the establishment of health posts in 44 remote villages and has provided training and support for the development of quality eye care services in rural health facilities.

translations.source: Ilé-iṣẹ́ náà tún ṣe ìwádìí àti ìgbẹnusọ fún àwọn iṣẹ́ láti yàwòrán ojú ìwòye àti láti dásí àwọn òfin tó kan àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera ìgbèríko ní Nepal. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Vichai Tienthavorn ni Ààrẹ Praboromarajchanok Institute ní Ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba tó ń rí sí Ètò Ìlera àwùjọ ní Thailand. Ọ̀kan lára àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ jákèjádò-àgbáyé tí ó ti léwájú ni òfin ìpolongo ìlera onímọ̀ ọ̀tun láti báni kojú ìṣòro àìtó hóró ẹ̀jẹ̀ agbẹ̀mí, pẹ̀lú bí iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ ṣe yọrí sí gbígba òfin orílẹ̀-èdè tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ fún ìdíwọ́ àti ìdènà ìṣòro àìtó hóró ẹ̀jẹ̀ agbẹ̀mí wọlé. Ó tún ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ irinṣẹ́ ìbáraẹnisọ̀rọ̀ tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ àti ìlànà àyẹ̀wò láti ran àwọn ará ìlú lọ́wọ́ láti gba ìhùwàsí wọn fún ìdíwọ́ àti ìdènà àtọgbẹ àti ẹ̀jẹ̀ ríru. Nítorí iṣẹ́ yìí, wọ́n gba òfin orílẹ̀-èdè kan wọlé pẹ̀lú ipa rere tó lápẹrẹ lórí ẹrù ààrùn orílẹ̀-èdè.
translations.reference: The Institute also conducts research and advocacy activities to shape perceptions and influence policies that affect rural health workers in Nepal. Professor Vichai Tienthavorn is President of the Praboromarajchanok Institute at the Ministry of Public Health of Thailand. One of the nation-wide initiatives he has led is an innovative health promotion policy to help address thalassemia, with his work resulting in the adoption of an effective national policy for thalassemia prevention and control. He also designed an effective communications tool and screening method to help citizens to adapt their behaviours in order to prevent and control diabetes and hypertension. As a result of this work, a national policy was adopted with significant positive impact on the country’s disease burden.
translations.hypothesis: The organization conducted research and advocacy efforts to promote the use of visual aids and to address policies that affect healthcare workers in Nepal. Vichai Tienthavorn, the President of the Praboromarajchanok Institute, a government agency overseeing public health in Thailand, is one of the leading figures in this global movement. One of the key initiatives involved developing a policy framework to address the issue of violence against healthcare workers, with the goal of encouraging national governments to enact laws to protect and support these individuals. They also established a communication network and a monitoring system to help communities advocate for the safety and well-being of healthcare workers and to report incidents of violence. As a result of this work, they have successfully influenced the adoption of national policies that have had a positive impact on the health sector.

translations.source: Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Tienthavorn tún kó ipa pàtàkì nínú kíkojú ìpèníjà pínpín àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera lọ́nà tí ò dọ́gba, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn agbègbè ibodè àti ìgbèríko Thailand. Ó léwájú ìdásílẹ̀ àjọṣepọ̀ kan pẹ̀lú àwọn ilé-ẹ̀kọ́ ìgbèríko láti gba àwọn akẹ́kọ̀ọ́ ilé-ẹ̀kọ girama, kọ́ wọn ó sì tún ràn wọ́n lọ́wọ́ láti padà sí ìgbèríko lẹ́yìn tí wọ́n ti parí kọ̀ríkúlọ́mù ètò ìlera tó yára. Ẹ̀bùn United Arab Emirates Health Foundation ọdún 2023 fún ẹni iyì: Dr Maria Asuncion Silvester láti Philippines ní àmì-ẹ̀yẹ Ní àfikún sí jíjẹ́ gbajúgbajà òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera àti oníwádìí, Dr Maria Asuncion Silvester jẹ́ agbẹnusọ fún ògédé ìfọ́mọlọ́yàn tọkàn-tọkàn àti olùdásílẹ̀ Kalusugan ng Mag-Ina (Ìlera Ìyá àti Ọmọ) - Ilé-iṣẹ́ tí kìí ṣe ti ìjọba ní orílẹ̀-èdè Philippine. Dr Silvester ti ṣàmúlò ìrírí rẹ̀ gẹ́gẹ́ bíi òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera láwùjọ láti ṣe ìwádìí ó sì ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìlànà láti mú àyípadà bá ìlera àwọn ìyá àti àwọn ọmọ tuntun.
