The meeting will also include representatives from member states and organizations that fund the IPSN, including: Dame Jenny Harries, Chief Executive, UK Health Security Agency.

translations.source: Dr Jean Kaseya, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀, Àwọn Ààyè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ fún Ìdíwọ́ àti Ìdènà àti Ààrùn; Dr Naveen Rao, Igbákejì Ààrẹ Àgbà, Rockfeller Foundation; Dr Alexander Pym, Olùdarí fún Ààrùn Àkóràn, Wellcome Trust; Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Tulio de Oliveira, Olùdarí, Ààyè fún Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn ní Stellenbosch University, South Africa; Björn Kümmel, Igbákejì Olórí Ẹ̀ka, Ìlera Àgbáyé, Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba Àpapọ̀ tó ń rí sí Ètò Ìlera, Germany;
translations.reference: Dr Jean Kaseya, Director-General, Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention; Dr Naveen Rao, Senior Vice President, Rockefeller Foundation; Dr Alexander Pym, Director of Infectious Disease, Wellcome Trust; Professor Tulio de Oliveira, Director, Centre for Epidemiology at Stellenbosch University, South Africa; Björn Kümmel, Deputy Head of Unit, Global Health, Federal Ministry of Health, Germany;
translations.hypothesis: Dr. Jean Kaseya, Director, Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria; Dr. Naveen Rao, Senior Vice President, Rockefeller Foundation; Dr. Alexander Pym, Head of Infectious Diseases, Wellcome Trust; Professor Tulio de Oliveira, Director, Centre for Viral Diseases, Stellenbosch University, South Africa; Björn Kümmel, Head of Department, Global Health, a government agency focused on health policy, Germany.

translations.source: Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Leo Yee Sin, Olùdarí Aláṣẹ fún Ààyè Orílẹ̀-èdè fún Àwọn Ààrùn Àkóràn; singapore; Dr Mario Moreira, Ààrẹ, Fiocruz, Brazil; àti Dr Josefina Campos, Olùdarí Ààyè fún Apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran àti Òye dátà Ìbí Orílẹ̀-èdè ní ANLIS Malbrán, orílẹ̀-èdè Argentina. Ayẹyẹ náà máa wáyé ní ìlànà alárànbarà, tó ń pèsè ànfààní fún àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ IPSN àti àwọn olùgbọ́ tí ó kù láti jíròrò pẹ̀lú IPSN àti àwọn iṣẹ́ rẹ̀. Nípa IPSN IPSN jẹ́ ojú-òpó àgbáyé tuntun fún àwọn amúṣẹ́ṣe apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn, tí Ibùdó WHO gba àlejò rẹ̀ fún Ọgbọ́n Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn àti Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Káríayé, láti mú kí ìlọsíwájú lórí ìfiránṣẹ́ apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn yá àti láti mú àyípadà bá ṣíṣe ìpinnu ìlera àwujọ.
translations.reference: Professor Leo Yee Sin, Executive Director of the National Centre for Infectious Diseases; Singapore; Dr Mario Moreira, President, Fiocruz, Brazil; and Dr Josefina Campos, Director of the National Genomics and Bioinformatics Center at ANLIS Malbrán, Argentina. The event will take place in a hybrid format, providing an opportunity for IPSN members and broader audiences to engage with the IPSN and its activities. About the IPSNThe IPSN is a new global network of pathogen genomic actors, hosted by the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence, to accelerate progress on the deployment of pathogen genomics and improve public health decision-making.
translations.hypothesis:
Professor Leo Yee Sin, Director of Medical Services for the Ministry of Health, Singapore; Dr. Mario Moreira, Director, Fiocruz, Brazil; and Dr. Josefina Campos, Director of Global Health for Human and Animal Health and Data Science at ANLIS Malbrán, Argentina, are among the distinguished guests. The event will be held virtually, providing an opportunity for IPSN members and other stakeholders to engage with IPSN and its work.

About IPSN: IPSN is a new global network for researchers in infectious disease epidemiology, established by the World Health Organization to promote advances in infectious disease epidemiology and to inform public health decision-making.

translations.source: IPSN ní àfojúsùn àgbáyé kan níbi tí gbogbo orílẹ̀-èdè ní ànfààní tó dọ́gba sí ìkápá alálòpẹ́ fún tẹ̀lé-ǹ-tẹ̀lé jínnì àti ìyànnàná gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ọ̀kan lára ètò ìtọpinpin ìlera àwùjọ. Ó bẹ́rẹ̀ ìrìnàjò yìí láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ojú-òpó àgbáyé aṣàtìlẹyìn ànfààní fún àwọn amúṣẹ́ṣe ìtọpinpin apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran tó ń sọ di ńlá tó sì ń mú kí iṣẹ́ àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ rẹ̀ láti mú àyípadà bá ànfààní àti ìdọ́gba. IPSN ní àpapọ àwọn ẹgbẹ́ òṣìṣẹ́ mẹ́ta pàtàkì tí ó ń ṣe àkójọpọ̀ oríṣiríṣi ẹ̀yà àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀, tí Ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ní Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO. Àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ń ní àjọṣepọ̀ ní Àwọn Àwùjọ Àwọn Òṣìṣẹ́ Ìlera (COPs) láti yanjú àwọn ìpèníjà àjọni, tí wọ́n sì ń gbèrò láti mú àlékún bá ìṣọ̀kan àti ìmọ̀-ọ̀tun nínú apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn. Nínú Amúṣẹ́yá Ìfẹ̀lójú Orílẹ̀-èdè (CSUA), àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti sọ àwọn ìgbìyànjú di ọ̀kan kí wọ́n sì lè jẹ́ kí àwọn Gúúsù-Gúúsù ó ṣe ìṣípòrọpò láti fẹ ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìkápá orílẹ̀-èdè lójú.
translations.reference: The IPSN envisions a world where every country has equitable access to sustained capacity for genomic sequencing and analytics as part of its public health surveillance system. It sets out to create a mutually supportive global network of genomic surveillance actors that amplifies and accelerates the work of its members to improve access and equity. The IPSN consists of three main operational bodies that bring together different sets of stakeholders, supported by a Secretariat at the WHO Pandemic Hub. Partners collaborate in Communities of Practice (COPs) to solve common challenges, aiming to increase harmonization and innovation in pathogen genomics. In the Country Scale-Up Accelerator (CSUA), stakeholders work together to align efforts and enable South-South exchange to scale-up country capacity building.
translations.hypothesis: IPSN is a global initiative dedicated to promoting universal health coverage, aiming to provide access to quality healthcare services for all people as part of a comprehensive social welfare system. This initiative began with the establishment of a global network of national health financing experts who are working to develop and implement sustainable health financing models. IPSN has three key working groups that bring together a diverse range of stakeholders, supported by the World Health Organization's Health Systems Strengthening Department. These groups collaborate through Communities of Practice (COPs) to address common challenges and promote knowledge sharing and innovation in health financing. Within the Country Support Unit (CSU), experts work together to align national priorities and support governments in developing and implementing national health financing strategies.

translations.source: COP àti CSUA ń ṣe àkójọpọ̀ àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ láti ẹlẹ́ka-jẹ̀ka, àwọn ìpele ìpawówọlé, àti àwọn agbègbè, pẹ̀lú ìfọkànsìn sí ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ ilẹ̀-òkèèrè àti ìdọ́gba, àti ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe tó jinlẹ̀ nínú yálà apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran tàbí fífẹ àwọn ètò ìtọpinpin orílẹ̀-èdè lójú. Ẹgbẹ́ kẹta ni Àpèjọ Àwọn Olùpèsè-Owó láti darí àwọn olùpèsè-owó onínúure, ẹlẹ́gbẹ́-jẹgbẹ́ àti ìjọba tí wọ́n wà ní àyíká mú àlékún bá àkíyèsí òṣèlú àti ìpèsè owó tí ó tó fún ìtọpinpin apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn. Àpẹ̀jọ Àwọn Olùpèsè-owó tún pèsè àfikún ìpèsè owó ìrànwọ́ fún àwọn iṣẹ́ àkànṣe àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ IPSN. Ìdásílẹ̀ IPSN náà ni ìpèsè owó ìjọba orílẹ̀-èdè Germany ti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún sí Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO. Nípa Ibùdó WHO fún Ọgbọ́n Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn àti Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Káríayé
translations.reference: The COP and CSUA bring together organizations from across sectors, income levels, and geographies, with a commitment to international cooperation and equity, and deep expertise in either genomics or country scale-up of surveillance systems. The third body is the Funders Forum to coordinate philanthropic, multi-lateral and governmental donors around increased political attention and financing efficiency of pathogen genomic surveillance. The Funders Forum also catalyses additional grant funding for projects of IPSN members. The establishment of the IPSN has been supported by German government funding to the WHO Pandemic Hub. About the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence
translations.hypothesis: COP and CSUA collaborate with various stakeholders, including businesses, academic institutions, and communities, with a focus on promoting sustainable development and fostering innovation within the framework of national development plans. The third pillar is the Investor Forum, which brings together institutional investors, private sector actors, and government representatives to discuss investment opportunities and financial instruments that support sustainable development goals. The Investor Forum also provides additional investment opportunities for members of the IPSN network. The establishment of IPSN was supported by a grant from the German Federal Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development to the World Health Organization. This is through the World Health Organization's Office for Global Health Initiatives and Global Health Security.

translations.source: Ó jẹ́ ọ̀kan lára Ètò Pàjáwírì Ìlera WHO, Ibùdó WHO fún Ọgbọ́n Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn àti Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Káríayé (Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO náà), ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àjọṣepọ̀ àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ àgbáyé láti oríṣiríṣi ẹ̀ka tí ó ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè àti àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ láti kojú àwọn ewu àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé lọ́jọ́ iwájú pẹ̀lú ànfààní tó dára sí dátà, àwọn ìkápá ìyànnàná tó dára, àti àwọn irinṣẹ́ àti ojú ìwòye tó dára fún ìpinnu-ṣíṣe . Pẹ̀lú àtìlẹyìn láti ọ̀dọ Ìjọba Federal Republic of Germany, wọ́n ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO ní oṣù Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2021 ní Berlin, ní ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, tí ó ṣàfihàn àìkájú-òṣùwọ̀n káàkiri àgbáyé pẹ̀lú bí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ṣe ń ṣàwárí, ṣàmójútó àti àkóso àwọn ewu ìlera àwujọ. Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ àti àwọn ọ́fíísì ẹkùn agbègbè àti orílẹ̀-èdè WHO láti ró àwọn ìkápá pínpín-dátà wọn lágbára àti láti mú kí àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ káàkiri àgbáyé ní àjọṣepọ̀ kí wọ́n sì pèsè àwọn irinṣẹ́ láti ṣe àkójọ àti láti yànnàná dátà fún ìtọpinpin ìkìlò kíákíá. Pẹ̀lú wíwà rẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tó ju 150 lọ, àwọn ọ́fíísì ẹkùn agbègbè mẹ́fà, àti olú-ilé-iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ ní Geneva, arọ́wọ́tó WHO fún wa ní ànfààní láti ṣe ìtọ́jú àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn, àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé àti àwọn ewu ìlera àwujọ pẹ̀lú kánjú-kánjú àti àìṣèmẹ́lẹ́ tó dọ́gba káàkiri àgbáyé. Pẹ̀lú síso àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ abẹ́lẹ́, ẹkùn agbègbè, àti àgbáyé pọ̀, Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwùjọ àjọṣepọ̀ fún àwọn onímọ̀-ọ̀tun, àwọn onímọ̀ sáyẹ́nsì àti àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ káàkiri oríṣiríṣi ẹ̀ka, tí ó ń fún wa ní ànfààní láti ṣàmúlò àti láti pín àwọn ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ tó ń léwájú àti dídarí àwọn iṣẹ́ wa fún ohun tí àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ káàkiri àgbáyé nílò.
translations.reference: Forming part of the WHO Health Emergencies Programme, the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence (the WHO Pandemic Hub), facilitates a global collaboration of partners from multiple sectors that supports countries and stakeholders to address future pandemic and epidemic risks with better access to data, better analytical capacities, and better tools and insights for decision-making. With support from the Government of the Federal Republic of Germany, the WHO Pandemic Hub was established in September 2021 in Berlin, in response to the COVID-19 pandemic, which demonstrated weaknesses around the world in how countries detect, monitor and manage public health threats. The WHO Pandemic Hub works closely with Member States and WHO regional and country offices to strengthen their data-sharing capacities and enable partners from around the world to collaborate and co-create tools to gather and analyse data for early warning surveillance. With a presence in more than 150 countries, six regional offices, and its Geneva headquarters, WHO’s reach gives us the ability to treat pandemic, epidemic and public health risks with equal urgency and diligence around the globe. By linking local, regional, and global initiatives, the WHO Pandemic Hub fosters a collaborative environment for innovators, scientists and experts from across a wide spectrum of disciplines, allowing us to leverage and share cutting-edge technology and anchoring our work in the needs of stakeholders around the world.
translations.hypothesis: It is one of the WHO's Global Health Initiatives, and the WHO Office for Global Health Security and Preparedness (GOHSP) supports partnerships among global stakeholders to assist countries and communities in preparing for and responding to future health emergencies with evidence-based data, effective interventions, and tailored policies and guidelines. With support from the German Federal Government, the WHO Office for Global Health Security and Preparedness was established in Berlin in September 2021, in response to the COVID-19 pandemic, which highlighted the interconnectedness of the world and how countries are affected by global health threats. The WHO Office for Global Health Security and Preparedness works closely with Member States, regional and country offices of the WHO to strengthen their data-driven capacity and to facilitate collaboration among global partners to develop and implement evidence-based strategies and policies. With a presence in over 150 countries, six regional offices, and its headquarters in Geneva, the WHO provides invaluable insights into emerging health threats, global health security, and public health emergencies. Through its global network, regional hubs, and country offices, the WHO Office for Global Health Security and Preparedness fosters a collaborative environment for experts, scientists, and policymakers across various sectors, enabling us to develop and implement innovative solutions to address the evolving needs of communities worldwide.

translations.source: Gígùn lé àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ káàkiri àwọn ẹ̀ka-ẹ̀kọ́, àwọn ẹ̀ka àti àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè, ó máa ṣàmúlò àkójọ agbára WHO láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ọ̀nà àbáyọ àgbáyé tí wọ́n gbé sórí àgbékalẹ̀ àjọṣepọ̀ àgbáyé àti ìgbẹkẹ̀lé.
translations.reference: Building on expertise across disciplines, sectors, and regions, it will leverage WHO’s convening power to foster global solutions built on an architecture of global collaboration and trust.
translations.hypothesis: The organization works with various stakeholders, including educational institutions, community groups, and local authorities, to mobilize the WHO's resources in support of global health initiatives that promote international cooperation and well-being.

translations.source: Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin máa gbájúmọ́ "dídóólà ẹ̀mí, mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn" bí WHO ṣe pé 75 Pẹ̀lu bí gbogbo àgbáyé ṣe ń kojú àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera àti ọmọnìyàn tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́, Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin máa gbájúmọ́ mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn. Ìjókòó Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé ọdún yìí máa ṣe ìpinnu lórí ọjọ́ iwájú ojú ẹsẹ̀ àti onígbà-pípẹ́ WHO, bẹ̀rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú ètò ìṣúná fún ọdún méjì tó ń bọ̀, àwọn ìpinnu tó ṣe pàtàkì nípa ìpèsè owó alálòpẹ́ fún Ilé-iṣẹ́ náà àti àwọn àyípadà tí wọ́n ti ṣe láti mú àyípadà bá àwọn ìlànà àti ìṣirò WHO. Àwọn aṣojú ìjọba tún máa jíròrò nípa ipa pàtàkì tí WHO ní nínú Àgbékalẹ̀ Pàjáwírì Ìlera Àgbáyé. Ìṣíṣọ lójú àwọn ìṣòro wọ̀nyìí ni ayẹyẹ ọdún 75th àjọ náà, pẹ̀lú ṣíṣe àfihàn àwọn àṣeyọrí ìlera àgbáyé àjọni rẹ̀ láti ìgbà tí wọ́n ti dá WHO sílẹ̀ ní ọdún 1948.
translations.reference: Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly to focus on “saving lives, driving health for all” as WHO turns 75 As the world faces ongoing health and humanitarian emergencies, the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly will focus on driving forward health for all. This year’s session of the World Health Assembly will determine the immediate and longer-term future of WHO, starting with the program budget for the next two years, key decisions about the sustainable financing of the Organization and changes put in place to improve WHO’s processes and accountability. Delegates will also deliberate about the critical role that WHO has in the Global Health Emergency Architecture. The backdrop to these issues is the organization’s 75th anniversary, with the highlights of its collective global health achievements since WHO’s creation in 1948.
translations.hypothesis: The 75th World Health Assembly has adopted the theme "Health for All: Commitment to Action," as stated by the WHO. With global health challenges and inequalities persisting, the World Health Assembly is focusing on accelerating progress towards health for all. This year's Assembly will address key issues, including the upcoming two-year strategic plan, critical decisions regarding the organization's budget, and reforms aimed at strengthening the WHO's effectiveness and governance. Government representatives will also discuss the vital role of the WHO in addressing global health emergencies. The 75th anniversary celebration will highlight the organization's achievements in global health over the past 75 years, since its establishment in 1948.

translations.source: Ó máa ṣe àtúnyẹwò ìlọsíwájú ọdún tí ó kọjá, àwọn àṣeyọrí àti àwọn ìpèníjà pẹ̀lú àwọn kàn-án-pá ọjọ́ iwájú káàkiri àwọn kókó ìpìlẹ iṣẹ́ WHO: Kíkárí Ìlera Lágbàyé, Àwọn Pàjáwírì, Ṣíṣe Ìpolongo Ìlera àti Ìgbáyégbádùn. WHA76 máa wáyé ní 21-30 oṣù Èbìbí ní Palais des Nations ní Geneva. "Bí a ṣe ń ṣe àjọ̀dun ọdún 75th WHO, a lè ṣe ìyanràn pẹ̀lú àwọn àṣeyọrí àtẹ̀yìnwá wa, ṣùgbọ́n a gbọ́dọ̀ tún fi ọkàn sí àwọn ẹ̀kọ́ tí a ti kọ́, pẹ̀lú bí a ṣe ń ṣe ìṣípòrọpò láti abala pàjáwírì COVID-19, kí á sì pèsè ọjọ́ iwájú níbi tí gbogbo ènìyàn máa ní ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera tí wọ́n nílò," Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. Èyí túnmọ̀ sí ríró àgbékalẹ̀ àgbáyé fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún pàjáwírì ìlera, ìdáhùn àti ìfaradà, ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ní ojú ọ̀nà sí kíkárí ìlera lágbàyé, àti ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ WHO tó lágbára, ìpèsè owó alálòpẹ́ àti ìṣirò. Láti bẹ̀rẹ WHA76 ní ọjọ́ Àìkú 21 oṣù Èbìbí, àtúnṣe kẹrin Walk the Talk: Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn máa bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní Palais des Nations ní 8:30 am CEST (ìmúrasílẹ̀ máa bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní 7:30 am CEST), láti ṣe ìpolongo ìlera ara àti ọpọlọ àti láti ṣe ayẹyẹ pàtàkì àjọṣepọ̀ lásìkò àjọ̀dún ọdún 75th WHO.
translations.reference: It will review last year’s progress, accomplishments and challenges along with future priorities across the key pillars of WHO’s work: Universal Health Coverage, Emergencies, Promoting Health and Well-being. WHA76 will convene 21-30 May at the Palais des Nations in Geneva. “As we mark WHO’s 75th anniversary, we can be proud of our past achievements, but we must be mindful of lessons learned, as we transition from the emergency phase of COVID-19, and create a future where every person has access to the health services they need,” said Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “This means strengthening the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response and resilience, supporting countries on the road to universal health coverage, and building a stronger, sustainably-funded and accountable WHO. "To kickstart WHA76 on Sunday 21 May, the fourth edition of Walk the Talk: Health for All challenge will start at Place des Nations at 8:30 am CEST (warm-up starts at 7:30 am CEST), to promote physical and mental health and celebrate the importance of partnership during the WHO’s 75th anniversary.
translations.hypothesis: The World Health Assembly (WHA) will review the progress made over the past year, celebrate achievements, and address challenges, focusing on the key pillars of the WHO's work: Global Health Security, Health Emergencies, Promoting Health and Well-being. WHA76 will take place from May 21-30 at the Palais des Nations in Geneva. "As we mark the 75th anniversary of the WHO, we can celebrate the remarkable progress we have made, but we must also learn from our mistakes, including how we responded to the COVID-19 pandemic, and work together to build a future where everyone has access to the health services they need," said WHO Director-General Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. This includes a call for increased global investment in health, strengthening health systems, providing support to countries on their journey to universal health coverage, and a more effective, equitable, and sustainable WHO. To kick off WHA76 on Monday, May 21, the fourth edition of "Walk the Talk: Health for All" will begin at the Palais des Nations at 8:30 am CEST (broadcasting will begin at 7:30 am CEST), focusing on promoting mental health and well-being and celebrating the important partnerships during the WHO's 75th anniversary year.

translations.source: Ní bíi 2:00pm CEST, lẹ́yìn fọ́tò ẹbí pẹ̀lú Àwọn Olórí Aṣojú Ìjọba, ìkíni káàbọ̀ onípele-gíga Àpéjọ náà ní Palais des Nations máa ṣàfihàn àwọn ọ̀rọ̀ àkọ́sọ láti ẹnu Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO, Àwọn Olórí Orílẹ̀-èdè àti àwọn àlejò pàtàkì. Àkójọpọ̀ àwọn olùdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ wà ní ìsàlẹ̀). Ní àkókò ìjókòó yìí, DG máa gbé àmì-ẹ̀yẹ àṣeyọrí ìgbésí-ayé fún àwọn olórí ìlera àgbáyé méjì. Ní ọjọ́ Ajé 22 oṣù Èbìbí, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ náà máa ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ọ̀rọ rẹ̀ gangan ní déédé àkókò 10:00am. Ní ọjọ́ Ẹtì, 26 oṣù Èbìbí, àwọn aṣojú àwọn ìpìlẹ̀ àgbáyé márààrún máa darapọ̀ mọ́ Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO, láti pín àmì-ẹ̀yẹ ìlera àwujọ márùn-ún àti àwọn ẹ̀bùn fún àwọn ẹni iyì méje fún ọdún 2023. Wọ́n wá láti Bahrain, China, Guatemala, Lebanon, Nepal, Thailand àti Philippines.
translations.reference: At about 2:00 pm CEST, following a family photo with Heads of Delegations, the Assembly high-level welcome at Palais des Nations will feature speeches from the WHO Director-General, Heads of State and special guests. List of speakers below). During this session, the DG will present life-time achievement awards to two global health leaders. On Monday 22 May, the Director-General will give his main address at approximately 10:00 am. On Friday, 26 May, the WHO Director-General will be joined by representatives of five global foundations, to present five public health awards and prizes to the seven laureates for 2023. They come from Bahrain, China, Guatemala, Lebanon, Nepal, Thailand and the Philippines.
translations.hypothesis: At 2:00 PM CEST, following a family photo with the Heads of State, the High-Level Meeting of the World Health Assembly at the Palais des Nations will feature remarks from the WHO Director-General, Heads of State, and other distinguished guests. (A list of speakers is provided below). During this session, the Director-General will present awards for lifetime achievement to two global health leaders. On Thursday, February 22nd, the Director-General will deliver a keynote address at 10:00 AM. On Friday, February 26th, representatives from seven countries will join the WHO Director-General to present seven awards for public health innovation and seven awards for excellence to individuals for their work in 2023. They are coming from Bahrain, China, Guatemala, Lebanon, Nepal, Thailand, and the Philippines.

translations.source: Àwọn ìṣòro tó jẹ́ kókó Àwọn ìjírórò tó ṣe kókó àti àwọn ìpinnu ni wọ́n ń retí lórí: Àtúnyẹwò iṣẹ́ WHO nínú pàjáwírì ìlera, tó fi mọ́ Àwọn Òfin Ìlera Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè àti ṣíṣe ìrólágbára fún ìgbaradì WHO fún àti ìdáhùn sí àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera; Àwọn ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àti ìgbésẹ̀ àgbáyé lórí àwọn agbègbè bíi ìlera àwọn obìnrin, àwọn ọmọde àti ọ̀dọ́, ìtúnirọ, kíkárí ìlera lágbàyé àti ètò ìlera alábọ́dé, ìṣègùn ìbílẹ̀, ìdíwọ́ àti ìdènà àkóràn, oògùn tí kìí ṣe ojúlówó àti ayédèrú, ìlera àwọn asásàálà àti aṣíkiri, àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn, ìlera ọpọlọ, àwọn aṣokùnfà àwùjọ, èròjà aṣara lóore àti àwọn ìpèníjà ara; Ìbuwọ́lù Ètò Ìṣúná WHO fún 2024-2025, tó fi mọ́ ìpinnu láti mú àlékún bá owó àjọ tí wọ́n yẹ̀wò rẹ̀ àti àwọn ọ̀rọ̀ mìíràn tó ń wá láti ọ̀dọ Ẹgbẹ́ Òṣìṣẹ́ lórí Ìpèsè owó Alálòpẹ́.
translations.reference: Key issues Key discussions and decisions are expected on: A review of WHOs work in health emergencies, including the International Health Regulations and strengthening WHO preparedness for and response to health emergencies; Strategies and global action on areas such as women’s, children’s and adolescents’ health, rehabilitation, universal health coverage and primary health care, traditional medicine, infection prevention and control, substandard and falsified medicines, health of refugees and migrants, non-communicable diseases, mental health, social determinants, nutrition and disabilities; Approval of the WHO Programme Budget for 2024-2025, including the decision to increase assessed contributions and other matters emanating from the Working Group on Sustainable Financing.
translations.hypothesis: Key priorities, challenges, and decisions: Review of the WHO's work in public health, including its collaboration with the Regional Offices and the implementation of the WHO's strategic plan and priorities for health. Global health initiatives and progress on areas such as women's and children's health, immunization, global health security, universal health coverage, health systems strengthening, prevention and control of non-communicable diseases, essential medicines and health products, mental health and substance use, neglected tropical diseases, multiple sclerosis, social determinants of health, and climate change and health. Update on the WHO's Medium-Term Budget Framework for 2024-2025, including decisions on the allocation of resources and other matters raised by the Executive Board regarding the proposed budget.

translations.source: Ìjírórò Orí-tábílì Ojoojúmọ́ máa wáyé ní àkókò oúnjẹ ọ̀sán láti ọjọ́ Ajé sí ọjọ́ Àbámẹ́ta ní Yàrá XVIII. Àwọn ìjókòó yìí máa ní àwọn ìjírórò láàrín àwọn aṣojú ìjọba fún àpéjọ, àwọn àjọ tó jẹ́ alájọṣepọ̀, àwọn aṣojú àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ àti àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ WHO lórí àwọn kàn-án-pá ìlera àwujọ. Àwọn ìjókòó náà máa bẹ̀rẹ̀ láti 22-27 oṣù Èbìbí, láti 13:00 sí 14:15 CEST ó sì ṣe é tẹ̀lé lórí ẹ̀rọ ayélujára. Gẹ́gẹ́ bí ẹgbẹ́ tí ń ṣe ìpinnu WHO tó ga jùlọ, Àpéjọ Ìlera náà ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn òfin Àjọ náà ó sì ń buwọ́lù ètò ìṣúná rẹ̀. Àwọn aṣojú ìjọba láti 149 Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ ló máa ń wá sí WHA.
translations.reference: Daily Strategic roundtables will take place at lunch time from Monday to Saturday in Room XVIII. These events will host discussions among assembly delegates, partner agencies, representatives of civil society and WHO experts on public health priorities. Sessions run from 22-27 May, from 13:00 to 14:15 CEST and can be followed online. As WHO’s highest decision-making body, the Health Assembly sets out the Organization’s policy and approves its budget. WHA is attended by delegations from all WHO 194 Member States.
translations.hypothesis: The World Health Assembly's daily press briefings were held during the lunch break, from Monday to Friday, during the 18th session. These briefings featured presentations by government representatives, partner organizations, representatives of civil society, and WHO experts on key health issues. The briefings typically took place from 22-27 February, from 13:00 to 14:15 CEST, and were broadcast online. As the highest decision-making body of the WHO, the World Health Assembly establishes the organization's priorities and approves its budget. Representatives from 149 Member States attended the WHA.

translations.source: Ètò Àpéjọ Ìlera náà ni ìpàdé 30th fún Ètò náà, Ìgbìmọ̀ fún Ìṣúná àti Alámòjútó ti Ìgbìmọ̀ Alàkóso máa ṣíwájú rẹ̀, tí wọ́n máa pàdé ní 17-19 oṣù Èbìbí, ọdún 2023. Lẹ́yìn Àpéjọ náà, ìpàdé 153th ti Ìgbìmọ̀ Alàkóso máa wáyé láti ọjọ́ 31 oṣù Èbìbí sí 1 Òkudù. Àpéjọ Ìlera náà wà fún Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ tí wọ́n forúkọsílẹ̀ tí wọ́n sì gba àṣẹ, Àwọn Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ Alájọṣepọ̀, Àwọn Alákíyèsí, àwọn aṣojú tí wọ́n fi ìwé pè láti UN àti àwọn àjọ ìjọba-sí-ìjọba àti àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ tí kìí ṣe ti ìjọba tí wọ́n ń kópa. WHA76 náà ni wọn yóò gbé sórí ẹ̀rọ ayélujára bó ṣe ń ṣẹlẹ̀ lórí ìbùdó-ìtàkun WHO. Ètò tí wọ́n ti ṣe, àtẹ-iṣẹ́ àkọ́kọ́ ṣe, àwọn ìwé Àpéjọ àti ìwé ìròyìn ojoojúmọ́ lè di wíwò níbi. Fífún àwọn oníròyìn ní àṣẹ àti àwọn ìfitóni fún Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé
translations.reference: The Health Assembly’s agenda is preceded by the 30th meeting of the Programme, Budget and Administration Committee of the Executive Board, which will meet on 17-19 May, 2023. After the Assembly, the 153th meeting of the Executive Board will take place from 31 May to 1 June. The Health Assembly is open to registered and accredited Member States, Associate Members, Observers, invited representatives of the UN and other participating inter-governmental organizations and non-State actors. WHA76 will be webcast live from the WHO website. The provisional agenda, preliminary timetable, Assembly documents and daily journal can be accessed here.Media accreditation and updates for the World Health Assembly
translations.hypothesis: The 30th session of the Programme Coordination Committee, the Standing Committee on Health, and the Executive Board meeting will be held from February 17-19, 2023. Following these meetings, the 153rd session of the Executive Board will convene from February 31 to March 1. The Health Assembly is open to all Member States that are members, as well as associate members, observers, representatives of the United Nations and other intergovernmental organizations, and non-governmental organizations that have been recognized by the WHO. WHA76 will be held virtually, with participants accessing the proceedings through the WHO's online platform. Agendas, documents, and daily updates will be available online. Media accreditation and press kits for the World Health Assembly are now available.

translations.source: Àjọ Iṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé ní Geneva ló máa ṣàkóso fínfún àwọn oníròyìn ní àṣẹ fún WHA76. Forúkọsílẹ̀ nípasẹ̀ Indico. Láti gba àkọmọ̀nà rẹ: Ní ọjọ́ Ẹtì 19 oṣù Èbìbí (àkókò iṣẹ́: 9:00 am sí 16:30 pm CEST) ní Villa Feuillantines, Place des Nations . Àlàyé àti àdírẹ́sì : https://unece.org/important-information-how-obtain-your-badge Ní ọjọ́ Àbámẹ́ta 20 oṣù Èbìbí: UN Palais des Nations ní Pregny Gate (àdojúkọ ICRC Building) láti 13:00 sí 17:00 CEST. Ní ọjọ́ Àìkú 21 oṣù Èbìbí, wà á gba àkọmọ̀nà oníròyìn rẹ ní UN Palais des Nations ní Pregny Gate (àdojúkọ ICRC Building) láti 9:00am sí 16:00 pm CEST
translations.reference: Media accreditation for WHA76 will be handled by the United Nations of Geneva. Register via Indico. To collect your badge: On Friday 19 May (working hours: 9:00 am to 16:30 pm CEST) at Villa Feuillantines, Place des Nations . Details and address : https://unece.org/important-information-how-obtain-your-badge On Saturday 20 May: UN Palais des Nations at Pregny Gate (opposite ICRC Building) from 13:00 to 17:00 CEST. On Sunday 21 May, you shall collect your media badge at the UN Palais des Nations at Pregny Gate (opposite ICRC Building) from 9:00am to 16:00 pm CEST
translations.hypothesis: The United Nations Office in Geneva will be responsible for distributing accreditation badges for WHA76. Registration is through Indico. To obtain your badge: On Monday, February 19th (working hours: 9:00 am to 4:30 pm CEST) at Villa Feuillantines, Place des Nations. For details and address: https://unece.org/important-information-how-obtain-your-badge On Tuesday, February 20th: At the UN Palais des Nations, Pregny Gate (adjacent to the ICRC Building), from 1:00 pm to 5:00 pm CEST. On Wednesday, February 21st, you can collect your media badge at the UN Palais des Nations, Pregny Gate (adjacent to the ICRC Building), from 9:00 am to 4:00 pm CEST.

translations.source: Ní ọ̀sẹ̀ tó ń bọ̀ - 22-26 oṣù Èbìbí- láti gba àwọn àkọmọ̀nà oníròyìn: Villa Les Feuillantines at Place des Nations. Ìfitóni lori Àpéjọ Ìlera ni wọ́n máa fi sí orí ibùdó ìtàkùn WHO wọn ó sì fi àwọn ohun èlò tó ṣe pàtàkì náà ránṣé sí àkójọpọ̀ àwọn oníròyìn àgbáyé. Forúkọsílẹ̀ fún ìfitóni. Àwọn fọ́tò àti fídíò Àwọn fọ́tò WHO wà nílẹ̀ fún lílò àwọn oníròyìn nípasẹ àká fọ́tò WHO.
translations.reference: Next week - 22-26 May- to collect the media badges: Villa Les Feuillantines at Place des Nations. Updates on the Health Assembly will be posted on the WHO website and relevant materials will be sent to the global media list. Register for updates. Photographs and videos WHO photos are available for media use via the WHO photo library.
translations.hypothesis: Next week, from February 22nd to 26th, a press conference will be held at the Villa Les Feuillantines, located at Place des Nations. The conference will focus on the World Health Assembly, and the WHO will present key findings and initiatives to a group of international journalists. Registration for the press conference is required. Photos and videos: WHO photos are available on the WHO website for use by journalists through the WHO photo library.

translations.source: Forúkọsílẹ̀ ní ẹ̀ẹ̀kan fún ànfàní sí ìròyìn sí àká àwọn fọ́tò WHO pẹ̀lú títẹ "media registration" lórí ìkànnì yìí. Fún àwọn ìbéèrè tó jọ mọ́ àwọn fọ́tò WHO tẹ ímeèlì sí [ímeèlì tí wọ́n dáàbòbò] Àwọn ohun èlò fídíò orí afẹ́fẹ́ tí wọ́n ká sílẹ̀ àti àwọn àkóónú ìròyìn máa wà nílẹ̀ fún àwọn oníròyìn láti gbà á sílẹ̀ lórí Canto. Fún èyíkéyìí ìbéèrè tó jọ mọ́ àgbéká fídíò bó ṣe ń ṣẹlẹ̀ jọ̀wọ́ tẹ ímeèlì sí [ímeèlì tí wọ́n dáàbòbò]. Ìkànnì ìbánidọ́rẹ̀ẹ́ WHO máa pèsè ìfitóni àtìgbàdégbà láti ibi Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé nípasẹ Twitter -tẹ̀lé @WHO àti ìsàmìááṣì #WHA76. Tẹ̀lé àwọn àkántì ìkànnì ìbánidọ́rẹ̀ẹ́ WHO tókù fún ìfitóni láti#WHA76: Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, YouTube
translations.reference: Register once for media access to the WHO photo library by clicking “media registration” on this page. For questions related to WHO photos email [email protected] Recorded broadcast video material and other press content will be available for media to download on Canto. For any questions related to live video transmission please email [email protected]. Social mediaWHO will provide regular updates from the World Health Assembly via Twitter –follow @WHO and hashtag #WHA76.Follow WHO’s other social media accounts for updates from #WHA76: Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, YouTube
translations.hypothesis: Please register through the link provided for access to WHO photos, labeled "media registration." For inquiries regarding WHO photos, please email [provided email address]. Video content and press materials will be available for journalists to download on Canto. For any questions regarding the video content, please email [provided email address]. The WHO will be providing live updates and press releases from the World Health Assembly via Twitter at @WHO and using the hashtag #WHA76. Follow these official WHO accounts for live updates: Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, YouTube.

translations.source: A nílò ìgbẹ́sẹ̀ ojú ẹsẹ̀ láti kojú ìlọsíwájú tó ti dúró lórí àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ ajẹmọ́-ìlera WHO ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò ọdún 2023 fún ìjábọ̀ àwọn Ìṣirò Ìlera Àgbáyé ọlọ́dọọdún rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn ònkà tuntun lórí ipa àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 àti àwọn ìṣirò tuntun lórí ìlọsíwájú fún Àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ (SDG) ajẹmọ́-ìlera. Ìjábọ̀ náà pẹ̀lú dátà tí ó dé ọdún 2022 ṣàfihàn ìdúróṣinṣin ìlọsíwájú ìlera lórí àwọn atọ́ka ìlera pàtàkì láti bíi ọdún díẹ̀ sẹ́yìn yàtọ̀ sí àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tí a rí ní àkókò ọdún 2000-2015. Ó tún pe àkíyèsí wa sí ewu àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn (NCDs) tó ń lọ sókè àti àyípadà ojú ọjọ́, ó sì pè fún ìdáhùn àjọṣe àti ìrólágbára. Iye tí COVID-19 kó nínú ẹ̀mí tí ó sọnù àti ìlọsíwájú ìlera Àwọn ìwé ìjábọ̀ náà ṣe ìsọdọ̀tun ìṣirò lórí aburú àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà lórí ìlera àgbáyé, tó sì ń ṣokùnfà ìfàsẹ́yìn tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ nínú ìlọsíwájú láti dé SDGs.
translations.reference: Urgent action needed to tackle stalled progress on health-related Sustainable Development Goals WHO is releasing the 2023 edition of its annual World Health Statistics report with new figures on the impact of COVID-19 pandemic and the latest statistics on progress towards the health-related Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). The report with data up to 2022 underscores a stagnation of health progress on key health indicators in recent years compared with trends seen during 2000-2015. It also alerts us to the growing threat of noncommunicable diseases (NCDs) and climate change, and calls for a coordinated and strengthened response. COVID-19 cost in lost lives and health progress The report documents updated statistics on the toll of the pandemic on global health, contributing to the ongoing decline in progress towards the SDGs.
translations.hypothesis: A comprehensive analysis is needed to understand the progress made by the World Health Organization's (WHO) Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) health-related targets in its annual report on global health trends, particularly in light of new data on the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic and advancements in achieving the SDGs. The report, which includes data up to 2022, highlights significant progress in health outcomes for key health indicators compared to the period between 2000 and 2015. It also draws attention to the growing burden of non-communicable diseases (NCDs) and the increasing prevalence of visual impairment, and calls for increased investment and innovation. The impact of COVID-19 on mortality and global health progress is a key focus of the report. The report provides an assessment of the pandemic's impact on global health, and examines the implications for progress towards the SDGs.

translations.source: Ní àkókò 2020-2021, COVID-19 yọrí sí 336.8 mílíọ̀nù ọdún ẹ̀mí tí ó sọnù káàkiri àgbáyé tó sì yanilẹ́nu. Èyí dọ́gba pẹ̀lú ìdajì ọdún 22 ẹ̀mí tí ó sọnù fún àpọ̀ju ikú kọ̀ọ̀kan, tí ó gẹ́ ẹ̀mí mílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn kéré ní òjijì pẹ̀lú ìbànújẹ́. Láti ọdún 2000, a rí àyípadà tó lápẹrẹ nínú ìlera ìyá àti ọmọ pẹ̀lú ikú tí ó wálẹ̀ ní ìdá-kan-nínú-mẹ́ta àti ìdajì-kan. Ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àwọn ààrùn àkóràn bíi KASA, ikọ́-ife (TB) àti àìsàn ibà náà tún dínkù, papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àdínkù ewu ikú àìtọ́jọ́ látara NCDs àti àwọn ìfarapa. Lápapọ̀, àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà ti ṣe àfikún sí àlékún nínú ìrètí ìṣẹ̀mí lágbàyé láti ọdún 67 ní ọdún 2000 sí ọdún 73 ní ọdún 2019.
translations.reference: During 2020-2021, COVID-19 resulted in a staggering 336.8 million years of life lost globally. This equates to an average of 22 years of life lost for every excess death, abruptly and tragically cutting short the lives of millions of people. Since 2000, we saw significant improvements in maternal and child health with deaths falling by one-third and one-half, respectively. The incidence of infectious diseases such as HIV, tuberculosis (TB) and malaria also declined, along with a lowered risk of premature deaths from NCDs and injuries. Together, these contributed to an increase in global life expectancy from 67 years in 2000 to 73 years in 2019.
translations.hypothesis: During the period of 2020-2021, COVID-19 caused an estimated 336.8 million years of life lost globally, a significant burden. This is in addition to the 22 million years of life lost annually to all causes, resulting in the premature deaths of millions of people. Since 2000, there has been a noticeable increase in maternal and child mortality, with deaths occurring at rates of one in three and one in ten, respectively. The incidence of infectious diseases such as HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis, and malaria has also declined, along with an increase in the risk of death from non-communicable diseases and injuries. Overall, these health events have contributed to an increase in global life expectancy, from 67 years in 2000 to 73 years in 2019.

translations.source: Síbẹ̀síbẹ̀, àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà ti fi mú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn atọ́ka ajẹmọ́-ìlera kúrò ní ojú ọ̀nà síwájú sí i ó sì ti yọrí sí àìdọ́gba nínú ànfààní sí ìtọ́jú ìlera tó jẹ́ ojúlówó-ńlá, ìlàna-iṣẹ́ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti ààbò ìpèsè owó. Nítorí ìdí èyí, àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ìlọsíwájú nínú àìsàn ibà àti TB ti padà sẹ́yìn, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ènìyàn díẹ̀ ni wọ́n tọ́jú fún àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì (NTDs). Ìṣirò Ìlera Àgbáyé jẹ́ àyẹ̀wò ọlọ́dọọdún WHO lórí ipò ìlera àgbáyé. Ìjábọ̀ náà fi ìkìlọ̀ alágbára ránṣẹ́ lórí ewu àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn, tí ó ní ọ̀pọ̀ àti àlékún aburú lórí àwọn ẹ̀mí, ọ̀nà ìgbà rí ìjẹ-ìmu, àwọn ètò ìlera, àwọn àwùjọ, àwọn ọrọ̀-ajé àti àwọn ẹgbẹ́," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. Ìjábọ̀ náà pè fún àlékún nínú àwọn ìdókówò fún ìlera àti àwọn ètò ìlera láti padà sí ojú ònà lọ sí Àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́."
translations.reference: However, the pandemic has put many health-related indicators further off-track and contributed to inequalities in access to high-quality health care, routine immunizations and financial protection. As a result, improving trends in malaria and TB have been reversed, and fewer people were treated for neglected tropical diseases (NTDs). “The World Health Statistics is WHO’s annual check-up on the state of the world’s health. The report sends a stark message on the threat of noncommunicable diseases, which take an immense and increasing toll on lives, livelihoods, health systems, communities, economies and societies,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “The report calls for a substantial increase in investments in health and health systems to get back on track towards the Sustainable Development Goals.”
translations.hypothesis: However, this progress has come at a cost, with many essential health services disrupted and significant setbacks in areas such as immunization, maternal and child health, and access to essential medicines. As a result, progress in combating HIV, tuberculosis, and malaria has been reversed, and fewer people are receiving treatment for neglected tropical diseases. The World Health Report, an annual assessment by the World Health Organization (WHO) of the global health situation, paints a stark picture. The report highlights the devastating impact of the pandemic on health systems, economies, and societies worldwide. "The pandemic has exposed the fragility of our health systems and the interconnectedness of health, economy, and society," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the WHO. "The report calls for increased investment in health and health systems to build resilience and prepare for future health emergencies." The report urges countries to prioritize investments in primary healthcare and essential health services to ensure that everyone has access to the care they need.

translations.source: NCDs-ewu ìlera tó máa ń lékún ní gbogbo ìgbà fún àwọn ìran ọjọ́ iwájú Pẹ̀lú àpapọ ìlọsíwájú ìlera, ìpín iye ikú tí NCDs ń ṣe okùnfà rẹ̀ lọ́dọọdún ti lọ sókè léra-léra ó sì ti wá ń gbà tó ìdá-mẹ́ta-nínú-mẹ́rin gbogbo àwọn ẹ̀mí tó ń sọnù ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan. Tí ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ yìí bá tẹ̀síwájú, NCDs ni wọ́n fojú sùn pé yóò ṣe okùnfà fún bíi 86% nínú 90 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ikú ọdọọdún ní ìdajì-ọgọ́rùn ọdún; fún ìdí èyí, 77 mílíọ̀nù àwọn wọ̀nyìí ni ó máa jẹ́ nítorí NCDs - àlékún tó fẹ́rẹ̀ súnmọ́ 90% nínú iye ònkà láti ọdún 2019. Ìdúróṣinṣin ìlọsíwájú pè fún amúṣẹ́yá Ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tuntun ṣe àfihàn ìfàsẹ́yìn nínú iye àdínkù ọdọọdún (ARR) fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn atọ́ka. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, iye ikú ìyá lágbàyé nílò láti dínkù pẹ̀lú 11.6% ní ọdún kan láàrín ọdún 2021 àti 2030 láti dọ́gba pẹ̀lú àfojúsùn SDG. Bákan náà, àpapọ̀ àdínkù nínú ìṣẹ̀lẹ TB láti ọdún 2015 sí 2021 jẹ́ ìdá-kan-nínú-márùn ọ̀nà láti débi àṣeyọrí 2025 ti Ìlàna-iṣẹ́ Fi Òpin sí TB WHO.
translations.reference: NCDs ̶ an ever-increasing health threat for future generationsDespite overall health progress, the share of deaths caused annually by NCDs has grown consistently and is now claiming nearly three quarters of all lives lost each year. If this trend continues, NCDs are projected to account for about 86% of the 90 million annual deaths by mid-century; consequently, 77 million of these will be due to NCDs – a nearly 90% increase in absolute numbers since 2019. Stagnating progress calls for acceleration More recent trends show signs of slowdown in the annual rate of reduction (ARR) for many indicators. For example, the global maternal mortality ratio needs to decline by 11.6% per year between 2021 and 2030 to meet the SDG target. Similarly, the net reduction in TB incidence from 2015 to 2021 was only one-fifth of the way to the 2025 milestone of WHO’s End TB Strategy.
translations.hypothesis:
Non-communicable diseases (NCDs) are a major public health concern worldwide, posing a significant threat to future generations. Despite advancements in healthcare, the burden of NCDs continues to rise, accounting for nearly three-quarters of all deaths each year. If this trend continues, NCDs are projected to be responsible for 86% of the 90 million deaths annually by 2030; of these, 77 million will be premature deaths – representing nearly 90% of the projected mortality increase from 2019.

Progress is needed for sustainable development. A number of new developments highlight the importance of progress towards achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). For example, global maternal mortality needs to decrease by 11.6% per year between 2021 and 2030 to meet the SDG target. Furthermore, the overall increase in TB incidence from 2015 to 2021 was only one-sixth of the way to achieving the 2025 target of the WHO End TB Strategy.

translations.source: Pẹ̀lú àdínkù nínú ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú oríṣiríṣi ewu ìlera - bíi lílo tábà, ọtí mímu, rògbòdìyàn, omi tó léwu àti ìmọ́tótó, àti àìdàgbà sókè àwọn ọmọ - ìtẹ̀síwájú náà ò péye bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú àwọn ewu kọ̀ọ̀kan bíi bíba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ sì pọ̀ gan-an. Eyí tó banilẹ́rù ni pé, ọ̀nkà àpapọ ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìsan ìsanra ń lékún láì sí ààmì ìdápadà ojú ẹsẹ̀. Síwájú sí i, ìfẹ̀lójú ànfààní sí àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì ti fà sẹ́yìn ní àfiwé àwọn èrè ṣáájú-2015, papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àìsí ìtẹ̀síwájú tó lápẹrẹ nínú àdínkù ìnira ìpèsè owó nítorí iye owó ètò ìlera. Èyí máa mú àdínkù tó lápẹrẹ bá agbára wa láti ṣe àṣeyọrí Ìkárí Ìlera Àgbáyé ní ọdún 2030. Àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 jẹ́ ìrántí pàtàkì pé ìtẹ̀síwájú ò lọ tààrà kò sì ní ìdánilójú," Dr Samira Asma, Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún Dátà, Ìyànnàná àti Àgbékalẹ̀ fún Ipa WHO kílọ̀.
translations.reference: Despite a reduction in exposure to many health risks – such as tobacco use, alcohol consumption, violence, unsafe water and sanitation, and child stunting – progress was inadequate and exposure to some risks such as air pollution remains high. Alarmingly, the prevalence of obesity is rising with no immediate sign of reversal. Furthermore, expanded access to essential health services has slowed compared to pre-2015 gains, coupled with no significant progress in reducing financial hardship due to health-care costs. This drastically limits our ability to achieve Universal Health Coverage by 2030. “The COVID-19 pandemic is an important reminder that progress is neither linear nor guaranteed,” warns Dr Samira Asma, WHO Assistant Director-General for Data, Analytics and Delivery for Impact.
translations.hypothesis: Despite significant progress in reducing the burden of various diseases – including tobacco use, harmful alcohol consumption, road traffic accidents, air pollution, and childhood malnutrition – and in addressing specific health challenges like HIV/AIDS, progress has plateaued, and some areas have seen significant setbacks. This is particularly concerning given that the overall burden of non-communicable diseases continues to rise. Furthermore, recent cuts to essential health services, implemented in the aftermath of the 2015 economic crisis, coupled with the ongoing impact of reduced funding for health programs, are hindering progress. This makes it increasingly difficult to achieve the goals of the 2030 Sustainable Development Agenda. "The COVID-19 pandemic serves as a stark reminder that progress is fragile and not guaranteed," warned Dr. Samira Asma, Assistant Director-General for Data, Analytics, and Monitoring at the World Health Organization.

translations.source: "Láti wà lójú ọ̀nà fún ètò SDG ọdún 2030, a gbọ́dọ̀ gbé ìgbésẹ̀ ìpinnu àti lápapọ̀ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ipa tó ṣe é wọ̀n ní gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè." Lára ìjábọ̀ ọdún yìí ni a ti rí fún ìgbà àkọ́kọ́ ìsọ̀rí àkànṣe lórí àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ àti ìlera, a sì ń fojú sọ́nà pé èyí máa tún ṣe pàtàkì nínú ìjábọ̀ náà láti àsìkò yìí lọ. Fún ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ yìí àti àwọn agbègbè mìíràn, dátà ní àsìkò, aṣeégbáralé tí wọ́n yà sọ́tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀ ṣe pàtàkì láti tọpa ìlọsíwájú àti láti mú àyípadà bá àwọn òfin ìlera orílẹ̀-èdè èdè àti àgbáyé. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ Olóòtú: Ìjábọ̀ Ìṣirò Ìlera Àgbáyé ni àkójọpọ̀ ọdọọdún Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé (WHO) fún àwọn dátà tuntun tí ó wà lórí ìlera àti àwọn atọ́ka ajẹmọ́-ìlera. Fún ìbéèrè, kàn sí [ímeèlì tí wọ́n dáàbòbò]
translations.reference: “To stay on track towards the 2030 SDG agenda, we must act decisively and collectively to deliver a measurable impact in all countries.” This year’s report includes for the first time a dedicated section on climate change and health, and we anticipate that this will be of more relevance in the report going forward. For this issue and all other areas timely, reliable and disaggregated data are critical to track progress and improve national and global health policies. Editor’s note: The World Health Statistics report is the World Health Organization’s (WHO) annual compilation of the most recent available data on health and health-related indicators. For inquiries, contact [email protected]
translations.hypothesis: "To achieve the goals of the 2030 Sustainable Development Agenda, we must take decisive and collective action to maximize its impact in all countries." This year's report highlights the critical importance of addressing maternal mortality and health, and we anticipate that this will remain a key priority moving forward. For this and other areas, timely and accurate data are essential to ensure progress and to inform national and global health policies. Key Findings: The World Health Organization's annual World Health Statistics report provides the latest data on health and health-related indicators. For inquiries, please contact [email address].

translations.source: Olùdarí-àpapọ̀ WHO kéde Àmì-ẹ̀yẹ Àwọn Olórí Ìlera Lágbàyé Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum àti Peter Piot ni wọ́n fún ní àmì-ẹ̀yẹ fún ìfọkànsìn ìgbésí ayé wọn sí ìlera Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus máa ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àmì-ẹ̀yẹ àṣeyọrí ìgbésí ayé méjì náà láti mọ rírì àfikún aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ sí mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlera àgbáyé ní àkókò ìbẹ̀rẹ onípele-gíga fún Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin. Àmì-ẹ̀yẹ Àwọn Olórí Àgbáyé ti Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ náà, tí wọ́n ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní ọdún 2019, máa lọ sí ọ̀dọ Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum àti Peter Piot. Àwọn méjèèjì tó gba àmì-ẹ̀yẹ yìí jẹ́ onímọ̀ sáyẹ́nsì àti olórí ìlera àwùjọ tó yàtọ̀ tí wọ́n jọ ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ nínú ìṣàwárí ààrun kòkòrò Ẹ̀bọ́là ṣíwájú kí wọ́n tó dé ipò aṣíwájú nínú ìlera àgbáyé.
translations.reference: WHO Director-General announces Global Health Leaders Awards Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum and Peter Piot awarded for lifelong commitment to health WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus will present two lifetime achievement awards to recognize outstanding contributions to advancing global health during the high-level opening session of the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly. The Director-General’s Global Leaders Awards, which were established in 2019, will go to Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum and Peter Piot. Both recipients are distinguished scientists and public health leaders who were closely involved in the discovery of Ebola virus disease before advancing to leadership positions in global health.
translations.hypothesis: The Director-General of the World Health Organization (WHO), Jean-Jacques Muyembe-Tamfum and Peter Piot, have been awarded the Nobel Prize for their contributions to global health. The WHO Director-General, Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, will present the award to the two recipients at a special ceremony during the upcoming World Health Assembly. The Nobel Prize for Global Health, established in 2019, recognizes individuals who have made significant contributions to improving global health. Both Muyembe-Tamfum and Piot are renowned scientists and public health leaders who have worked together on a variety of global health challenges, including the fight against the Ebola virus.

translations.source: Àmì-ẹ̀yẹ náà bu iyì fún àṣeyọrí ìgbésí ayé wọn nínú ìlera àwùjọ. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Muyembe-Tamfum láti Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) ni wọ́n ń bu ọlá fún fún ìmọ̀-ọ̀tun, aṣíwájú àti àwọn ìdásí tó lápẹẹrẹ rẹ̀ sí àmójútó àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ààrùn bíi ààrun kòkòrò Ẹ̀bólà. Ó ti kó ipa pàtàkì nínú mímú àyípadà bá àbájáde ìlera ní Democratic Republic of Congo, ní ẹkùn agbègbè ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀, àti ní àwùjọ àgbáyé. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Muyembe-Tamfum ni Olùdarí Àpapọ̀ fún National Institute of Biomedical Research ti DRC ní Kinshasa, Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n tààrà fún ní University of Kinshasa Medical School àti ààrẹ àkọ́kọ́ fún Congolese Academy of Science. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Muyembe-Tamfum wà lára ikọ̀ tí wọ́n ṣàwarí kòkòrò ẹ̀bólà Zaïre ní àkókò tí wọ́n kọ́kọ́ ní àkọsílẹ̀ ìtànkálẹ̀ náà ní ọdún 1976, ní Equateur Province, Central DRC.
translations.reference: The award honours their lifetime achievements in public health. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) is being recognized for his innovation, leadership and ground-breaking contributions to the management and treatment of diseases such as Ebola virus disease. He has played a critical role in improving health outcomes in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, the African region, and the global community. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum is the Director General of the DRC’s National Institute of Biomedical Research in Kinshasa, full Professor of Microbiology at the University of Kinshasa Medical School and the inaugural president of the Congolese Academy of Science. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum was part of the team that discovered the Zaïre ebolavirus during the first recorded outbreak in 1976, in Equateur Province, Central DRC.
translations.hypothesis: The award recognizes his outstanding contributions to public health. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum, from the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), is being honored for his pioneering work in epidemiology, virology, and his leadership in the fight against diseases such as Ebola. He has played a crucial role in improving public health in the Democratic Republic of Congo, in the African region, and globally. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum is the Director General of the National Institute of Biomedical Research in the DRC, based in Kinshasa. He is also a professor at the University of Kinshasa Medical School and a member of the Congolese Academy of Science. Professor Muyembe-Tamfum was part of the team that first identified the Zaire Ebola virus during the initial outbreak in 1976 in the Equateur Province of the Central DRC.

translations.source: Ó tún padà ṣe ìrànwọ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ọ̀kan lára àwọn ìtọ́jú tó ṣiṣẹ́ jùlọ kojú kòkòrò ààrùn náà àti ṣíṣe àmúlò àyẹ̀wo àwọn àjẹsára kòkòrò ààrùn Ẹ̀bólà. Ó ti ṣiṣẹ́ láì sinmi láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìkápá ajẹmọ́-sáyẹ́nsì Democratic Republic of Congo, tí ó ń ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ oríṣiríṣi àwọ̀ ààyè fún ìwádìí ní orílẹ̀-èdè náà, ó sì ti kó ipa pàtàkì pẹ̀lú WHO nínú ìgbógun ti àwọn ààrùn àkóràn. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Piot láti orílẹ̀-èdè Belgium ni wọ́n ń bu ọlá fún fún aṣíwájú aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ rẹ̀ nínú ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn KASA àti àwọn ìpèníjà ìlera àgbáyé mìíràn tó ṣe pàtàkì. Ó ti kó ipa pàtàkì nínú ìfilọ́lẹ̀ àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ ìlera àgbáyé tó ṣe pàtàkì bíi UNAIDS, Global Fund, àti àwọn mìíràn tí wọ́n ti mú àyípadà bá àwọn àbájáde ìlera káàkiri àgbáyé. Lábẹ́ ìdarí rẹ̀, UNAIDS di agbẹnusọ àgbà fún ìgbésẹ̀ jákèjádò àgbáyé takò KASA, tó sì tún léwájú àtúntò UN pẹ̀lu ṣíṣe àkójọpọ̀ àwọn àjọ UN 10 ọ̀tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀.
translations.reference: He later helped design one of the first effective treatments against the virus and the deployment of experimental Ebola virus disease vaccines. He has worked tirelessly to build the Democratic Republic of the Congo’s scientific capacity, establishing multiple research facilities in the country, and has played key roles with WHO in fighting infectious diseases. Professor Piot from Belgium is being recognized for his extraordinary leadership in the response to the HIV/AIDS epidemic and other significant global health challenges. He has played a vital role in launching major global health initiatives such as UNAIDS, the Global Fund, and others that have improved health outcomes around the world. Under his leadership, UNAIDS became the chief advocate for worldwide action against AIDS, also spearheading UN reform by bringing together 10 different UN organizations.
translations.hypothesis: She also dedicated her efforts to strengthening one of the most effective motorcycle ambulance programs for treating Ebola and improving access to healthcare services in the Democratic Republic of Congo. She has worked tirelessly to support the development of scientific capacity in the country, and has played a crucial role with the WHO in the fight against infectious diseases. Dr. Piot, originally from Belgium, is recognized for her outstanding leadership in the response to the Ebola outbreak and other global health emergencies. She has been instrumental in the establishment of important global health initiatives, such as UNAIDS, the Global Fund, and others, which have made significant contributions to improving global health outcomes. Under her leadership, UNAIDS became a leading advocate for ending the AIDS epidemic, and she also championed the UN's efforts to consolidate ten different UN agencies.

translations.source: Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Piot tún ti kó ipa nínú ìṣàwárí kòkòrò ààrùn Ẹ̀bólà ní ìbẹ̀rẹ̀, àti kíkápá ìtànkálẹ̀ àkọ́kọ́ tí a mọ̀, tó sì ń kópa nínú àwọn ìwádìí ẹ̀kọ́ àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn ní ààyè ìwádìí. Ó ti jẹ́ alága fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ onípele-gíga, kópa nínú àìmọye àwọn ipò olùbánidámọ́ràn onípele-gíga, tó fi mọ́ COVID-19, ó sì ti gba ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àmì-ẹ̀yẹ. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Piot ni Olùbánidámọ́ràn Pàtàkì fún Ààrẹ EC, Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n nínú Ìlera Àgbáyé àti Olùdarí tẹ́lẹ̀ fún London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine. "Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n tayọ wọ̀nyìí jẹ́ àpẹẹrẹ ìfọkànsìn ayérayé, agbẹnusọ aláìdáwọ́dúró, ìfọkànsìn fún ìdọ́gba, àti iṣẹ́ àìnímọtara-ẹni-nìkan fún ọmọnìyàn àti ìlera àwọn ènìyàn," Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO, Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Ní àkókò tí oríṣiríṣi pàjáwírì, àwọn ìdààmú ìlera, àti rògbòdìyàn wà, a mọ wí pé asíwájú nínú ìlera àwùjọ àti sáyẹ́nsì ṣe pàtàkì púpọ̀ ju bó ṣe yẹ lọ láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlera àgbáyé.
translations.reference: Professor Piot also played a part in the earlier discovery of the Ebola virus, and the containment of the first known outbreak, participating in field epidemiological investigations. He has chaired a number of high-level initiatives, served in numerous high-level advisory roles, including on COVID-19, and has received multiple awards. Professor Piot is the Special Advisor to the EC President, a Professor of Global Health and former Director of the London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine. “These outstanding individuals embody lifelong dedication, relentless advocacy, a commitment to equity, and selfless service of humanity and people’s health,” said WHO Director-General, Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “In a time of multiple emergencies, health crises, and conflict, we recognize that leadership in public health and science are more important than ever to advance global health.
translations.hypothesis: Professor Piot has played a significant role in the early stages of the Ebola crisis, contributing to the development of the first effective treatment, and has been actively involved in global health research and policy. She has led numerous large-scale research projects, contributed to the understanding of the impact of large-scale epidemics, including COVID-19, and has received numerous awards. Professor Piot is the Director of the Epicenter, a leading global health research organization, and a former Director of the London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine. "These individuals are shining examples of global citizenship, unwavering dedication, innovation, and selfless service to humanity and global health," said WHO Director-General Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "In a world facing multiple crises, health challenges, and inequalities, we know that progress in global health and science is more important than ever to advance the well-being of all."

translations.source: Àwọn alámì-ẹ̀yẹ wa tòní jẹ́ àpẹẹrẹ ìfọkànsìn yẹn." "Àpapọ àwọn ìtàn àti àwọn àṣeyọrí wọn ṣàfihàn bí gbogbo àgbáyé ṣe ti ní ìlọsíwájú ní èyí tó níṣe pẹ̀lú àjọṣepọ̀ ní àwọn ìpele àgbáyé, ẹkùn agbègbè àti orílẹ̀-èdè láti ró ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún pàjáwírì ìlera, ìdáhùn àti ìfaradà lágbára. Àwọn onímọ̀ sáyẹ́nsì tó tayọ wọ̀nyìí máa tẹ̀síwájú láti jẹ́ ìwúrí fún wa ní ojoojúmọ́," ó fi kún-un Ayẹyẹ fún àmì-ẹ̀yẹ náà jẹ́ apá kan nínú sáà ìbẹ̀rẹ onípele-gíga fún Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé 76th tí ó ń wáyé ní ọjọ́ Àìkú 21 oṣù Èbìbí ọdún 2023 ní Geneva.
translations.reference: Our awardees today personify that passion.” “Their combined stories and successes show how much the world has progressed in terms of collaboration at the global, regional and country levels to strengthen health emergency preparedness, response and resilience. These outstanding scientists will continue to inspire us every day,” he added The ceremony for the awards form part of the high-level opening session of the 76th World Health Assembly being held on Sunday 21 May 2023 in Geneva.
translations.hypothesis: "Our laureates are shining examples of this innovation." "The stories and achievements of these individuals demonstrate how global collaboration, across sectors and countries, can drive progress in areas such as public health, well-being, and sustainable development. These distinguished scientists continue to inspire us every day," said the award committee. The ceremony was part of the opening of the 76th World Health Assembly, which took place from May 21 to May 26, 2023, in Geneva.

translations.source: WHO kí orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali kú oríire fún Ìṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìṣòro ìlera àwujọ WHO ti dá orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali láre pé wọ́n ti ṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìṣòro ìlera àwujọ, tó sọ wọ́n di àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè karùn-ún àti ìkẹfà ní Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ WHO láti ṣe àṣeyọrí aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ yìí. Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n ti gba ìdáláre WHO tẹ́lẹ̀ fún ìṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ni orílẹ̀-èdè Ghana (oṣù Òkudù ọdún 2018), Gambia (oṣù Igbe ọdún 2021), Togo (òṣì Èbìbí ọdún 2022), àti Malawi (oṣù Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2022). "WHO kí àwọn aláṣẹ ìlera fún orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali àti àwọn ojú òpó wọn fún àwọn àjọṣepọ̀ àgbáyé àti abẹ́lé kú oríire fún àwọn àṣeyọrí wọ̀nyìí", Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Lẹ́yìn àṣeyọrí orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali, ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ṣì dúró gẹ́gẹ́ bíi àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 23 ní Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ WHO, tó ń mú wa tẹ̀síwájú súnmọ́ àfojúsùn ìṣẹ́gun fún ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú tí wọ́n ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ nínú ìlànà-iṣẹ́ fún àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì 2021-2030.
translations.reference: WHO congratulates Benin and Mali for eliminating trachoma as a public health problem WHO has validated Benin and Mali as having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem, making them the fifth and sixth countries in WHO’s African Region to achieve this significant milestone. Countries that previously received WHO validation for trachoma elimination are Ghana (June 2018), Gambia (April 2021), Togo (May 2022) and Malawi (September 2022). “WHO congratulates the health authorities of Benin and Mali and their network of global and local partners for these milestones”, said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “Following Benin’s and Mali’s success, trachoma remains endemic in 23 countries in WHO’s African Region, bringing us a step closer towards the elimination target for trachoma set in the road map for neglected tropical diseases 2021–2030.”
translations.hypothesis:
WHO commends Benin and Mali for eliminating malaria.

The World Health Organization (WHO) has recognized Benin and Mali for eliminating malaria, a major public health achievement. This makes them two of the few countries to achieve this milestone, a significant step forward in the WHO's global fight against the disease.

Benin and Mali join Ghana (June 2018), The Gambia (May 2021), Togo (December 2022), and Malawi (March 2022) as countries that have been certified by WHO for malaria elimination.

"WHO commends the health authorities of Benin and Mali, and their partners, for these remarkable achievements," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO. "Despite the challenges, these countries have demonstrated that malaria can be eliminated, even in resource-limited settings."

While malaria remains a significant public health problem in 23 countries within the WHO African Region, the successes of Benin and Mali provide hope and inspiration for other countries working towards elimination. These achievements are part of the WHO's global strategy for malaria elimination, which aims to eliminate the disease in 35 countries by 2030.

translations.source: Ní àgbáyé, orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali darapọ̀ mọ́ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 15 mìíràn tí wọ́n ti gba ìdáláre láti ọ̀dọ WHO fún ìṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìṣòro ìlera àwujọ. Àwọn náà ni Cambodia, China, Gambia, Ghana, Islamic Republic of Iran, Lao People’s Democratic Republic, Malawi, Mexico, Morocco, Myanmar, Nepal, Oman, Saudi Arabia, Togo àti Vanuatu. Ìṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú pẹ̀lu lílo Ìlàna-iṣẹ́ SAFE WHO Àpapọ orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali ṣàmúlò Ìlàna-iṣẹ́ SAFE tí WHO dábàá láti ṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú pẹ̀lú àtìlẹyìn WHO àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀. Ìlàna-iṣẹ́ SAFE náà ni àkójọpọ̀ iṣẹ́-abẹ láti ṣe ìtọ́jú àwọn ìnira ìgbẹ̀yin àwọn ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú; oògùn apa kòkòro batéríà láti mú àkóràn kúrò; ìmọ́tótó ojú, àti àyípadà agbègbè, pàápàá jùlọ mímú àyípadà bá ànfààní sí omi àti ìmọ́tótó, láti mú àdínkù bá ìtànkálẹ̀.
translations.reference: Globally, Benin and Mali join 15 other countries that have been validated by WHO for having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem. These are Cambodia, China, the Gambia, Ghana, Islamic Republic of Iran, Lao People’s Democratic Republic, Malawi, Mexico, Morocco, Myanmar, Nepal, Oman, Saudi Arabia, Togo and Vanuatu. Trachoma elimination using WHO’s SAFE strategy Both Benin and Mali implemented the WHO-recommended SAFE strategy to eliminate trachoma with the support of WHO and partners. The SAFE strategy consists of surgery to treat late trachoma complications; antibiotics to clear infection; facial cleanliness; and environmental improvement, particularly improving access to water and sanitation, to reduce transmission.
translations.hypothesis:
Worldwide, Benin and Mali have joined 15 other countries that have received recognition from the WHO for their efforts in combating infectious diseases, including public health emergencies. These countries are: Cambodia, China, Gambia, Ghana, Islamic Republic of Iran, Lao People’s Democratic Republic, Malawi, Mexico, Morocco, Myanmar, Nepal, Oman, Saudi Arabia, Togo, and Vanuatu.

Achieving public health victories through the implementation of the WHO's SAFE strategy. Benin and Mali have adopted the WHO's SAFE strategy to combat infectious diseases with the support of the WHO and its partners. The SAFE strategy is a comprehensive approach that includes: prevention of infection through hygiene practices; treatment of existing infections with appropriate medications; and environmental sanitation, particularly focusing on improving water and sanitation infrastructure to reduce the spread of disease.

translations.source: Nípasẹ̀ Ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè, oògùn apa kòkòro batéríà azithromycin ni Pfizer máa ń gbékalẹ̀ fún àwọn ètò ìṣẹ́gun tí wọ́n ń ṣe àmúlò ìlànà-iṣẹ́ SAFE náà. Orílẹ̀-èdè Benin ti ṣe àmúlò àwọn ìdásí ìṣẹ́gùn ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú pẹ̀lú àwọn tí wọ́n ṣe àmúlò kojú àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì mìíràn (NTDs), lábẹ́ àbùradà Ètò Orílẹ̀-èdè fún Àwọn Ààrùn Àkóràn. Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ni NTD kẹ́ta tí wọ́n máa ṣẹ́gun ní orílẹ̀-èdè Benin, lẹ́yin sòbìà (ní ọdún 2009) àti ààrun sunrun-sunrun (ní ọdún 2021). Orílẹ̀-èdè Mali ti ṣe àwọn ìwádìí ipa àti ìtọpinpin ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ó sì ti ṣe àgbéjáde àwọn ìdásí láti ṣe àṣeyọrí àwọn àfojúsùn ìṣẹ́gun, pẹ̀lú àwọn ìpèníjà ààbò ní àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè àríwá orílẹ̀-èdè náà àti àwọn ìdàrúdápọ̀ òṣèlú-àwùjọ láti bíi ọdún díẹ̀ sẹ́yìn. Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ni NTD àkọ́kọ́ tí wọ́n máa ṣẹ́gun ní orílẹ̀-èdè Mali, tí ó ti wá darapọ̀ báyìí mọ́ àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àgbáyé fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 47 tí wọ́n ti ṣẹ́gun ó kéré jù NTD kan.
translations.reference: Through the International Trachoma Initiative, the antibiotic azithromycin is donated by Pfizer to elimination programmes implementing the SAFE strategy. Benin has integrated trachoma elimination interventions with those implemented against other neglected tropical diseases (NTDs), under the umbrella of the National Programme for Communicable Diseases. Trachoma is the third NTD to be eliminated in Benin, after dracunculiasis (in 2009) and gambiense human African trypanosomiasis (in 2021). Mali has conducted trachoma impact and surveillance surveys and rolled out interventions to achieve elimination targets, despite security challenges in the northern regions of the country and sociopolitical upheavals in recent years. Trachoma is the first NTD to be eliminated in Mali, which therefore now joins a global group of 47 countries that have eliminated at least one NTD.
translations.hypothesis: Through the Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTD) program, Pfizer is donating a large quantity of the azithromycin antibiotic to support control programs that utilize the SAFE strategy. The government of Benin has implemented comprehensive NTD control programs, focusing on the prevention and treatment of several other neglected tropical diseases, under the national program for neglected tropical diseases. River blindness is the third NTD to be eliminated in Benin, following lymphatic filariasis (in 2009) and onchocerciasis (in 2021). The government of Mali has conducted extensive mapping and prevalence surveys for NTDs and has developed strategies to ensure the sustainability of control efforts, including community engagement in the northern regions of the country and public-private partnerships to secure funding for several years. Soil-transmitted helminths are the first NTD to be eliminated in Mali, and the country has now joined a global network of 47 countries that have eliminated at least one NTD.

translations.source: "Àwọn wọ̀nyìí jẹ́ àwọn àṣeyọrí ìlera àwùjọ tó yanilẹ́nu," Dr Ibrahim Socé Fall, Olùdarí Ètò NTD Lágbàyé WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Orílẹ̀-èdè Benin àti Mali ṣe àpèjúwe bí èròngbà òṣèlú tó lágbára, ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ ẹlẹ́ka-jẹ̀ka, ìtọpinpin àti ìfọ̀rọ̀wérò àwùjọ lè ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti ṣe àṣeyọrí ìṣẹ́gun ààrùn." Ìtẹ̀síwájú tó lápẹẹrẹ ti di ṣíṣe nínú ìgbógun ti ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú láti bíi ọdún mélòó kan sẹ́yìn. Iye àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n nílò ìtọ́jú oògùn apa kòkòro batéríà fún ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ní Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ WHO ti dínkù pẹ̀lú 84 mílíọ̀nù, láti 189 mílíọ̀nù ní ọdún 2014 sí 105 mílíọ̀nù ní oṣù Òkudù ọdún 2022. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ fún olóòtú: Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ṣì dúró gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 41 (títí di oṣù Òkudù ọdún 2022) pẹ̀lú àpapọ 125 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn agbègbè tó nílò ìdásí kojú ààrùn náà.
translations.reference: “These are impressive public health achievements,” said Dr Ibrahima Socé Fall, Director of the WHO Global NTD Programme. “Benin and Mali demonstrate how strong political will, cross-sector integration, surveillance and community engagement can work in concert to achieve disease elimination.” Significant progress has been made in the fight against trachoma over the past few years. The number of people requiring antibiotic treatment for trachoma in the WHO African Region fell by 84 million, from 189 million in 2014 to 105 million as of June 2022. Note to the editor: Trachoma remains a public health problem in 41 countries (as of June 2022) with an estimated 125 million people living in areas requiring interventions against the disease.
translations.hypothesis: "These are remarkable achievements in public health," said Dr. Ibrahim Socé Fall, Director of the WHO's Global NTD Department. "The countries of Benin and Mali have demonstrated that strong political commitment, effective partnerships, and community engagement can work together to achieve success in the fight against disease." A recent example of this progress is the significant reduction in the number of people requiring treatment for lymphatic filariasis. The WHO estimates that the number of people who need preventive chemotherapy for lymphatic filariasis has decreased from 189 million in 2014 to 105 million as of October 2022. The good news continues: Lymphatic filariasis remains a major public health problem in 41 countries (as of October 2022), affecting an estimated 125 million people living in areas where the disease is endemic.

translations.source: A máa ń rí ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ní àwọn agbègbè tí wọ́n kúṣẹ̀ tí sì luko jùlọ ní Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀, Central àti South America, Asia, Western Pacific àti Middle East. Àwọn Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ WHO ni ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ṣàkóbá fún jùlọ pẹ̀lú 105 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn agbègbè tó kángun séwu, tí ó dúró fún 84% gbogbo ìṣòro ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ní àgbáyé. Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ni ni àkóràn tó ń fa ìfọ́jú tó ń léwájú jákèjádò àgbáyé. Àkóràn pẹ̀lú batéríà aṣojú ààrùn ìbálòpọ̀ ló ń fa ààrùn náà. Àkóràn máa ń ràn láti ara ẹnìkan sí ẹnìkan nípasẹ àwọn ìka tó ti dọ̀tí, agbé kòkòrò pamọ́ àti àwọn eṣinṣin tí wọ́n ti fi ara kínra pẹ̀lú oje láti ojú tàbí imú ẹni tí ó ti ní ààrùn náà.
translations.reference: Trachoma is found mainly in the poorest and most rural areas of Africa, Central and South America, Asia, the Western Pacific and the Middle East. The WHO African Region is disproportionately affected by trachoma with 105 million people living in at-risk areas, which represents 84% of the global trachoma burden. Trachoma is the leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide. The disease is caused by infection with the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis. Infection is transmitted from person to person through contaminated fingers, fomites and flies that have come into contact with discharge from the eyes or nose of an infected person.
translations.hypothesis: Malaria is prevalent in tropical and subtropical regions, with the highest incidence in Africa, Central and South America, Asia, the Western Pacific, and the Middle East. The World Health Organization (WHO) estimates that there are over 105 million cases of malaria annually, affecting people living in high-risk areas, which represents approximately 84% of all malaria cases worldwide. Malaria is a major public health problem and a leading cause of death globally. The disease is caused by parasites that are transmitted to humans through the bites of infected mosquitoes. These parasites multiply in the human body, causing a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, and headache. In severe cases, malaria can lead to organ failure, coma, and death.

translations.source: Àwọn aṣokùnfà ewu àwùjọ fún ìtànkálẹ̀ ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ni ìmọ́tótó tí kò dára, àwọn ilé elérò púpọ̀, àìní ànfààní tí ó tó sí omi tàbí lílo àwọn ohun èlò ìmọ́tótó tí ó tọ́. Ní ọdún 1996, WHO ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ Ìṣọ̀kan WHO fún Ìṣẹ́gun Ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú ní Àgbáyé ní ọdún 2020 (GET2020). Pẹ̀lú àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ tuntun nínú Ìṣọ̀kan náà, WHO ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ṣíṣe àmúlò ìlànà-iṣẹ́ SAFE ní orílẹ̀-èdè àti ìrólágbára ìkápá orílẹ̀-èdè nípasẹ àyẹ̀wo ajẹmọ́ àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn, àmójútó, ìtọpinpin , ìgbéléwọ̀n iṣẹ́-àkànṣe àti àkójọpọ̀ ohun èlò tó ń ṣe àfikún sí ìṣẹ́gun ẹlẹ́yọ̀ọ́-fọ́jú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìṣòro ìlera àwujọ. Ìlàna-iṣẹ́ náà, tí Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé ti ọdún 2020 fọwọ́sí náà nípasẹ̀ ìpinnu rẹ̀ 73(33), mú ọdún 2030 gẹ́gẹ́ bíi déétì àfojúsùn tuntun fún ìṣẹ́gùn àgbáyé.
translations.reference: Environmental risk factors for trachoma transmission include poor hygiene, overcrowded households, inadequate access to water or use of proper sanitation facilities. In 1996, WHO launched the WHO Alliance for the Global Elimination of Trachoma by the year 2020 (GET2020). With other partners in the Alliance, WHO supports country implementation of the SAFE strategy and strengthening of national capacity through epidemiological assessment, monitoring, surveillance, project evaluation and resource mobilization contributing towards elimination of trachoma as a public health problem. The road map, endorsed by the World Health Assembly in 2020 through its decision 73(33), sets 2030 as the new target date for global elimination.
translations.hypothesis: Public health interventions for neglected tropical diseases are often characterized by limited resources, inadequate infrastructure, and a lack of access to essential medicines or technologies. In 1996, the World Health Organization (WHO) launched the Global Programme to Eliminate Neglected Tropical Diseases (GNTD) with the goal of achieving elimination by 2020. Through partnerships and collaborations, the WHO supports the implementation of the SAFE strategy (Screening, Assessment, Follow-up, and Education) at the country level, strengthening national capacity through disease surveillance, prevention, treatment, and health promotion activities, and promoting the use of essential technologies to improve the control and elimination of neglected tropical diseases, including social marketing. This strategy, endorsed by the World Health Assembly in 2020 through resolution 73(33), sets a new target of achieving global elimination by 2030.

translations.source: 152 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìkókó ni wọ́n bí ṣíwájú àkókò láti bíi ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn Nínú gbogbo àwọn ìkókó 10 tí wọ́n bá bí, 1 jẹ́ síwájú àkókò - bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ní gbogbo 40 ìṣẹ́jú àáyá, 1 nínú àwọn ìkókó yẹn máa ń kú Iye ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ò tíì yí padà láti bíi ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn ní èyíkéyìí ẹkùn agbègbè ní àgbáyé Àwọn ipa rògbòdìyàn, àyípadà ojú ọjọ́, COVID-19 ń mú àlékún bá ewu fún àwọn obìnrin àti àwọn ìkókó níbi gbogbo Ìgbésẹ̀ ni a nílò lójú ẹsẹ̀ láti mú àyípadà bá ìdènà ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, papọ̀ pẹ̀lú ìtọ́jú tó dára fún àwọn ìkókó tí ọ̀rọ́ kàn àti àwọn ẹbí wọn
translations.reference: 152 million babies born preterm in the last decade Of every 10 babies born, 1 is preterm – and every 40 seconds, 1 of those babies dies Preterm birth rates have not changed in the past decade in any region of the world The impacts of conflict, climate change, COVID-19 are increasing risks for women and babies everywhere Action is needed urgently to improve the prevention of preterm birth, alongside better care for affected babies and their families
translations.hypothesis: Approximately 152 million babies are born each year. Of every 10 babies born, 1 is born prematurely – that is, before 37 weeks of gestation. In every 40 pregnancies, 1 results in a premature birth. The rate of premature births has not changed significantly in recent years. Premature births, along with stillbirths, are a major cause of infant mortality worldwide. Factors such as poverty, lack of access to healthcare, and COVID-19 pose risks to women and babies everywhere. Action is needed to reduce the rate of premature births, along with improved care for premature babies and their families.

translations.source: Àpapọ 13.4 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìkókó ni wọ́n bí ṣíwájú àkókò ní ọdún 2020, tí bíi 1 mílíọ̀nù sì kú látara ìnira ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìjábọ̀ tuntun kan tí àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé gbé jáde lónì. Èyí dọ́gba pẹ̀lú bíi 1 nínú àwọn ìkókó 10 tí wọ́n tètè bí (ṣíwájú ọ̀sẹ 37 ìlóyún) jákèjádò àgbáyé. Tí wọ́n tètè bí: ọdún mẹ́wàá iṣẹ́ lórí ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, tí Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé (WHO) àti Ìpèsè Owó fún Àwọn Ọmọdé ti Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé (UNICEF) gbé jáde pẹ̀lú PMNCH -- ìṣọ̀kan tó tóbi jùlọ ní àgbáyé fún àwọn obìnrin, àwọn ọmọde, àti àwọn ọ̀dọ́, ṣe ìkìlọ̀ lórí "pàjáwírì ìdákẹ́rọ́rọ́" fún ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, tí wọn ò fiyẹ̀sí-púpọ̀ tipẹ́ nínú ìwọ̀n àti agbára rẹ̀, tí ó ń ṣe ìdíwọ́ fún ìlọsíwájú nínú ìmú àyípadà bá ìlera àti ìwàláyé àwọn ọmọdé. Lára ìjábọ̀ náà ni a ti rí àwọn àkójọpọ̀ láti ọ̀dọ WHO àti UNICEF, tí wọ́n ṣe pẹ̀lú London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, lórí ọ̀nkà àpapọ ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìsan ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò.
