Lápapọ̀, ó ṣàwarí rẹ̀ pé iye ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ò tíì yí padà ní ẹkùn agbègbè kankan ní àgbáyé láti bíi ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn, pẹ̀lú 152 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìkókó tí wọ́n tètè bí tó kángun séwu láti ọdún 2010 sí 2020.
translations.reference: An estimated 13.4 million babies were born pre-term in 2020, with nearly 1 million dying from preterm complications, according to a new report released by United Nations agencies and partners today. This is equivalent to around 1 in 10 babies born early (before 37 weeks of pregnancy) worldwide. Born too soon: decade of action on preterm birth, produced by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF) together with PMNCH -- the world’s largest alliance for women, children, and adolescents, sounds the alarm on a “silent emergency” of preterm birth, long under-recognized in its scale and severity, which is impeding progress in improving children’s health and survival. The report includes updated estimates from WHO and UNICEF, prepared with the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, on the prevalence of preterm births. Overall, it finds that preterm birth rates have not changed in any region in the world in the past decade, with 152 million vulnerable babies born too soon from 2010 to 2020.
translations.hypothesis: In 2020, an estimated 13.4 million babies were born prematurely, and approximately 1 million died due to preterm complications, according to a new report released today by leading global health organizations and partners. This represents about 1 in 10 babies born worldwide, with a gestational age of less than 37 weeks. The report, a collaborative effort by the World Health Organization (WHO), the United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF), and PMNCH – the world's largest alliance for women, children, and adolescents – highlights the "silent epidemic" of preterm birth, which is a major contributor to stillbirths and child mortality, hindering progress in reducing maternal and child health outcomes. The report includes data from WHO and UNICEF, in collaboration with the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, on the global burden of preterm birth. Overall, the report reveals that the number of preterm births has not declined in any region of the world in the past decade, with 152 million babies born prematurely between 2010 and 2020.

translations.source: Ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ti di aṣokùnfà ikú àwọn ọmọdé tó ń léwájú, tó dúró fún 1 nínú 5 lé gbogbo ikú àwọn ọmọdé tó ń ṣẹlẹ̀ ṣíwájú ọjọ́-ìbí 5th. Àwọn alájàbọ́ ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò lè kojú àwọn ìpadàbọ̀ ìlera títí ayérayé, pẹ̀lú àlékún nínú ìpèníjà ara àti àwọn ìdíwọ́ ìdàgbàsókè tó ṣe é ṣe. Àwọn àlàfo àjàbọ́ nípasẹ̀ ẹkùn agbègbè, ìpawówọlé, ìran Gígùn lórí ìjábọ̀ tó lápẹẹrẹ lórí àkòrí-ọ̀rọ̀ náà ní ọdún 2012, ìjábọ̀ "ọlọ́dún mẹ́wàá" Ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò tuntun yìí pèsè ìwòye tó kún nípa ọ̀nkà àpapọ ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìsan ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò àti ipa rẹ̀ tó lápẹẹrẹ lára àwọn obìnrin, àwọn ẹbí, àwọn àwùjọ àti àwọn ọrọ̀-ajé. Ní ọ̀pọ̀ ìgbà, ibi tí wọ́n bí àwọn ìkókó sí máa ń sọ bóyá wọn ó ja àjàbọ́.
translations.reference: Preterm birth is now the leading cause of child deaths, accounting for more than 1 in 5 of all deaths of children occurring before their 5th birthday. Preterm survivors can face lifelong health consequences, with an increased likelihood of disability and developmental delays. Survival gaps by region, income, race Building from a landmark report on the topic in 2012, this new Born too soon “decade” report provides a comprehensive overview of the prevalence of preterm birth and its profound impact on women, families, societies and economies. Too often, where babies are born determines if they survive.
translations.hypothesis: Prenatal mortality, which refers to the death of a fetus after 20 weeks of gestation, accounts for 1 in 5 of all stillbirths occurring before the 28th week of pregnancy. Factors contributing to early prenatal mortality can be complex and range from underlying maternal health conditions to socioeconomic factors. Research efforts, including studies conducted by local organizations and the publication of reports like the "Ten-Year Report" in 2012, have shed light on the prevalence of early prenatal mortality and its impact on women, families, communities, and the economy. In many cases, the circumstances surrounding these losses are investigated.

translations.source: Ìjábọ̀ náà sọ wí pé 1 nínú 10 nìkan nínú àwọn ìkókó oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò tó lágbára (<28 ọ̀sẹ̀) máa ń ja àjàbọ́ ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré, yàtọ̀ sí 9 nínú 10 lé ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn pọ̀. Àwọn àlàfo àìṣedéédé tó jọ mọ́ ìran, ẹ̀yà, ìpawówọlé, àti ànfààní sí ìlera ojúlówó máa ń sọ ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, ikú, àti ìpèníjà ara tó ṣe é ṣe, kódà ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn pọ̀. Southern Asia àti ìwọ̀-oọ̀rùn ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ ni wọ́n ní iye ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò tó pọ̀ jùlọ, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ìkókó ṣíwájú àkókò ìbí ní àwọn agbègbè wọ̀nyìí ń kojú ewu ikú tó pọ̀ jùlọ. Lápapọ̀, àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè méjì wọ̀nyìí dúró fún 65% lé àwọn ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò káàkiri àgbáyé. Ìjábọ̀ náà tún ṣàfihàn pé àwọn ipa rògbòdìyàn, àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ àti ìbàjẹ́ àwùjọ, COVID-19, àti àlékún nínú owó iná ń mú àlékún bá ewu fún àwọn obìnrin àti àwọn ìkókó níbi gbogbo.
translations.reference: The report notes that only 1 in 10 extremely preterm babies (<28 weeks) survive in low-income countries, compared to more than 9 in 10 in high-income countries. Gaping inequalities related to race, ethnicity, income, and access to quality care determine the likelihood of preterm birth, death, and disability, even in high-income countries. Southern Asia and sub-Saharan Africa have the highest rates of preterm birth, and preterm babies in these regions face the highest mortality risk. Together, these two regions account for more than 65% of preterm births globally. The report also highlights that the impacts of conflict, climate change and environmental damage, COVID-19, and rising living costs are increasing risks for women and babies everywhere.
translations.hypothesis: The report indicates that 1 in 10 pregnancies in low-income countries end in stillbirth, compared to 9 in 10 in high-income countries. Factors such as inadequate nutrition, poor maternal health, limited access to quality healthcare, and complications during pregnancy and childbirth contribute to stillbirths, even in countries with higher incomes. South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa have the highest rates of stillbirth, and these early pregnancy losses are associated with a greater risk of maternal mortality. Together, these two regions account for 65% of all stillbirths worldwide. The report also highlights that factors such as domestic violence, teenage pregnancy, substance abuse, COVID-19, and lack of access to essential medicines pose significant risks to women and newborns globally.

translations.source: Fún àpẹẹrẹ, bíba afẹ́fẹ́ jẹ́ ni wọ́n ní ó ń ṣokùnfà 6 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan. Bíi 1 nínú 10 àwọn ìkókó oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ni wọ́n bí ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 10 tí wọ́n lẹ jùlọ tí rògbòdìyàn ọmọnìyàn ní ṣẹlẹ̀ níbẹ̀, gẹ́gẹ́ bí àlàyé tuntun kan nínú ìjábọ̀ náà. Àwọn ewu ìlera ìyá, bíi ìlóyún ọ̀dọ́ àti ẹ̀jẹ̀-ríru, ni wọ́n tún ní àjọṣepọ̀ tó gúnmọ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò. Ẹ̀yí tọ́ka sí ìdí láti ṣe àrídájú ànfààní sí àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìbímọ, tó fi mọ́ ìfètòsọ́mọ-bíbí tó ń ṣiṣẹ́, pẹ̀lú ìtọ́jú ojúlówó-ńlá nínú oyún àti ní àkókò ìbí. Ètò fún iṣẹ́: àlékún ìdókówò orílẹ̀-èdè àti ìpolongo tí àwọn òbí léwájú rẹ̀
translations.reference: For example, air pollution is estimated to contribute to 6 million preterm births each year. Nearly 1 in 10 preterm babies are born in the 10 most fragile countries affected by humanitarian crises, according to a new analysis in the report. Maternal health risks, such as adolescent pregnancy and pre-eclampsia, are closely linked to preterm births. This underlines the need to ensure access to sexual and reproductive health services, including effective family planning, with high-quality care in pregnancy and around the time of birth. Agenda for action: more country investment and parent-led activism
translations.hypothesis: For example, it is estimated that approximately 6 million unintended pregnancies occur each year. One in ten pregnancies are unintended, with the majority occurring in the ten countries with the highest rates of maternal mortality, highlighting a critical need for improved access to family planning and reproductive health services, including contraception and safe abortion. Maternal health risks, such as postpartum hemorrhage and infections, are also strongly linked to unintended pregnancies. This underscores the importance of strengthening comprehensive healthcare services and promoting evidence-based family planning programs, including access to contraception and safe abortion services. Policy recommendations: strengthening national family planning policies and programs, and empowering women to make informed choices about their reproductive health.

translations.source: Ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn náà tún ti rí ìdagbàsókè nínú ìpolongo àwùjọ lórí ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò àti ìdènà àbíkú, tí àjọṣepọ̀ àwọn òbí, àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ìlera, àwọn ilé-ẹ̀kọ́, àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ, àti àwọn mìíràn ń léwájú rẹ̀. Jákèjádò àgbáńlá ayé, àwọn ẹgbẹ́ fún àwọn ẹbí oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò tí ọ̀rọ́ kàn ti ń léwájú tí wọ́n ń fún ìpèsè fún ànfààní sí ìtọ́jú tó dára àti àyípadà òfin àti àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ẹbí mìíràn. Ní ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún Àpérò Ìlera ọmọtuntun àti Ìyá, ní Cape Town, ní orílẹ̀-èdè South Africa láti 8-11 oṣù Èbìbí, WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA àti PMNCH ń pè fún àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ wọ̀nyìí láti mú àyípadà bá ìtọ́jú fún àwọn obìnrin àti àwọn ọmọtuntun àti láti dènà àwọn ewu látara àwọn ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò: Àlékún àwọn ìdókówò: Ṣíṣe àkójọ àwọn ohun èlò ilẹ̀-òkèèrè àti lábẹ́lé láti ṣàmúlò ìlera ìyá àti ọmọ tuntun, ṣíṣe àrídájú pé ojúlówó ìtọ́jú ńlá di ṣíṣe ní ìgbà àti ibi tí wọ́n bá ti nílò rẹ̀. Ìṣàmúlò kíákíá: Ṣíṣe àwọn àfojúsùn orílẹ̀-èdè fún ìtẹ̀síwájú nípasẹ ìṣàmúlò àwọn òfin orílẹ̀-èdè tó ti wà tẹ́lẹ̀ fún ìtọ́jú ìyá àti ọmọ tuntun.
translations.reference: The past decade has also seen a growth of community activism on preterm birth and stillbirth prevention, driven by networks of parents, health professionals, academia, civil society, and others. Throughout the world, groups for affected families of preterm birth have been at the forefront of advocating for access to better care and policy change and supporting other families. Leading up to the International Maternal Newborn Health Conference, in Cape Town, South Africa from 8-11 May, WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA and PMNCH are calling for the following actions to improve care for women and newborns and mitigate risks from preterm births: Increased investments: Mobilizing international and domestic resources to optimize maternal and newborn health, ensuring high quality care is provided when and where it is needed. Accelerated implementation: Meeting country targets for progress through implementing established national policies for maternal and newborn care.
translations.hypothesis: The past decade has seen significant progress in the social agenda related to adolescent pregnancy and maternal mortality, with partnerships between governments, healthcare providers, schools, community organizations, and others driving this change. Globally, organizations supporting adolescent mothers are working to provide resources and support systems that promote healthy development and ensure the well-being of both the mother and her child. At the recent Adolescent Health and Youth Development Conference in Cape Town, South Africa, from February 8-11, the WHO, UNICEF, UNFPA, and PMNCH called for continued efforts to improve the health and well-being of adolescent girls and women and to reduce the risks associated with adolescent pregnancy. Key recommendations included: Expanding access: Increasing the availability of essential health services and supplies for mothers and newborns, ensuring that quality care is accessible when and where it is needed. Strengthening policies: Implementing national policies that promote adolescent health and well-being by building on existing national policies for maternal and child health.

translations.source: Àfikún yíká àwọn ẹ̀ka: Ṣíṣe àtìlẹyìn fún ètò-ẹ̀kọ́ nípasẹ ìṣẹ̀mí náà; ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ìdókówò ọrọ̀-ajé tó já fáfá, pẹ̀lú ìpèsè-owó-àjọṣe káàkiri àwọn ẹ̀ka; ríró àwọn ìdáhùn ìfaradà ojú ọjọ́ lágbára kárí ìgbésí-ayé; àti mímú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá ìṣàkóso àti ìfaradà àwọn èto pàjáwírì. Ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun tó ń wáyé lábẹ́lé: Ìdókówò nínú àwọn ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun àti ìwádìí tó ń wáyé lábẹ́lé láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn àyípadà nínú ìjẹ́ ojúlówó ìtọ́jú àti ìdọ́gba nínú ànfààní. Àgbàsọ: Prof. Joy Lawn, Ile-ẹ̀kọ́ fún Ìmọ́tótó àti Ìmọ̀-Ìṣègùn ti London Aṣíwájú-àjọṣe, Ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò: ọdún mẹ́wàá iṣẹ́ lórí ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò (2023) àti Ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò (2012) "Ìjábọ̀ tuntun yìí ṣe àfihàn pé èrè àìgbé ìgbésẹ̀ láti bíi ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn ni 152 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìkókó oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò.
translations.reference: Integration across sectors: Promoting education through the life cycle; supporting smarter economic investments, with co-financing across sectors; strengthening climate adaptation responses across the life-course; and advancing the coordination and resilience of emergency systems. Locally driven innovation: Investing in locally led innovation and research to support improvements in quality of care and equity in access. Quotes: Prof. Joy Lawn, London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine Co-lead, Born too soon: decade of action on preterm birth (2023) and Born too soon (2012) "This new report shows that the cost of inaction over the last decade was 152 million babies born too soon.
translations.hypothesis: Here are the key takeaways: Advocate for education through innovation; support sustainable economic development, including investments in various sectors; promote strong family planning and reproductive health services; and integrate advancements in control and prevention of infectious diseases. Emerging trends: Innovations in maternal and child health are driving improvements in reproductive health outcomes and reducing disparities in access to care. Expert: Prof. Joy Lawn, London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine. Key publication: "The new global landscape of family planning," published in 2023 and "Family planning: global, regional, and country data," published in 2012. This new report reveals that the unmet need for modern contraception among women of reproductive age is 152 million.

translations.source: Nígbà tí ó kan àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè kan púpọ̀, ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ń dúkokò mọ́ ìlọsíwájú ìlera ní gbogbo orílẹ̀-èdè. Ìdókòwò tó pọ̀ nínú ìtọ́jú fún àwọn ọmọtuntun akángun séwu lè dóólà mílíọ̀nù gbé mílíọ̀nù àwọn ẹbí níbi ìbanilọ́kànjẹ́ náà. Ó tún nílò ọ̀pọ iṣẹ́ láti dènà ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò, tí yóò tún mú àyípadà bá ìlọsíwájú nínú mímú àdínkù bá àbíkú àti ikú ìyá. Lápapọ̀ àwọn ìtọpinpin oníbejì fún ìdènà ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò àti ìtọ́jú máa pèsè àwọn ènìyàn àti àwọn àwùjọ tí wọ́n ní àlàáfíà láti ṣiṣẹ́ lórí ìdàgbàsókè ọrọ̀-ajé àti àwùjọ. Àwọn ìran wa tó ń bọ̀ gbáralé kí gbogbo wa gbé ìgbésẹ̀ báyìí -- ìdókówò náà lè má kéré ṣùgbọ́n àwọn ère lórí ìdókówò yìí máa ṣe pàtàkì fún gbogbo orílẹ̀-èdè "
translations.reference: While some regions are more affected, preterm birth threatens health progress in every country. Greater investment in the care of vulnerable newborns can save millions of families from heartbreak. More work is also needed to prevent preterm birth, which will also improve progress in reducing stillbirths and maternal deaths. Together these twin tracks of preterm prevention and care will produce healthier individuals and societies to deliver on economic and social development. Our next generation depends on us all acting now -- the investment may not be small but the returns on this investment will be major for every country"
translations.hypothesis: Globally, declining fertility rates are posing a significant challenge to economic development. The potential for a large population decline could have devastating consequences for families and communities. It also requires significant policy interventions to address the causes of declining fertility, which could include increased investment in childcare and support for families. Overall, the implications of declining fertility rates are complex and far-reaching, and they will require careful consideration by policymakers and societies around the world. Our current trajectory is concerning – while the numbers may seem small, the implications of these trends are crucial for every nation.

translations.source: Dr Nahya Salim, Tanzania: ònkọ̀wé-àjọṣe Born Too Soon àti adarí orílẹ̀-èdè fún Newborn Essential Solutions and Technologies 360 (NEST360) "Kò wá sí àwíjàre báyìí fún idákẹ́ rọ́rọ́. A ti wá ní àwọn ohun èlò àti ìmọ̀ lọ́wọ́ wa báyìí láti mú àyípadà àwọn àbájáde fún àwọn ọmọtuntun wa tí wọ́n kángun séwu jùlọ. Ìwúrí ló jẹ́ fún mi láti rí ìjọba mi tó ń dókówò báyìí tó sì ní àfọkànsìn sí ìṣàmúlò rẹ̀ káàkiri kódà àwọn agbègbè ìgbèríko. Ọdún mẹ́wàá tó ń bọ̀ lè ó sì gbọdọ̀ yàtọ̀ fún àwọn tó ń kojú ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò níbí àti ní ibi gbogbo."
translations.reference: Dr Nahya Salim, Tanzania: Born Too Soon co-author and national lead of Newborn Essential Solutions and Technologies 360 (NEST360) “There is now no excuse for silence. We now have in our hands the devices and the knowledge to change outcomes for our most vulnerable newborns. I am proud to see my government now investing and committed to implementation across even rural areas. The next decade can and must be different for those facing preterm birth here and everywhere.”
translations.hypothesis: Dr. Nahya Salim, Tanzania: Author of "Born Too Soon" and Country Director for Newborn Essential Solutions and Technologies 360 (NEST360). "I am deeply saddened by this news. We have come so far with our tools and knowledge to improve the survival rates of premature babies. It is disheartening to see my country, which has made significant progress in its commitment to reducing child mortality, even in rural areas, facing this setback. The next ten years must be a period of renewed focus and action for those working to prevent preterm birth everywhere."

translations.source: Dr Anshu Banerjee, Olùdarí fún Ìlera Ìyá, Ọmọtuntun, Ọmọ àti Ọ̀dọ́ àti Arúgbó ní WHO "Ṣe àrídájú ìtọ́jú tó jẹ́ ojúlówó fún àwọn ìkókó kékeré, tí wọ́n kángun séwu jùlọ wọ̀nyìí àti àwọn ẹbí wọn ṣe pàtàkì púpọ̀ fún mímú àyípadà bá ìlera ọmọ àti àjàbọ́. A tún nílò ìlọsíwájú láti báni dènà àwọn ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò -- èyí túnmọ̀ sí wí pé gbogbo obìnrin gbọ́dọ̀ lè ní ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera ojúlówó ṣíwájú àti ní àkókò ìlóyún láti mọ̀ àti láti mójútó àwọn ewu." Steven Lauwerier, Olùdarí Ìlera (a.i.), UNICEF "Lẹ́yìn gbogbo ikú ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ni ipa ìpàdánù àti ìbànújẹ́.
translations.reference: Dr Anshu Banerjee, Director for Maternal, Newborn, Child and Adolescent Health and Ageing at WHO “Ensuring quality care for these tiniest, most vulnerable babies and their families is absolutely imperative for improving child health and survival. Progress is also needed to help prevent preterm births -- this means every woman must be able to access quality health services before and during pregnancy to identify and manage risks.” Steven Lauwerier, Director of Health (a.i.), UNICEF “After every preterm death is a trail of loss and heartbreak.
translations.hypothesis:
Dr. Anshu Banerjee, Head of Maternal, Newborn, Child and Adolescent Health and Ageing at the WHO, stated, "The increased risk of complications for young mothers and their babies is a major concern for maternal and child health. We also need to focus on improving access to quality healthcare services for young mothers, ensuring that all women have access to comprehensive reproductive health services before, during, and after pregnancy to prevent and manage risks."

Steven Lauwerier, Director of Health, UNICEF, added, "Every early pregnancy has consequences and creates vulnerabilities."

translations.source: Pẹ̀lú gbogbo ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ itẹ̀síwájú tí gbogbo ayé ti ṣe láti bíi ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn, a ò tíì ní ìlọsíwájú kankan láti mú àdínkù bá iye àwọn ọmọ kékèké tí wọ́n bí ṣíwájú àkókò tàbí ìgbékúrò ewu ikú wọn. Ònkà náà burú jáì. Àkókò ti tó fún wa láti mú àyípadà bá ànfààní sí ìtọ́jú fún àwọn ìyá olóyún àti àwọn ìkókó oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò àti láti rí i dájú pé gbogbo ọmọ fi ìlera bẹ̀rẹ̀ kí wọ́n sì tayọ ní ayé. Helga Fogstad, Olùdarí Aláṣẹ, PMNCH "Ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò tọ́ka sí ìdí tí a fi gbọ́dọ̀ mú àlékún bá ìdókówò àti ìṣirò fún ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò - aṣokùnfa ikú àwọn ọmọ tó wà lábẹ́ ọdún márùn-ún tó tóbi jùlọ ní àgbáyé.
translations.reference: Despite the many advances the world has made in the past decade, we have made no progress in reducing the number of small babies born too soon or averting the risk of their death. The toll is devasting. It’s time we improve access to care for pregnant mothers and preterm infants and ensure every child gets a healthy start and thrives in life.” Helga Fogstad, Executive Director, PMNCH “Born too soon underlines why we must increase investment and accountability for preterm birth – the world’s largest cause of death of children under the age of five.
translations.hypothesis: Despite all the progress the world has made in the past few decades, we have not made enough progress in reducing the number of stillbirths or the risk of death for newborns. This is a tragedy. It is time for us to prioritize maternal and newborn health, and to ensure that all children have a healthy start to life. Helga Fogstad, Director General, PMNCH. "Preventing stillbirths is a key reason why we must invest in maternal and newborn health – it is the leading cause of death for babies under one year old worldwide."

translations.source: Ìlọsíwájú ti ń lọlẹ̀ fún ìlera ìyá àti ọmọtuntun, bákan náà ni ìdènà àbíkú, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n ti ń tì í sẹ́yìn lóhùn nípasẹ àpapọ COVID-19, àyípadà ojú ọjọ́, àwọn rògbòdìyàn tó ń fẹjú sí i àti àlékún nínú òwó ìná tó burú jáì. "Pẹ̀lú ìṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ ní àjọṣepọ̀ - àwọn ìjọba, àwọn onínúdídùn-ọlọ́rẹ, ẹ̀ka aládàni, àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ, àwọn òbí, àti àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ìlera ", kí a sì mú àwọn ìgbìyànjú ìdèna ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò àti ìtọ́jú wá sí gbangba ìlera orílẹ̀-èdè àti àwọn ìgbìyànjú ìdàgbàsókè, ṣíṣe ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ fún àwọn ènìyàn pẹ̀lú ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ẹbí, àwọn àwùjọ àti àwọn ọrọ̀-ajé níbi gbogbo. ”Gabriela àti Jerome Foster, Las Vegas, United States: àwọn òbí Jalen Foster, àti olùdásílẹ̀ Jalen’s Gift Foundation "A dá àwùjọ wa sílẹ̀ lẹ́yìn tí a pàdánù ọmọkùnrin wa, Jalen Foster, tí ó jẹ́ àbíkú oní ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò ní 27 ọ̀sẹ̀ nítorí ìjẹyọ batéríà ṣíwájú ìbí kan tí a mọ̀ sí streptococcus irọ̀ B. Pípàdánù ọmọkùnrin wa burú jáì, bẹ́ẹ̀ kò sí àtìlẹyìn kankan nínú àwùjọ náà nígbà náà ni a pinnu láti pèsè ààyè ìtùnú àti ìrètí fún àwọn ẹbí tí wọ́n ti pàdánù ọmọ.
translations.reference: Progress is flatlining for maternal and newborn health, as well as the prevention of stillbirths, and is now pushed back further through the devastating combination of COVID-19, climate change, expanding conflicts and rising living costs. “By working together in partnership – governments, donors, the private sector, civil society, parents, and health professionals – we can sound the alarm about this “silent emergency”, and bring preterm prevention and care efforts to the forefront of national health and development efforts, building human capital by supporting families, societies and economies everywhere. ”Gabriela and Jerome Foster, Las Vegas, United States: parents of Jalen Foster, and founders of Jalen’s Gift Foundation “Our foundation was founded after the loss of our son, Jalen Foster, who was a preterm stillbirth at 27 weeks due to prenatal onset of a bacteria known as group B streptococcus. Losing our son was devastating, and there was no support in the community and that’s when we decided to provide a place of comfort and hope for families who have lost a baby.
translations.hypothesis: The alarming rise in maternal and infant mortality, exacerbated by the COVID-19 pandemic, increased healthcare disparities, and economic hardship, demands urgent action. "Through collaborative efforts involving governments, philanthropic organizations, healthcare providers, community groups, businesses, and individuals, we can accelerate progress towards achieving national and global health goals, providing equitable access to care, and offering support to families, communities, and economies worldwide." - Gabriela and Jerome Foster, Las Vegas, United States: parents of Jalen Foster, and founders of Jalen's Gift Foundation. "Our foundation was established after the tragic loss of our son, Jalen Foster, who died at 27 weeks gestation due to a preventable Group B Streptococcus infection. The devastating experience of losing our son led us to create a foundation dedicated to providing hope and support to families who have experienced the loss of a child."

translations.source: A kò fẹ́ kí àwọn ẹbí mìíràn ní ìmọ̀lára gẹ́gẹ́ bí a ṣe ní i - láwa nìkan. Torí náà, a bẹ̀rẹ Jalen’s Gift Foundation láti pèsè ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn ẹbí tókù tí wọ́n ń la ìpàdánù kọjá, láti ṣe ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ọ̀fẹ́ lóríṣiríṣi, bíi fọ́tò, àtìlẹ́yìn nínú ìbànújẹ àti ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìrántí." ***Ṣe ìgbàsílẹ̀ àwọn ohun èlò ìròyìn Ìbí ṣíwájú àkókò
translations.reference: We didn’t want other families to feel like we did – alone. So, we started Jalen’s Gift Foundation to help other families going through a loss, to provide multiple free services, such as photograph, grief support and help towards memorial services.” ***Download the Born too soon media resources
translations.hypothesis: We didn't want other families to experience the same profound grief – the emptiness. That's why we started the Jalen's Gift Foundation to provide support for families who have lost a child, offering various services such as photography, grief counseling, and assistance with memorial services. ***Forward-looking media coverage of the foundation.

translations.source: UAE àti WHO ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ọkọ̀ òfurufú fún ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn oògùn pàtàkì sí orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan Orílẹ̀-èdè United Arab Emirates (UAE) àti Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ 30 tọ́ọ́nù àwọn oògùn pàtàkì sí orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan lónì. Ọkọ̀ òfurufú kan tó gbé àwọn oògùn fún ìtọ́jú ìpalára, àwọn iṣẹ́-abẹ pàjáwírì, àti àwọn oògùn pàtàkì dé sí Port Sudan Airport ní àárọ̀ yìí. Ẹrù náà, tí owó rẹ̀ tó US$ 444 000, ni àkọ́kọ́ tí WHO tí WHO rí fi ránṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú ọkọ̀ òfurufú láti ìgbà tí rògbòdìyàn náà ti bẹ́ sílẹ̀. WHO pín àwọn oògùn fún àwọn ilé ìwòsàn ṣíwájú kí rògbòdìyàn náà tó búrẹ́kẹ́.
translations.reference: UAE and WHO deliver air lift of critical medical supplies to Sudan The United Arab Emirates (UAE) and the World Health Organization have delivered 30 tons of urgent medical supplies to Sudan today. A plane carrying supplies for injury treatment, emergency surgeries, and essential drugs arrived in Port Sudan Airport early this morning. The shipment, valued at US$ 444 000, is the first that WHO has been able to deliver by air to Sudan since the outbreak of the conflict. WHO distributed supplies to heath facilities prior to the escalation of conflict.
translations.hypothesis: The UAE and WHO have dispatched a cargo plane carrying essential medical supplies to Sudan. Today, the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and the World Health Organization (WHO) sent a plane carrying 30 tons of essential medical supplies to the country of Sudan. The cargo plane, which carried medications for treating injuries, emergency supplies, and other vital medical items, arrived at Port Sudan Airport earlier today. The shipment, valued at US$444,000, is the first to be delivered by air since the conflict began. The WHO has been distributing the medical supplies to hospitals in advance of the arrival of the plane.

translations.source: Wọ́n lo èyí tán lẹ́yìn ọjọ́ díẹ̀ nítorí iye àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n farapa. Ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn ọkọ̀ òfurufú náà wáyé gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìtẹ̀síwájú ọ̀kan lára àwọn ìgbìyànjú ìdẹ̀run UAE láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ọmọ orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan àti láti ṣàfihàn àjọṣepọ̀ tó gúnmọ́ pẹ̀lú orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan, pàápàá jùlọ èyí tó níṣe pẹ̀lú ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tí orílẹ̀-èdè náà ń kojú lọ́wọ́lọ́wọ́. Ìdẹ̀rùn náà tún jẹ́ ìtẹ̀síwájú àfojúsùn ọmọnìyàn UAE ó sì ṣe àfihàn àjọṣepọ̀ rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè mìíràn tó dá lórí àjọṣepọ̀ ọmọnìyàn àti ìfọkànsìn rẹ̀ láti pèsè ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn mìíràn ní àkókò rògbòdìyàn àti ìṣẹ̀lẹ pàjáwírì. Dr Reem bint Ebrahim Al Hashimy, Mínísítà fún Orílẹ̀-èdè fún Ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè, sọ wí pé UAE "ń tẹ̀síwájú láti ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé láti rí i dájú pé àṣeyọrí àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn ohun èlò pàtàkì láti fi ìpèsè òògùn àti oúnjẹ pàjáwírì ránṣẹ́ láti lè báni kojú rògbòdìyàn tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ ní orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan. Ní ìbámu pẹ̀lú àwọn ìpè fún gbogbo igun tó wà nínú rògbòdìyàn tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ náà láti tètè dáwọ́ ìgbóguntì dúró àti láti mú àdínkù bá ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tó ń fa ìjìyà tí ò ṣe é fẹnu sọ fún àwọn ọmọ orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan, UAE dúró lórí ìfọkànsìn rẹ̀ láti pèsè ìrànwọ́ àti ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ní àkókò tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀.
translations.reference: These were exhausted after a few days given the number of injured. The dispatch of the aircraft comes as part of the UAE's continuous relief efforts in support of the Sudanese people and reflects its deep relations with Sudan, particularly in light of the current situation the country is facing. The relief is also an extension of the UAE’s humanitarian vision and underscores its relations with other countries based on human fraternity and its commitment to assisting others during crises and emergency situations. Dr Reem bint Ebrahim Al Hashimy, Minister of State for International Cooperation, said that the UAE "continues to work closely alongside the World Health Organization to ensure the successful deployment of the critical logistics operations to send urgent medical and food aid to help address the current crisis in Sudan. In parallel with calls for all sides of the current conflict to immediately cease hostilities and de-escalate a situation that is causing untold suffering for the Sudanese people, the UAE is steadfast in its commitment to providing aid and assistance to countries in times of need.
translations.hypothesis: This initiative was launched just a few days ago in response to the urgent humanitarian needs. It is a joint effort by one of the leading humanitarian organizations in the UAE to provide assistance to the people of Sudan and to strengthen the partnership between the UAE and Sudan, especially in light of the ongoing crisis in the country. The initiative also reflects the UAE's commitment to humanitarian aid and its dedication to working with other countries to provide support to those in need during times of crisis and disaster. Dr. Reem bint Ibrahim Al Hashemy, Minister of State for International Cooperation, stated that the UAE "is committed to working closely with the World Health Organization to ensure the successful delivery of essential medical supplies and food aid to those affected by the crisis in Sudan." In addition to providing financial assistance, the UAE is also providing logistical support to help transport the aid to those who need it most. The UAE is also working with local organizations in Sudan to ensure that the aid is distributed fairly and efficiently. The UAE's commitment to providing assistance to Sudan is a testament to its dedication to humanitarian aid and its willingness to help those in need, regardless of their location or circumstances.

translations.source: UAE ní pàtàkì jùlọ gbájúmọ́ ìpèsè fún àwọn ẹgbẹ́ tí wọ́n kángun séwu jùlọ tí ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ní orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan ṣàkóbá fún jùlọ, pàápàá jùlọ àwọn aláìsàn, àwọn ọmọdé, àwọn àgbàlagbà, àwọn obìnrin tí wọ́n kángun séwu jùlọ látara rògbòdìyàn tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ tó banilọ́kànjẹ́ náà - bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ọkọ̀ òfurufú ìdẹ̀rùn yìí máa kojú àwọn àlàfo tó ṣe pàtàkì jùlọ nínú ìpèsè oògùn àti oúnjẹ. Iyì ọmọnìyàn tí UAE mú ní ọ̀kùkúdùn túmọ̀ sí wí pé ó ti rí i dájú pé ó ti tẹ̀síwájú láti bá gbogbo àgbáyé sọ̀rọ̀ nípa ìfọkànsìn tó lágbára tí ò sì dúró láti ṣe ìrólágbára fún àlàáfíà, ààbò àti ìdúróṣinṣin, tí kìí ṣe ní ẹkùn agbègbè nìkan, ṣùgbọ́n jákèjádò àgbáyé, papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ rẹ̀ àti àwọn àwùjọ àgbáyé, orílẹ̀-èdè náà ń tẹ̀síwájú láti ran àwọn ọmọ orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan lọ́wọ́ ní àkókò rògbòdìyàn." Àwọn ohun èlò ìlera náà ni àwọn ohun èlò egbò, ohun èlò iṣẹ́-abẹ pàjáwírì tí ó tó, àti àwọn oògùn pàtàkì láti tètè dé ọ̀dọ 165 000 àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n nílò ìrànwọ́ ọmọnìyàn gidi gan-an. Àwọn ilé ìwòsàn jákèjádò orílẹ̀-èdè náà jábọ̀ wí pé àwọn ò ní àwọn ohun èlò ìlera pàtàkì mọ́ àtiwípé àwọn ò ní ànfààní sí àwọn ààyè ìkóògùn pamọ́ ní orílẹ̀-èdè náà mọ́ nítorí ìṣòro ààbò. Pẹ̀lú àwọn àtìlẹyìn mìíràn, WHO ń fi àwọn ohun èlo pàjáwírì méjì ránṣẹ́ tí yóò lọ pẹ̀lú ọkọ̀-akẹ́rù náà láti rí i dájú pé wọ́n pín àwọn ohun èlò wọ̀nyìí sí àwọn ilé ìwòsàn pàtàkì 13 lójú ẹsẹ̀ láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera àti láti mú ìtọ́jú dé ọ̀dọ̀ àwọn tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀.
translations.reference: The UAE is particularly focused on providing for the most vulnerable groups affected by the situation in Sudan, especially the sick, children, the elderly, and women who are most at risk from the ongoing concerning conflict – and these relief flights will directly address the most pressing gaps in medical and food provision. The UAE's deeply held humanitarian values have meant that it has ensured it has continued to communicate to the world its robust and unrelenting dedication to strengthening peace, security and stability, not just regionally, but also worldwide, whilst alongside its partners and the international community, the country continues to assist the Sudanese people in times of crisis.” The health supplies include enough trauma, emergency surgical supplies, and essential medicines to immediately reach 165 000 people who are in desperate need of humanitarian aid. Health facilities across the country report they have run out of basic health supplies and that national medical stores are no longer accessible due to the security situation. Among other support, WHO is deploying two emergency logisticians travelling with the cargo to ensure that these supplies are immediately distributed to 13 major health facilities to support healthcare workers and extend care to those in need.
translations.hypothesis: The UAE has announced a significant increase in humanitarian aid for vulnerable groups affected by the ongoing crisis in Sudan, particularly focusing on refugees, children, the elderly, and women who are most at risk due to the escalating conflict. These emergency supplies are crucial for addressing the most pressing needs in the areas of health and food security. The UAE's commitment reflects its dedication to working with the international community to provide urgent assistance, promote peace, and ensure the safety and well-being of those affected, both regionally and globally, in collaboration with its partners and international organizations. The country is committed to supporting the people of Sudan during this difficult time. The aid includes medical supplies, essential food items, and other critical resources to assist the estimated 165,000 people who urgently need humanitarian assistance. Hospitals across the country have reported shortages of essential medical supplies, and the ongoing conflict has exacerbated existing health challenges. In addition to the initial aid, the WHO is sending two emergency medical teams via cargo plane to ensure that these supplies are distributed to the 13 most affected hospitals and to provide support to healthcare workers and those in need.

translations.source: WHO ní tọ́ọ́nù mẹ́tíríkì 30 àwọn ohun èlò mìíràn fún àìsàn ibà àti àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn bíi àtọgbẹ àti ẹ̀jẹ̀-ríru, àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tó lè ṣokùnfà ikú tí wọ́n bá fi sílẹ̀ láì tọ́jú. Àwọn wọ̀nyìí àti bíi 23 000 àpò ẹ̀jẹ̀ ni wọ́n ń múra rẹ̀ sílẹ̀ láàrín ibùdó ohun èlo àgbáyé WHO ní Agbègbè Ọmọnìyàn Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni WHO ń ṣàmúlò gbogbo ọ̀nà fún ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn ohun èlò wọ̀nyìí sí orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan ní kíákíá tó bá ṣe é ṣe ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba tó ń rí sí Ètò Ìlera ní orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan. Dr Michael Ryan, Olùdarí Aláṣẹ, fún Àwọn Èto Pàjáwírì Ìlera WHO, sọ wí pé, "Ibùdó Ohun Èlò Àgbáyé WHO ní UAE ti di ilé-iṣẹ́ fún ìdáhùn ìfiránṣẹ́ ojú ẹsẹ̀ fún àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ alágbára jákèjádò àgbáyé. Ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún 140 lé àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè jákèjádò gbogbo àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè WHO 6, ibùdó náà ń pèsè ọ̀nà àbáyọ kan fún àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ tí wọ́n ń kojú àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera látara ìtànkálẹ̀ ààrùn, àwọn àjálù burúkú bíi ẹ̀kún omi àti ilẹ̀ rírì, àti ipa rògbòdìyàn lórí àwọn ètò ìlera. Àtìlẹ́yìn tí UAE ń pèsè jẹ́ kí WHO lè léwájú àwọn ìdáhùn pàjáwírì ìlera wọ̀nyìí - fífi ìpèsè ìrànwọ́ ajẹmọ́-ìlera akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ránṣẹ́ sí àwọn tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀ níbikíbi àti nígbà-kúgbà tí wọ́n bá nílò rẹ̀.
translations.reference: WHO has another 30 metric tons of supplies for malaria and noncommunicable diseases like diabetes and hypertension, conditions that can become deadly if left untreated. These and about 23 000 blood bags are being readied within the WHO global logistics hub in the International Humanitarian City, and WHO is currently exploring all possibilities for delivering these supplies to Sudan as quickly as possible in collaboration with the Sudanese Ministry of Health. Dr Michael Ryan, Executive Director, of the WHO Health Emergencies Programme, said, “The WHO Global Logistics Hub in the UAE has become a centerpiece of rapid supply response to acute events globally. Supporting over 140 countries across all 6 WHO geographic regions, the hub provides a lifeline to Member States experiencing health emergencies arising from outbreaks of diseases, disasters such as floods and earthquakes, and the impact of conflict on health systems. The support provided by the UAE enables WHO to be at the forefront of these health emergency responses – delivering specialist medical aid to those in need wherever and whenever it is needed.
translations.hypothesis: The WHO has deployed 30 tons of essential medical supplies to address the urgent needs of patients suffering from injuries and illnesses resulting from the recent floods and landslides, which have caused widespread devastation and loss of life. These supplies, along with approximately 23,000 water purification tablets, are being distributed through the WHO's network of partners in the Eastern Mediterranean region. The WHO is also working closely with the Sudanese government to ensure the timely delivery of these supplies to the affected areas. Dr. Michael Ryan, Executive Director of the WHO's Health Emergencies Programme, stated, "The WHO's Global Logistics Hub in the UAE has become a critical center for the rapid deployment of medical supplies in response to major global emergencies. By providing support to all 140 WHO member states in the six affected regions, the hub is offering a vital lifeline to countries struggling to cope with the aftermath of the floods, including providing essential supplies like water purification tablets and medical equipment, and supporting their health systems." The support provided by the UAE is enabling the WHO to accelerate the delivery of these life-saving medical supplies to those who need them most, ensuring that they receive the care they need when and where they need it.

translations.source: Lára àwọn ìgbìyànjú láti ró àgbékalẹ̀ àgbáyé fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún pàjáwírì ìlera lágbára, ìdáhùn, àti ìfaradà UAE, WHO, àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ohun èlò láti dé ọ̀dọ àwọn tí wọ́n kángun séwu jùlọ ní àgbáyé ní àsìkò tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀ jùlọ." Ní apá ọ̀dọ tirẹ̀, Dr Ahmed Al-Mandhari, Olùdarí Ẹkùn Agbègbè WHO fún Eastern Mediterranean, sọ wí pé, "Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ń tẹ̀síwájú láti ṣe àmójútó pẹ̀lú àwọn aláṣẹ ètò ìlera orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan láti fi àwọn ohun èlò ìlera pàtàkì ránṣẹ́ nípasẹ̀ gbogbo àwọn ojú ọ̀nà tí ó bá wà. Nítorí rògbòdìyàn náà, àwọn ilé ìwòsàn, àwọn ilé ìtàjà oògùn àti ilé ìwòsàn ètò ìlera alábọ́dé ò ní àwọn oògùn pàtàkì, adóólà-ẹ̀mí mọ́. Ìgúnlẹ̀ àfikún tọ́ọ́nu mẹ́tíríkì 30 ti ohun èlò egbò àti iṣẹ́-abẹ pàjáwírì àti àwọn oògùn pàtàkì máa fún WHO láánfàní láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ilé ìwòsàn pàtàkì 13 àti láti ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera tuntun fún àwọn tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀. Ibùdó ohun èlò àgbáyé wa ní orílẹ̀-èdè Dubai kó ipa pàtàkì láti ṣe àrídájú ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn ohun èlò ìlera láìsí ìdíwọ́ a sì dúpẹ́ fún àtìlẹyìn tí orílẹ̀-èdè United Arab Emirates pèsè láti mú kí ọkọ̀ òfurufú akérò àkọ́kọ́ wa fún àwọn ohun èlò tí wọ́n nílò gidi gan-an yìí ṣe é ṣe.
translations.reference: As part of efforts to strengthen the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response, and resilience the UAE, WHO, and partners are working hand-in-hand to deliver supplies to reach the world’s most vulnerable in their time of greatest need.” For his part, Dr Ahmed Al-Mandhari, WHO Regional Director for the Eastern Mediterranean, said, “The World Health Organization continues to coordinate with the Sudanese health authorities to deliver essential health supplies through all available pathways. Due to the conflict, hospitals, pharmacies and primary health care facilities have stocked out of essential, life-saving medicines. The arrival of an additional 30 metric tons of trauma and emergency surgery supplies as well as essential medicines will enable WHO to support 13 major health facilities and re-establish health care services for those in need. Our global logistics hub in Dubai plays a vital role in ensuring an uninterrupted supply of health commodities and we are grateful for the support provided by the United Arab Emirates to facilitate our first charter flight of these desperately needed supplies.
translations.hypothesis:
In a collaborative effort to strengthen global health security for pandemic preparedness, the UAE, WHO, and partners are working together to mobilize essential medical supplies to reach those most in need around the world.

Dr. Ahmed Al-Mandhari, Regional Director of the WHO Eastern Mediterranean, stated, "The World Health Organization is continuing to work closely with the Sudanese health authorities to ensure the delivery of essential medical supplies through all available channels. Unfortunately, hospitals, pharmacies, and primary healthcare centers are currently lacking critical medications and medical supplies. An additional shipment of 30 million doses of vaccines and essential medical supplies is being prepared by the WHO to support 13 key health facilities and to enable the launch of new health programs for those in need. The global logistics hub in Dubai is playing a crucial role in facilitating the rapid delivery of these medical supplies, and we are grateful for the support of the United Arab Emirates in ensuring that these vital resources reach those who need them most."

translations.source: Àwọn àtìlẹyìn bí irú èyí ni ó máa ń ṣàfihàn àfojúsùn Ẹkùn Agbègbè wa nípa ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn, láti ọ̀dọ gbogbo ènìyàn nínú ìgbésẹ̀." Ìràwọ́ oògùn tí Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé pèsè nípasẹ UAE dúró fún àpapọ 55% nínú gbogbo àwọn ìfiránṣẹ́ oògùn tí wọ́n pèsè fún orílẹ̀-èdè Sudan láti ilẹ̀-òkèèrè ní ọdún 2022, tó ń ṣàfihàn ipò UAE gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ibùdó pàtàkì fún ìrànlọ́wọ́ ọmọnìyàn ní ilẹ̀-òkèèrè.
translations.reference: It is support such as this that shows in action our Regional vision of health for all, by all.” The medical aid provided by the World Health Organization through the UAE accounted for approximately 55% of the total medical supplies provided to Sudan from abroad in 2022, reflecting the UAE's position as a major hub for international humanitarian assistance.
translations.hypothesis: Statements like these highlight the commitment of our regional partners to promoting health for all, from the youngest to the oldest members of society." The vaccine doses, provided by the UAE through the World Health Organization, represent 55% of all vaccine doses delivered to Sudan from donor countries in 2022, demonstrating the UAE's significant role as a key partner in humanitarian aid for the country.

translations.source: Àjọṣepọ̀ lábẹ́lé ṣe pàtàkì fún ìlọsíwájú káàkiri àgbáyé fún SDGs tó jẹmọ́ ìlera Ìjábọ̀ ìtẹ̀síwájú tuntun ni wọ́n jọ ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ lónì láti ọwọ́ àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ 13 tó buwọ́lùwé fún Ètò Ìgbésẹ Àgbáyé fún Ìgbé-ayé Àlàáfíà àti Ìgbáyégbádùn fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn (SDG3 GAP). Ìjábọ̀ náà tí wọ́n pe àkòrí rẹ̀ ní "What worked? What didn't? What's next?" ṣàyẹ̀wo ọdún mẹ́rin iṣẹ́ àjọṣe láti ọ̀dọ àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ lóriṣiríṣi tó ń ṣàfihàn àwọn nǹkan tó ti àti èyí tí kò ṣiṣẹ́ láti ró àjọṣepọ̀ àti àtìlẹ́yìn lágbára láti mú kí ìlọsíwájú orílẹ̀-èdè lọ síbi Àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ (SDG) ajẹmọ́-ìlera yá. Ọdún yìí ló pé ìdajì ọdún lọ síbi SDGs, ṣùgbọ́n gbogbo àgbáyé ṣì ń wà lẹ́yìn láti débi àwọn èròngbà àgbáyé náà. Láti ìgbà tí wọ́n ti ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní ọdún 2019, SDG3 GAP ti fi ààyè gba ìdásílẹ̀ àwọn àgbékalẹ̀ àjọṣepọ̀ tuntun láàrín àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n buwọ́lùwé ní àwọn ààyè pàtàkì bíi ìpèsè owó alálòpẹ́ àti ètò ìlera alábọ́dé pẹ̀lú àwọn mìíràn.
translations.reference: Collaborating locally is key for progress globally towards health-related SDGs A new progress report is being jointly released today by the 13 signatory agencies of the Global Action Plan for Healthy Lives and Well-being for All (SDG3 GAP). The report entitled “What worked? What didn’t? What’s next?” reflects four years of joint work by major multilateral agencies outlining what has and has not worked in strengthening collaboration and support to accelerate country progress towards health-related Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). This year marks the halfway point toward the SDGs, but the world is lagging behind to reach the global goals. Since its launch in 2019, the SDG3 GAP enabled creation of new collaboration structures between the signatory agencies in key areas such as sustainable financing and primary health care among others.
translations.hypothesis: Partnerships are crucial for global progress towards the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), particularly those related to health. A new initiative was launched today by 13 organizations committed to the Global Action Plan for Healthy Lives and Well-being for All (SDG3 GAP). The initiative, titled "What worked? What didn't? What's next?", will review four years of partnership efforts from various organizations to identify what has been effective and what has not in fostering strong partnerships and driving national progress towards the health-related SDGs. While the SDGs were launched a decade ago, the world is still behind in achieving these global targets. Since its launch in 2019, the SDG3 GAP has facilitated the creation of new partnerships among organizations, resulting in significant achievements such as increased financial investments in health and the implementation of innovative health programs.

translations.source: Ó kéré jù àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 67 ni wọ́n kópa nínú ọ̀kan tàbí ọ̀pọ nínú àwọn àkòrí amúṣiṣẹ́ SDG3 GAP. A ti ní ìlọsíwájú pàtàkì, ṣùgbọ́n a ṣì ní ọ̀nà tó jìn láti rìn láti mú àyípadà bá ọ̀nà tí àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ lóriṣiríṣi ń gbà ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè. A gbọ́dọ̀ gbọ́ nǹkan tí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ń sọ fún wa kí á sì gbé ìgbésẹ̀ lábẹ́ ìtọ́nisọ́nà wọn," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO àti Alága fún Ẹgbẹ́ Àwọn Olórí SDG3 GAP ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Mo máa dúpẹ́ lọ́wọ́ àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ fún àjọṣepọ̀ wọn àti fún àyẹ̀wo-ara-ẹni tòótọ́ tó wà nínú ìjábọ̀ yìí. "Ìjábọ̀ náà pèsè àwọn àbá mẹ́fà pàtàkì láti rí i dájú pé àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè jẹ ànfààní látara àtìlẹyìn tó rọrùn àti láti mọ àwọn ìfọkànsìn tí wọ́n ṣe nínú SDG3 GAP.
translations.reference: At least 67 countries engaged in on one or more of the SDG3 GAP accelerator themes. "We have made important progress, but we still have a long path to travel to improve the way that multilateral organizations work together to support countries. We must listen to what countries tell us and act upon their guidance,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General and Chair of the SDG3 GAP Principals Group. “I thank the partners for their collaboration and for the honest self-assessments contained in this report. "The report provides six key recommendations to ensure that countries benefit from a more streamlined support and to realize the commitments made in the SDG3 GAP.
translations.hypothesis: Out of 67 countries participating in one or more of the SDG3 GAP accelerator programs, a significant number have made progress. However, there is still a long way to go to achieve the goals set by these programs, and it is crucial for different organizations to work together to support these countries. "We must listen to what the countries are telling us and work with them to achieve their goals," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the World Health Organization and Chair of the SDG3 GAP Leadership Group. "I am grateful to our partners for their commitment and for the valuable insights contained in this report." The report highlights six key areas that are essential for ensuring that countries benefit from the support they receive and for measuring the impact of their participation in the SDG3 GAP.

translations.source: Àwọn wọ̀nyìí ni: tẹ̀síwájú ìdagbàsókè ìṣẹ̀mi SDG3 GAP fún ìlera pẹ̀lú wíwá ojú ìwòye àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ lórí bí a ṣe ń ṣe àjọṣepọ̀ ní ìpele orílẹ̀-èdè àti ìdáhùn sí àwọn àbá tó fara jọ ọ́; tẹ̀síwájú GAP gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìkànnì fún àjọṣepọ̀; ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àjọṣepọ̀ alágbára ní ìpele orílẹ̀-èdè lórí ètò ìlera alábọ́dé àti láti ṣàmúlò àwọn àkòrí-ọ̀rọ̀ tuntun bíi àwọn ètò ìlera onífaradà ojú ọjọ́; jọ ṣàmúlò àwọn ìlànà tuntun ní ìpele orílẹ̀-èdè gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìlànà àgbékalẹ̀ fún ipa;
translations.reference: These include: continue the SDG3 GAP improvement cycle for health by seeking the views of Member States on how we collaborate at country-level and responding to related recommendations; maintain GAP as a platform for collaboration; foster stronger collaboration at the country level on primary health care and explore new thematic topics such as climate resilient health systems; jointly apply new approaches at country level such as the delivery for impact approach;
translations.hypothesis: These include: advancing the SDG3 GAP for health equity by analyzing the experiences of member states on how to collaborate at the national level and addressing the barriers they face; promoting a comprehensive GAP approach to collaboration; supporting effective national-level collaboration on essential health services and addressing emerging issues such as telehealth; and developing new national-level strategies, such as frameworks for action.

translations.source: gba àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ tó pọ̀ wọlé; kí wọ́n ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti ṣe ìrólágbára fún amóríyá àjọṣepọ̀ nípasẹ adarí òṣèlú, ìdarí ìjọba àti ìpèsè owó láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àjọṣepọ̀ náà. Àwọn kókó ìwádìí ìjábọ̀ náà ni wọ́n máa ṣàfikún rẹ̀ sínú ìjírórò Àpérò SDG Àpéjọ Àpapọ Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé àti Ìpàdé Onípele-Gíga lórí Kíkárí Ìlera Lágbàyé tó ń bọ̀ ní oṣù Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2023. Àwọn oṣù tó ṣíwájú àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ pàtàkì wọ̀nyìí pèsè ànfààní fún ìgbìyànjú àjọṣe fún Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ àti àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ oríṣiríṣi láti wá àwọn ọ̀nà lórí bí wọn ó ṣe ṣe àmúlò àwọn àbá láti pèsè ààyè láti ṣàmúlò àjọṣepọ̀ alágbára àti láti mú kí ìlọsíwájú lọ síbi àwọn SDG ajẹmọ́-ìlera yá nínú abala kejì àkókò SDG. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ fún olóòtú:
translations.reference: engage more with civil society; and work with Member States to strengthen incentives for collaboration through political leadership, governance direction and funding to support collaboration. Key findings of the report will feed into discussions of the next United Nations General Assembly SDG Summit and the High-level Meeting on Universal Health Coverage in September 2023. The months leading up to these key events provide an opportunity for a joint push by Member States and multilateral agencies to identify ways on how to implement the recommendations and to prepare the ground to fully leverage stronger collaboration and accelerate progress towards the health-related SDGs in the second half of the SDG timeframe. Note to the editor:
translations.hypothesis: Invite major social groups; work with member states to strengthen global health security through leadership, governance, and financial investments to support that security. These initiatives will be further detailed in the upcoming report on the Sustainable Development Goals, the High-Level Political Forum on Sustainable Development, and the Global Health Security Summit, scheduled for September 2023. The preceding months will provide opportunities for member states and various organizations to explore ways to accelerate progress towards achieving a more resilient and effective global health security architecture, and to advance the health-related Sustainable Development Goals during the second decade of the SDGs. Key takeaways:

translations.source: SDG3 GAP ṣàpèjúwe bí àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ 13 tó tọwọ́ bọ̀wé ṣe máa ṣàmúlò àwọn ọ̀nà ìṣeṣẹ́ tuntun, gígùn lé àwọn àṣeyọrí àjọṣepọ̀ tó ti wà tẹ́lẹ̀, kí wọ́n sì jọ ṣe àkójọpọ̀ àtìlẹyìn wọn fún àwọn ètò orílẹ̀-èdè àti àwọn ìlàna-iṣẹ́ tí ó jẹ́ ti orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n sì léwájú rẹ̀. Àwọn tó tọwọ́ bọ̀wé fún SDG3 GAP ni Gavi, Ìṣọ̀kan Àjẹsára náà; Ilé-iṣẹ́ Apèsè Owó Àgbáyé fún Àwọn Obìnrin, Àwọn Ọmọdé àti Àwọn Ọ̀dọ́ (GFF); Ìpèsè Owó Àgbáyé láti Gbógun ti KASA, Ikọ́-ife àti àìsàn ibà (Global Fund); Àjọ Àwọn Òṣìṣẹ́ Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè (ILO);
translations.reference: The SDG3 GAP describes how the 13 signatory agencies will adopt new ways of working, building on existing successful collaborations, and jointly align their support around national plans and strategies that are country-owned and -led. The signatories to the SDG3 GAP are Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance; Global Financing Facility for Women, Children and Adolescents (GFF); Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria (Global Fund); International Labour Organization (ILO);
translations.hypothesis: The SDG3 GAP describes how 13 leading philanthropic organizations are leveraging their expertise and past successes to collaborate and pool their resources for national programs and policies that advance health within their respective countries. The philanthropic organizations supporting the SDG3 GAP include: Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance; the Global Fund for Women, Children and Adolescents (GFF); the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria; the International Labour Organization (ILO);

translations.source: Ètò Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé àjọṣe lórí KASA (UNAIDS); Ètò Ìdàgbàsókè Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé (UNDP); Ìpèsè Owó Àwọn Ènìyàn Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé (UNFPA); Ìpèsè Owó fún Àwọn Ọmọdé ti Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé (UNICEF); Unitaid; Ilé-iṣẹ́ Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé fún Ìdọ́gba Takọ-tabo àti Ìrónilágbára fún àwọn Obìnrin (UN Women);
translations.reference: Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS (UNAIDS); United Nations Development Programme (UNDP); United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA); United Nations Children’s Fund (UNICEF); Unitaid; United Nations Entity for Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women (UN Women);
translations.hypothesis:
Here are some organizations:

*   UNAIDS (Joint United Nations Programme on HIV/AIDS)
*   UNDP (United Nations Development Programme)
*   UNFPA (United Nations Population Fund)
*   UNICEF (United Nations Children's Fund)
*   Unitaid
*   UN Women (United Nations Entity for Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women)

translations.source: Ẹgbẹ́ Ilé-ìfowópamọ́ Àgbáyé; Èto Oúnjẹ Àgbáyé (WFP); àti Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Lágbàyé (WHO). Wo ìròyìn kíkún, Kí ló ṣiṣẹ́? Kí ni ó kàn?
translations.reference: World Bank Group; World Food Programme (WFP); and World Health Organization (WHO). See full report, What Worked? What’s next?
translations.hypothesis: The United Nations, the World Food Programme (WFP), and the World Health Organization (WHO). Read the full story: What happened? What caused it?

translations.source: Àbájáde ìwádìí tuntun fi hàn pé àwọn ètò ìlera ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí padàbọ̀sípò lẹ́yìn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn Lẹ́yìn ọdún mẹ́ta àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, àwọn ètò ìlera ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí ṣàfihàn àwọn ààmì pàtàkì àkọ́kọ́ fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò ètò ìlera, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìjábọ̀ fìdíhẹẹ́ WHO lórí "Àtúnṣe kẹrin fún ìwádìí ìsinmi àgbáyé lórí ìtẹ̀síwájú àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì ní àkókò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19: oṣù Bẹlu ọdún 2022-oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2023". Ní ìbẹ̀rẹ ọdún 2023, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè jábọ̀ kíkojú àdínkù àwọn ìdíwọ́ nínú àgbékalẹ̀ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ ìlera, ṣùgbọ́n ṣàfihàn ìdí láti dókówò nínú ìpadàbọ̀sípò àti ìfaradà tó lágbára fún ọjọ́ iwájú. Láàrín àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 139 tí wọ́n fésì sí àtúnṣe kẹrin fún ìwádìí ìsinmi WHO, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè jábọ̀ ìtẹ̀síwájú àwọn ìdíwọ́ nínú bíi ìdá kan nínú mẹ́rin ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ní òdiwọn. Ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 84 níbi tí àlàyé ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ti ṣe é ṣe, ìdá àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ tó ní ìdíwọ́ dínkù ní òdiwọ̀n láti 56% ní oṣù Agẹmọ-Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2020 sí 23% ní oṣù Bẹlu ọdún 2022- oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2023.
translations.reference: New survey results show health systems starting to recover from pandemic After three years of the COVID-19 pandemic, health systems in countries have started showing the first major signs of health system recovery, according to the WHO interim report on the “Fourth round of the global pulse survey on continuity of essential health services during the COVID-19 pandemic: November 2022–January 2023”. By early 2023, countries reported experiencing reduced disruptions in the delivery of routine health services, but highlighted the need to invest in recovery and stronger resilience for the future. Among the 139 countries that responded to the fourth round of WHO’s pulse survey, countries reported continued disruptions in almost one-quarter of services on average. In 84 countries where trend analysis is possible, the percentage of disrupted services declined on average from 56% in July-September 2020 to 23% in November 2022- January 2023.
translations.hypothesis: A recent report indicates that health systems are beginning to recover from the disruptions caused by the COVID-19 pandemic. Three years after the start of the pandemic, health systems in many countries are showing signs of improvement, according to a recent WHO report on "The fourth progress report on global health security: advancing essential health services during the COVID-19 pandemic: April 2022-March 2023." At the beginning of 2023, countries reported increased investments in health systems, but also highlighted challenges in sustaining recovery and building resilience for the future. Of the 139 countries that reported to the WHO's fourth progress report, countries reported increased investments in at least one of the four essential health services. In the 84 countries for which data were available, the proportion of essential health services with increased investment rose from 56% in September 2020 to 23% in April 2022-March 2023.

translations.source: Àwọn ìdíwọ́ tó ń tẹ̀síwájú jẹ́ nítorí àwọn aṣokùnfà ìbéèrè- àti apá-ìdáhùn, tó fi mọ́ àwọn ìpele kékeré wíwá-ètò ìlera ní àwọn àwùjọ bákan náà ni àìtó àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera àti àwọn ohun èlò ìtọ́jú mìíràn bíi àwọn ilé ìwòsàn tó ṣí sílẹ̀ tàbí àwọn ìfipamọ́ oògùn àti àwọn ohun èlò tí ó wà. "Ó jẹ́ ìròyìn ayọ̀ pé àwọn ètò ìlera ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí ṣe ìdápadà àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì fún mílíọ̀nù àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ṣe àfẹ́rí wọn ní àkókò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà," Dr Rudi Eggers, Olùdarí fún Ìpèsè iṣẹ́ Ìlera Aláfibọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Ṣùgbọ́n a nílò láti rí i dájú pé gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tẹ̀síwájú láti dí àlàfo yìí láti ṣe àdápadà àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera, àti láti ṣàmúlò àwọn ẹ̀kọ́ tí wọ́n kọ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ètò ìlera tó múra sílẹ̀ tó sì ní àfaradà gidi gan-an fún ọjọ́ iwájú". Àwọn ààmì ìdánimọ̀ àkọ́kọ́ fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò Nínú ìjábọ̀ tuntun yìí, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè díẹ̀ jábọ̀ mí mọ̀ọ́mọ̀ ṣe ìgbéléwọ̀n ànfààní padà jákèjádò gbogbo àwọn ìkànnì fún ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ iṣẹ́ àti àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera àwùjọ tó ṣe pàtàkì láti ìjábọ̀ ọdún 2020-2021, tó ń ṣàfihàn ìgbésẹ̀ pàtàkì láti padà sí àwọn ìpele ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ iṣẹ́ ṣíwájú-àjàkálẹ̀-ààrùn àti ìṣiṣẹ́ ètò tó fẹjú.
translations.reference: Persisting disruptions are due to both demand- and supply-side factors, including low levels of health care-seeking in communities as well as limited availability of health workers and other health-care resources such as open clinics or available stocks of medicines and products. “It is welcome news that health systems in the majority of countries are starting to restore essential health services for millions of people who missed them during the pandemic,” said Dr Rudi Eggers, WHO Director for Integrated Health Services. “But we need to ensure that all countries continue to close this gap to recover health services, and apply lessons learnt to build more prepared and resilient health systems for the future”. First notable signs of recovery In this new survey, fewer countries reported intentionally scaling back access across all service delivery platforms and essential public health functions since 2020-2021 reporting, showing an important step to return to pre-pandemic levels of service delivery and broader system functioning.
translations.hypothesis: The progress is driven by the increased availability of data and resources, which are enabling smaller countries to implement effective health programs. This has led to a reduction in the number of healthcare workers and other resources, such as hospitals and clinics, that are needed to provide care. "It is encouraging to see that health programs in many countries are beginning to deliver essential health services to millions of people who were previously underserved during the pandemic," said Dr. Rudi Eggers, Director for Health Workforce at the World Health Organization. "However, we need to ensure that all countries continue to invest in these programs, and that they build on the lessons learned to create sustainable health systems that are truly fit for the future." Key indicators of progress In a new report, a number of countries have reported significant improvements in key indicators for health service delivery and community health programs compared to the 2020-2021 report, which highlighted the challenges of providing care during the pandemic and the impact on program operations.

translations.source: Ní ìparí ọdún 2022, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ni wọ́n jábọ̀ àwọn ààmì díẹ̀ fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò ìpèsè iṣẹ́, tó fi mọ́ nínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ fún ìlera ìbálòpọ̀, ìbímọ, ìyá, ọmọtuntun, ọmọ àti ọ̀dọ́; aṣaralóore; àjẹsára; àwọn ààrùn tó máa ń ràn (tó fi mọ́ àìsàn ibà, KASA TB, àti àwọn ààrùn látara ìbálòpọ̀ mìíràn); àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì; àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn; àmójútó ọpọlọpọ, àwọn ààrùn ajẹmọ́-ọpọlọ àti ìlòkulò oògùn; ìtọ́jú fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ti darúgbó; àti ìtọ́jú ìbílẹ̀ àti/tàbí ìbádọ́gba. Iye àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n ń jábọ̀ ìdíwọ́ fún ètò orílẹ̀-èdè wọn dínkù láti bíi ìdajì (29 nínú 59 àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n ń fèsì) sí bíi ìdámẹ́rin (18 nínú 66 àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tó ń fèsì) láàrín ọdún tí ó kọjá. Pẹ̀lú àwọn ààmì ìpadàbọ̀sípò, àwọn ìdíwọ́ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ṣì ń wáyé jákèjádò àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ní gbogbo àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè àti àwọn ìpele ìpawówọlé, àti jákèjádò ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ààyè ìṣe àgbékalẹ̀ iṣẹ́ àti àwọn ààyè iṣẹ́ ìtọpinpin. Àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tún kojú àlékún àṣepatì iṣẹ́ - ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìgbà níní ìpèsè iṣẹ́ fún àyẹ̀wò, àyẹ̀wò fún idámọ̀ ààrùn àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn - tí ó lè yọrí sí nǹkan tí ò dára pẹ̀lu bí àwọn ènìyàn bá ṣe ń ní ìdíwọ́ ànfààní sí ìtọ́jú lásìkò. Ìpadàbọ̀sípò àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì ṣe pàtàkì nítorí àwọn ìdíwọ́ - tó fi mọ́ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ fún ìpolongo ìlera, ìdènà ààrùn, àyẹ̀wò fún idámọ̀ ààrùn, ìtọ́jú, ìtúnirọ àti ẹ̀rọ̀ - lè ṣe àkóbá tó burú púpò fún ìlera lára àwọn ènìyàn àti ìpele ẹnìkan ju àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn fúnra rẹ̀ lọ, pàápàá jùlọ láàrín àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n kángun séwu.
translations.reference: By the end of 2022, most countries reported partial signs of service recovery, including in services for sexual, reproductive, maternal, newborn, child and adolescent health; nutrition; immunization; communicable diseases (including malaria, HIV, TB, and other sexually-transmitted infections); neglected tropical diseases; noncommunicable diseases; management of mental, neurological and substance use disorders; care for older people; and traditional and/or complementary care. The number of countries reporting disruption to their national supply chain system reduced from nearly half (29 of 59 responding countries) to about a quarter (18 of 66 responding countries) within the last year. Despite signs of recovery, service disruptions persist across countries in all regions and income levels, and across most service delivery settings and tracer service areas. Countries are also dealing with increasing service backlogs – most frequently in services for screening, diagnosis and treatment of noncommunicable diseases – which can lead to negative consequences as people are delayed access to timely care. Recovering essential health service delivery is critical because disruptions – including to services for health promotion, disease prevention, diagnosis, treatment, rehabilitation and palliation – may have even greater adverse health effects at population and individual level than the pandemic itself, especially among vulnerable populations.
translations.hypothesis: In 2022, many countries allocated a portion of their health budgets to service delivery, focusing on essential health services for women, children, adolescents, and older adults; immunization; neglected tropical diseases (including malaria, tuberculosis, and other waterborne diseases); chronic diseases; non-communicable diseases; mental health; nutrition; emergency preparedness and response; and access to essential medicines and technologies. The proportion of countries allocating funds to their national health programs ranged from a low of 29 out of 59 countries surveyed to a high of 44 out of 66 countries surveyed over the past year. Alongside these allocations, health service delivery continued to be unevenly distributed across regions and levels of care, and across various health service areas and programs. Countries also reported challenges in service delivery – particularly in areas such as immunization, disease screening, and management of non-communicable diseases – which may be linked to factors such as limited access to timely and appropriate care. Strategic investments in health service delivery are crucial because investments in essential health services – including primary care, disease prevention, screening, treatment, rehabilitation, and technology – can have a profound and lasting impact on the health of individuals and populations, often exceeding the benefits of disease-specific interventions, particularly among vulnerable populations.

translations.source: Ṣíṣe àfikún àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ COVID-19 sínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì Nínú ìgbésẹ̀ pàtàkì mìíràn fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò ètò àti ìṣípòrọpò, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ti ní ìlọsíwájú nínú àfikún ìpèsè iṣẹ́ COVID-19 sínú ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àgbékalẹ̀ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera. Bíi 80-90% àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ni wọ́n ti ṣe àfikún ìpèsè iṣẹ́ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára COVID-19, àyẹ̀wò fún idámọ̀ ààrùn àti àmójútó ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ pátápátá bákan náà ni ìpèsè iṣẹ́ fún ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ lẹ́yin COVID-19 sínú àgbékalẹ̀ ìlànà-iṣẹ́. Síbẹ̀, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè (80% nínú 83 àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n ń fèsì) jábọ̀ pé ó kéré jù ìdíwọ́ kan fún ṣíṣe ìfẹ̀lójú ànfààní sí àwọn irinṣẹ́ COVID-19 tó ṣe pàtàkì (b.a. àyẹ̀wò fún ìdámọ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, ìtọ́jú, àjẹsára àti àwọn irinṣẹ́ ìdáàbòbò àdáni - PPE), pẹ̀lú Ìṣòro iye àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera àti àìsí ìpèsè owó tó dúró fún àwọn ìdíwọ́ tó wọ́pọ̀ júlọ. A nílò ìtẹ̀síwájú àtìlẹyìn fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò, ìfaradà àti ìmúrasílẹ̀ Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí ṣàmúlò nǹkan tí wọ́n kọ́ ní àkókò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, tó fi mọ́ nípa ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìdíwọ́ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun tó ń ṣe àkóbá fún àwọn ìlànà-iṣẹ́ sínú àgbékalẹ̀ ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera.
translations.reference: Integrating COVID-19 services into essential health services In another important step towards system recovery and transition, most countries have made progress in integrating COVID-19 services into routine health service delivery. About 80-90% of countries have fully integrated COVID-19 vaccination, diagnostic and case management services as well as services for post COVID-19 condition into routine service delivery. Still, most countries (80% of 83 responding countries) reported at least one bottleneck to scaling up access to essential COVID-19 tools (e.g. COVID-19 diagnostics, therapeutics, vaccines and personal protective equipment - PPE), with health workforce issues and lack of funding representing the most common barriers. Further support needed for recovery, resilience and preparedness Most countries have started to apply what they have learnt during the COVID-19 pandemic, including through the institutionalization of a number of innovative service disruption mitigation strategies into routine health service delivery.
translations.hypothesis:
Integrating COVID-19 Workflows into Essential Health Service Workflows

In another significant step towards streamlining processes and improving efficiency, many countries are making progress in integrating COVID-19-related workflows into existing health service workflows. Approximately 80-90% of countries have incorporated COVID-19 vaccination, testing, and contact tracing workflows into their health service systems. Furthermore, workflows for managing post-COVID-19 conditions are also being integrated. However, a significant number of countries (80% of the 83 countries surveyed) reported that there are still challenges in scaling up these essential COVID-19 services (e.g., COVID-19 testing, treatment, vaccination, and personal protective equipment), primarily due to workforce shortages and insufficient funding for these expanded services. Continued investment and support are needed for sustainable implementation, expansion, and maintenance. Many countries are leveraging lessons learned during the COVID-19 pandemic to improve the integration of various essential health services into the health service system.

translations.source: Nínú èyí ni ìṣàmúlò àwọn ìlànà ìṣẹ̀gùn orí ẹ̀rọ ayélujára, ìpolongo ìtọ́jú ajẹmọ́-ilé tàbí àwọn ìdásí ìtọ́jú-ara-ẹni, àwọn ìlànà fún ríró wíwà níkálẹ̀ àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera lágbára, àwọn ìkápá àti àwọn ètò àtìlẹ́yìn, àwọn ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun fún rírà àti ṣíṣe ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn oògùn àti àwọn ohun èlò, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìlàna-iṣẹ́ ìfọ̀rọ̀wérò àwùjọ, àti àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn olùpèsè ní ẹ̀ka aládàni. Ìdá mẹ́ta nínú mẹ́rin àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ni wọ́n jábọ̀ àfikún ìpèsè owó fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò, ìfaradà àti ìmúrasílẹ̀ ètò ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́. Àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ṣàlàyé ìdí fún àtìlẹ́yìn WHO láti kojú àwọn ìpèníjà tí ó kù ní ọ̀gangan ipò COVID-19 àti jù bẹ́ẹ̀ lọ, ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìgbà tí ó ní ṣe pẹ̀lú ìrólágbára àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera, ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìkápá àmójútó fún ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera, ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò ìlera alábọ́dé -- àwọn àwòṣe ìtọ́jú tó dọ́gba, ìṣèjọba, òfin àti ètò ṣíṣe àti ṣíṣe ètò owó àti ìpèsè owó. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ fún àwọn olóòtú: Nínú abala kẹrin ìwádìí ìsinmi àgbáyé WHO, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 222, àwọn agbègbè àti àwọn ààyè ni wọ́n fi ìwé pè láti fèsì sí ìwádìí orí-ibùdó-ìtàkùn tó jẹ́ ojúlówó láàrín oṣù Bẹlu àti oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2023. Ìwádìí kẹrin ṣe ìtẹ̀lé lórí àwọn ìwádìí ìsinmi àtẹ̀yìnwá ọdún 2020 àti 2021 WHO: Abala 1 (oṣù Èbìbí-Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2020), Abala 2 (oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ-Èrenà ọdún 2021), àti Abala 3 (oṣù Bẹlu-Ọ̀pẹ ọdún 2021) tí ó ṣàfihàn bí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà ṣe ń kóbá ìtẹ̀síwájú àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì àti bí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ṣe ń gbé ìgbésẹ̀.
translations.reference: These include deployment of telemedicine approaches, promotion of home-based care or self-care interventions, approaches for strengthening health workers availability, capacities and support mechanisms, innovations in procuring and delivering medicines and supplies, more routine community communications, and partnerships with private sector providers. Three quarters of countries reported additional funding allocation towards longer term system recovery, resilience and preparedness. Countries expressed need for WHO support to address remaining challenges in the COVID-19 context and beyond, most frequently related to health workforce strengthening, building the monitoring capacities of health services, designing primary health care -- oriented models of care, governance, policy and planning and financial planning and funding. Note to editors: In the fourth round of WHO’s global pulse survey, 222 countries, territories and areas were invited to respond to a standardized web-based survey between November 2022 and January 2023. The fourth survey followed up on WHO’s previous 2020 and 2021 pulse surveys: Round 1 (May-September 2020), Round 2 (January-March 2021), and Round 3 (November-December 2021) which showed the extent to which the pandemic was affecting the continuity of essential health services and how countries are taking action.
translations.hypothesis: This includes the implementation of digital health strategies, telemedicine programs, or other remote care solutions; initiatives to strengthen the capacity of the health workforce; the development and deployment of vaccines and essential medicines; various public health interventions; and partnerships with the private sector. Three out of four countries reported increased investment in health system strengthening, pandemic preparedness, and long-term health planning. Countries highlighted the importance of WHO support in addressing the ongoing challenges of the COVID-19 pandemic, including workforce shortages, the development of essential health services, the establishment of robust health systems – encompassing prevention, treatment, governance, and financing – and the mobilization of resources and financial support. Key takeaways for leaders: In the fourth report of the WHO's Thirteenth General Programme of Work, 222 countries, areas, and entities have committed to addressing the critical health priorities outlined between January and September 2023. The fourth report follows up on the WHO's 2020 and 2021 Biennial Reports: Part 1 (February 2020), Part 2 (September 2021), and Part 3 (September 2021), which detailed the impact of the pandemic on essential health services and the progress countries were making.

translations.source: Nígbà tí ìwádìí ìsinmi ní àwọn kùdìẹ̀-kudiẹ bíi ìjábọ̀ aṣègbè àti ṣíṣe aṣojú, agbára ìgbìyànjú yìí ni wí pé ó kún fọ́fọ́ ó sì ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìròyìn kíákíá. Ọ̀rọ̀ "orílẹ̀-èdè" ni a gbọ́dọ̀ ní òye rẹ̀ pé ó ní àfikún gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè, àwọn agbègbè àti àwọn ààyè. Wọ́n parí àlàyé ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ náà pẹ̀lú ìdáhùn láti àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 84, àwọn agbègbè àti ààyè tí wọ́n dáhùn sí ó kéré jù abala ìwádìí kan nínú gbogbo àwọn àṣetẹ̀léra ìwádìí ìsinmi mẹ́rin náà.
translations.reference: While pulse surveys have limitations such as reporting bias and representativeness, the strength of this effort is that it is comprehensive and delivers information rapidly. The term “country” should be understood to include all countries, territories and areas. Trend analyses was completed based on responses from 84 countries, territories, or areas that responded to at least one survey section in all four pulse survey rounds.
translations.hypothesis: While news agencies like local newspapers and online platforms play a crucial role, this particular source is known for its in-depth reporting and comprehensive coverage. The term "country" should be understood to encompass all nations, regions, and territories. The report details events with contributions from 84 countries, regions, and territories, making it one of the most extensive reports among the four major news agencies.

translations.source: Àlàyé ti ayẹyẹ fún àjọ̀dún 50th HRP: Sweden àti United Kingdom of Great Britain àti Northern Ireland Láti ṣàlàyé ìdùnnú àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ Ìgbìmọ̀ fún Òfin àti Ìdarí (PCC) tí wọ́n ṣe ayẹyẹ àjọ̀dún 50th HRP àti àwọn àṣeyọrí rẹ̀ lóríṣiríṣi, Sweden àti United Kingdom of Great Britain àti Northern Ireland ka ìwé ìfi ẹ̀mí ìmoore hàn àti ìkíni kú orí-ire níbi ìpàdé 36th HRP PPC. Ìran wa ti rí ìlọsíwájú tó pọ̀ nínú ìmọ̀ sáyẹ́nsì àti ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ ju gbogbo àwọn ìran tí wọ́n ti gbawájú lápapọ̀ lọ a sì ti rí oríṣiríṣi àyípadà ọgbọ́n, ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ, ìṣègùn àti àwùjọ tí àyípadà àwọn ohun adèna oyún léwájú rẹ̀ tí àwọn ìlọsíwájú nínú ìlera ìbímọ, àwọn ìlera ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun náà sì tẹ̀lé e bákan náà ni àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ àti ìdọ́gba takọ-tabo. Síbẹ̀síbẹ̀, àwọn ìpèníjà tó pọ̀ ní ẹ̀ka SRHR ń tẹ̀síwájú, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni, ó ṣe é ṣe, àwọn ìlọsíwájú kọ̀ọ̀kan tún wà nínú ìbéèrè lónì. A gbóríyìn fún àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ HRP, àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ àti àwọn alátílẹyìn lórí àìmọye àṣeyọrí títí di òní a sì mọ rírì àwọn ìgbéṣẹ̀ ńlá tí wọ́n ti gbé ní àkókò ìdajì ọgọ́run ọdún nínú ìpèsè ẹ̀rí tó ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn kíkún àti ànfààní tó dọ́gba sí ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìbímọ àti àwọn ẹ̀tọ́.
translations.reference: Statement of celebration for HRP’s 50th anniversary: Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland To capture the spirit of Policy and Coordination Committee (PCC) members celebrating HRP’s 50th anniversary and its numerous achievements, Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland read the following note of appreciation and congratulations at the 36th meeting of the HRP PCC. Our generation has seen more progress in science and technology than all preceding generations together and witnessed several intellectual, technological, medical and social transformation spearheaded by the contraceptive revolution and followed by advances in reproductive health, health innovations as well as rights and gender equality. However, considerable challenges in the field of SRHR continue, and, arguably, some advances are in question again today. We applaud HRP-staff, partners and supporters on the numerous achievements to date and recognise the enormous strides accomplished during half a century in producing the evidence that supports full and equal access to sexual and reproductive health and rights.
translations.hypothesis:
Celebration of the 50th Anniversary of HRP: Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland.

To commemorate the 50th anniversary of the Human Rights Programme (HRP) and its various achievements, Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland presented a commemorative book and a plaque at the 36th HRP Partner Consultative Committee (PPC) meeting.

We have witnessed significant advancements in science and technology, exceeding all previous expectations. We have also seen remarkable progress in education, innovation, and social development, driven by the advancement of new technologies, particularly in areas such as healthcare, medical research, and human rights. However, significant challenges remain in the field of sexual and reproductive health and rights (SRHR), and progress in these areas is uneven.

We commend the staff of HRP, its partners, and its supporters for their dedication to achieving these successes, and we recognize the significant contributions that have been made over the past five decades to promoting health, well-being, and human rights.

translations.source: Yóò wùn wá láti ṣàlàyé àwọn ìsọ̀rí àṣeyọrí pàtàkì mẹ́ta: Akọ́kọ́, ìwádìí ojúlówó ńlá tí HRP ṣe ní ipa pàtàkì lórí òfin ìlera àwujọ, àwọn òdiwọ̀n, àti ìfikọ́ra jákèjádò àgbáyé tó ń kojú àwọn ìdí fún oríṣiríṣi àwọn ènìyàn tó ń yí padà. Ẹ̀kejì, HRP, ní pàtàkì jùlọ nípasẹ ìṣọ̀kan HRP, ti ṣe ìrólágbára fún àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ ìwádìí lábẹ́lé àti káàkiri àgbáyé, ó sì ti, jẹ́ kí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn oníwádìí ní àwọn ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe ìwádìí tí ó ń rí i dájú pé ìwádìí SRHR ojúlówó-ńlá lè tẹ̀síwájú, àti pẹ̀lu bẹ́ẹ̀ náà lékún, lọ sí ọjọ́ iwájú.
